\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} (w_j^{T} x_i) w_j \right\rVert^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} (w_j^{T} x_i)^{2} \quad \text{(orthonormality)} \\
&= \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} w_j^{T} \Sigma w_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the trace property and since $\sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j^{T} \Sigma w_j = \text{Tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ (constant):

$$
\min J \iff \max \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j^{T} \Sigma w_j
$$

This confirms **MaxVariance = MinReconstructionError**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Given 2D data points: $X = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5)\}$, find the first principal component manually.

**Step 1 — Compute Mean:**

$$\mu = \left( \frac{1+2+3+4}{4}, \frac{2+3+4+5}{4} \right) = (2.5, 3.5)$$

**Step 2 — Center the Data:**

$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -1.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Covariance Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{T} X_c = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} (-1.5)^{2}+(-0.5)^{2}+(0.5)^{2}+(1.5)^{2} & \text{same} \\ \text{same} & \text{same} \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 & 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 \\ 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 & 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 1.667 \\ 1.667 & 1.667 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:** For the matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & a \\ a & a \end{bmatrix}$ with $a = 1.667$:

- Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 2a = 3.333$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$
- First eigenvector: $w_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (1, 1)^{T} = (0.707, 0.707)^{T}$

**Step 5 — Variance Explained:**

$$R_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{3.333}{3.333 + 0} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

This means **1 principal component captures 100% of variance** — the data lies on a perfect line $y = x$, which PCA correctly identifies.

## 3.4 Choosing the Number of Components $k$ — Kaiser Criterion & Scree Plot

**Method 1 — Kaiser Rule:** Retain components with $\lambda_i > 1$ (when working with standardized data).

**Method 2 — Cumulative Variance Threshold:** Pick smallest $k$ such that $R_k \geq 0.95$ (95% rule).

**Method 3 — Scree Plot Inspection:** Plot $\lambda_i$ vs $i$; choose $k$ at the "elbow" of the curve.

**Method 4 — Cross-Validation:** Test downstream classifier performance for various $k$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw High Dim Data X n by d"] --> B["Step 1 Standardize or Mean Center"]
    B --> C["Centered Data Xc n by d"]
    C --> D["Step 2 Compute Covariance Matrix"]
    D --> E["Covariance Matrix Sigma d by d"]
    E --> F["Step 3 Eigendecomposition"]
    F --> G["Eigenvalues lambda 1 to d"]
    F --> H["Eigenvectors v 1 to d"]
    G --> I["Step 4 Sort by Descending Lambda"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Step 5 Select Top k Components"]
    J --> K["Projection Matrix W d by k"]
    K --> L["Step 6 Project Data"]
    L --> M["Reduced Data Y n by k"]
    M --> N["Output Low Dim Representation"]
    N --> O["Optional Step 7 Inverse Transform for Reconstruction"]
    O --> P["Reconstructed Xhat n by d"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Stage"]
        A1["Original Feature Vector x in R d"]
    end
    subgraph PreProc["Preprocessing Block"]
        B1["Mean Centering x minus mu"]
        B2["Optional Z Score Standardization"]
    end
    subgraph CoreEng["PCA Engine"]
        C1["Covariance Estimator Sigma"]
        C2["Eigensolver via SVD or QR"]
        C3["Eigenvalue Sorter Descending"]
        C4["Top k Selector"]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage["Output Stage"]
        D1["Projection Matrix W"]
        D2["Score Matrix Y equals Xc W"]
        D3["Explained Variance Report"]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    B2 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Geometry of PCA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OriginalSpace["Original High Dim Space d dimensions"]
        P1["Data Point x i"]
        V1["Eigenvector v 1 First PC Direction Max Variance"]
        V2["Eigenvector v 2 Second PC Orthogonal to v1"]
    end
    subgraph ProjSpace["Reduced Space k dimensions"]
        Q1["Score y i equals W transpose x i"]
    end
    P1 -- "Linear Projection" --> Q1
    V1 -- "Defines Axis 1" --> Q1
    V2 -- "Defines Axis 2" --> Q1
```

## 4.4 Decision Diagram — Choosing $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start Compute All Eigenvalues"] --> Q1{"Total Components d"}
    Q1 --> Kaiser["Kaiser Rule Keep lambda greater than 1"]
    Q1 --> Cumul["Cumulative Variance Pick k where Rk exceeds 0.95"]
    Q1 --> Scree["Scree Plot Pick k at Elbow Point"]
    Q1 --> CV["Cross Validation Optimize Downstream Task Metric"]
    Kaiser --> Final["Final k Selected"]
    Cumul --> Final
    Scree --> Final
    CV --> Final
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two advantages of using PCA in machine learning.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a linear unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique that transforms a $d$-dimensional dataset into a $k$-dimensional ($k \ll d$) representation by projecting it onto the directions of **maximum variance**, called principal components. These directions are the **eigenvectors** of the data's covariance matrix.

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Reduces computational cost** by lowering the input dimension for downstream ML models.
2. **Removes multicollinearity** by producing orthogonal (uncorrelated) components.
3. *Bonus point:* Mitigates the **curse of dimensionality** and enables visualization of high-dimensional data in 2D/3D.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the significance of eigenvalues and eigenvectors in PCA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In PCA, eigendecomposition is performed on the covariance matrix $\Sigma$.

- **Eigenvectors** (1.5 marks): Each eigenvector $v_i$ represents a **principal component direction** — a unit vector in feature space along which the data has a specific variance. They form an orthonormal basis for the new feature space.

- **Eigenvalues** (1.5 marks): Each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ is the **magnitude of variance** captured by its corresponding eigenvector. The relative size $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$ gives the **explained variance ratio**, used to rank components and decide $k$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse eigenvectors with principal components. **Eigenvector = direction (unit vector).** Principal Component = the **new axis (the eigenvector itself)**, while the **scores** $Y = XW$ are the **coordinates of data along that axis**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Derive the mathematical formulation of Principal Component Analysis using the maximum variance approach. Show that the optimal projection direction is the eigenvector of the covariance matrix corresponding to the largest eigenvalue.**

#### **Part (a) — Max-Variance Formulation & Lagrangian Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):**
Given centered data $\{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $\sum x_i = 0$, find unit vector $w$ ($\lVert w \rVert = 1$) that maximizes the variance of projections $\{w^{T} x_i\}$.

**Step 2 — Variance Computation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Var}(w^{T} x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w^{T} x_i)^{2} = w^{T} \Sigma w$$

where $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n} X^{T} X$ is the covariance matrix.

**Step 3 — Constrained Optimization (2 marks):**

$$\max_{w} \; w^{T} \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{T} w = 1$$

Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{T} \Sigma w - \lambda (w^{T} w - 1)$.

**Step 4 — Solving the KKT Conditions (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{w} \mathcal{L} &= 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \Sigma w &= \lambda w
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $w$ must be an eigenvector of $\Sigma$. Substituting back: $w^{T} \Sigma w = \lambda w^{T} w = \lambda$.

To **maximize** variance, choose $w = v_1$ — the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$.

#### **Part (b) — Extension to $k$ Components & Algorithm (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Multi-Component Setup (2 marks):**
For $k$ components, maximize:

$$\max_{W} \; \text{Tr}(W^{T} \Sigma W) \quad \text{subject to} \quad W^{T} W = I_k$$

Solution: $W = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$, the top-$k$ eigenvectors.

**Step 2 — Complete Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Standardize: $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$
2. Compute covariance: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} Z^{T} Z$
3. Solve $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ for all $i$
4. Sort eigenvalues descending: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$
5. Form $W = [v_1, \dots, v_k]$
6. Compute scores: $Y = Z W$

**Step 3 — Choosing $k$ (2 marks):**

- Kaiser rule: keep $\lambda_i > 1$
- Cumulative variance: smallest $k$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i \geq 0.95$
- Scree plot: locate the "elbow"

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Failing to **mean-center** the data before computing $\Sigma$. Without centering, the first PC points toward the mean, not the variance direction.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to **standardize** when features have different units. PCA becomes dominated by high-magnitude features.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost):** Stating "eigenvector = principal component" without distinguishing eigenvector (axis) from scores (projected coordinates). Use precise terminology.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost):** Not mentioning that **PCs are uncorrelated** (orthogonal) by construction — a key advantage.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Explain the reconstruction-error view of PCA. Prove its equivalence to the maximum variance formulation. Discuss the role of the SVD alternative for numerical stability.**

#### **Part (a) — Reconstruction Error Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Given centered data $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ and orthonormal basis $\{w_1, \dots, w_k\}$ spanning a $k$-dim subspace, the projection of $x_i$ is:

$$\hat{x}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (w_j^{T} x_i) w_j = W W^{T} x_i$$

**Step 2 — Objective (2 marks):**

$$J = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^{2} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert x_i - W W^{T} x_i \rVert^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplification (2 marks):**
Using $x_i - W W^{T} x_i = (I - W W^{T}) x_i$ and idempotency $(I - W W^{T})^{2} = (I - W W^{T})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{T} (I - W W^{T}) x_i \\
&= \text{Tr}\left( (I - W W^{T}) \Sigma \right) \\
&= \text{Tr}(\Sigma) - \text{Tr}(W^{T} \Sigma W)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimization (2 marks):**
Since $\text{Tr}(\Sigma)$ is constant, minimizing $J$ is **equivalent to maximizing** $\text{Tr}(W^{T} \Sigma W)$, which gives the same eigenvalue problem.

#### **Part (b) — SVD Alternative & Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — SVD Formulation (3 marks):**
Compute the **Singular Value Decomposition** of the centered data:

$$X_c = U S V^{T}$$

where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (diagonal with singular values $\sigma_i$), $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$.

The relationship to eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{T} X_c = \frac{1}{n-1} V S^{T} U^{T} U S V^{T} = \frac{1}{n-1} V S^{2} V^{T} \\
\Rightarrow V &= \text{eigenvectors of } \Sigma, \quad \lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^{2}}{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Why SVD is Preferred (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Eigendecomposition | SVD |
|--------|-------------------|-----|
| Numerical stability | Sensitive to round-off | Highly stable |
| Computational cost | $O(d^{3})$ | $O(\min(n,d)^{3})$ |
| Sparse data handling | Poor | Excellent |
| Recommended for $n \gg d$ | No | **Yes** |

**Step 3 — Practical Algorithm via SVD (2 marks):**
1. Center: $X_c = X - \mu$
2. Compute thin SVD: $X_c \approx U_k S_k V_k^{T}$
3. Set $W = V_k$ (top-$k$ right singular vectors)
4. Project: $Y = X_c W = U_k S_k$
5. Reconstruct: $\hat{X} = U_k S_k V_k^{T} + \mu$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in SVD-PCA Exam Answers:**
> - Forgetting the $\frac{1}{n-1}$ factor when relating singular values to eigenvalues.
> - Confusing $U$ (left singular vectors, sample space) with $V$ (right singular vectors, **feature space** — these are the PCs).
> - Not stating that SVD is **numerically stable** because it works directly on $X_c$ without forming $\Sigma$ explicitly.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PCA is a linear, unsupervised, feature-extraction** (not selection) technique.
- ✅ **Two equivalent objectives:** MaxVariance and MinReconstructionError.
- ✅ **Always mean-center** before computing $\Sigma$; **standardize** when feature scales differ.
- ✅ **Eigenvectors = principal component directions**; **Eigenvalues = variance along those directions**.
- ✅ **Sort eigenvalues descending**; top-$k$ eigenvectors form projection matrix $W$.
- ✅ **Projected scores:** $Y = X_c W$; **Reconstruction:** $\hat{X} = Y W^{T} + \mu$.
- ✅ **Choose $k$ via:** Kaiser rule ($\lambda > 1$), cumulative variance ($\geq 95\%$), scree plot elbow, or cross-validation.
- ✅ **SVD form** $X_c = U S V^{T}$ is the numerically stable alternative — $W = V_k$.
- ✅ **PCs are orthogonal (uncorrelated)** by construction.
- ✅ **Total variance preserved:** $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i = \text{Tr}(\Sigma)$.
- ✅ **Limitations:** PCA assumes **linearity**, is **sensitive to outliers**, and assumes **variance = information** (which may not hold for non-Gaussian data — use **Kernel PCA** or **t-SNE** instead).
- ✅ **NOT rotation-invariant in the same way for non-centered data** — always center first.
- ✅ **Explained variance ratio formula:** $r_i = \lambda_i / \sum_{j} \lambda_j$.
- ✅ **Geometrically:** PCs are axes of the best-fit ellipsoid to the data cloud.
- ✅ **Most common exam blunder:** Confusing **eigenvectors** (axes) with **scores** (data coordinates along axes) — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SVD, Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** SVD, Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) ### Formal Definition **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** is a fundamental matrix factorization technique in linear algebra that decomposes any rectangular matrix $A$ of dimensions $m \times n$ into the product of three special matrices: $$A = U \Sigma V^T$$ where: - $U$ is an $m \times m$ **orthogonal matrix** (left singular vectors — eigenvectors of $AA^T$) - $\Sigma$ is an $m \times n$ **diagonal matrix** of non-negative **singular values** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$, where $r = \text{rank}(A)$ - $V^T$ is the transpose of an $n \times n$ **orthogonal matrix** $V$ (right singular vectors — eigenvectors of $A^T A$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SVD is one of the most exam-relevant matrix decomposition techniques. The KTU Board frequently tests the *truncated SVD*, its use in **dimensionality reduction**, and the *low-rank approximation* formula $A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^T$. ### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine you have a **huge library** with thousands of books (your data matrix $A$). SVD is like a smart librarian who reads every book and creates three summaries: 1. **$U$** — A list of "main themes" in how readers perceive the books. 2. **$\Sigma$** — A ranking of how important each theme is (a list of scores). 3. **$V^T$** — A list of the actual "content fingerprints" of each book. If you only keep the top $k$ themes (the largest scores in $\Sigma$), you can approximately reconstruct the original library using far less storage — this is the magic of **dimensionality reduction via SVD**. ### Compact vs Truncated SVD - **Compact SVD**: Uses only the $r$ non-zero singular values, giving $A = U_r \Sigma_r V_r^T$. - **Truncated SVD**: Keeps only the top-$k$ singular values ($k < r$), giving a *rank-$k$ approximation* $A_k$. ## 1.2 Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning ### Formal Definitions **Instance-Based Learning (Lazy Learning):** A learning paradigm where the system **memorizes the entire training dataset** and postpones generalization until a query (test point) arrives. The prediction is computed *at inference time* by comparing the query to stored instances. Canonical example: **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)**. **Model-Based Learning (Eager Learning):** A learning paradigm where the system **induces a compact parametric model** from the training data during a dedicated training phase, then *discards* the training data. Predictions are made by evaluating the learned model on the query. Examples: **Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Neural Networks**. ### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Instance-Based = A Doctor Who Memorizes Patient Records** > The doctor doesn't form a general rule. When a new patient arrives, she flips through all previous records to find the most similar cases and bases her diagnosis on them. > **Model-Based = A Doctor Who Studies Anatomy** > The doctor learns general medical rules (a model) from past patients during medical school. After learning, she can diagnose a new patient instantly using the rules, without ever opening the old files. ### Key Distinguishing Metrics - **Training Time**: Instance-based = **near zero**; Model-based = **high** - **Prediction Time**: Instance-based = **slow** (must scan dataset); Model-based = **fast** - **Memory**: Instance-based = **stores full data**; Model-based = **stores parameters only** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SVD — Theoretical Foundation ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Compute the Gram matrix** $A^T A$ (an $n \times n$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix). 2. **Eigen-decompose** $A^T A = V \Lambda V^T$ where $V$ contains eigenvectors and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)$ contains eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$. 3. **Singular values** are obtained as $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$. 4. **Right singular vectors** are the columns of $V$. 5. **Left singular vectors** are computed as $u_i = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$ for each non-zero $\sigma_i$. 6. **Assemble** $U$, $\Sigma$, $V^T$ to form $A = U \Sigma V^T$. ### Key Properties (High-Yield for KTU) - The rank of $A$ equals the number of non-zero singular values. - $U$ and $V$ are orthonormal: $U^T U = I$, $V^T V = I$. - Frobenius norm of $A$: $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$. - Spectral (operator) norm: $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_1$ (the largest singular value). - **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem**: $A_k$ is the *best* rank-$k$ approximation of $A$ in both Frobenius and spectral norms. ## 2.2 Instance-Based vs Model-Based — Analytical Breakdown ### Instance-Based Learning - Stores dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ verbatim. - Hypothesis: $h(x) = f(x, \mathcal{D})$. - No explicit abstraction; prediction is a function of similarity $\text{sim}(x, x_i)$. - **Cost of learning**: $O(1)$ in terms of computation, but $O(N)$ in memory. - **Cost of prediction**: $O(N \cdot d)$ for $d$-dimensional data. - Examples: k-NN, Locally Weighted Regression, Case-Based Reasoning, RBF networks. ### Model-Based Learning - Learns parameters $\theta$ of hypothesis $h(x; \theta)$ by minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$. - Hypothesis: $h(x) = g(\theta^T x)$ (e.g., sigmoid for logistic regression). - Training data is discarded after parameter estimation. - **Cost of learning**: $O(\text{iterations} \cdot N \cdot d)$. - **Cost of prediction**: $O(d)$ — constant time w.r.t. dataset size. - Examples: Linear/Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, Neural Networks, Naive Bayes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | Full SVD | $A_{m \times n} = U_{m \times m} \, \Sigma_{m \times n} \, V^T_{n \times n}$ | $U, V$ orthogonal | | 2 | Compact SVD | $A = U_r \, \Sigma_r \, V_r^T$ | Only non-zero $\sigma_i$ | | 3 | Truncated SVD | $A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \, u_i \, v_i^T$ | Rank-$k$ approx., $k < r$ | | 4 | Singular values | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(A^T A)}$ | $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of $A^T A$ | | 5 | Left singular vectors | $u_i = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$ | For $\sigma_i > 0$ | | 6 | Frobenius norm | $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$ | Sum of squares of singular values | | 7 | Spectral norm | $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max} = \sigma_1$ | Largest singular value | | 8 | Reconstruction error | $\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$ | Eckart–Young theorem | | 9 | Energy retained | $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2} \times 100\%$ | Used to choose $k$ | | 10 | k-NN prediction (regression) | $\hat{y} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} y_i$ | Average of $k$ nearest targets | | 11 | k-NN prediction (classification) | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in N_k(x)\}$ | Majority vote | | 12 | Model-based objective | $\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h(x_i; \theta), y_i)$ | Empirical risk minimization | | 13 | SVD–PCA link | Principal components = right singular vectors $V$; variance $\propto \sigma_i^2 / (N-1)$ | After mean-centering $A$ | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Image Compression**: Truncated SVD on image matrices retains visual quality at $10$–$20\%$ storage. - **Recommender Systems**: Netflix Prize solution used SVD on the user–item rating matrix. - **NLP — Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)**: SVD on term-document matrices uncovers latent topics. - **Noise Filtering**: Discarding small singular values removes noise. - **k-NN in Medical Diagnosis, Fault Detection, Anomaly Detection** (instance-based). - **Model-Based in Autonomous Driving, Spam Filtering, Credit Scoring** (efficient, real-time inference). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why $A = U \Sigma V^T$ Exists for Any Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Prove that *any* real matrix $A$ of rank $r$ has a Singular Value Decomposition. ### Step 1 — Form the symmetric matrix $A^T A$ Since $A$ is real, $A^T A$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite. By the **Spectral Theorem**, $A^T A$ can be eigen-decomposed as: $$A^T A = V \Lambda V^T$$ where $V$ is an orthogonal $n \times n$ matrix of eigenvectors, and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)$ with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_n \geq 0$. ### Step 2 — Define singular values Because $A^T A$ is positive semi-definite, all $\lambda_i \geq 0$. Define: $$\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i} \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, r$$ where $r = \text{rank}(A)$ is the number of positive eigenvalues. Thus $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$. ### Step 3 — Show that $V$ provides right singular vectors The columns of $V$ — call them $v_1, \dots, v_n$ — are eigenvectors of $A^T A$. From $A^T A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$: $$\|A v_i\|^2 = (A v_i)^T (A v_i) = v_i^T A^T A v_i = v_i^T (\lambda_i v_i) = \lambda_i \|v_i\|^2 = \lambda_i$$ Hence $\|A v_i\| = \sqrt{\lambda_i} = \sigma_i$ for the first $r$ vectors, and $A v_i = 0$ for $i > r$. ### Step 4 — Construct left singular vectors For $i = 1, \dots, r$, define: $$u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$$ We verify orthonormality: $$u_i^T u_j = \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^T A^T A v_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^T v_j = \frac{\sigma_j}{\sigma_i} \delta_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$$ For $i > r$, $A v_i = 0$, so we extend $\{u_1, \dots, u_r\}$ to an orthonormal basis $\{u_1, \dots, u_m\}$ of $\mathbb{R}^m$ using **Gram–Schmidt**. ### Step 5 — Assemble the decomposition We have $A v_i = \sigma_i u_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, r$. Stacking these as columns: $$A V_r = U_r \Sigma_r$$ Multiplying both sides on the right by $V_r^T$ (using $V_r^T V_r = I$): $$A = U_r \Sigma_r V_r^T$$ Padding with the additional $m - r$ and $n - r$ orthonormal vectors yields the full SVD: $$A = U \Sigma V^T \qquad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Best Rank-$k$ Approximation (Eckart–Young Theorem) ### Step 1 — Set up the optimization Find matrix $A_k$ of rank $\leq k$ minimizing $\|A - A_k\|_F^2$: $$A_k^* = \arg\min_{\text{rank}(B) \leq k} \|A - B\|_F^2$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute SVD expansion Write $A = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^T$. Any rank-$k$ matrix $B$ can be written as $B = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i p_i q_i^T$ for some orthonormal sets $\{p_i\}, \{q_i\}$. ### Step 3 — Apply Pythagorean decomposition Expanding the squared Frobenius norm: $$\|A - B\|_F^2 = \left\| \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^T - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i p_i q_i^T \right\|_F^2$$ The minimum is achieved when we retain the top $k$ terms of the SVD itself: $\alpha_i = \sigma_i$, $p_i = u_i$, $q_i = v_i$. Then: $$A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^T$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the resulting error The remaining terms are orthogonal, so: $$\|A - A_k\|_F^2 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$$ $$\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The error depends *only* on the *discarded* singular values. This is why SVD is the optimal compression — no other rank-$k$ approximation can do better. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 2×2 SVD **Given:** $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$ (already diagonal, so trivial). **SVD:** $U = I$, $\Sigma = \text{diag}(3, 4)$ (using absolute values), $V = I$. > **Non-trivial example:** $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ **Step 1:** Compute $A^T A = \begin{pmatrix} 13 & 12 \\ 12 & 13 \end{pmatrix}$. **Step 2:** Eigenvalues: $\det(A^T A - \lambda I) = (13 - \lambda)^2 - 144 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 25, 1$. **Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_1 = 5, \sigma_2 = 1$. **Step 4:** Right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $A^T A$): - For $\lambda_1 = 25$: $v_1 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ - For $\lambda_2 = 1$: $v_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ **Step 5:** Left singular vectors: - $u_1 = \frac{1}{5} A v_1 = \frac{1}{5} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ - $u_2 = \frac{1}{1} A v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ **Step 6:** Final SVD: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{pmatrix}$$ **Verification:** $U \Sigma V^T = \dfrac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 5+1 & 5-1 \\ 5-1 & 5+1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = A \;\checkmark$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SVD Block Decomposition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Matrix A
m x n"] --> U["Matrix U
m x m
Left Singular Vectors"] A --> S["Matrix Sigma
m x n
Diagonal Singular Values"] A --> V["Matrix V transpose
n x n
Right Singular Vectors"] S --> note1["sigma1 >= sigma2 >= ... >= sigmar > 0"] U --> noteU["Columns are orthonormal
U transpose U = I"] V --> noteV["Rows are orthonormal
V transpose V = I"] S --> SVDRec["Reconstruction
A equals U Sigma V transpose"] U --> SVDRec V --> SVDRec SVDRec --> App1["Application 1
Dimensionality Reduction"] SVDRec --> App2["Application 2
Image Compression"] SVDRec --> App3["Application 3
Noise Filtering"] SVDRec --> App4["Application 4
Recommender Systems"] ``` ## 4.2 Truncated SVD — Low-Rank Approximation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Original Matrix A
rank r"]) --> Compute["Step 1: Full SVD
A = U Sigma V transpose"] Compute --> Sort["Step 2: Sort Singular Values
Descending Order"] Sort --> Choose["Step 3: Choose k < r
Energy threshold 90 to 99 percent"] Choose --> Truncate["Step 4: Truncate U Sigma V
Keep top k columns/rows"] Truncate --> Recon["Step 5: Reconstruct A_k
A_k equals sum of sigma_i u_i v_i transpose"] Recon --> Eval["Step 6: Evaluate Error
Error = sqrt of sum of discarded sigma_i squared"] Eval --> Decision{"Acceptable?"} Decision -- Yes --> Use["Use A_k for downstream task"] Decision -- No --> Increase["Increase k and repeat"] Increase --> Choose ``` ## 4.3 Instance-Based vs Model-Based Learning Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IB["INSTANCE-BASED LEARNING (LAZY)"] direction TB IB1["Step 1: Receive Training Data D"] --> IB2["Step 2: Store D Verbatim
No abstraction built"] IB2 --> IB3["Step 3: Query x arrives
at prediction time"] IB3 --> IB4["Step 4: Compute Similarity
distance from x to all x_i in D"] IB4 --> IB5["Step 5: Select k Nearest Neighbors"] IB5 --> IB6["Step 6: Aggregate labels
Mean or Majority Vote"] end subgraph MB["MODEL-BASED LEARNING (EAGER)"] direction TB MB1["Step 1: Receive Training Data D"] --> MB2["Step 2: Optimize Parameters
theta star equals arg min Loss"] MB2 --> MB3["Step 3: Discard D
Keep only model parameters"] MB3 --> MB4["Step 4: Query x arrives
at prediction time"] MB4 --> MB5["Step 5: Evaluate h of x, theta
Direct function evaluation"] MB5 --> MB6["Step 6: Output prediction
Constant time inference"] end style IB fill:#e1f5ff style MB fill:#fff4e1 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Architectural Overview ```mermaid flowchart TD ML["Machine Learning Paradigms"] --> IB["Instance-Based Learning"] ML --> MB["Model-Based Learning"] IB --> IB1["k-Nearest Neighbors"] IB --> IB2["Locally Weighted Regression"] IB --> IB3["Case-Based Reasoning"] IB --> IB4["RBF Networks"] MB --> MB1["Linear and Logistic Regression"] MB --> MB2["Decision Trees and Random Forests"] MB --> MB3["Support Vector Machines"] MB --> MB4["Neural Networks and Deep Learning"] MB --> MB5["Naive Bayes Classifier"] IB1 --> Ch1["Characteristics: Lazy, Memory-intensive,
No training phase, Slow prediction"] MB1 --> Ch2["Characteristics: Eager, Compact model,
Costly training, Fast prediction"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. Define Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a matrix. State any two important properties of the SVD.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** The Singular Value Decomposition of a real $m \times n$ matrix $A$ of rank $r$ is the factorization: $$A = U \Sigma V^T$$ where $U$ is an $m \times m$ orthogonal matrix, $V$ is an $n \times n$ orthogonal matrix, and $\Sigma$ is an $m \times n$ diagonal matrix with $r$ positive singular values $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$ on its diagonal. (2 marks) **Two important properties:** 1. The columns of $U$ are eigenvectors of $AA^T$, and the columns of $V$ are eigenvectors of $A^T A$. (0.5 marks) 2. The singular values are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $A^T A$, i.e., $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(A^T A)}$. (0.5 marks) --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between instance-based learning and model-based learning with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Instance-Based | Model-Based | |---|---|---| | **Approach** | Memorizes training data | Builds a parametric model | | **Training Phase** | No abstraction is built | Parameters are learned | | **Prediction Phase** | Compares query to all stored instances | Evaluates a learned function | | **Example** | k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) | Linear Regression | (2.5 marks for the comparison table; 0.5 marks for valid examples) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. In SVD problems, students **often forget to sort singular values in descending order** — this loses 1–2 marks. > 2. Many students write $A = U \Sigma V$ instead of $A = U \Sigma V^T$ — the transpose on $V$ is **non-negotiable**. > 3. For k-NN questions, students forget to mention the **distance metric** (Euclidean, Manhattan) used. > 4. In the model-based learning question, students confuse the **loss function** with the **hypothesis** — keep them distinct. > 5. Always **state the dimensions** of $U$, $\Sigma$, and $V$ explicitly in SVD answers to earn full marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **For the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, compute the Singular Value Decomposition. Find the best rank-1 approximation $A_1$.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Form $A^T A$: $$A^T A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Find eigenvalues by solving $\det(A^T A - \lambda I) = 0$: $$(2 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 3, \lambda_2 = 1$$ **Step 3:** Singular values: $\sigma_1 = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$, $\sigma_2 = 1$. **Step 4:** Right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $A^T A$): - For $\lambda_1 = 3$: $v_1 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ - For $\lambda_2 = 1$: $v_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ So $V = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$. **Step 5:** Left singular vectors: $$u_1 = \frac{1}{\sigma_1} A v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ $$u_2 = \frac{1}{\sigma_2} A v_2 = \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ The third left singular vector (perpendicular to $u_1, u_2$) can be obtained via cross product: $u_3 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. **Step 6:** Assemble full SVD: $$U = \begin{pmatrix} 2/\sqrt{6} & 0 & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3} \end{pmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad V^T = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$ **Step 7:** Best rank-1 approximation: $$A_1 = \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^T = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/(2\sqrt{2}) & 1/(2\sqrt{2}) \\ 1/(2\sqrt{2}) & 1/(2\sqrt{2}) \end{pmatrix}$$ **Frobenius reconstruction error:** $\|A - A_1\|_F = \sigma_2 = 1$. (7 marks) --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm as an instance-based learning method. Discuss how the choice of $k$ and the distance metric affect performance.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Algorithm Description (3 marks):** k-NN is the canonical example of *instance-based* (lazy) learning. The training phase simply stores the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. For a query $x_q$: 1. Compute distance $d(x_q, x_i)$ from $x_q$ to every $x_i \in \mathcal{D}$. 2. Identify the $k$ training points with smallest distances — these form $N_k(x_q)$. 3. **Regression:** $\hat{y} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{x_i \in N_k(x_q)} y_i$. 4. **Classification:** $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in N_k(x_q)\}$ (majority vote). **Step 2 — Effect of $k$ (2 marks):** - **Small $k$ (e.g., $k=1$):** Low bias, high variance; sensitive to noise → *overfitting*. - **Large $k$ (e.g., $k=N$):** High bias, low variance; ignores local structure → *underfitting*. - **Optimal $k$** is chosen by **cross-validation** to balance bias and variance. **Step 3 — Distance Metrics (2 marks):** - **Euclidean (L2):** $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_j (x_j - y_j)^2}$ — default for continuous data. - **Manhattan (L1):** $d(x, y) = \sum_j \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ — robust to outliers. - **Minkowski (Lp):** Generalization $d(x, y) = \left(\sum_j \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$. - **Cosine similarity:** $d(x, y) = 1 - \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \cdot \|y\|}$ — used in text/NLP. - **Curse of Dimensionality:** In high dimensions, all distances become similar, degrading k-NN. *Feature scaling* (normalization) is essential. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Derive the Singular Value Decomposition for a $2 \times 2$ matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$. Show that the singular values are non-negative.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $A^T A = \begin{pmatrix} a^2 + c^2 & ab + cd \\ ab + cd & b^2 + d^2 \end{pmatrix}$. **Step 2:** Eigenvalues of $A^T A$ are roots of: $$\lambda^2 - (a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2)\lambda + (ad - bc)^2 = 0$$ The sum of eigenvalues = $\text{trace}(A^T A) = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2$ (the Frobenius norm squared), and the product equals $\det(A^T A) = (\det A)^2 = (ad - bc)^2 \geq 0$. **Step 3:** By the quadratic formula: $$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{T \pm \sqrt{T^2 - 4D^2}}{2}$$ where $T = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 \geq 0$ and $D = ad - bc$. Note that $T^2 - 4D^2 = (a^2 + c^2 - b^2 - d^2)^2 + 4(ab + cd)^2 \geq 0$. **Step 4:** Since $A^T A$ is positive semi-definite, all eigenvalues are $\lambda_i \geq 0$. The **singular values** are: $$\sigma_{1,2} = \sqrt{\lambda_{1,2}} = \frac{\sqrt{T \pm \sqrt{T^2 - 4D^2}}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ These are inherently non-negative as they are defined as the non-negative square roots of the non-negative eigenvalues of $A^T A$. (7 marks) --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare and contrast model-based and instance-based learning. Describe a real-world scenario where each would be the preferred approach, justifying your choice.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Comparative Analysis (3 marks):** | Dimension | Instance-Based | Model-Based | |---|---|---| | **Training Cost** | $O(1)$ — trivial | $O(N \cdot d \cdot T)$ — costly | | **Prediction Cost** | $O(N \cdot d)$ — slow | $O(d)$ — fast | | **Memory** | $O(N \cdot d)$ — large | $O(\vert\theta\vert)$ — compact | | **Generalization** | Local; no global model | Global; explicit model | | **Interpretability** | High (similar cases) | Depends on model | | **Adaptability** | Naturally adapts to new data | Requires retraining | | **Examples** | k-NN, LWR | Linear Reg., SVM, NN | **Step 2 — Real-World Scenario for Instance-Based (2 marks):** **Medical Diagnosis for Rare Diseases:** With only a handful of training cases for a rare disease, it is impractical to fit a parametric model. A **k-NN** system can retrieve the most similar historical cases (based on symptoms, lab results, genetic markers) and let an oncologist compare the new patient to those cases directly. Since new data points can be added to the database incrementally with no retraining, this is highly suitable for evolving medical knowledge. **Step 3 — Real-World Scenario for Model-Based (2 marks):** **Real-Time Credit Card Fraud Detection at Scale:** Billions of transactions occur daily. An instance-based system would be infeasible due to memory and latency. Instead, a **Logistic Regression** or **Neural Network** model is trained on historical transactions (with labels: fraud or legitimate). The model — typically a few megabytes — is deployed on edge servers and produces a fraud probability in microseconds, enabling real-time blocking. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **SVD exists for *any* real (or complex) matrix** — rectangular, square, full-rank, or rank-deficient. - [ ] **Always remember the transpose**: $A = U \Sigma V^T$ (not $V$). - [ ] **Singular values are always non-negative** and conventionally ordered in **descending** order: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$. - [ ] **Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem**: Truncated SVD gives the **best** rank-$k$ approximation in both Frobenius and spectral norms — no other method can beat it. - [ ] **Reconstruction error** equals the square root of the sum of *discarded* singular values squared: $\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$. - [ ] **Energy retention** formula: $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$ — used to choose $k$ in dimensionality reduction. - [ ] **SVD ↔ PCA Connection**: For mean-centered data, principal axes are columns of $V$; variance along axis $i$ is $\sigma_i^2 / (N-1)$. - [ ] **Instance-based learning** = lazy, memory-intensive, slow prediction, no training phase, naturally adaptive. - [ ] **Model-based learning** = eager, compact model, fast prediction, costly training, requires retraining for new data. - [ ] **k-NN pitfalls**: curse of dimensionality, requires feature scaling, sensitive to $k$ and distance metric choice. - [ ] **Hybrid approaches exist**: Locally Weighted Regression combines aspects of both paradigms. - [ ] **KTU favourites**: SVD derivation, Eckart–Young theorem, k-NN algorithm, comparison tables, and the choice of $k$ via cross-validation. - [ ] **Real-world domains** for SVD: image compression, recommender systems, NLP (LSA), signal processing, and computer vision. - [ ] **Real-world domains** for k-NN: medical diagnosis, recommendation engines, anomaly detection, and pattern recognition with small datasets. - [ ] **Real-world domains** for model-based: autonomous vehicles, large-scale classification, real-time prediction systems, and production-grade ML deployments.

topics.title: Machine Learning Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Machine Learning (ML) Model** is a parameterized mathematical function or computational structure that maps an input space $\mathcal{X}$ to an output space $\mathcal{Y}$ by learning optimal parameters $\theta \in \Theta$ from a finite dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The learning is driven by the minimization (or maximization) of a pre-defined objective function, typically a **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ that quantifies the discrepancy between the model's predictions and the ground-truth targets.

Mathematically, the model is represented as a **hypothesis function**:

$$\hat{y} = h(x; \theta)$$

where:
- $x \in \mathcal{X}$ is the input feature vector
- $\hat{y} \in \mathcal{Y}$ is the predicted output
- $\theta$ is the learnable parameter set (weights, biases, kernel parameters, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEECT413 emphasizes the *taxonomy* of ML models (supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, reinforcement), the components of a learning system, and the formal problem formulation. Students must clearly distinguish between **model**, **algorithm**, and **hypothesis**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are teaching a toddler to recognize the fruit "**mango**". You don't write a rulebook listing pixel patterns of mangoes — you simply show the child *hundreds of mangoes* and *non-mangoes*, and say "this is a mango" or "this is not". Gradually, the child builds an internal mental model that captures the essence (shape, color, texture) of a mango. When shown a new fruit, the child uses this internalized model to make a judgment.

> A **Machine Learning Model behaves exactly like the toddler's brain**:
> - The **mangoes & non-mangoes** = Training Dataset $\mathcal{D}$
> - The **child's mental rules** = Model parameters $\theta$
> - The **act of showing & correcting** = Training (optimization of loss)
> - The **judgment on a new fruit** = Inference / Prediction

## 1.3 Essential Components of an ML Model

> [!NOTE]
> Every ML system, regardless of complexity, contains these **four pillars**:

| Pillar | Symbol | Role in the System |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$ | The fuel; the empirical evidence from which patterns are extracted |
| **Model / Hypothesis** | $h(x;\theta)$ | The functional mapping whose structure (linear, tree, neural) defines the **inductive bias** |
| **Loss Function** | $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ | Quantifies the *cost of being wrong*; the compass guiding learning |
| **Optimizer** | $\text{Alg}(\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L})$ | The mechanism that adjusts $\theta$ to reduce loss (e.g., **Gradient Descent**) |

## 1.4 KTU Board Examiner's Perspective

From the valuation desk, Module 1 questions test:
1. **Definition-level clarity** (1–2 marks): Define model, hypothesis, learning.
2. **Taxonomy-level reasoning** (3–5 marks): Categorize models, give examples.
3. **Comparative analysis** (7–14 marks): Compare discriminative vs generative, supervised vs unsupervised, etc.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Problem Formulation

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn i.i.d. from an unknown joint distribution $P(X, Y)$, the goal of an ML model is to find the hypothesis $h^* \in \mathcal{H}$ (the **hypothesis space**) that minimizes the **expected risk** (generalization error):

$$h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P} \left[ \mathcal{L}(h(x), y) \right]$$

Since $P(X,Y)$ is unknown, we approximate the expectation using the **empirical risk** over the training set:

$$\hat{h}^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h(x_i), y_i)}_{\text{Empirical Risk } \hat{R}(h)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The gap between the **expected risk** and the **empirical risk** is what gives rise to the central ML dilemma — the **Generalization Problem**, which is governed by the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Machine Learning Models

### A. Supervised Learning Models
- **Definition:** Learn $h: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ from *labeled* data.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Classification:** $y \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ (discrete). Example models: Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, SVM, k-NN.
  - **Regression:** $y \in \mathbb{R}$ (continuous). Example models: Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso, Polynomial Regression.

### B. Unsupervised Learning Models
- **Definition:** Discover structure in *unlabeled* data.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Clustering:** Group similar points. Example: K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical.
  - **Dimensionality Reduction:** Project to low-dimensional manifold. Example: PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders.
  - **Density Estimation:** Model $P(X)$. Example: GMM, KDE.

### C. Semi-Supervised Learning
- Mixes a small labeled set with a large unlabeled set. Useful when labeling is expensive (e.g., medical imaging).

### D. Reinforcement Learning (RL)
- An **agent** learns a **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ by interacting with an **environment** and receiving **rewards**. Goal: maximize cumulative reward $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r_t$.

### E. Self-Supervised Learning
- The model creates its own supervisory signal from raw data (e.g., next-word prediction in LLMs).

## 2.3 Discriminative vs. Generative Models (High-Yield KTU Distinction)

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|---|---|---|
| **What it learns** | Decision boundary $P(Y \vert X)$ | Joint distribution $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X \vert Y)$ |
| **Output** | A class label / value | A class label **and** a synthesized $X$ |
| **Examples** | Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Nets | Naive Bayes, GMM, GAN, VAE |
| **Data requirement** | Generally less | Generally more |
| **Use case** | Prediction | Prediction + Synthesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** (Part B). Memorize the table above verbatim.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | $\hat{y} = h(x; \theta)$ | Function of inputs and parameters |
| Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$ | Scalar; mean loss on training set |
| 0–1 Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1} = \mathbb{1}\{\hat{y} \neq y\}$ | Non-differentiable; used for evaluation |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression; unit = (target)$^2$ |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Cross-Entropy (Binary) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$ | Classification; nats or bits |
| Cross-Entropy (Multi-class) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | With softmax output |
| Hinge Loss (SVM) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$ | For max-margin classifiers |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(\hat{y} - y)^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Irreducible error = $\sigma^2$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Discriminative models classify tumors as benign/malignant from histopathology images.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Generative models synthesize adversarial driving scenarios for robust RL training.
- **Finance:** Semi-supervised models detect fraud using a few labeled transactions and millions of unlabeled ones.
- **Manufacturing:** Unsupervised clustering identifies defect patterns in production-line sensor data.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Self-supervised pretraining (BERT-style) enables powerful sequence-aware recommendations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form Solution of Linear Regression (Normal Equation)

### Setup
We are given a univariate dataset of $N$ points:
$$\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$

The model is a simple linear hypothesis:
$$\hat{y}_i = w \, x_i + b$$

We choose the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** as the loss function:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (w x_i + b) \right)^2$$

### Step 1: Expand the squared term
$$\mathcal{L}(w,b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i^2 - 2 y_i (w x_i + b) + (w x_i + b)^2 \right)$$

### Step 2: Compute partial derivative with respect to $b$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( (w x_i + b) - y_i \right) \cdot 1$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w x_i + b - y_i) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = w \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + N b$$

Solving for $b$:
$$b = \bar{y} - w \bar{x} \quad \text{where } \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i,\; \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum y_i$$

### Step 3: Compute partial derivative with respect to $w$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( (w x_i + b) - y_i \right) \cdot x_i$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (w x_i + b - y_i) = 0$$

### Step 4: Substitute $b = \bar{y} - w\bar{x}$ into the $w$ equation
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( w x_i + (\bar{y} - w\bar{x}) - y_i \right) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( w(x_i - \bar{x}) - (y_i - \bar{y}) \right) = 0$$

$$w \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (x_i - \bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \bar{y})$$

### Step 5: Final closed-form expression
$$w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (x_i - \bar{x})} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** solution. The denominator is the variance of $x$ (up to a factor), and the numerator is the covariance of $x$ and $y$. Hence $w = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

### Setup
Using the same MSE loss:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (w x_i + b) \right)^2$$

### Step 1: Compute the gradient with respect to $w$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( y_i - (w x_i + b) \right)$$

### Step 2: Compute the gradient with respect to $b$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (w x_i + b) \right)$$

### Step 3: Apply the gradient descent update
$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$$

Component-wise:
$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \left( -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i) \right)$$

$$b^{(t+1)} = b^{(t)} - \eta \left( -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i) \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **negative sign cancellation**: the gradient points in the direction of *steepest ascent*, so subtracting it gives *steepest descent*. KTU students often forget this sign and lose 2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bias-Variance Decomposition (Expected Test Error)

For a regression model, let $y = f(x) + \epsilon$ with $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$. The expected squared prediction error at point $x$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{y})^2 \right] = \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) + \epsilon - \hat{y})^2 \right]$$

Expanding:
$$= \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{y})^2 \right] + 2 \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{y}) \epsilon \right] + \mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2]$$

Since $\epsilon$ is independent of $\hat{y}$, the cross term is $0$. Adding and subtracting $\mathbb{E}[\hat{y}]$ inside the first term:
$$= \underbrace{\left( f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}] \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{y})} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{y} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}])^2 \right]}_{\text{Variance}(\hat{y})} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}$$

Therefore:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{y})^2 \right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

> This is the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: increasing model complexity reduces bias but inflates variance. The optimal complexity minimizes their sum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic ML Model Training & Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data X] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Train Validation Test Split]
    D --> E[Model Hypothesis h x theta]
    E --> F[Prediction yhat]
    F --> G[Loss Function L yhat y]
    G --> H[Compute Gradient]
    H --> I[Optimizer Update theta]
    I --> J{Converged}
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Trained Model]
    K --> L[Inference on Test Data]
    L --> M[Final Prediction]
```

**Description of the flow:**
1. **Raw Data $X$** enters the preprocessing unit (cleaning, normalization).
2. **Feature Engineering** transforms raw signals into discriminative features.
3. The data is split into **train / validation / test** sets.
4. The **model hypothesis** $h(x;\theta)$ generates predictions $\hat{y}$.
5. The **loss function** measures prediction error.
6. The **optimizer** updates $\theta$ using the gradient of the loss.
7. The loop continues until convergence (e.g., gradient norm $<\epsilon$).
8. The **trained model** is deployed for inference on new (unseen) inputs.

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy Diagram — Types of Machine Learning Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Machine Learning Models] --> S[Supervised Learning]
    R --> U[Unsupervised Learning]
    R --> P[Semi Supervised Learning]
    R --> L[Reinforcement Learning]
    R --> F[Self Supervised Learning]

    S --> S1[Classification]
    S --> S2[Regression]
    S1 --> S1a[Logistic Regression]
    S1 --> S1b[Decision Tree]
    S1 --> S1c[SVM]
    S1 --> S1d[k Nearest Neighbors]
    S2 --> S2a[Linear Regression]
    S2 --> S2b[Ridge and Lasso]
    S2 --> S2c[Polynomial Regression]

    U --> U1[Clustering]
    U --> U2[Dimensionality Reduction]
    U --> U3[Density Estimation]
    U1 --> U1a[K Means]
    U1 --> U1b[DBSCAN]
    U2 --> U2a[PCA]
    U2 --> U2b[t SNE]
    U3 --> U3a[GMM]
    U3 --> U3b[KDE]
```

---

## 4.3 Discriminative vs. Generative — Architectural Contrast

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIS[Discriminative Pipeline]
        A1[Input x] --> A2[Model f x theta]
        A2 --> A3[Output P y given x]
    end
    subgraph GEN[Generative Pipeline]
        B1[Class label y] --> B2[Model g y theta]
        B2 --> B3[Synthesized x]
        B3 --> B4[Apply Bayes Rule]
        B4 --> B5[Posterior P y given x]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **discriminative** path goes *directly* from $x$ to $P(Y \vert X)$, while the **generative** path first models $P(X \vert Y)$ and then inverts via Bayes' rule. This contrast is a high-yield KTU discussion point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Machine Learning Model. List any two key differences between supervised and unsupervised learning models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A Machine Learning Model is a parameterized function $h(x;\theta)$ that maps input data to output predictions by learning optimal parameters $\theta$ from a training dataset.
- **(1 mark)** Supervised learning uses **labeled** data $(x_i, y_i)$ to learn a mapping; unsupervised learning uses **unlabeled** data $x_i$ to discover hidden structure.
- **(1 mark)** Supervised learning is used for prediction tasks (classification/regression), while unsupervised learning is used for pattern discovery (clustering/dimensionality reduction).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ in a machine learning model? Why is its choice important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ is the set of all candidate functions that the learning algorithm is allowed to consider as the final model.
- **(1 mark)** It defines the **inductive bias** of the learner — e.g., choosing linear functions restricts $\mathcal{H}$ to hyperplanes, while choosing decision trees allows axis-aligned partitions.
- **(1 mark)** A well-chosen $\mathcal{H}$ matches the underlying data structure, enabling better generalization; a poor choice leads to underfitting (too restrictive) or overfitting (too flexible).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

#### **(a) Explain the different types of machine learning models with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Supervised Learning Models (2 marks)**
Learn a mapping $h: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ from labeled training data.
- **Classification** (discrete $y$): Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, k-NN.
- **Regression** (continuous $y$): Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso, Random Forest Regressor.
- *Example:* Email spam detection (classification), house price prediction (regression).

**(ii) Unsupervised Learning Models (2 marks)**
Discover hidden structure in unlabeled data.
- **Clustering:** K-Means partitions data into $K$ spherical clusters.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** PCA projects data onto principal components of maximum variance.
- **Density Estimation:** GMM models $P(X)$ as a mixture of Gaussians.
- *Example:* Customer segmentation via K-Means.

**(iii) Semi-Supervised Learning (1 mark)**
Combines a small labeled set with a large unlabeled set. Used when labeling is expensive (e.g., medical imaging with few annotated scans and many raw scans).

**(iv) Reinforcement Learning (1 mark)**
An agent learns a policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ by interacting with an environment and maximizing cumulative reward $\sum \gamma^t r_t$. *Example:* AlphaGo, robotic locomotion.

**(v) Self-Supervised Learning (1 mark)**
The model generates its own labels from raw data (e.g., masked word prediction). *Example:* BERT, GPT.

---

#### **(b) Derive the closed-form solution (normal equation) for the parameters of a linear regression model trained using MSE loss. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above)*

- **(1 mark)** Define the model $\hat{y}_i = w x_i + b$ and the MSE loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - w x_i - b)^2$.
- **(1 mark)** Compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial b$ and set to 0 to obtain $b = \bar{y} - w\bar{x}$.
- **(2 marks)** Compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial w$ and set to 0.
- **(2 marks)** Substitute the expression for $b$ and simplify to obtain the final closed-form expression:

$$w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \quad b = \bar{y} - w\bar{x}$$

- **(1 mark)** Interpretation: $w$ is the ratio of the covariance of $(X, Y)$ to the variance of $X$.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a typical machine learning system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A typical ML system consists of the following interacting components:

1. **Data Acquisition & Preprocessing (2 marks):** Collection of raw data, handling missing values, outlier removal, normalization (min-max or z-score).
2. **Feature Engineering (1 mark):** Selection, extraction, and transformation of discriminative features (e.g., PCA, one-hot encoding).
3. **Model & Hypothesis Space (1 mark):** Choosing the family of models $\mathcal{H}$ (linear, tree-based, neural) based on inductive bias.
4. **Loss Function & Optimizer (1 mark):** Defining the objective (MSE, cross-entropy) and the algorithm (gradient descent, Adam) to minimize it.
5. **Evaluation & Validation (1 mark):** Cross-validation, train/val/test split, metrics (accuracy, F1, RMSE).
6. **Deployment & Monitoring (1 mark):** Serving the model, tracking data drift, periodic retraining.

---

#### **(b) Explain the Bias-Variance Tradeoff. Derive the bias-variance decomposition of expected prediction error. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Conceptual Explanation (2 marks)**
- **Bias:** Error from *wrong assumptions* in the learning algorithm. High bias $\Rightarrow$ **underfitting** (model too simple).
- **Variance:** Error from *sensitivity to fluctuations* in the training set. High variance $\Rightarrow$ **overfitting** (model too complex).
- **Tradeoff:** Increasing model complexity decreases bias but increases variance. The optimal model minimizes their sum.

**(ii) Derivation (5 marks)** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.3 above)*

- **(1 mark)** Define expected prediction error at point $x$ for model $y = f(x) + \epsilon$.
- **(1 mark)** Expand $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{y})^2]$ and use independence of $\epsilon$ and $\hat{y}$ to drop the cross term.
- **(1 mark)** Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{y}]$ to split the remaining term.
- **(1 mark)** Identify Bias$^2 = (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}])^2$ and Variance = $\mathbb{E}[(\hat{y} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}])^2]$.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude with the final decomposition:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{y})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Confusing "model" with "algorithm"** (–1 mark): The *model* is the function $h(x;\theta)$; the *algorithm* is the procedure (e.g., gradient descent) that fits $\theta$. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in gradient descent** (–2 marks): The update is $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$, not $+\eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$.
> 3. **Missing $\sigma^2$ (irreducible error) in the bias-variance decomposition** (–1 mark): Many students write only Bias$^2$ + Variance, omitting the noise term.
> 4. **Writing $L = \frac{1}{2N}\sum (y - \hat{y})^2$ without justification** (–1 mark): The $\frac{1}{2}$ is a convention to cancel the 2 in the derivative. Either include the $\frac{1}{2}$ and mention why, or use $\frac{1}{N}$ without it.
> 5. **Vague supervised/unsupervised definitions** (–1 mark): Always emphasize the presence or absence of labels explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Machine Learning Models"**

- **Definition:** A model is a parameterized hypothesis $h(x;\theta)$ learned by minimizing a loss on training data.
- **Four Pillars:** Data + Model + Loss + Optimizer.
- **Hypothesis Space $\mathcal{H}$:** Set of all candidate functions; defines the inductive bias.
- **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM):** We minimize $\hat{R}(\theta)$ as a proxy for the unknown expected risk.
- **Taxonomy:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, Reinforcement, Self-Supervised.
- **Supervised** = labeled data; **Unsupervised** = unlabeled data; **RL** = reward signals.
- **Discriminative Models** learn $P(Y \vert X)$; **Generative Models** learn $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X \vert Y)$.
- **MSE Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ for regression.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ for classification.
- **OLS Closed-Form Solution:** $w = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$.
- **Gradient Descent Update:** $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$.
- **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- **Overfitting** = high variance, low bias; **Underfitting** = high bias, low variance.
- **Inductive Bias** = the set of assumptions a learner makes to predict unseen data.
- **KTU Favourite:** Always draw the block diagram of an ML system for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- **Valuation Key:** Definitions must be precise; derivations must show every algebraic step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyperparameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEECT413 (Machine Learning)**, a **hyperparameter** is a configuration variable whose value is set *prior* to the training process and remains external to the model itself. Unlike model parameters (such as the weights $\mathbf{W}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$ in a neural network) which are **learned** from the training data through optimization (e.g., gradient descent), hyperparameters are **not learned from data** but are instead chosen by the practitioner to govern the learning process itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hyperparameters are external to the model. They cannot be estimated from the training data using any standard learning algorithm (e.g., MLE, MAP, or backpropagation). They must be set *before* training and tuned using a meta-process such as validation, cross-validation, or search.

Formally, in a supervised learning setting, given a hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ and a learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$:

$$\mathcal{A}: \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \times \Lambda \rightarrow h$$

where $\Lambda = \{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_k\}$ is the **hyperparameter space** and $h \in \mathcal{H}$ is the induced hypothesis (trained model). The goal of hyperparameter selection is to find:

$$\lambda^* = \underset{\lambda \in \Lambda}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}\big(\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}, \lambda)\big)$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ is the loss measured on a held-out validation set.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef and Recipe" Model**

Imagine you are learning to cook a new dish (the *machine learning model*). The dish itself is built from ingredients that you adjust *while* you cook — tasting and adding salt, sugar, or spice as you go. These are the **parameters** of the recipe, learned iteratively.

However, *before* you ever start cooking, you must decide:
- What temperature to set the oven to (**learning rate** or **regularization strength**)
- How long to bake the dish (**number of epochs**)
- How many trays of the dish to make at once (**batch size**)
- Whether to use a convection or conventional oven (**model architecture**)

These decisions are made *before* the cooking begins, they are not adjusted by tasting the food — they are **hyperparameters**. Just as a chef runs many trial bakes to find the perfect oven temperature (a process of *tuning*), a machine learning engineer runs many training experiments to find the optimal hyperparameter configuration through a process known as **hyperparameter optimization (HPO)**.

## 1.3 Categories of Hyperparameters

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Model Hyperparameters** | Define the structural topology of the model — its capacity and representational power | Number of hidden layers, number of neurons per layer, activation function |
| **Algorithm (Optimization) Hyperparameters** | Govern the behavior of the learning algorithm itself | Learning rate $\eta$, momentum $\beta$, batch size $m$, number of epochs $E$ |
| **Regularization Hyperparameters** | Control overfitting and bias–variance tradeoff | $\ell_2$ penalty $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$, dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$, early stopping patience |
| **Data/Preprocessing Hyperparameters** | Influence how input data is transformed | Normalization scheme, feature selection threshold, image augmentation parameters |

## 1.4 Why Hyperparameters Matter in KTU 2024 Perspective

In KTU board examinations, the distinction between **parameters** and **hyperparameters** is a frequently tested concept. Examiners specifically look for clarity on:

1. Parameters are **internal** and **learned**.
2. Hyperparameters are **external** and **set**.
3. Hyperparameters *cannot* be learned by the same training algorithm.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam trick is to ask: *"Is the learning rate a parameter or a hyperparameter?"* The correct answer is **hyperparameter**, because it is set before training and controls *how* the parameters (weights) are updated — it is not updated by gradient descent itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Tier Optimization Hierarchy

Training a machine learning model involves a **two-level optimization problem**:

### Level 1 — Inner Loop (Parameter Learning)
Given a fixed hyperparameter configuration $\lambda$, solve:

$$\theta^*(\lambda) = \underset{\theta \in \Theta}{\arg\min} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\big) + \Omega(\theta; \lambda_{\text{reg}})$$

where:
- $\theta$ represents the **model parameters** (weights, biases)
- $\mathcal{L}$ is the task loss (e.g., cross-entropy, MSE)
- $\Omega(\theta; \lambda_{\text{reg}})$ is the regularization term governed by $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$

### Level 2 — Outer Loop (Hyperparameter Selection)
Find the hyperparameter configuration that minimizes validation error:

$$\lambda^* = \underset{\lambda \in \Lambda}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}\big(f_{\theta^*(\lambda)}\big)$$

This is a **bi-level optimization** problem — every evaluation of the outer objective requires solving the inner problem fully (a complete model retraining).

## 2.2 Common Hyperparameters — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Learning Rate ($\eta$)
- **Role:** Step size in gradient-based optimization.
- **Update rule:** $\theta_{t+1} \leftarrow \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$
- **Effect:**
  - Too small $\eta$ → slow convergence, may get stuck.
  - Too large $\eta$ → divergence, oscillation.
- **Default ranges:** $[10^{-6}, 10^0]$ on log-scale.

### 2.2.2 Number of Epochs ($E$)
- **Role:** Number of complete passes over the training set.
- **Effect:** Too few → underfitting; too many → overfitting.
- **Often paired with:** Early stopping to dynamically choose $E$.

### 2.2.3 Batch Size ($m$)
- **Role:** Number of samples per gradient update.
- **Tradeoff:**
  - Small $m$ → noisy gradients, regularization effect, faster per-iteration but unstable.
  - Large $m$ → smooth gradients, faster per-epoch but more memory.

### 2.2.4 Regularization Coefficient ($\lambda_{\text{reg}}$)
- **Role:** Strength of penalty on model complexity.
- **Loss:** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{emp}} + \lambda_{\text{reg}} \cdot \Omega(\theta)$
- **Tradeoff:** Large $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$ → high bias, low variance (underfitting); small $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$ → low bias, high variance (overfitting).

### 2.2.5 Network Architecture Hyperparameters
- Number of hidden layers $L$
- Number of units per layer $n_l$
- Activation function choice (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh)

## 2.3 The Bias–Variance Tradeoff via Hyperparameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Hyperparameters are the primary *knobs* through which an engineer controls the **bias–variance tradeoff**.

The expected prediction error of a model decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat{y} - y)^2\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

| Hyperparameter | Effect of Increasing |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Model capacity (layers/units) | ↓ Bias, ↑ Variance |
| $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$ | ↑ Bias, ↓ Variance |
| Learning rate $\eta$ | Can ↑ Variance if unstable |
| Training epochs $E$ | ↓ Bias, ↑ Variance (after a point) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Context |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Gradient descent update (governed by hyperparameter $\eta$) |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{emp}}(\theta) + \lambda_{\text{reg}} \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$ | $\ell_2$-regularized loss (governed by $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$) |
| 3 | $\lambda^* = \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\lambda)$ | Hyperparameter optimization objective |
| 4 | $\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{L}_k^{\text{val}}(\lambda)$ | K-fold cross-validation estimate for hyperparameter $\lambda$ |
| 5 | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Bias–variance decomposition |
| 6 | $\text{Grid size} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert \mathcal{G}_i \vert$ | Total configurations in Grid Search (where $\mathcal{G}_i$ is the grid for $i$-th hyperparameter) |
| 7 | $\mathbf{v}_{t+1} = \beta \mathbf{v}_t + (1-\beta) \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Momentum update (governed by $\beta \in [0,1)$) |
| 8 | $\text{Batch update: } \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i$ | Mini-batch SGD update (governed by $\eta$ and $m$) |
| 9 | $p_{\text{drop}}$ | Dropout rate — fraction of units randomly zeroed |
| 10 | $\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \text{decay} \cdot t}$ | Learning rate decay schedule (governed by $\eta_0$, decay rate) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In the KTU valuation key, examiners expect students to *explicitly state* the bi-level nature of the problem (parameter learning inside, hyperparameter selection outside). Forgetting to mention the validation set is a common cause of 2-mark deductions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hyperparameter tuning is the single most expensive computational activity in applied machine learning. In industrial settings:
- **Search budgets** often run from hundreds to thousands of GPU-hours.
- **Transfer learning** of hyperparameters across datasets is an active research area.
- **AutoML systems** (e.g., Google Vizier, Optuna, Ray Tune) automate the outer loop.
- In production, a poorly tuned learning rate or batch size can cause a model to silently fail to converge — making hyperparameter engineering a critical MLOps skill.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Hyperparameters Cannot Be Learned by Gradient Descent

### Setup
Suppose we have a model $f_\theta(\mathbf{x})$ with loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta; \lambda)$ that depends on a hyperparameter $\lambda$. A naive student might ask: *"Why can't we just take $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda}$ and do gradient descent on $\lambda$ too?"*

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Definition of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM).**
The training objective is:

$$\hat{\theta}(\lambda) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}(\theta; \lambda)$$

**Step 2 — Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) Insight.**
Since $\hat{\theta}(\lambda)$ is defined implicitly as the minimizer, it satisfies the **first-order optimality condition**:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}(\hat{\theta}(\lambda); \lambda) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiating the Validation Loss w.r.t. $\lambda$.**
The validation loss as a function of $\lambda$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\lambda) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}\big(\hat{\theta}(\lambda)\big)$$

By the chain rule:

$$\frac{d\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}}{d\lambda} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{\theta = \hat{\theta}} \cdot \frac{d\hat{\theta}}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Computing $\frac{d\hat{\theta}}{d\lambda}$ via IFT.**
Differentiating the optimality condition $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}(\hat{\theta}(\lambda); \lambda) = 0$ w.r.t. $\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d\lambda}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} &= \nabla_\theta^2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} \cdot \frac{d\hat{\theta}}{d\lambda} + \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\hat{\theta}}{d\lambda} &= -\big(\nabla_\theta^2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}\big)^{-1} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Hypergradient Expression.**
Substituting back:

$$\frac{d\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}}{d\lambda} = -\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}}{\partial \theta}^{\top} \big(\nabla_\theta^2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}\big)^{-1} \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
This expression is the **hypergradient**. Computing it requires:
- The **Hessian** $\nabla_\theta^2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}$ — an $n \times n$ matrix (intractable for large $n$).
- Its **inverse** — $O(n^3)$ in general.

This is why **naive gradient-based hyperparameter learning is infeasible** for modern models, and we resort to **black-box search methods** (Grid, Random, Bayesian).

> [!NOTE]
