> **KTU Insight:** While the *full* hypergradient is intractable, approximate methods (e.g., REINFORCE, finite differences, implicit differentiation) exist. This is an *advanced* topic and is **not** part of the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus, but understanding *why* hyperparameters are not learned sets the foundation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Hyperparameter Tuning Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | Computational Cost |
|----------|-----------|------|------|---------------------|
| **Grid Search** | Exhaustive enumeration over a discrete grid $\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{G}_1 \times \dots \times \mathcal{G}_k$ | Simple, parallelizable, deterministic | **Curse of dimensionality**: cost grows as $\prod \vert \mathcal{G}_i \vert$ | High in high dimensions |
| **Random Search** | Sample $\lambda \sim p(\lambda)$ i.i.d. for $T$ trials | Empirically more efficient than Grid in high-D (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012) | No learning from past trials | Moderate |
| **Bayesian Optimization** | Build a probabilistic surrogate $p(\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}} \vert \lambda)$ (e.g., GP) and use an acquisition function $\alpha(\lambda)$ | Sample-efficient, exploits past evaluations | Complex to implement; assumes smoothness | Low evaluations, but high per-eval overhead |
| **Hyperband / BOHB** | Successive halving with bandit resource allocation | Excellent for expensive models with fidelity control | Requires tuneable budget parameter | Adaptive |
| **Population-Based Training (PBT)** | Evolutionary hyperparameter schedule search | Discovers schedules, not just static values | Requires distributed infra | High |

## 3.3 Derivation: K-Fold Cross-Validation for Hyperparameter Selection

**Step 1 — Partition the training set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ into $K$ equal folds:**

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \bigsqcup_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{F}_k$$

**Step 2 — For each fold $k$ and candidate hyperparameter $\lambda$:**
Train on $K-1$ folds, validate on the held-out fold:

$$\mathcal{L}_k^{\text{val}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert} \sum_{(\mathbf{x}, y) \in \mathcal{F}_k} \mathcal{L}\big(f_{\theta_{-k}}(\mathbf{x}), y\big)$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate the $K$ fold losses:**

$$\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{L}_k^{\text{val}}(\lambda)$$

**Step 4 — Select the best hyperparameter:**

$$\lambda^* = \underset{\lambda \in \Lambda}{\arg\min} \; \hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}}(\lambda)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note (KTU):** Students frequently confuse *cross-validation* with *test set evaluation*. The cross-validation estimate is used to **select hyperparameters**; the test set is used **only once, at the very end**, to report generalization performance. Mixing these is a 2-mark deduction in valuation keys.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Grid Search on 2 Hyperparameters

**Problem:** A model has two hyperparameters: learning rate $\eta \in \{0.1, 0.01, 0.001\}$ and regularization $\lambda_{\text{reg}} \in \{0, 0.1, 1.0\}$. Validation accuracies (in %) are tabulated:

| $\eta \backslash \lambda_{\text{reg}}$ | 0 | 0.1 | 1.0 |
|---------------------------------------|------|------|------|
| **0.1** | 60 | 65 | 68 |
| **0.01** | 80 | 85 | 82 |
| **0.001** | 75 | 78 | 76 |

**Step 1 — Compute total grid configurations:** $\vert \mathcal{G}_\eta \vert \times \vert \mathcal{G}_{\lambda_{\text{reg}}} \vert = 3 \times 3 = 9$.

**Step 2 — Identify maximum:** $85\%$ at $(\eta = 0.01, \lambda_{\text{reg}} = 0.1)$.

**Step 3 — Verify via cross-validation (3-fold):** $\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}} = (84 + 86 + 85)/3 = 85.0\%$ — consistent with single-split estimate.

**Step 4 — Final selection:** $\lambda^* = (\eta^*, \lambda_{\text{reg}}^*) = (0.01, 0.1)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Two-Level Optimization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Outer Loop: Hyperparameter Search"] --> B["Select candidate lambda"]
    B --> C["Inner Loop: Train Model with lambda"]
    C --> D["Update parameters theta via Gradient Descent"]
    D --> E["Compute Validation Loss L_val"]
    E --> F{"Convergence or budget exhausted?"}
    F -- "No" --> C
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Record L_val for this lambda"]
    G --> H{"All lambda tested?"}
    H -- "No" --> B
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Select lambda* = argmin L_val"]
    I --> J["Report Final Test Performance"]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style I fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hyperparameter Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HP["Hyperparameters Lambda"] --> MH["Model Hyperparameters"]
    HP --> OH["Algorithm / Optimization Hyperparameters"]
    HP --> RH["Regularization Hyperparameters"]
    HP --> DH["Data / Preprocessing Hyperparameters"]

    MH --> M1["Number of layers L"]
    MH --> M2["Units per layer n_l"]
    MH --> M3["Activation function"]

    OH --> O1["Learning rate eta"]
    OH --> O2["Batch size m"]
    OH --> O3["Number of epochs E"]
    OH --> O4["Momentum beta"]

    RH --> R1["L2 penalty lambda_reg"]
    RH --> R2["Dropout rate p_drop"]
    RH --> R3["Early stopping patience"]

    DH --> D1["Normalization scheme"]
    DH --> D2["Augmentation parameters"]
    DH --> D3["Feature selection threshold"]

    style HP fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style MH fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style OH fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style RH fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style DH fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Grid Search vs. Random Search (Functional Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GRID["Grid Search Mechanism"]
        G1["Define discrete grid G1 x G2 x ... x Gk"] --> G2["Enumerate all combinations"]
        G2 --> G3["Train and evaluate each"]
        G3 --> G4["Select best configuration"]
    end

    subgraph RAND["Random Search Mechanism"]
        R1["Define distributions p_lambda_i"] --> R2["Sample T independent trials"]
        R2 --> R3["Train and evaluate each"]
        R3 --> R4["Select best configuration"]
    end

    style GRID fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style RAND fill:#FFF5EE,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Hyperparameter Tuning Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Define Hyperparameter Space"] --> S2["Step 2: Choose Search Strategy"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Define Validation Protocol k-fold CV"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Execute Search Loop"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Analyze Results and Select lambda*"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Retrain on full training set with lambda*"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Evaluate ONCE on test set"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Deploy Model"]

    style S1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for KTU Students:** In the diagrams above, blue-toned boxes represent *inputs/initialization* steps, green-toned boxes mark the *decision/selection* moments, and red/yellow boxes flag the *final evaluation and deployment* stage. This color code matches the KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) assessment rubric for CO1 (Apply) and CO2 (Analyze) of PEECT413.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between model parameters and hyperparameters with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Model Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Definition** | Internal configuration variables learned from training data | External configuration variables set before training |
| **Learning** | Learned via optimization (e.g., gradient descent) | Set by practitioner; not learned from data |
| **Notation** | $\theta = \{\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{b}\}$ | $\lambda = \{\eta, \lambda_{\text{reg}}, E, m, \dots\}$ |
| **Examples** | (i) Weights $\mathbf{W}$ of a neural network; (ii) Coefficients $\beta$ in linear regression | (i) Learning rate $\eta$; (ii) Number of epochs $E$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the basic distinction, 1 mark for one example each, 1 mark for clarity of presentation. A bare definition with no examples scores at most 2/3.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the learning rate considered a hyperparameter and not a model parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The learning rate $\eta$ is a **hyperparameter** because:

1. **External to the model:** $\eta$ is *not* a property of the hypothesis function $f_\theta$. It controls *how* the model parameters $\theta$ are updated, not *what* the model represents.

2. **Not learned by gradient descent:** During training, gradient descent updates $\theta$ as $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$. The gradient is taken w.r.t. $\theta$ only — $\eta$ is held fixed (or decayed by a schedule) and is not subject to differentiation in the parameter-learning step.

3. **Tuning requirement:** The optimal $\eta$ depends on the dataset, architecture, and loss landscape, and must be selected via a meta-process (e.g., validation or cross-validation), which is the defining characteristic of a hyperparameter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of hyperparameters in machine learning. Discuss the difference between model parameters and hyperparameters, and explain the **two-level optimization** framework for hyperparameter selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Grid Search** and **Random Search** methods for hyperparameter optimization. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. Why is Random Search often preferred in high-dimensional hyperparameter spaces? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A hyperparameter is a configuration setting whose value is used to control the learning process and is set *before* training begins. Unlike model parameters (e.g., weights $\mathbf{W}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$), hyperparameters cannot be estimated from training data by the learning algorithm itself.

**Difference Table (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Model Parameters $\theta$ | Hyperparameters $\lambda$ |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Source | Learned from data | Set by practitioner |
| Optimization | Gradient descent, MLE, MAP | Grid, Random, Bayesian search |
| Example | $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ in linear regression | $\eta, \lambda_{\text{reg}}, E$ |
| Notation in loss | Appears inside $\mathcal{L}$ | Appears as a meta-parameter |

**Two-Level Optimization Framework (3 Marks):**

- **Inner level:** For a fixed $\lambda$, learn optimal parameters:
$$\theta^*(\lambda) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}(\theta; \lambda)$$

- **Outer level:** Find the best $\lambda$ that minimizes validation loss:
$$\lambda^* = \underset{\lambda}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}\big(\theta^*(\lambda)\big)$$

- **Practical note:** The outer objective is non-differentiable, expensive, and noisy — hence the use of black-box search methods.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Grid Search (3 Marks):**
- Define a discrete grid $\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{G}_1 \times \mathcal{G}_2 \times \dots \times \mathcal{G}_k$.
- Evaluate all $\prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert \mathcal{G}_i \vert$ combinations.
- **Pros:** exhaustive, deterministic, parallelizable. **Cons:** suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**; wasteful if only a few hyperparameters matter.

**Random Search (2 Marks):**
- Sample $T$ independent configurations from a user-specified distribution $p(\lambda)$.
- **Pros:** empirically more efficient in high dimensions (Bergstra & Bengio); explores a wider range of values per axis. **Cons:** no exploitation of past evaluations.

**Comparison in High Dimensions (2 Marks):**
In a $k$-dimensional space where only $r \ll k$ hyperparameters actually influence the loss, Grid Search wastes $\prod \vert \mathcal{G}_i \vert$ trials across irrelevant axes. Random Search, in contrast, samples each axis $T$ times independently, so it gets $\sim T$ distinct values for the *relevant* $r$ dimensions — providing finer resolution where it matters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students describe Random Search as "selecting random points" without explaining **how** the random points are sampled (e.g., uniform on a log-scale for $\eta$). This omission costs 1 mark. Always specify the **sampling distribution** explicitly.

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is **K-fold cross-validation**? Explain step-by-step how it is used to select the best hyperparameter configuration. Why is it preferred over a single train–validation split? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bayesian Optimization** approach for hyperparameter tuning. Describe the role of the **surrogate model** and the **acquisition function** with one example of each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):** K-fold cross-validation is a resampling technique in which the training set is partitioned into $K$ equally-sized folds. The model is trained $K$ times, each time using $K-1$ folds for training and 1 fold for validation. The average validation error across the $K$ runs is used as the model's performance estimate.

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Hyperparameter Selection (3 Marks):**

1. Partition $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ into $K$ folds: $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \dots, \mathcal{F}_K$.
2. For each candidate hyperparameter $\lambda \in \Lambda$:
   - For $k = 1$ to $K$:
     - Train on $\bigcup_{j \neq k} \mathcal{F}_j$ → obtain $\hat{\theta}_{-k}(\lambda)$.
     - Compute validation loss $\mathcal{L}_k^{\text{val}}(\lambda)$ on $\mathcal{F}_k$.
   - Aggregate: $\hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathcal{L}_k^{\text{val}}(\lambda)$.
3. Select: $\lambda^* = \arg\min_{\lambda} \hat{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{CV}}(\lambda)$.

**Why Preferred over Single Split (2 Marks):**
- **Reduced variance:** Averaging across $K$ folds gives a more stable estimate of generalization error.
- **Better data utilization:** Every training sample is used for both training and validation.
- **Robustness to split luck:** Avoids the scenario where an "unlucky" single split hides a poor hyperparameter.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Bayesian Optimization Overview (2 Marks):**
Bayesian Optimization is a **sample-efficient**, **sequential** model-based optimization method for the expensive, black-box function $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\lambda)$. It builds a probabilistic surrogate of the loss surface and uses it to decide *where* to sample next.

**Surrogate Model (3 Marks):**
- **Role:** Approximates the unknown $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\lambda)$ using a probabilistic model that gives both a *predictive mean* $\mu(\lambda)$ and a *predictive uncertainty* $\sigma(\lambda)$.
- **Common choice:** **Gaussian Process (GP)** — non-parametric, provides closed-form posterior updates, naturally quantifies uncertainty.
- **Alternatives:** Random Forests (used in **SMAC**), Tree-structured Parzen Estimators (used in **Hyperopt**).

**Acquisition Function (2 Marks):**
- **Role:** Uses the surrogate's mean and uncertainty to score candidate $\lambda$ values, balancing **exploration** (high $\sigma$) vs **exploitation** (low $\mu$).
- **Common choices:**
  - **Expected Improvement (EI):** $\text{EI}(\lambda) = \mathbb{E}[\max(\mathcal{L}_{\text{best}} - \mathcal{L}(\lambda), 0)]$
  - **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB):** $\text{UCB}(\lambda) = \mu(\lambda) - \kappa \cdot \sigma(\lambda)$
  - **Probability of Improvement (PI)**
- **Example:** UCB with $\kappa = 1.96$ prefers points with low predicted loss **or** high uncertainty — thus driving exploration of the search space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q.B):** A common error is to confuse the **surrogate model** with the **actual ML model being tuned**. The surrogate is a cheap-to-evaluate probabilistic model of the validation loss, *not* a neural network for the prediction task. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion. Also, students frequently forget to state the **exploration–exploitation tradeoff** explicitly when describing the acquisition function.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEECT413 — Module 1: Hyperparameters**

- **Core distinction:** Parameters are *learned*; hyperparameters are *set*.
- **Canonical examples of hyperparameters:** Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $m$, number of epochs $E$, regularization strength $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$, number of hidden layers $L$, dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$, momentum $\beta$.
- **Bi-level optimization:** Inner loop learns $\theta$ for fixed $\lambda$; outer loop selects $\lambda$ via validation.
- **Selection criterion:** $\lambda^* = \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\lambda)$.
- **Three principal search strategies:** Grid Search (exhaustive), Random Search (sampled), Bayesian Optimization (model-based, sample-efficient).
- **Cross-validation is for hyperparameter selection; the test set is for final reporting only.** Mixing them is a common loss of marks.
- **Bias–variance tradeoff:** Increasing model capacity (a hyperparameter knob) decreases bias but increases variance.
- **Hypergradient is intractable** for large models due to Hessian-inverse cost — this justifies black-box search.
- **Log-scale sampling** is standard for $\eta$ and $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$ because they span multiple orders of magnitude.
- **Early stopping** is itself a hyperparameter-driven technique (patience, min-delta).
- **KTU exam buzzwords to use:** "set *a priori*", "external to the model", "meta-optimization", "bi-level", "validation loss", "cross-validation".

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain hyperparameter selection", always present (i) the formal bi-level formulation, (ii) at least one search method with its equation, and (iii) a one-line remark on cross-validation vs. test-set usage. This three-part structure maps to CO1, CO2, and CO3 of the KTU 2024 OBE framework and reliably scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
