<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Regularization** is a fundamental technique in machine learning used to **prevent overfitting** by adding an additional penalty term to the model's loss function that constrains the magnitude of the learned parameters. Formally, it modifies the standard empirical risk minimization objective

$$J(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ is the original loss function (e.g., Mean Squared Error or Cross-Entropy), $\Omega(\theta)$ is the **regularizer** (penalty term) that encodes prior beliefs about the parameter space, and $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **regularization hyperparameter** controlling the trade-off between fitting the training data and keeping the model weights small.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT413, Module 1):** Regularization is a core concept listed under *Methods to Handle Overfitting in Supervised Learning*. The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on its mathematical formulation (L1, L2, Elastic Net) and its relationship to the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for an examination using a thick reference textbook. You try to memorize **every single line** of the book, including footnotes, examples, and typos. When the actual exam arrives with slightly different questions, you perform poorly because you memorized noise instead of patterns.

**Regularization is like a wise teacher who tells you: "Understand the core concepts, ignore the trivial details."** In mathematical terms, it discourages the model from learning overly complex (high-magnitude weight) functions that capture random fluctuations in the training data, while still allowing it to capture the underlying signal.

| Regularization Term | Real-Life Analogy | Effect on Weights |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 (Lasso)** | A strict budget: you can only spend ₹100 total on groceries | Forces many weights to be **exactly zero** (sparse solution) |
| **L2 (Ridge)** | A soft budget: you should prefer affordable items, but overspending is allowed | Shrinks weights towards zero but **never exactly zero** |
| **Elastic Net** | A combination of strict and soft budgeting | Both feature selection **and** shrinkage simultaneously |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Do not confuse **Regularization** with **Normalization**. Regularization penalizes large weights *during training*; Normalization rescales input features *before training* (e.g., Min-Max, Z-score standardization). They solve different problems.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Regularization Strength ($\lambda$):** A non-negative scalar. Common search range: $\lambda \in \{10^{-4}, 10^{-3}, \ldots, 10^{3}\}$. Selected via **cross-validation**.
- **L1 Norm:** $\Omega_{L1}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$
- **L2 Norm (squared):** $\Omega_{L2}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i^{2}$
- **L2,1 Mixed (Elastic Net):** $\Omega_{EN}(\theta) = \rho \sum \vert \theta_i \vert + (1-\rho)\sum \theta_i^{2}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Regularization Works: The "Why" and "How"

Regularization is rooted in **statistical learning theory**, specifically **Structural Risk Minimization (SRM)** introduced by Vapnik–Chervonenkis. The reasoning unfolds in four structured steps:

1. **High-capacity models can overfit.** A polynomial of degree 20 fit to 10 data points will pass through every point perfectly (zero training error) yet generalize terribly. The model has *too much* representational freedom.
2. **Large weights indicate overfitting.** When a model assigns very large coefficients to certain features, it is often compensating for noise. Geometrically, the decision boundary becomes erratic.
3. **Penalize complexity.** By adding a term $\lambda \Omega(\theta)$ to the loss, we make the optimization problem *ill-behaved* to favor simpler hypotheses. This is essentially encoding **Occam's Razor**.
4. **Bias-Variance trade-off is tuned.** Increasing $\lambda$ *increases bias* (model underfits) but *decreases variance* (model generalizes better). The optimal $\lambda$ minimizes **generalization error**, not training error.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars: L1, L2, and Elastic Net

### L2 Regularization (Ridge / Tikhonov)
- **Penalty:** $\lambda \sum_{i} \theta_i^{2}$
- **Effect:** Shrinks weights proportionally; produces a unique closed-form solution.
- **Use case:** When *all* features are believed to be relevant.

### L1 Regularization (Lasso — Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)
- **Penalty:** $\lambda \sum_{i} \vert \theta_i \vert$
- **Effect:** Drives many weights to **exactly zero**, performing *automatic feature selection*.
- **Use case:** High-dimensional data where only a few features matter (e.g., genomics, text).

### Elastic Net
- **Penalty:** $\lambda \left[ \rho \sum_{i} \vert \theta_i \vert + (1-\rho) \sum_{i} \theta_i^{2} \right]$, where $\rho \in [0,1]$.
- **Effect:** Combines sparsity (L1) with stability (L2). Overcomes Lasso's limitation of selecting at most $n$ features when $p \gg n$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Property | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Loss (MSE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | No penalty | Baseline regression |
| 2 | **L2 Regularized Loss (Ridge)** | $J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (h_\theta(x)-y)^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum \theta_j^{2}$ | Convex, differentiable | General regularization |
| 3 | **L1 Regularized Loss (Lasso)** | $J_{L1}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (h_\theta(x)-y)^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ | Convex, **not** differentiable at 0 | Feature selection |
| 4 | **Elastic Net Loss** | $J_{EN}(\theta) = MSE + \lambda \left[ \rho \sum \vert \theta_j \vert + (1-\rho)\sum \theta_j^{2} \right]$ | Two hyperparameters: $\lambda, \rho$ | $p \gg n$ problems |
| 5 | Ridge Closed-Form | $\theta = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Always invertible | Direct computation |
| 6 | L2 Gradient Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m}\sum(h_\theta(x)-y)x_j + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j \right]$ | Decay term $\propto \theta_j$ | Gradient Descent |
| 7 | L1 Gradient Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m}\sum(h_\theta(x)-y)x_j + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(\theta_j) \right]$ | Constant step towards 0 | Sparse learning |
| 8 | Bayesian MAP view | $\Omega_{L2} \Leftrightarrow$ Gaussian prior $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2}I)$ | Max a Posteriori estimate | Probabilistic regularization |
| 9 | Bayesian MAP view | $\Omega_{L1} \Leftrightarrow$ Laplace prior $\theta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)$ | Promotes sparsity | Probabilistic regularization |
| 10 | Effective DOF (Ridge) | $\text{df}(\lambda) = \sum_{i} \frac{d_i^{2}}{d_i^{2} + \lambda}$ | Decreases with $\lambda$ | Model complexity metric |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value. In all exam scripts and notes, ensure you use the **correct pipe syntax** to avoid markdown rendering errors (e.g., use `\vert` inside LaTeX).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Regularization is deployed across virtually every industrial ML pipeline:

- **Computer Vision:** Prevents deep CNNs (e.g., ResNet, VGG) from memorizing ImageNet noise. **Weight Decay** in PyTorch/TensorFlow is mathematically equivalent to **L2 regularization**.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Lasso-style sparsity produces interpretable topic models and compact word embeddings.
- **Medical Diagnostics:** Elastic Net on genomic data ($p \approx 20{,}000$ genes, $n \approx 200$ patients) selects a small subset of disease markers.
- **Autonomous Systems:** Regularized reinforcement learning prevents policy collapse due to reward hacking.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Ridge regression is the de-facto baseline for economic indicators and weather prediction due to numerical stability of the closed-form solution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ridge Regression Closed-Form Solution

**Goal:** Find $\theta$ that minimizes the L2-regularized cost

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Vectorize the cost function.** Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ be the design matrix (with bias column of ones), $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ the target vector, and $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ the parameter vector. Then $X\theta$ is the prediction vector and the cost becomes

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y) + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\theta^{T}\theta$$

> *Conversion logic:* MSE expands to $\frac{1}{2m}\|X\theta - y\|^{2}$ and the L2 penalty is $\frac{\lambda}{2m}\|\theta\|^{2} = \frac{\lambda}{2m}\theta^{T}\theta$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\theta$.** Apply the matrix calculus rule

$$\nabla_\theta \left[ (X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y) \right] = 2X^{T}(X\theta - y)$$

and

$$\nabla_\theta \left[ \theta^{T}\theta \right] = 2\theta$$

Therefore,

$$\nabla_\theta J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}X^{T}(X\theta - y) + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta = 0$$

> *Conversion logic:* Both terms have the factor $\frac{1}{m}$ which is a constant and can be removed before setting equal to zero.

**Step 3 — Set the gradient to zero and solve.**

$$X^{T}(X\theta - y) + \lambda \theta = 0$$

$$X^{T}X\theta - X^{T}y + \lambda \theta = 0$$

$$(X^{T}X + \lambda I)\theta = X^{T}y$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain the closed-form solution.**

$$\boxed{\theta_{ridge} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T}y}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The crucial consequence of adding $\lambda I$ to $X^{T}X$ is that the matrix is now strictly **positive definite** (all eigenvalues $\geq \lambda > 0$), which guarantees invertibility. This solves the classic ill-conditioning problem when $X^{T}X$ is singular or near-singular.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: L2-Regularized Gradient Descent Update

**Goal:** Derive the iterative update rule for $\theta_j$.

**Step 1 — Write the cost for a single feature $j$ (typically $j \geq 1$, i.e., excluding the bias).**

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the gradient descent rule $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j \right]$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $\theta_j$ to reveal the decay term.**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j \left( 1 - \alpha \frac{\lambda}{m} \right) - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The factor $\left( 1 - \alpha \frac{\lambda}{m} \right)$ is the **weight decay** coefficient. It is *slightly less than 1*, so every iteration shrinks $\theta_j$ by a small percentage before applying the gradient step. This is exactly the **weight decay** algorithm used in deep learning.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lasso Gradient (Sub-Gradient at Zero)

L1 regularization is non-differentiable at $\theta_j = 0$. We define the **sub-gradient**:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j)$$

where $\text{sign}(\theta_j) = +1$ if $\theta_j > 0$, $-1$ if $\theta_j < 0$, and is in $[-1, +1]$ if $\theta_j = 0$. The update rule becomes

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \text{gradient term} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(\theta_j) \right]$$

> *Conversion logic:* The L1 sub-gradient is **constant in magnitude** (independent of $\theta_j$), whereas L2 grows linearly. Hence, with a sufficiently large $\lambda$, the sub-gradient of the penalty can outweigh the data term and push $\theta_j$ all the way to exactly zero, producing sparsity.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bayesian MAP Equivalence

**L2 ↔ Gaussian Prior:** Suppose we place a Gaussian prior on $\theta$, $\theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ independently. The log-posterior is

$$\log p(\theta \mid X, y) \propto \log p(y \mid X, \theta) + \log p(\theta)$$

$$\propto -\frac{1}{2\sigma_\epsilon^{2}}\sum (y^{(i)} - x^{(i)T}\theta)^{2} - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum \theta_j^{2}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Maximizing this posterior is equivalent to minimizing the Ridge cost with $\lambda = \sigma_\epsilon^{2}/\sigma^{2}$. Thus **L2 regularization = MAP estimation under a Gaussian prior on the weights**.

**L1 ↔ Laplace Prior:** A Laplace prior $p(\theta_j) \propto \exp(-\vert \theta_j \vert / b)$ yields the same MAP equivalence with Lasso, with $\lambda \propto 1/b$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) | Elastic Net |
|---|---|---|---|
| Penalty term | $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ | $\lambda \sum \theta_j^{2}$ | Both combined |
| Differentiability at 0 | **Not differentiable** | Smooth | Not differentiable |
| Solution sparsity | **Yes (exact zeros)** | No (small, non-zero) | Yes (controlled by $\rho$) |
| Unique solution | Not always | **Always unique** | Always unique |
| Computational solver | Coordinate descent, LARS | Closed-form, GD | Coordinate descent |
| Feature selection | **Yes** | No | Yes |
| Handles multicollinearity | Poorly (picks one) | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Best suited for | Sparse high-dim. data | All features relevant | $p \gg n$ correlated features |
| Bayesian prior | Laplace distribution | Gaussian distribution | Gaussian + Laplace mixture |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regularization Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Hypothesis h_theta_x] --> B[Choose Base Loss Function L]
    B --> C[Choose Regularizer Type]
    C --> D{L1, L2, or Elastic Net?}
    D --> D1[L1 Penalty: lambda * sum of absolute value of theta]
    D --> D2[L2 Penalty: lambda * sum of theta squared]
    D --> D3[Elastic Net: rho L1 + 1 minus rho L2]
    D1 --> E[Form Regularized Cost J of theta]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Choose Optimization Algorithm]
    F --> G{Analytic or Iterative?}
    G --> G1[Closed Form: X transpose X + lambda I inverse]
    G --> G2[Gradient Descent with Weight Decay]
    G --> G3[Coordinate Descent for L1]
    G1 --> H[Cross Validation to Tune Lambda]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I{Optimal Lambda Found?}
    I --> J[Train Final Model]
    I --> K[Return to Step F with New Lambda]
    J --> L[Evaluate on Test Set]
    L --> M[End: Deploy Regularized Model]
```

## 4.2 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODEL_BEHAVIOR[Model Behavior Space]
        A1[High Lambda] --> A2[Strong Regularization]
        A2 --> A3[High Bias, Low Variance]
        A3 --> A4[Underfitting]
        A4 --> A5[Simple Decision Boundary]
    end
    subgraph SWEET_SPOT[Optimal Zone]
        B1[Balanced Lambda] --> B2[Moderate Regularization]
        B2 --> B3[Bias and Variance Balanced]
        B3 --> B4[Best Generalization]
        B4 --> B5[Ideal Test Error]
    end
    subgraph OVERFIT_ZONE[Overfitting Zone]
        C1[Lambda equals 0] --> C2[No Regularization]
        C2 --> C3[Low Bias, High Variance]
        C3 --> C4[Overfitting]
        C4 --> C5[Erratic Decision Boundary]
    end
    MODEL_BEHAVIOR --> SWEET_SPOT
    SWEET_SPOT --> OVERFIT_ZONE
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: L1 vs L2 Constraint Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parameter Space Theta 1 Theta 2] --> B[Unconstrained Minimum at MSE Star]
    A --> C[Apply L1 Constraint: Diamond Region]
    A --> D[Apply L2 Constraint: Circular Region]
    C --> E[First Contact: On Axis Means Sparsity]
    D --> F[First Contact: Off Axis Means Shrinkage]
    E --> G[Sparse Solution with Zero Weights]
    F --> H[Non-zero Weights with Reduced Magnitude]
    G --> I[Final Lasso Solution]
    H --> J[Final Ridge Solution]
```

## 4.4 End-to-End Regularized Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Input Features] --> R2[Step 1: Feature Normalization Z score]
    R2 --> R3[Step 2: Add Bias Column of Ones]
    R3 --> R4[Step 3: Construct Design Matrix X]
    R4 --> R5[Step 4: Initialize Theta to Zero]
    R5 --> R6[Step 5: Compute Regularized Cost J]
    R6 --> R7{J Converged?}
    R7 --> R8[Yes: Theta is Final]
    R7 --> R9[No: Update Theta via Gradient Descent]
    R9 --> R6
    R8 --> R10[Step 6: Compute Training and Validation Error]
    R10 --> R11{Generalization Acceptable?}
    R11 --> R12[Yes: Deploy Model]
    R11 --> R13[No: Adjust Lambda and Return to Step 5]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q:** Define the term **regularization** in the context of machine learning. State any two commonly used regularization techniques.

**Model Answer:**
Regularization is a technique used to prevent overfitting in machine learning models by adding a penalty term to the loss function that discourages excessively large model parameters. The regularized objective is

$$J(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ is the empirical loss, $\Omega(\theta)$ is the penalty on parameter magnitude, and $\lambda$ is the regularization coefficient that controls the strength of the penalty.

Two commonly used regularization techniques are:
1. **L1 Regularization (Lasso):** Adds $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ to the loss, inducing sparsity.
2. **L2 Regularization (Ridge):** Adds $\lambda \sum \theta_j^{2}$ to the loss, shrinking weights smoothly.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q:** Differentiate between **L1** and **L2** regularization with respect to the nature of the penalty term, the resulting model sparsity, and the closed-form solution availability.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty term | $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ | $\lambda \sum \theta_j^{2}$ |
| Sparsity | Produces **exact zeros** (sparse solution) | Produces only **small non-zero** weights |
| Closed-form solution | **Not directly available** (requires iterative solvers like coordinate descent) | **Yes:** $\theta = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T}y$ |
| Differentiability | Non-differentiable at 0 | Differentiable everywhere |
| Best suited for | Feature selection | Multicollinearity handling |

L1 is preferred when sparsity and interpretability are critical; L2 is preferred when all features contribute and a smooth, stable solution is needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** in machine learning. How does regularization help to manage this tradeoff? Illustrate with a suitable diagram.

**Model Answer:**

The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** describes the decomposition of the expected prediction error of a model into three components:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}$$

- **Bias²:** Error due to the model's inability to represent the true function (underfitting).
- **Variance:** Sensitivity of the model to small fluctuations in the training data (overfitting).
- **$\sigma^{2}$:** Irreducible noise.

**How Regularization Manages the Tradeoff:**

1. As model complexity (e.g., polynomial degree) increases, **bias decreases** but **variance increases**.
2. **Increasing $\lambda$** in the regularized loss adds a penalty that constrains model complexity, *raising bias* but *lowering variance*.
3. The optimal $\lambda$ minimizes the **sum** of bias² and variance, hence minimizing test error.
4. **L1 regularization** additionally performs feature selection, reducing effective model complexity and variance dramatically.
5. **L2 regularization** shrinks all weights uniformly, smoothing the hypothesis space.

**Diagrammatic Description (U-shape):**

| $\lambda$ | Bias² | Variance | Test Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda = 0$ | Low | High | High |
| $\lambda = \lambda^*$ | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** |
| $\lambda \to \infty$ | High | Low | High |

Test error forms a characteristic **U-shape** with the minimum at $\lambda^*$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **closed-form solution for Ridge Regression** (L2-regularized linear regression). Explain how the term $\lambda I$ addresses the issue of non-invertibility in $X^{T}X$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** The Ridge cost function is

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^{2}$$

**Step 2:** In vectorized form,

$$J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y) + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\theta^{T}\theta$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$,

$$\nabla_\theta J_{L2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}X^{T}(X\theta - y) + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta$$

**Step 4:** Set gradient to zero,

$$X^{T}X\theta - X^{T}y + \lambda \theta = 0$$

$$(X^{T}X + \lambda I)\theta = X^{T}y$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\theta$,

$$\boxed{\theta_{ridge} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T}y}$$

**Addressing Non-Invertibility:**

- When features are highly correlated or $m < n$, the matrix $X^{T}X$ is **singular** or **ill-conditioned** (determinant $\approx 0$), making the OLS estimator $(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$ numerically unstable or impossible.
- Adding $\lambda I$ to $X^{T}X$ shifts all eigenvalues upward by $\lambda$. Since $\lambda > 0$, **every eigenvalue is now strictly positive**, making $X^{T}X + \lambda I$ **strictly positive definite** and hence **invertible**.
- Geometrically, this corresponds to adding a small ridge to the diagonal of the covariance matrix, hence the name "Ridge Regression."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **(a)** Many students confuse the U-shape with respect to model complexity vs. $\lambda$. State explicitly: "As $\lambda$ increases, bias increases and variance decreases."
> - **(b)** Forgetting to **exclude the bias term $\theta_0$** from the penalty. In standard practice (and what KTU expects), the penalty is applied to $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$ only, not the intercept. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> - Failing to mention that $\lambda I$ is what guarantees invertibility costs marks — this is the **core insight** of the derivation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **L1 (Lasso) Regularization**. Derive the **gradient descent update rule** for Lasso and discuss why it produces sparse solutions.

**Model Answer:**

**L1 (Lasso) Regularization** adds the sum of absolute values of weights to the loss:

$$J_{L1}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert \theta_j \vert$$

**Step 1:** The absolute value function is non-differentiable at $\theta_j = 0$, so we use the **sub-gradient**:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(\theta_j)$$

where $\text{sign}(\theta_j) = +1$ if $\theta_j > 0$, $-1$ if $\theta_j < 0$, and $\in [-1, +1]$ if $\theta_j = 0$.

**Step 2:** Gradient descent update:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\text{sign}(\theta_j) \right]$$

**Why Lasso Produces Sparse Solutions:**

- The L1 sub-gradient has **constant magnitude $\lambda/m$**, regardless of $\theta_j$'s current value.
- In contrast, the L2 penalty's gradient grows linearly: $\partial(\lambda\theta_j^{2}/2)/\partial\theta_j = \lambda\theta_j/m$.
- For small $\theta_j$, the L2 update becomes negligible, but the L1 update remains a **fixed constant step** towards zero.
- If $\lambda$ is large enough, this constant step can drive $\theta_j$ across zero in a single iteration, locking it at zero thereafter.
- Geometrically, the L1 constraint region is a **diamond** (sharp corners on the axes), and the unconstrained minimum often first touches this region on a corner — corresponding to one or more weights being exactly zero.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With suitable diagrams, explain the **geometric interpretation of L1 and L2 regularization** as constrained optimization problems. Comment on the role of $\lambda$ in each case.

**Model Answer:**

Regularization can be viewed as a **constrained optimization** problem:

$$\min_\theta J(\theta) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Omega(\theta) \leq t$$

where $t$ is a budget on parameter magnitude. This is **equivalent** to the Lagrangian form $J(\theta) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$ via duality.

**Geometric Interpretation (in 2D parameter space $\theta_1, \theta_2$):**

- The unconstrained MSE optimum lies at the **center of the elliptical contours** of the cost function.
- We seek the point on the constrained region that **first touches** these ellipses as we expand outward.

| Regularization | Constraint Region | Geometry | First Contact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L2 (Ridge)** | $\theta_1^{2} + \theta_2^{2} \leq t$ | **Circle** (smooth boundary) | Ellipse touches **smoothly** → both $\theta_1, \theta_2$ small but **non-zero** |
| **L1 (Lasso)** | $\vert \theta_1 \vert + \vert \theta_2 \vert \leq t$ | **Diamond** (sharp corners) | Ellipse likely touches a **corner** → one of $\theta_1, \theta_2 = 0$ |

**Role of $\lambda$ (or equivalently $t$):**

- **Small $\lambda$ (large $t$):** Loose constraint → unconstrained optimum is feasible → minimal shrinkage.
- **Large $\lambda$ (small $t$):** Tight constraint → solution is forced to be very small or sparse.
- **$\lambda \to \infty$ ( $t \to 0$):** All weights collapse to zero (only the intercept remains).
- **$\lambda = 0$:** No regularization → pure empirical risk minimization → risk of overfitting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> - **(a)** Many students write $\text{sign}(0) = 0$. This is wrong — the sub-gradient at 0 is in $[-1, +1]$. Mentioning sub-gradient is **mandatory** for full marks.
> - **(b)** Geometric explanation must include both the **diamond and circle** explicitly. Skipping the diagram description costs 2 marks. Also, clarify that $\lambda$ and $t$ are inversely related (Lagrangian duality).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Regularization)**

- ☐ **Regularization** = penalty on model complexity added to loss to prevent overfitting.
- ☐ Core equation: $J(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$.
- ☐ **L1 (Lasso):** penalty $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ → **sparse** (exact zeros), non-differentiable, no closed-form.
- ☐ **L2 (Ridge):** penalty $\lambda \sum \theta_j^{2}$ → smooth shrinkage, differentiable, **closed-form** $\theta = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T}y$.
- ☐ **Elastic Net:** combines L1 and L2 with mixing parameter $\rho$.
- ☐ **$\lambda$ = regularization strength**; chosen by **cross-validation**; large $\lambda$ → high bias / low variance.
- ☐ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \text{Noise}$; regularization navigates the U-shape of test error.
- ☐ **Weight Decay** in deep learning = L2 regularization.
- ☐ **Bayesian view:** L2 ↔ Gaussian prior; L1 ↔ Laplace prior; regularization = MAP estimation.
- ☐ **Ridge closed-form** guarantees invertibility because $\lambda I$ makes $X^{T}X + \lambda I$ positive definite.
- ☐ **Geometric view:** L1's diamond has corners on axes → sparsity; L2's circle → smooth shrinkage.
- ☐ L1 uses **coordinate descent**; L2 uses gradient descent or closed-form.
- ☐ **Bias term $\theta_0$ is typically NOT penalized** — only $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$ are.
- ☐ **Dropout** (neural networks) and **early stopping** are also forms of regularization.
- ☐ **Standardization of features** is recommended *before* applying regularization (since penalty is sensitive to scale).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training - Batch and Online Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Training - Batch and Online Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Training in Machine Learning?

In the context of supervised and unsupervised Machine Learning, **Training** is the computational process of optimizing the parameters (weights and biases) of a learning model by minimizing a predefined loss function using a dataset. The goal of training is to enable the model to generalize well to **unseen** data drawn from the same underlying distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> Training refers to the iterative estimation of model parameters $\theta$ such that a loss (cost) function $J(\theta)$ is minimized, either by processing the **entire dataset at once (Batch)** or by updating parameters **incrementally using one or a few samples at a time (Online)**.

The mathematical objective of training can be formally written as:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}} J(\theta)$$

where $\theta^{*}$ denotes the optimal parameter vector that minimizes the empirical risk $J(\theta)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Cooking Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — Chef, Spice & Dish Analogy:**
> Imagine a chef preparing a dish and adjusting salt at every step.
> - **Batch Learning** → The chef adds **all ingredients first**, then tastes the final dish once and adjusts everything. Slow, accurate, but uses massive counter space.
> - **Online Learning** → The chef tastes after **each spoonful** and adds salt one pinch at a time. Fast, adaptive, can cook forever, but noisy.
> - **Mini-batch Learning** → The chef tastes after **every handful of bites** and adjusts. A balanced middle ground.

This analogy perfectly captures the trade-offs between **stability vs. adaptability** and **memory cost vs. responsiveness**.

### 1.3 Formal Definitions

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Batch Learning (Offline Learning)** | A training paradigm where the model is trained on the **entire available dataset at once**. The training procedure accesses all $m$ training examples to compute the gradient before each parameter update. |
| 2 | **Online Learning (Incremental Learning)** | A training paradigm where the model parameters are updated **sequentially, one sample (or a small mini-batch) at a time**, as data arrives in a stream. |
| 3 | **Mini-batch Learning** | A hybrid paradigm where updates are performed on **small randomly-sampled subsets** of size $b$ (where $1 < b < m$) of the training data. |
| 4 | **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** | The canonical online learning algorithm that updates parameters using the gradient computed from a **single sample**. |
| 5 | **Epoch** | One complete pass of the **entire training dataset** through the learning algorithm. |
| 6 | **Iteration** | A **single update step** of the model parameters. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions, students often confuse "Batch Learning" (a paradigm) with "Batch Gradient Descent" (an optimization algorithm). Be precise — **Batch Learning uses Batch Gradient Descent**, and **Online Learning uses SGD or Mini-batch SGD**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Training

Regardless of the paradigm chosen, every training procedure in supervised learning follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Hypothesis Selection**: Choose a model family (e.g., Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Neural Network) parameterized by $\theta$.
- **Step 2 — Loss Function Definition**: Define a measure of error, e.g., Mean Squared Error (MSE) for regression or Cross-Entropy for classification.
- **Step 3 — Data Exposure**: Present the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ to the learning algorithm.
- **Step 4 — Gradient Computation**: Calculate $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$ — the direction of steepest ascent of the loss.
- **Step 5 — Parameter Update**: Move parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient (steepest descent).
- **Step 6 — Convergence Check**: Verify whether $\|\nabla J(\theta)\| < \epsilon$ or whether the loss has plateaued.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?**
> Training essentially performs **iterative numerical optimization** over a (typically) non-convex loss landscape. The choice between Batch, Mini-batch, and Online determines the *accuracy* of each gradient estimate and the *frequency* of updates.

### 2.2 Batch Learning — Deep Mechanics

In **Batch Learning**, the cost function is the average loss over **all** $m$ training examples:

$$J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}),\, y^{(i)}\big)$$

The parameter update for Batch Gradient Descent is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J_{batch}(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate (hyperparameter)** and $\nabla_{\theta} J_{batch}(\theta_t)$ is the **true gradient** computed over all $m$ samples.

**Key characteristics of Batch Learning:**
- Deterministic trajectory — same data, same updates.
- Stable convergence due to low-variance gradient estimates.
- Requires the **entire dataset to reside in memory** (RAM/VRAM).
- Cannot adapt to new data without retraining from scratch.
- One update per epoch.

### 2.3 Online Learning — Deep Mechanics

In **Online Learning**, the cost function is approximated using a **single sample** at each step:

$$J_{online}(\theta;\, x^{(i)},\, y^{(i)}) = \mathcal{L}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}),\, y^{(i)}\big)$$

The parameter update for Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta_{t} \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(t)}),\, y^{(t)}\big)$$

**Key characteristics of Online Learning:**
- Stochastic, noisy trajectory — each update is a "noisy estimate" of the true gradient.
- High variance per update, but updates are extremely fast.
- **No requirement** to store the entire dataset — works on streaming data.
- Can adapt in **real-time** to concept drift (changing data distributions).
- $m$ updates per epoch.

### 2.4 Mini-batch Learning — The Engineering Compromise

Mini-batch learning uses a batch size $b$ where $1 < b < m$. The cost is computed over the mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$:

$$J_{mini}(\theta) = \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \mathcal{L}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}),\, y^{(i)}\big)$$

The update rule becomes:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J_{mini}(\theta)$$

This is the **de facto standard** in modern deep learning (e.g., PyTorch `batch_size=32, 64, 128`).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear regularly in KTU ESE Module 1 questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Update Rule | Key Variable | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$ | $m$ = total samples | Foundation of all training |
| 2 | Batch Gradient Descent | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J_{batch}(\theta_t)$ | Uses all $m$ samples | 1 update / epoch |
| 3 | Stochastic GD (SGD) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x^{(t)}), y^{(t)})$ | Uses 1 sample | $m$ updates / epoch |
| 4 | Mini-batch GD | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \big(\frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \mathcal{L}_i\big)$ | Uses $b$ samples | $m/b$ updates / epoch |
| 5 | MSE Loss | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Quadratic penalty | Standard for regression |
| 6 | Gradient of MSE | $\nabla_\theta J = \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta - y)$ | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Used in linear regression |
| 7 | Updates per Epoch | $u = \lfloor m / b \rfloor$ | $b$ = batch size | Total updates in 1 pass |
| 8 | Effective Memory | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | $d$ = feature dim | Memory per update |
| 9 | SGD Noise Variance | $\text{Var}(\nabla \mathcal{L}_i) \approx \sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2$ decreases as $b \uparrow$ | Theoretical noise bound |
| 10 | Learning Rate Decay | $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \lambda t)$ | $\lambda$ = decay rate | Used in SGD schedules |

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Batch Learning**: Used in industrial predictive maintenance where the entire failure dataset is collected offline and a stable, reproducible model is required.
- **Online Learning**: Deployed in stock market prediction engines, recommendation systems (YouTube, Netflix), and IoT sensor networks where data arrives continuously.
- **Mini-batch Learning**: Powers every modern deep learning model — ResNet, BERT, GPT — using GPUs that parallelize matrix multiplications of size $b \times d$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Batch Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

**Setup:** Consider the standard linear regression hypothesis:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \theta_j x_j$$

where $\theta_0$ is the bias term (with $x_0 = 1$). The training set contains $m$ examples:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$$

**Step 1 — Define the per-example squared loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is introduced purely for mathematical convenience so that the derivative cancels the constant 2.

**Step 2 — Aggregate into the Batch Cost Function:**

$$J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient with respect to a single parameter $\theta_j$:**

Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)$$

Since $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \theta^T x^{(i)} = \sum_k \theta_k x_k^{(i)}$, we get:

$$\frac{\partial h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial J_{batch}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Express in matrix-vector form:**

Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ be the design matrix and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ the target vector. The vectorized gradient is:

$$\nabla_{\theta} J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta - y)$$

**Step 5 — Write the Batch Update Rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} \;=\; \theta_t \;-\; \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta_t - y)}$$

This is the **canonical Batch Gradient Descent update** required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stochastic Gradient Descent (Online) Update Rule

**Step 1 — Approximate the full-batch gradient by a single-sample gradient:**

The true gradient is:

$$\nabla_\theta J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta)$$

In SGD, we approximate this expectation by sampling **one index $i$ uniformly at random**:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta) \approx \nabla_\theta J_{batch}(\theta)$$

By the law of large numbers, this is an **unbiased estimator**:

$$\mathbb{E}_i\big[\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta)\big] = \nabla_\theta J_{batch}(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Form the per-sample loss gradient:**

For linear regression with squared loss:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta) = \big(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Write the SGD Update Rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} \;=\; \theta_t \;-\; \eta_t \cdot \big(h_\theta(x^{(i_t)}) - y^{(i_t)}\big) \cdot x^{(i_t)}}$$

where $i_t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ is the randomly sampled index at iteration $t$, and $\eta_t$ is the **decaying learning rate** satisfying the **Robbins-Monro conditions**:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$

These conditions **guarantee almost-sure convergence** of SGD to a local minimum.

### 3.3 Mini-batch Gradient Descent Derivation

**Step 1 — Sample a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $b$:**

$\mathcal{B}_t = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_b\}$ drawn uniformly without replacement.

**Step 2 — Compute the mini-batch gradient:**

$$\nabla_\theta J_{mini}(\theta; \mathcal{B}_t) = \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t}\big(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Update Rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} \;=\; \theta_t \;-\; \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t}\big(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot x^{(i)}}$$

**Variance analysis** — The variance of the mini-batch gradient estimate is:

$$\text{Var}\big(\nabla_\theta J_{mini}\big) \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{b}$$

where $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)})$. As $b \to m$, variance $\to 0$ (Batch); as $b = 1$, variance is maximum (SGD).

### 3.4 KTU Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Batch Learning | Mini-batch Learning | Online (SGD) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Samples per update | $m$ (all) | $b$ (typically 32–512) | $1$ |
| Updates per epoch | $1$ | $m/b$ | $m$ |
| Gradient estimate | Exact (true gradient) | Unbiased, low variance | Unbiased, high variance |
| Memory requirement | $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ |
| Convergence | Smooth, deterministic | Stable, slightly noisy | Noisy but adaptive |
| Suitability for streaming data | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Adaptability to concept drift | None (must retrain) | Partial | Strong |
| Computational cost per update | High (matrix-vector on full $X$) | Moderate | Very low |
| Used in deep learning | Rare | **Universal** | Common in RL |
| Implementation difficulty | Easy | Easy | Easy |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Training Paradigm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize theta_0] --> B{Choose Training Paradigm?}
    B -->|Batch| C[Load Entire Dataset into Memory]
    B -->|Mini-batch| D[Shuffle and Partition into Batches of Size b]
    B -->|Online| E[Receive Single Sample x_i, y_i from Stream]
    C --> F[Compute Full Gradient over all m samples]
    D --> G[For each mini-batch: Compute Average Gradient]
    E --> H[Compute Gradient on Single Sample]
    F --> I[Update theta using Full Gradient]
    G --> J[Update theta using Mini-batch Gradient]
    H --> K[Update theta using Stochastic Gradient]
    I --> L{Convergence Reached?}
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -->|No| M{Continue Paradigm?}
    L -->|Yes| N[Stop: Output theta_star]
    M -->|Yes - Batch| C
    M -->|Yes - Mini-batch| D
    M -->|Yes - Online| E
    M -->|No - Stop Epoch| N
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Training System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Pipeline]
        A1[Raw Data Source] --> A2[Preprocessing Module]
        A2 --> A3[Data Loader / Stream Manager]
    end

    subgraph ParadigmRouter[Paradigm Selection Router]
        A3 --> B1{Batch Size Selector}
        B1 -->|b equals m| C1[Batch Mode]
        B1 -->|1 less than b less than m| C2[Mini-batch Mode]
        B1 -->|b equals 1| C3[Online SGD Mode]
    end

    subgraph OptimizationEngine[Optimization Engine]
        C1 --> D1[Gradient Computation on Full X]
        C2 --> D2[Gradient Computation on Mini-batch B_t]
        C3 --> D3[Gradient Computation on Single Sample]
        D1 --> E1[Parameter Update: theta less eta times grad]
        D2 --> E1
        D3 --> E1
        E1 --> E2[Loss Logging and Convergence Checker]
        E2 -->|Continue| B1
        E2 -->|Stop| F1[Final Model theta_star]
    end
```

### 4.3 Gradient Descent Trajectory Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Convex Loss Landscape with Global Minimum] --> B[Batch GD Path]
    A --> C[Mini-batch GD Path]
    A --> D[SGD Path]
    B --> B1[Direct, smooth line to minimum]
    C --> C1[Slightly oscillating line, converges quickly]
    D --> D1[Highly noisy zigzag path, eventually converges]
    B1 --> E[Final Optimum]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New ML Problem] --> B{Is data arriving in real-time stream?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Online Learning with SGD]
    B -->|No| D{Dataset fits in memory?}
    D -->|No| E[Mini-batch with DataLoader]
    D -->|Yes| F{Requires high stability and reproducibility?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Batch Learning]
    F -->|No| E
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A contains short-answer questions worth 3 marks each. Answers should be concise (½ to 1 page), definition-oriented, and must include one relevant formula or example.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define **Batch Learning** and **Online Learning** in machine learning. State **one** advantage and **one** disadvantage of each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Batch Learning (Offline Learning):** A training paradigm in which the model is trained on the **entire available dataset at once**. The cost function is computed over all $m$ training examples, and parameters are updated using the full-batch gradient:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta_t)$$

- **Advantage:** Stable, deterministic convergence with low-variance gradient estimates.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires the entire dataset to be loaded into memory; cannot adapt to new data without retraining.

**Online Learning:** A training paradigm where model parameters are updated **incrementally, one sample at a time**, as data arrives in a stream. The canonical algorithm is **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i_t)}(\theta_t)$$

- **Advantage:** Adapts in real-time to streaming data and concept drift; minimal memory footprint.
- **Disadvantage:** Noisy, high-variance updates make convergence oscillatory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
What is an **epoch** in the context of training? How does the number of parameter updates per epoch differ between **Batch GD**, **Mini-batch GD**, and **SGD** for a dataset of size $m$ with mini-batch size $b$? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

An **epoch** is defined as **one complete pass of the entire training dataset** through the learning algorithm.

| Algorithm | Samples Used per Update | Updates per Epoch |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Batch Gradient Descent | $m$ | $1$ |
| Mini-batch Gradient Descent | $b$ | $\lfloor m / b \rfloor$ |
| Stochastic Gradient Descent (Online) | $1$ | $m$ |

For example, if $m = 100{,}000$ and $b = 100$, then Batch GD performs **1** update per epoch, Mini-batch GD performs **1000** updates per epoch, and SGD performs **100,000** updates per epoch.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with **internal choice** (i.e., either OR). Each sub-question is typically worth 7 marks. Provide a rigorous, structured answer with formulas, derivations, and diagrams where applicable.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**(a) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Explain in detail the **Batch Learning** paradigm in machine learning. Derive the **Batch Gradient Descent update rule** for linear regression with the Mean Squared Error loss function. Discuss the convergence properties. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Batch Learning (also called Offline Learning) is a training paradigm where the model parameters are updated using the gradient computed over the **entire training dataset of $m$ samples**. No parameter update occurs until the algorithm has scanned all examples.

**Loss Function Setup:** For a hypothesis $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$, the MSE cost is:

$$J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)^2$$

**Derivation of Gradient (repeated for emphasis):**

$$\frac{\partial J_{batch}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

In vectorized form with design matrix $X$:

$$\nabla_\theta J_{batch}(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta - y)$$

**Update Rule:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta_t - y)$$

**Convergence Properties:**

- For a **convex** cost function (e.g., linear regression with MSE), Batch GD is guaranteed to converge to the **global minimum** provided the learning rate $\eta$ satisfies $0 < \eta < 2 / \lambda_{max}$, where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $X^T X$.
- For **non-convex** cost functions (e.g., neural networks), Batch GD converges to a **local minimum** or saddle point.
- Convergence rate is **linear** (geometric) for strongly convex problems.
- Each update is computationally expensive: $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot n)$ operations.

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Discuss the **limitations of Batch Learning** for large-scale and streaming applications. Explain with examples why **online learning methods** are preferred in such scenarios. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Batch Learning:**

1. **Memory Bottleneck:** Loading $m$ samples (with $m$ in millions) requires $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot d)$ memory, infeasible for big-data applications.
2. **No Real-Time Adaptation:** Once trained, the model is **static**. New data requires retraining from scratch, which is computationally prohibitive.
3. **Concept Drift:** In dynamic environments (e.g., user preferences on Netflix), the underlying data distribution changes over time. Batch models become stale.
4. **Computational Cost per Update:** Each step requires processing the full matrix $X$, making per-update cost high.
5. **Single Point of Failure:** If data is corrupt or noisy, batch training over the entire dataset may yield poor models.

**Why Online Learning is Preferred:**

| Scenario | Why Online? |
|----------|-------------|
| Stock price prediction | Data arrives tick-by-tick; model must adapt |
| Click-through rate prediction (ads) | User behavior changes hourly |
| Spam detection | New spam patterns emerge daily |
| IoT sensor analytics | Continuous stream from millions of devices |
| Recommendation systems | Cold-start items need immediate ranking |

Online learning with SGD enables **per-sample parameter updates** with **constant memory $\mathcal{O}(d)$** and natural handling of concept drift. Algorithms like **Passive-Aggressive**, **Truncated Gradient**, and **Follow-The-Regularized-Leader (FTRL)** are popular industrial choices.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**(a) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Explain the **Online Learning** paradigm with **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**. Derive the **SGD update rule** and state the **Robbins-Monro conditions** for convergence. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Online Learning Paradigm:** In online learning, training data arrives sequentially as a stream $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots\}$ and the model parameters are updated **immediately after each sample** is observed. The canonical algorithm is **Stochastic Gradient Descent**.

**Derivation of SGD Update Rule:**

The true gradient over all $m$ samples is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\theta)$$

SGD approximates this expectation by sampling a single index $i_t$ uniformly at random:

$$\hat{g}_t = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i_t)}(\theta_t)$$

By the law of large numbers, $\mathbb{E}[\hat{g}_t] = \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t)$ — i.e., the estimator is **unbiased**.

**SGD Update:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i_t)}(\theta_t)}$$

**Robbins-Monro Conditions (1951):** For almost-sure convergence of SGD to a local minimum, the learning rate sequence $\{\eta_t\}$ must satisfy:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$

The first condition ensures the algorithm can reach the optimum from any starting point; the second ensures the noise decays fast enough for convergence.

A common schedule satisfying these is $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + \lambda t)$.

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Compare and contrast **Batch, Mini-batch, and Stochastic Gradient Descent** with respect to: (i) computational cost per update, (ii) variance of gradient estimate, (iii) suitability for parallel hardware, (iv) convergence behavior. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Batch GD | Mini-batch GD | SGD (Online) |
|--------|----------|---------------|--------------|
| **(i) Cost per Update** | $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot n)$ — high | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot n)$ — moderate | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — lowest |
| **(ii) Gradient Variance** | Zero (deterministic) | $\sigma^2 / b$ — low | $\sigma^2$ — highest |
| **(iii) Parallel Hardware (GPU)** | Poor — sequential scalar updates | **Excellent** — vectorized matrix ops on GPU | Poor — sequential scalar updates |
| **(iv) Convergence Behavior** | Smooth monotonic decrease | Stable with mild oscillations | Noisy oscillations, but escapes local minima |

**Key Insights:**

- Batch GD gives the **most accurate** gradient direction but pays a heavy computational cost.
- Mini-batch GD offers the **best engineering trade-off** — it leverages GPU parallelism and provides stable yet frequent updates. This is why every modern deep learning library uses mini-batches by default.
- SGD introduces **stochastic noise** that can actually help the algorithm **escape shallow local minima** and saddle points, which is beneficial in non-convex deep learning landscapes.
- The relationship is: **Batch ⊃ Mini-batch ⊃ SGD** in terms of dataset chunk size.

**Practical Recommendations (KTU Exam Tip):**

- Use **Mini-batch GD** with $b \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$ as default.
- Use **SGD** when data is a true infinite stream or when memory is severely constrained.
- Use **Batch GD** only when $m$ is small and high precision is required.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Batch Learning with Batch Normalization** — They are unrelated. Batch Learning is a training *paradigm*; Batch Normalization is a *regularization technique*.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{m}$ factor** in the cost function — Examiners specifically check whether students include the averaging factor.
> 3. **Misstating Robbins-Monro conditions** — Both conditions must be stated; partial credit lost if only one is written.
> 4. **Writing SGD as "Online Batch"** — In KTU terminology, Online Learning *uses* SGD, but they are not interchangeable terms.
> 5. **Skipping the learning rate decay schedule** — Online learning algorithms REQUIRE a decaying $\eta_t$, otherwise they oscillate forever.
> 6. **Confusing "iteration" with "epoch"** — An iteration is one update; an epoch is one full dataset pass.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Batch and Online Learning**

- **Training** = iterative minimization of empirical risk $J(\theta)$ to find $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_\theta J(\theta)$.
- **Batch Learning** uses the full dataset per update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\theta_t - y)$.
- **Online Learning (SGD)** uses one sample per update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i_t)}(\theta_t)$.
- **Mini-batch Learning** uses $b$ samples per update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}$.
- **Updates per epoch:** Batch = $1$, Mini-batch = $m/b$, SGD = $m$.
- **Memory cost per update:** Batch = $\mathcal{O}(md)$, Mini-batch = $\mathcal{O}(bd)$, SGD = $\mathcal{O}(d)$.
- **Variance of gradient estimate:** Batch = $0$, Mini-batch = $\sigma^2/b$, SGD = $\sigma^2$.
- **Robbins-Monro conditions:** $\sum \eta_t = \infty$ and $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$ guarantee SGD convergence.
- **Batch Learning** is preferred for small, stable datasets; **Online Learning** for streaming and concept-drifting environments.
- **Mini-batch** is the universal default in modern deep learning due to GPU parallelism.
- **One epoch ≠ One iteration** — remember the distinction for KTU numerical questions.
- SGD's noise is a **feature, not a bug** — it helps escape local minima in non-convex landscapes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges of Machine Learning: Data Issues - Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Challenges of Machine Learning: Data Issues - Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Quality in Machine Learning** refers to the degree to which a dataset satisfies the requirements of accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity necessary for training robust, generalizable, and unbiased predictive models. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, data quality is treated as a **prerequisite precondition** — *Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)* — meaning that the predictive performance ceiling of any supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning model is fundamentally bounded by the integrity of the input data.

Mathematically, if we denote the observed dataset as $\mathcal{D}_{obs}$ and the true underlying data-generating distribution as $\mathcal{D}_{true}$, then **data quality** can be quantified as the fidelity measure:

$$Q(\mathcal{D}) = 1 - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}_{obs}}\left[\,d\bigl(\mathcal{D}_{obs}(x),\ \mathcal{D}_{true}(x)\bigr)\right]$$

where $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is an appropriate divergence metric (e.g., KL-divergence, Wasserstein distance, or simple Euclidean distance for tabular data).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Chef"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking Depends on Ingredient Quality**
>
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a five-star meal. No matter how expensive your oven, how skilled your knife-work, or how perfect your recipe, if the vegetables are **rotten**, the **salt is contaminated**, the **rice has stones in it**, and the **measurement cups are mis-marked** — the dish will fail. Machine learning is identical. The model is the oven, the algorithm is the recipe, the chef is you — but the *ingredients* are the data. **Data Quality issues = Bad ingredients.**
>
> A master chef inspects, washes, sorts, and discards bad ingredients **before** cooking. Similarly, a machine learning engineer must perform **Data Quality Assurance (DQA)** before training.

## 1.3 The Six Pillars of Data Quality (ISO/IEC 25012 Standard)

| # | Pillar | Meaning in ML Context | Consequence of Violation |
|---|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | Values correctly represent the real-world entity | Model learns wrong mappings |
| 2 | **Completeness** | All required records and features are present | Biased estimates, missing patterns |
| 3 | **Consistency** | Same entity represented uniformly across sources | Conflicting gradients during training |
| 4 | **Timeliness** | Data is up-to-date for the prediction window | Concept drift, stale predictions |
| 5 | **Validity** | Values conform to domain constraints (e.g., age ≥ 0) | Ill-defined loss landscapes |
| 6 | **Uniqueness** | No unintended duplicate records | Inflated accuracy, overfitting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists *Data Quality* as a core challenge under Module 1, distinguishing it from *Data Quantity* (volume issues) and *Data Variety* (heterogeneity issues). Quality is about **internal integrity** of data values.

## 1.4 Why Data Quality Is a *Challenge* (Not Just a Step)

In KTU terminology, "challenges" are properties that make the ML pipeline *non-trivial*. Data quality qualifies because:

- It is **non-deterministic** — quality degradation can occur at collection, storage, transmission, or preprocessing stages.
- It is **domain-dependent** — medical data quality criteria differ from financial data quality criteria.
- It is **invisible in small samples** — bad data may go unnoticed until production deployment.
- It is **cascading** — one quality issue often triggers others (e.g., missing value → biased imputation → outliers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Quality Issues

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following primary data quality challenges:

### 2.1.1 Noise

**Definition:** Random perturbations in observed feature values or target labels, not representing the true underlying signal.

**Mathematical Model:** If $x_{true}$ is the true value and $\epsilon$ is stochastic noise, then the observed value is:

$$x_{obs} = x_{true} + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ \sigma^2)$$

**Common sources:** Sensor measurement error, transcription errors, transmission errors.

**Effect on ML:** Reduces the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), causing overfitting to spurious patterns.

**Quantification — Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_{signal}^{2}}{\sigma_{noise}^{2}}\right)\ \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **low SNR (< 10 dB)** indicates that noise dominates, and no algorithm can recover the true pattern reliably. This is a hard information-theoretic limit.

---

### 2.1.2 Outliers

**Definition:** Observations that deviate markedly from the bulk of the data, either due to genuine rare events (legitimate anomalies) or data corruption errors.

**Statistical Detection (Z-score Method):**

$$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

A point is flagged as an outlier if $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (under Gaussian assumption).

**Robust Alternative (IQR Method):**

$$\text{Lower Bound} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$
$$\text{Upper Bound} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$

where $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ is the interquartile range.

**Effect on ML:** Skews mean-based statistics, distorts regression coefficients, misleads distance-based algorithms (KNN, K-Means).

---

### 2.1.3 Missing Values

**Definition:** Absence of recorded values for one or more features in certain observations.

**Classification of Missingness (Rubin's Taxonomy):**

| Type | Notation | Meaning | Example |
|------|----------|---------|---------|
| Missing Completely At Random | **MCAR** | Missingness independent of any variable | Sensor randomly fails |
| Missing At Random | **MAR** | Missingness depends on *observed* variables | Older patients less likely to report income |
| Missing Not At Random | **MNAR** | Missingness depends on the *missing* value itself | High earners refuse to disclose income |

**Formal Definition:** Let $M$ be a missingness indicator matrix. Missingness is **MCAR** if:

$$P(M \mid X_{obs},\ X_{mis}) = P(M)$$

It is **MAR** if:

$$P(M \mid X_{obs},\ X_{mis}) = P(M \mid X_{obs})$$

**Effect on ML:** Most algorithms (Logistic Regression, Neural Networks, SVM) cannot natively handle NaN values; deletion or imputation is mandatory.

---

### 2.1.4 Inconsistent / Contradictory Data

**Definition:** Same entity represented differently across records, or records that violate logical/domain constraints.

**Examples:**
- A customer's date of birth recorded as both `1990-05-12` and `12/05/1990` (format inconsistency).
- A record with `age = 200` and `marital_status = "unmarried"`. 
- Duplicate customer records with different phone numbers.

**Detection — Constraint Violation Rate:**

$$R_{violation} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}\bigl[\,\text{record } i \text{ violates constraint } c\,\bigr]$$

**Effect on ML:** Violates the i.i.d. assumption, inflates variance, confuses tree-based splits.

---

### 2.1.5 Data Entry Errors & Typos

**Definition:** Human-introduced errors during manual data entry, including spelling mistakes, transposed digits, unit mismatches, and value range errors.

**Example:** Typing `1.75` as `175` (decimal point loss), entering temperature in Fahrenheit where Celsius is expected.

**Detection:** Use domain validation rules, regex patterns, value-range checks, and Benford's Law analysis.

---

### 2.1.6 Class Imbalance (Quality-Adjacent Issue)

**Definition:** Severe disparity in the number of samples across target classes.

**Quantification — Imbalance Ratio:**

$$IR = \frac{N_{majority}}{N_{minority}}$$

**Effect on ML:** Accuracy paradox — 99% accuracy on a 99:1 imbalance by predicting the majority class always.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus treats class imbalance as a *data quality* issue because it reflects an *unrepresentative sample* of the true data distribution.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — Data Quality Metrics**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Data Quality Index | $Q = 1 - \frac{N_{erroneous}}{N_{total}}$ | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_{signal}^{2} / \sigma_{noise}^{2})$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Z-Score | $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Threshold $\vert Z\vert > 3$ |
| IQR Bounds | $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$ | Tukey's Fence |
| Missingness Rate | $\rho_{miss} = \frac{1}{N \cdot d}\sum_{i,j}\mathbb{1}[x_{ij} = \text{NaN}]$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| Imputation (Mean) | $\hat{x}_{miss} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}x_i$ | Unbiased for MCAR |
| Imputation (Regression) | $\hat{x}_{miss} = \beta_0 + \sum_{j}\beta_j x_{j,obs}$ | Conditional expectation |
| Imputation Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ | Same units as feature |
| Imbalance Ratio | $IR = N_{maj} / N_{min}$ | $IR > 10$ is concerning |
| Constraint Violation Rate | $R_v = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}[\text{violate}(i)]$ | Dimensionless |
| Levenshtein Distance (typos) | $d_{lev}(s_1, s_2)$ | String-edit distance |
| Benford's Law | $P(d) = \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{d}\right)$ | Leading digit probability |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Data Quality Challenge | Impact |
|--------|----------------------|--------|
| **Medical Diagnosis (Healthcare ML)** | Missing patient test results, noisy sensor data from ICU monitors | Misdiagnosis, treatment errors |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Noisy LiDAR point clouds, mislabeled objects | Safety-critical failures |
| **Financial Credit Scoring** | Outliers from fraud, inconsistent address formats | Unfair credit denial |
| **Industrial IoT Predictive Maintenance** | Sensor drift, transmission errors in time-series | False alarms, missed failures |
| **NLP / Text Mining** | Typos, inconsistent capitalization, encoding errors | Reduced tokenizer accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Mean Imputation is Unbiased Under MCAR

**Claim:** If missingness is MCAR, replacing missing values with the sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the true population mean.

**Given:**
- Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ with $N$ samples.
- $N_{obs}$ observed values, $N_{miss} = N - N_{obs}$ missing values.
- The mean of the observed values is $\bar{x}_{obs} = \frac{1}{N_{obs}}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the imputation estimator of the population mean as:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\left(\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i + \sum_{j \in \text{miss}} \bar{x}_{obs}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the imputed sum. Since all $N_{miss}$ missing values are replaced by the same constant $\bar{x}_{obs}$:

$$\sum_{j \in \text{miss}} \bar{x}_{obs} = N_{miss} \cdot \bar{x}_{obs}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\left(\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i + N_{miss} \cdot \bar{x}_{obs}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Recall that $\bar{x}_{obs} = \frac{1}{N_{obs}}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i$, so $N_{miss} \cdot \bar{x}_{obs} = \frac{N_{miss}}{N_{obs}}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i$.

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\left(\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i + \frac{N_{miss}}{N_{obs}}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i\right)$$

**Step 5:** Factor the common sum:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i \left(1 + \frac{N_{miss}}{N_{obs}}\right) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i \left(\frac{N_{obs} + N_{miss}}{N_{obs}}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Since $N_{obs} + N_{miss} = N$:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i \cdot \frac{N}{N_{obs}} = \frac{1}{N_{obs}}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}} x_i = \bar{x}_{obs}$$

**Step 7:** Take the expectation over the missingness indicator. Under MCAR, the observed sample is a *random subset* of the full sample, so $\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}_{obs}] = \mu$ (the true population mean).

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mu}] = \mathbb{E}[\bar{x}_{obs}] = \mu$$

**Conclusion:** The estimator is **unbiased** under MCAR. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** Although mean imputation is unbiased for the *mean*, it **distorts the variance**. The standard deviation of the imputed dataset is systematically underestimated. This is a classic KTU board exam trap.

**Variance Distortion Derivation (Outline):**

$$\text{Var}_{imputed} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + \frac{N_{miss}}{N}(\bar{x} - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{obs}}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 < \text{Var}_{true}$$

The imputed values contribute **zero variance** because they all equal the mean, shrinking the apparent spread.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Z-Score Threshold Under Gaussian Assumption

**Claim:** Why the threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$ captures approximately 99.7% confidence under a normal distribution.

**Step 1:** For a standard normal variable $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, the cumulative distribution function is:

$$F(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2/2}\,dt$$

**Step 2:** The probability that $\vert Z \vert \leq 3$ is:

$$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 3) = F(3) - F(-3) = 2F(3) - 1$$

**Step 3:** Using the known value $F(3) \approx 0.99865$:

$$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 3) = 2(0.99865) - 1 = 0.9973$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the outlier probability is:

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027 = 0.27\%$$

**Conclusion:** Only **0.27% of data** is expected to lie beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ under Gaussian noise, making it a statistically justified outlier flag. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Data Quality Issue → Detection Method → Handling Strategy

| Quality Issue | Mathematical Symptom | Detection Method | Handling Strategy | KTU Exam Weight |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Noise** | Low SNR, residuals $\sim \mathcal{N}$ | Plot residuals, SNR computation | Smoothing (Savitzky-Golay, moving average), denoising autoencoders | High |
| **Outliers** | $\vert Z \vert > 3$, beyond IQR fence | Z-score, IQR, Isolation Forest, DBSCAN | Trimming, Winsorization, robust loss (Huber) | High |
| **Missing Values (MCAR)** | $\rho_{miss}$ uniform across features | Little's MCAR test | Mean/median imputation, listwise deletion | High |
| **Missing Values (MAR)** | Missingness correlated with observed features | Logistic regression of $M$ on $X_{obs}$ | Multiple imputation, EM algorithm, MICE | Medium |
| **Missing Values (MNAR)** | Missingness depends on missing value | Pattern mixture models, sensitivity analysis | Domain modeling, selection models | Low (Advanced) |
| **Inconsistent Records** | Constraint violations | Rule-based validators, schema enforcement | Standardization, deduplication | Medium |
| **Duplicates** | Inflated sample size | Hashing, similarity join | Deduplication using keys | Medium |
| **Class Imbalance** | $IR > 10$ | Class distribution plot | SMOTE, undersampling, class weighting | High |
| **Typos (Text)** | High Levenshtein distance | String distance, regex | Spell-check, fuzzy matching, normalization | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A feature column $X = [10, 12, 11, 13, 10, 200, 11, 12, 14, 11]$ is suspected to contain outliers. Using the IQR method, identify the outliers.

**Step 1:** Sort the data.

$$X_{sorted} = [10,\ 10,\ 11,\ 11,\ 11,\ 12,\ 12,\ 13,\ 14,\ 200]$$

**Step 2:** Compute quartiles. With $N = 10$:
- $Q_1$ = 25th percentile = position $2.75$ → interpolate between 2nd and 3rd values.

$$Q_1 = 10 + 0.75(11 - 10) = 10.75$$

- $Q_3$ = 75th percentile = position $7.25$ → interpolate between 7th and 8th values.

$$Q_3 = 12 + 0.25(13 - 12) = 12.25$$

- Median $Q_2 = 11.5$.

**Step 3:** Compute IQR.

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 12.25 - 10.75 = 1.50$$

**Step 4:** Compute Tukey's fences.

$$\text{Lower} = 10.75 - 1.5(1.50) = 10.75 - 2.25 = 8.50$$
$$\text{Upper} = 12.25 + 1.5(1.50) = 12.25 + 2.25 = 14.50$$

**Step 5:** Identify outliers — values outside $[8.50, 14.50]$.

$$200 > 14.50 \implies 200 \text{ is an outlier.}$$

**Conclusion:** The value $200$ is flagged as an outlier using the IQR method. $\blacksquare$
