The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a fundamental evaluation diagnostic tool in supervised Machine Learning used to assess the performance of **binary classification models**. Formally, it is a two-dimensional graphical plot in which the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — also called **Recall** or **Sensitivity** — is plotted on the **Y-axis**, and the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — equal to **1 − Specificity** — is plotted on the **X-axis**, as the discrimination threshold of the classifier is systematically varied across the range $[0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Model Evaluation):**
> The ROC curve answers the question: *"How well can my classifier separate the positive class from the negative class across ALL possible decision thresholds?"* — unlike accuracy, which is threshold-dependent and misleading on imbalanced data.

## 1.2 Historical Origin

The term *Receiver Operating Characteristic* originated during **World War II (1940s)** in the field of **signal detection theory** for analyzing radar images. Engineers needed a method to distinguish enemy objects (signal) from noise (false alarms). The methodology was later adopted in **psychophysics**, **medical diagnostics**, and eventually **Machine Learning** in the 1980s.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy

Imagine you are an airport security officer. You must decide who is a potential threat (Positive) and who is a normal traveler (Negative).

- **If you are very strict** (very low threshold): You catch every possible threat (high TPR), but you also flag many innocent travelers (high FPR).
- **If you are very lenient** (very high threshold): You let almost everyone through (low FPR), but you may miss some real threats (low TPR).
- **A perfect officer** would catch ALL threats while flagging NO innocent travelers — this corresponds to the point $(0, 1)$ on the ROC plot.

> [!NOTE]
> The ROC curve is essentially a **trace of all possible "officer strictness levels"** drawn on a single graph, allowing the decision-maker to pick the operating point best suited for the application (e.g., cancer screening prefers high TPR; spam filtering tolerates some false positives).

## 1.4 Why Accuracy Alone is Insufficient

| Scenario | Accuracy | Problem Hidden |
|---|---|---|
| 990 benign emails, 10 spam | 99% | A dumb classifier predicting "all non-spam" gets 99% accuracy but **misses all 10 spams** |
| Medical test on rare disease | 99.9% | A model predicting "all healthy" gets 99.9% accuracy but **fails at the actual diagnostic task** |

The ROC curve, by varying thresholds, exposes this hidden failure.

## 1.5 Core Metrics Recap

Let $TP, FP, TN, FN$ denote **True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, False Negatives** respectively.

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}, \quad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - FPR
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad \text{Recall} = TPR
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

### A. The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Every ROC curve begins from a **Confusion Matrix** — a 2×2 table that records classification outcomes on a labelled test set:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | $TP$ | $FN$ |
| **Actual Negative** | $FP$ | $TN$ |

### B. The Threshold Mechanism

Most classifiers (Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Networks, Naïve Bayes) do NOT directly output a class label — they output a **probability score** $p \in [0, 1]$. A **decision threshold** $\tau$ converts this score into a binary prediction:

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } p \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{if } p < \tau \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every value of $\tau$ produces a DIFFERENT confusion matrix, and therefore a DIFFERENT $(FPR, TPR)$ point on the ROC curve.**

### C. Building the ROC Curve — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Train the binary classifier on the training set.
2. Run the trained model on the test set to obtain a **probability score** for each instance.
3. Sort all test instances in **descending order** of predicted probability.
4. Begin at threshold $\tau = 1.0$ (classify nothing as positive) → point $(0, 0)$.
5. Gradually lower $\tau$, sweeping every unique score in the dataset.
6. At each step, recompute $TPR$ and $FPR$ using the running totals of $TP, FP, TN, FN$.
7. Plot each new $(FPR, TPR)$ point and connect them sequentially.

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation of Key Points

| Point $(FPR, TPR)$ | Meaning | Classifier Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $(0, 0)$ | $TPR = 0, FPR = 0$ | Predicts ALL as Negative (most cautious) |
| $(1, 1)$ | $TPR = 1, FPR = 1$ | Predicts ALL as Positive (most reckless) |
| $(0, 1)$ | $TPR = 1, FPR = 0$ | **Perfect Classifier** (no errors) |
| $(c, c)$ on the diagonal $y = x$ | $TPR = FPR$ | **Random Guess** classifier |
| Above the diagonal | $TPR > FPR$ | Classifier is better than random |
| Below the diagonal | $TPR < FPR$ | Classifier is WORSE than random (flip labels) |

## 2.3 The AUC (Area Under the Curve)

The single most important scalar summary of an ROC curve is the **Area Under the Curve (AUC)**.

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Probabilistic Interpretation of AUC:** The AUC represents the probability that the classifier will assign a **higher score to a randomly chosen positive instance** than to a randomly chosen negative instance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (TPR / Recall / Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual positives correctly identified |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives misclassified |
| **True Negative Rate (TNR / Specificity)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives correctly identified |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR / Miss Rate)** | $\dfrac{FN}{FN+TP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual positives missed |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Of predicted positives, how many are real |
| **Recall** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Same as TPR |
| **F1 Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall correctness |
| **AUC (Area Under ROC)** | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR)\,d(FPR)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Threshold-independent classifier quality |
| **Youden's J Statistic** | $TPR - FPR$ | $[0, 1]$ | Best threshold $\tau^{*} = \arg\max_{\tau}(TPR - FPR)$ |
| **Diagonal Line (Random)** | $TPR = FPR$ | — | $AUC = 0.5$ baseline |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Cancer detection — high TPR (sensitivity) is critical; missing a positive is costly.
- **Credit Card Fraud Detection:** Asymmetric costs — false negatives far costlier than false positives.
- **Spam Filtering:** False positives (legitimate email in spam) annoy users; tunable via threshold.
- **Information Retrieval & Search Engines:** Ranking quality assessment.
- **Biometric Authentication:** Face/fingerprint matching thresholds.
- **Anomaly Detection in Networks:** Intrusion detection systems.

## 2.6 Advantages & Limitations

**✅ Advantages:**
- **Threshold-independent** — evaluates the model across ALL operating points.
- **Robust to class imbalance** unlike accuracy.
- **Visually interpretable** trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.
- Scalar summarization via AUC enables easy model comparison.

**❌ Limitations:**
- Does NOT directly optimize for the **operating point** of interest.
- For highly skewed datasets, **Precision-Recall (PR) curves** are preferred.
- Assumes equal cost for $FP$ and $FN$, which is rarely true in practice.
- AUC may be overly optimistic on heavily imbalanced data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Building an ROC Curve from Scratch

### Problem Setup

A binary classifier outputs probability scores for 10 test instances. True labels and predicted scores are:

| Instance | True Label $y$ | Predicted Score $p$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 (Positive) | 0.95 |
| 2 | 1 (Positive) | 0.85 |
| 3 | 0 (Negative) | 0.80 |
| 4 | 1 (Positive) | 0.70 |
| 5 | 0 (Negative) | 0.55 |
| 6 | 1 (Positive) | 0.45 |
| 7 | 0 (Negative) | 0.40 |
| 8 | 0 (Negative) | 0.30 |
| 9 | 1 (Positive) | 0.20 |
| 10 | 0 (Negative) | 0.10 |

**Total:** $P = 5$ positives, $N = 5$ negatives.

### Step 1 — Sort by Descending Score

| Rank | Instance | $y$ | $p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 3 | 0 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | 0.70 |
| 5 | 5 | 0 | 0.55 |
| 6 | 6 | 1 | 0.45 |
| 7 | 7 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 8 | 8 | 0 | 0.30 |
| 9 | 9 | 1 | 0.20 |
| 10 | 10 | 0 | 0.10 |

### Step 2 — Initialize Counters

We maintain running totals $TP, FP, TN, FN$ as the threshold sweeps down:

$$
TP_0 = 0, \quad FP_0 = 0, \quad TN_0 = 5, \quad FN_0 = 5
$$

This corresponds to the starting point of the ROC curve at $\tau = 1.0$: **no instance is classified as positive yet**, hence $TPR = 0$ and $FPR = 0$.

### Step 3 — Sweep Threshold and Update Confusion Matrix

For each rank $i$, we lower $\tau$ just enough to include instance $i$ as predicted positive. The update rules are:

$$
\text{If } y_i = 1: \quad TP \leftarrow TP + 1, \quad FN \leftarrow FN - 1
$$

$$
\text{If } y_i = 0: \quad FP \leftarrow FP + 1, \quad TN \leftarrow TN - 1
$$

#### Row-by-Row Computation

| Threshold $\tau$ | $y$ | $TP$ | $FP$ | $TN$ | $FN$ | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau > 0.95$ | — | 0 | 0 | 5 | 5 | $\frac{0}{0+5} = 0.0$ | $\frac{0}{0+5} = 0.0$ |
| $0.85 \leq \tau < 0.95$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 5 | 4 | $\frac{0}{0+5} = 0.0$ | $\frac{1}{1+4} = 0.2$ |
| $0.80 \leq \tau < 0.85$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 4 | 4 | $\frac{1}{1+4} = 0.2$ | $\frac{1}{1+4} = 0.2$ |
| $0.70 \leq \tau < 0.80$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | 4 | 3 | $\frac{1}{1+4} = 0.2$ | $\frac{2}{2+3} = 0.4$ |
| $0.55 \leq \tau < 0.70$ | 0 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | $\frac{2}{2+3} = 0.4$ | $\frac{2}{2+3} = 0.4$ |
| $0.45 \leq \tau < 0.55$ | 1 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 | $\frac{2}{2+3} = 0.4$ | $\frac{3}{3+2} = 0.6$ |
| $0.40 \leq \tau < 0.45$ | 0 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | $\frac{3}{3+2} = 0.6$ | $\frac{3}{3+2} = 0.6$ |
| $0.30 \leq \tau < 0.40$ | 0 | 3 | 4 | 1 | 2 | $\frac{4}{4+1} = 0.8$ | $\frac{3}{3+2} = 0.6$ |
| $0.20 \leq \tau < 0.30$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | 1 | 1 | $\frac{4}{4+1} = 0.8$ | $\frac{4}{4+1} = 0.8$ |
| $0.10 \leq \tau < 0.20$ | 0 | 4 | 5 | 0 | 1 | $\frac{5}{5+0} = 1.0$ | $\frac{4}{4+1} = 0.8$ |
| $\tau < 0.10$ | 1 | 5 | 5 | 0 | 0 | $\frac{5}{5+0} = 1.0$ | $\frac{5}{5+0} = 1.0$ |

### Step 4 — List of ROC Points

$$
(0.0, 0.0), \; (0.0, 0.2), \; (0.2, 0.2), \; (0.2, 0.4), \; (0.4, 0.4), \; (0.4, 0.6), \; (0.6, 0.6), \; (0.8, 0.6), \; (0.8, 0.8), \; (1.0, 0.8), \; (1.0, 1.0)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the AUC Using the Trapezoidal Rule

Since the ROC curve is a step-function in the discrete case, the AUC is computed using the **trapezoidal rule** for numerical integration. Let the ordered ROC points be $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ where $x_i = FPR_i$ and $y_i = TPR_i$, sorted by increasing $x$.

$$
AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(y_i + y_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (x_{i+1} - x_i)
$$

### Step-by-Step Application to the Example Above

We compute the area of each trapezoid between consecutive points.

| Interval $i$ | $(x_i, y_i) \to (x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$ | $\Delta x$ | Avg. Height | Area |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0.0, 0.0) \to (0.0, 0.2)$ | $0.0$ | $0.1$ | $0.00$ |
| 2 | $(0.0, 0.2) \to (0.2, 0.2)$ | $0.2$ | $0.2$ | $0.04$ |
| 3 | $(0.2, 0.2) \to (0.2, 0.4)$ | $0.0$ | $0.3$ | $0.00$ |
| 4 | $(0.2, 0.4) \to (0.4, 0.4)$ | $0.2$ | $0.4$ | $0.08$ |
| 5 | $(0.4, 0.4) \to (0.4, 0.6)$ | $0.0$ | $0.5$ | $0.00$ |
| 6 | $(0.4, 0.6) \to (0.6, 0.6)$ | $0.2$ | $0.6$ | $0.12$ |
| 7 | $(0.6, 0.6) \to (0.8, 0.6)$ | $0.2$ | $0.6$ | $0.12$ |
| 8 | $(0.8, 0.6) \to (0.8, 0.8)$ | $0.0$ | $0.7$ | $0.00$ |
| 9 | $(0.8, 0.8) \to (1.0, 0.8)$ | $0.2$ | $0.8$ | $0.16$ |
| 10 | $(1.0, 0.8) \to (1.0, 1.0)$ | $0.0$ | $0.9$ | $0.00$ |

**Total AUC:**

$$
AUC = 0.00 + 0.04 + 0.00 + 0.08 + 0.00 + 0.12 + 0.12 + 0.00 + 0.16 + 0.00
$$

$$
\boxed{AUC = 0.52}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An AUC of $0.52$ is **only marginally better than the random baseline of $0.5$**, indicating that this particular classifier has weak discriminative power. An AUC of $1.0$ would denote a perfect classifier, while $0.5$ denotes random guessing.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Threshold — Youden's J Statistic

The optimal decision threshold $\tau^{*}$ is the one that **maximizes the distance from the random diagonal line**. This distance is captured by **Youden's J Statistic**:

$$
J(\tau) = TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau)
$$

The optimal threshold is:

$$
\tau^{*} = \arg\max_{\tau} J(\tau) = \arg\max_{\tau} \left[ TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau) \right]
$$

**Geometric Intuition:** The point on the ROC curve farthest from the diagonal $y = x$ (i.e., the upper-left corner) is the optimal operating point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of AUC Values

| AUC Value | Discriminative Ability | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| $AUC = 1.0$ | Perfect separation | Ideal — every positive ranked above every negative |
| $0.9 \leq AUC < 1.0$ | Excellent | Production-ready for most tasks |
| $0.8 \leq AUC < 0.9$ | Good | Strong model |
| $0.7 \leq AUC < 0.8$ | Fair | Acceptable for non-critical tasks |
| $0.6 \leq AUC < 0.7$ | Poor | Significant improvement needed |
| $0.5 \leq AUC < 0.6$ | Fail | Barely better than coin flip |
| $AUC = 0.5$ | Random | Equivalent to no skill |
| $AUC < 0.5$ | Worse than random | Label inversion likely |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** When two ROC curves cross, AUC alone is insufficient — you must specify the **operating region of interest** (e.g., low-FPR regime for medical screening) to determine the superior model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ROC Curve Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Binary Classifier] --> B[Predict Probability Scores on Test Set]
    B --> C[Sort Instances by Score Descending]
    C --> D[Initialize TP=0, FP=0, TN=P_total, FN=N_total]
    D --> E[Set Threshold tau to next unique score]
    E --> F{Is True Label y_i equal to 1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Increment TP, Decrement FN]
    F -- No --> H[Increment FP, Decrement TN]
    G --> I[Compute FPR and TPR]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Plot point FPR, TPR on ROC graph]
    J --> K{More instances to sweep?}
    K -- Yes --> E
    K -- No --> L[Connect all points sequentially]
    L --> M[Compute AUC via Trapezoidal Rule]
    M --> N[Final ROC Curve and AUC Value]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Threshold Sweep Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H1[High Threshold Region]
        H[tau near 1.0] --> H2[Predicts few positives]
        H2 --> H3[Low TPR, Low FPR]
    end
    subgraph H2a[Mid Threshold Region]
        M[tau near 0.5] --> M2[Balanced predictions]
        M2 --> M3[Medium TPR, Medium FPR]
    end
    subgraph H3a[Low Threshold Region]
        L[tau near 0.0] --> L2[Predicts many positives]
        L2 --> L3[High TPR, High FPR]
    end
    H3 --> M3
    M3 --> L3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — ROC Curve Interpretation Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ZONE1[Perfect Classifier Region]
        Z1[Top Left Corner at 0,1] --> Z1D[TP=All, FP=Zero]
    end
    subgraph ZONE2[Above Diagonal]
        Z2[Curve above y equals x] --> Z2D[TPR greater than FPR: Good Model]
    end
    subgraph ZONE3[Diagonal Line]
        Z3[y equals x reference] --> Z3D[Random Classifier, AUC=0.5]
    end
    subgraph ZONE4[Below Diagonal]
        Z4[Curve below y equals x] --> Z4D[Inverted labels needed, AUC less than 0.5]
    end
    ZONE1 --> ZONE2
    ZONE2 --> ZONE3
    ZONE3 --> ZONE4
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Trade-off Between TPR and FPR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lowering Threshold tau] --> B[More instances classified as Positive]
    B --> C{True Class}
    C -- Actual Positive --> D[TP increases, TPR increases]
    C -- Actual Negative --> E[FP increases, FPR increases]
    D --> F[Move Upwards on ROC]
    E --> G[Move Rightwards on ROC]
    F --> H[Trade-off Curve]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Select tau_star that Maximizes TPR minus FPR]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Model Comparison Using ROC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Trained Models M1 and M2] --> B[Generate ROC Curves for both]
    B --> C{Do the curves cross?}
    C -- No --> D[Model with Larger AUC wins]
    C -- Yes --> E[Identify Operating Region of Interest]
    E --> F{Is low FPR critical e.g. Medical?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compare curves in low FPR region]
    F -- No --> H[Compare curves in high TPR region]
    G --> I[Select model with better local performance]
    H --> I
    D --> J[Final Model Selection]
    I --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the ROC curve. Explain the significance of the Area Under the Curve (AUC) in evaluating a binary classifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a graphical plot that illustrates the performance of a binary classifier across all possible decision thresholds. It plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis.

**Significance of AUC (1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for significance):**

- AUC represents the **probability that the classifier ranks a randomly chosen positive instance higher than a randomly chosen negative instance**.
- An AUC of **1.0** indicates a **perfect classifier**, while **0.5** indicates performance equivalent to **random guessing**.
- AUC is **threshold-independent**, making it robust for comparing classifiers on imbalanced datasets where accuracy is misleading.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Precision, Recall, and F1-score. Why is the F1-score preferred over simple accuracy in imbalanced classification problems?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Metric | Formula | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Precision | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| Recall (TPR) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| F1-Score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic balance of P and R |

**Why F1 over Accuracy on imbalanced data (1.5 marks):**
- A model predicting only the majority class achieves **high accuracy but zero recall on the minority class**.
- F1-score **penalizes extreme imbalances** between Precision and Recall, providing a fairer assessment when class distributions are skewed.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Construct the ROC curve for the following test set predictions. The classifier outputs probability scores for 8 instances as shown below:**

| Instance | True Label | Predicted Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.92 |
| 2 | 0 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 1 | 0.74 |
| 4 | 1 | 0.65 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.50 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 7 | 1 | 0.25 |
| 8 | 0 | 0.10 |

**Total Positives** $P = 4$, **Total Negatives** $N = 4$. Compute TPR, FPR for every threshold, plot the ROC curve, and calculate the AUC.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order of score (already sorted):**

Ranks 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 with labels $1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0$ and scores $0.92, 0.85, 0.74, 0.65, 0.50, 0.40, 0.25, 0.10$.

**Step 2 — Initialize confusion matrix counters:**
$TP = 0, FP = 0, TN = 4, FN = 4$, giving starting point $(FPR, TPR) = (0/4, 0/4) = (0, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Sweep thresholds and update:**

| Step | $y_i$ | $TP$ | $FP$ | $TN$ | $FN$ | $FPR$ | $TPR$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | — | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 0.00 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 0.25 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 0.25 | 0.50 |
| 4 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 0.25 | 0.75 |
| 5 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 0.50 | 0.75 |
| 6 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0.75 | 0.75 |
| 7 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0.75 | 1.00 |
| 8 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 |

**Step 4 — Apply trapezoidal rule for AUC:**

$$
AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{7} \frac{(y_i + y_{i+1})}{2}(x_{i+1} - x_i)
$$

| Interval | $\Delta x$ | Avg. $y$ | Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(0.00, 0.00) \to (0.00, 0.25)$ | 0.00 | 0.125 | 0.000 |
| $(0.00, 0.25) \to (0.25, 0.25)$ | 0.25 | 0.250 | 0.0625 |
| $(0.25, 0.25) \to (0.25, 0.50)$ | 0.00 | 0.375 | 0.000 |
| $(0.25, 0.50) \to (0.25, 0.75)$ | 0.00 | 0.625 | 0.000 |
| $(0.25, 0.75) \to (0.50, 0.75)$ | 0.25 | 0.750 | 0.1875 |
| $(0.50, 0.75) \to (0.75, 0.75)$ | 0.25 | 0.750 | 0.1875 |
| $(0.75, 0.75) \to (0.75, 1.00)$ | 0.00 | 0.875 | 0.000 |
| $(0.75, 1.00) \to (1.00, 1.00)$ | 0.25 | 1.000 | 0.250 |

$$
AUC = 0.000 + 0.0625 + 0.000 + 0.000 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 + 0.000 + 0.250
$$

$$
\boxed{AUC = 0.6875}
$$

This indicates a **fair classifier** (per the AUC interpretation table in Section 3.4).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the relationship between Sensitivity, Specificity, and the ROC curve. How does the choice of decision threshold $\tau$ affect the operating point on the ROC curve? Discuss with reference to a real-world application such as disease diagnosis.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Specificity} = TNR = 1 - FPR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$

**Relationship with ROC curve (2.5 marks):**
- The ROC curve **traces Sensitivity (TPR) on the Y-axis** against $(1 - \text{Specificity})$ on the X-axis.
- Every point on the ROC curve represents a **pair (Sensitivity, 1 − Specificity)** achievable by a unique threshold $\tau$.
- The **upper-left corner** $(0, 1)$ corresponds to Sensitivity = 1, Specificity = 1 (perfect classification).
- The **diagonal** corresponds to Sensitivity = 1 − Specificity (random classification).

**Effect of threshold $\tau$ on operating point (2.5 marks):**
- **High $\tau$ (strict classifier):** Few positives predicted → high Specificity (low FPR) but low Sensitivity (many missed positives). Operating point moves **down-left** on the ROC curve.
- **Low $\tau$ (lenient classifier):** Many positives predicted → high Sensitivity (low FNR) but low Specificity (many false alarms). Operating point moves **up-right** on the ROC curve.
- **Optimal threshold $\tau^{*}$** is obtained by maximizing **Youden's J Statistic** $J = TPR - FPR$.

**Real-world application — Disease Diagnosis:**
- In **cancer screening**, a low threshold $\tau$ is preferred because **missing a cancer case (False Negative) is far more dangerous** than a False Positive (which can be ruled out by further confirmatory tests).
- Conversely, in **confirmatory diagnostic tests**, a high threshold is used to **avoid unnecessary biopsies** caused by False Positives.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the limitations of accuracy as a classification metric. With a suitable numerical example, demonstrate how the ROC curve and AUC overcome these limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Accuracy (3.5 marks):**
1. **Threshold-dependent:** A single accuracy value corresponds to one threshold $\tau$, missing the full trade-off picture.
2. **Misleading on imbalanced data:** A model predicting the majority class always can achieve high accuracy.
3. **Equal cost assumption:** Treats $FP$ and $FN$ equally, which is rarely realistic.
4. **No probabilistic insight:** Does not reveal how confidently the classifier ranks instances.

**Numerical Example (3.5 marks):**

Consider a fraud detection dataset with **1000 transactions**: 990 legitimate, 10 fraudulent.

| Model | Prediction Strategy | Accuracy | Issues |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ (Naïve) | Predicts "legitimate" for all 1000 | $\frac{990}{1000} = 99\%$ | Misses ALL 10 frauds |
| $M_2$ (Smart) | Catches 9 frauds, 5 false alarms | $\frac{985}{1000} = 98.5\%$ | Lower accuracy but catches fraud |

Although $M_1$ has higher accuracy, $M_2$ is operationally superior. The ROC curve of $M_2$ would show a high TPR for a small increase in FPR, yielding a **much higher AUC** than $M_1$'s flat diagonal. This demonstrates how AUC captures classifier quality that accuracy hides.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the formula for AUC as the probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative instance. Explain why this probabilistic interpretation is significant.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $S^{+}$ be the predicted score of a randomly chosen positive instance and $S^{-}$ be the predicted score of a randomly chosen negative instance. Define:

$$
AUC = P(S^{+} > S^{-})
$$

For continuous score distributions $f^{+}$ and $f^{-}$, the AUC integral can be rewritten as:

$$
AUC = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I(s^{+} > s^{-}) \, f^{+}(s^{+}) \, f^{-}(s^{-}) \, ds^{+} \, ds^{-}
$$

Using the identity $I(s^{+} > s^{-}) = \mathbb{1}_{[0,1]}(F^{+}(s^{+}))$ and substitution, this reduces to the geometric area form:

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)
$$

Alternatively, the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney U statistic** provides a discrete empirical estimate:

$$
AUC = \frac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{P} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(s_i^{+} > s_j^{-})
$$

where $P$ and $N$ are the counts of positives and negatives in the test set.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- This interpretation **decouples AUC from any specific threshold**, evaluating the classifier's **ranking ability** across the entire score spectrum.
- It enables **fair model comparison** regardless of class balance.
- It is closely related to the **Wilcoxon rank-sum test**, giving AUC a firm statistical foundation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing ROC with Precision-Recall (PR) curve:** PR is preferred for highly imbalanced data — examiners often test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to sort by descending score** before sweeping thresholds — leading to incorrect ROC points.
> 3. **Plotting Accuracy vs Threshold** instead of TPR vs FPR — fundamental conceptual error.
> 4. **Stating "AUC = 1 means perfect model"** without explaining it means *perfect ranking*, not zero errors at every threshold simultaneously (it implies zero errors across all thresholds simultaneously in the limit, but you must clarify the geometric meaning).
> 5. **Misinterpreting points below the diagonal:** These represent models WORSE than random, which can be rescued by label flipping.
> 6. **Using accuracy as the sole metric** in the presence of class imbalance — guaranteed mark deduction in Module 1 questions.
> 7. **Omitting units or the trapezoidal formula** in AUC numerical computations.

---

## 🔖 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROC = TPR vs FPR** plot, threshold-independent.
- **TPR** = Recall = Sensitivity = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **FPR** = $1 - \text{Specificity}$ = $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
- **AUC** ranges from **0 to 1**; 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect.
- **Perfect classifier** touches the point $(0, 1)$.
- **Youden's J** = $TPR - FPR$ → maximum gives optimal threshold $\tau^{*}$.
- **AUC = probability** that a positive is ranked above a negative (Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney equivalence).
- **Trapezoidal rule** computes AUC numerically:

$$
AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(y_i + y_{i+1})}{2}(x_{i+1} - x_i)
$$

- **ROC vs PR curve:** ROC is robust to class imbalance; PR is sharper for highly skewed data.
- **Curves crossing:** AUC alone is insufficient — specify the operating region.
- **Limitations:** Equal cost assumption, no operating-point optimization, less useful for multi-class (use one-vs-rest).
- **Real-world use cases:** Medical diagnosis, fraud detection, spam filtering, biometrics, intrusion detection.
- **Always sort predictions by descending score** before plotting ROC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression: Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression: Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Regression** is a fundamental **supervised machine learning** algorithm that establishes a **linear relationship** between a scalar dependent (response) variable and one or more explanatory (independent) variables. The model assumes that the conditional expectation of the response variable is an affine function of the input features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> Linear Regression is a parametric regression technique that minimizes the sum of squared residuals between observed and predicted values to learn the best-fitting hyperplane through the data points in the feature space.

### 1.1.1 Types of Linear Regression

1. **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)**: Involves exactly **one** independent variable.
2. **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)**: Involves **two or more** independent variables.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Best-Fit Line Through a Scatter Plot**
>
> Imagine a class teacher plotting students' **daily study hours (X-axis)** against their **exam scores (Y-axis)** on a graph. The data points will be scattered (not in a perfectly straight line). Linear regression draws **one single straight line** through this scatter such that the **total vertical distance** from each point to the line is **as small as possible**. This line is the "best fit" and can now be used to **predict** the exam score of a *new* student given their study hours.
>
> - The **slope (β₁)** tells us: *"For every extra hour of study, the score increases by β₁ marks."*
> - The **intercept (β₀)** tells us: *"A student who studies zero hours is expected to score β₀ marks (the baseline)."*

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Regression**: A supervised learning task where the output variable is **continuous** (e.g., price, temperature, salary).
- **Hypothesis Function (h(x))**: The linear equation used to make predictions.
- **Parameters (β)**: The learnable coefficients (weights) of the model.
- **Residual (ε)**: The difference between the actual value and the predicted value: ε = y − h(x).
- **Cost Function (J(β))**: A measure of how wrong the model's predictions are.
- **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**: The classical method used to estimate β by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model

### 2.1.1 Simple Linear Regression Model

The hypothesis function is expressed as:

$$h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} x$$

Where:
- **β₀** = the **intercept** (value of y when x = 0)
- **β₁** = the **slope** (rate of change of y per unit change in x)
- **x** = independent (input) feature
- **h_β(x)** = predicted (output) value

### 2.1.2 Multiple Linear Regression Model

When there are *n* independent variables, the model generalizes to:

$$h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} x_{1} + \beta_{2} x_{2} + \ldots + \beta_{n} x_{n} = \beta^{T} X$$

Where **X** is the feature vector and **β** is the parameter vector.

## 2.2 The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)

The quality of the fit is quantified by the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function. The objective of linear regression is to find the parameters β that **minimize J(β)**.

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why squared error and not absolute error?**
> 1. Squaring **penalizes large errors more heavily** (convex and differentiable).
> 2. The squared error function is **convex**, guaranteeing a **single global minimum**.
> 3. It has elegant **closed-form solutions** (Normal Equation).

## 2.3 Parameter Estimation Methods

There are two principal methods to minimize J(β):

| Method | Description | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Equation (Closed-Form)** | Direct matrix inversion gives the optimal β in one step. | Small datasets (n < 10⁴ features). |
| **Gradient Descent (Iterative)** | Repeatedly updates β by moving in the direction of the steepest descent of J(β). | Large datasets with many features. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of the numerical and theoretical questions in KTU exams.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hypothesis (SLR)** | $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1} x$ | Linear model in one variable. |
| **Hypothesis (Vectorized)** | $h_{\beta}(X) = X \beta$ | Matrix form, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$. |
| **Cost Function (MSE)** | $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$ | To be minimized. |
| **Normal Equation** | $\beta = \left( X^{T} X \right)^{-1} X^{T} y$ | Closed-form OLS solution. |
| **Slope (SLR, 1-D)** | $\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}$ | OLS estimate of slope. |
| **Intercept (SLR, 1-D)** | $\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1} \bar{x}$ | OLS estimate of intercept. |
| **Gradient Descent Update** | $\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_{j}} J(\beta)$ | Iterative learning rule. |
| **Gradient (Single Step)** | $\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}$ | Simultaneous update for all j. |
| **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^{2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | Fraction of variance explained (0 to 1). |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $MSE = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^{2}$ | Average squared prediction error. |
| **Root Mean Squared Error** | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Error in original units of y. |
| **Mean Absolute Error** | $MAE = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Average absolute deviation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipes and absolute values**: In LaTeX inside tables, never write `|x|` — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` to prevent breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 The Five Classical Assumptions of Linear Regression

For the OLS estimators to be **unbiased, efficient, and consistent**, the following assumptions must hold:

1. **Linearity**: The relationship between X and y is linear.
2. **Independence**: Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity**: Constant variance of residuals across all levels of X.
4. **Normality**: Residuals are normally distributed (important for inference).
5. **No Multicollinearity**: Independent variables are not highly correlated (for MLR).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil Engineering**: Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement, water, and aggregate ratios.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Modeling power consumption based on temperature and load.
- **Economics**: Forecasting GDP, stock prices, and demand.
- **Biomedical**: Predicting disease progression from clinical measurements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Normal Equation (Closed-Form OLS)

We want to find the value of **β** that minimizes the cost function J(β). Starting from the vectorized cost function:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X \beta - y)^{T} (X \beta - y)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the matrix product using the transpose property $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \beta^{T} X^{T} X \beta - \beta^{T} X^{T} y - y^{T} X \beta + y^{T} y \right)$$

**Step 2:** Note that $\beta^T X^T y$ is a scalar, hence equal to its transpose $y^T X \beta$. Combine the two middle terms:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \beta^{T} X^{T} X \beta - 2 \beta^{T} X^{T} y + y^{T} y \right)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate J(β) with respect to the vector **β** and set the gradient to **zero** (first-order optimality condition). Using standard matrix calculus identities $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T A \beta) = 2A\beta$ (for symmetric A) and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T b) = b$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{2m} \left( 2 X^{T} X \beta - 2 X^{T} y \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Multiply through by $2m$ and isolate $\beta$:

$$X^{T} X \beta - X^{T} y = 0$$

$$X^{T} X \beta = X^{T} y$$

**Step 5:** Assuming $X^T X$ is invertible (i.e., features are linearly independent), multiply both sides by its inverse:

$$\boxed{\beta = \left( X^{T} X \right)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

This is the **Normal Equation** — the closed-form OLS solution.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule

The cost function gradient for a single parameter $\beta_j$ is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_{j}} J(\beta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}$$

**Step 1:** Start with the initial values of $\beta$ (often $\beta = 0$).

**Step 2:** Simultaneously update all $\beta_j$ for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, n$ until convergence:

$$\beta_{j} := \beta_{j} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}$$

Where **α** is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter):

- If **α is too small** → slow convergence.
- If **α is too large** → may overshoot the minimum or diverge.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem:** Find the regression line for the data points (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4). Use the OLS formulas.

**Given Data:** m = 4, x = [1, 2, 3, 4], y = [2, 3, 5, 4].

**Step 1:** Compute the means.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4}{4} = 2.5$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2 + 3 + 5 + 4}{4} = 3.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$.

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | Product |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 | −1.5 | −1.5 | 2.25 |
| 2 | 3 | −0.5 | −0.5 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 5 | 0.5 | 1.5 | 0.75 |
| 4 | 4 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 0.75 |
| **Sum** | | | | **4.00** |

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$.

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (-1.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2 = 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.00$$

**Step 4:** Compute the slope $\beta_1$.

$$\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{4.00}{5.00} = 0.8$$

**Step 5:** Compute the intercept $\beta_0$.

$$\beta_{0} = \bar{y} - \beta_{1} \bar{x} = 3.5 - (0.8)(2.5) = 3.5 - 2.0 = 1.5$$

**Step 6:** Final regression equation.

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 1.5 + 0.8 x}$$

**Step 7 (Verification):** Predict the values and check residuals.

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i = 1.5 + 0.8 x_i$ | Residual $y_i - \hat{y}_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 | 2.3 | −0.3 |
| 2 | 3 | 3.1 | −0.1 |
| 3 | 5 | 3.9 | 1.1 |
| 4 | 4 | 4.7 | −0.7 |

The sum of residuals is approximately 0 (a property of OLS).

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | Computational Complexity | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Equation** | $O(n^{3})$ for inverting $X^T X$ | Exact, no α tuning. | Slow for large n. |
| **Batch Gradient Descent** | $O(mn)$ per iteration | Works for large m and n. | Needs α; can be slow. |
| **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** | $O(n)$ per update | Fast for huge m. | Noisy convergence. |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $O(Bn)$ where B is batch size | Best of both worlds. | Extra hyperparameter. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Regression — End-to-End Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete operational flow of a linear regression model — from raw data ingestion to model deployment and evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset X and y] --> B[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    B --> C[Handle Missing Values and Outliers]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering and Scaling]
    D --> E[Train Test Split 80 20]
    E --> F[Initialize Parameters beta0 and beta1]
    F --> G{Choose Optimization Method}
    G -->|Closed Form| H[Compute Normal Equation beta equals XtXinv Xty]
    G -->|Iterative| I[Run Gradient Descent Loop]
    H --> J[Predicted Values hat y]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Cost J beta]
    K --> L{Cost Converged}
    L -->|No| M[Update beta]
    M --> G
    L -->|Yes| N[Evaluate on Test Set]
    N --> O[Compute MSE RMSE MAE R2]
    O --> P[Model Ready for Deployment]
    P --> Q[Predict y_new from x_new]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow begins at node A with raw inputs and proceeds sequentially through preprocessing, training, optimization, and evaluation. The diamond-shaped decision nodes (G, L) introduce branching — the model either solves analytically (Normal Equation) or iteratively (Gradient Descent). The feedback loop L → M → G represents the iterative nature of gradient descent.

## 4.2 Gradient Descent Convergence Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start at Random Point on J beta Surface] --> B[Compute Gradient at Current Point]
    B --> C[Move Opposite to Gradient with Step alpha]
    C --> D{Is Change in J beta less than epsilon}
    D -->|No| B
    D -->|Yes| E[Reached Minimum Converged]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This schematic visualizes how gradient descent "rolls downhill" on the convex cost surface. The learning rate **α** controls the size of each step. The algorithm stops when the cost function change between iterations is below a small threshold **ε** (convergence tolerance).

## 4.3 Hypothesis Space Visualization (1-D and 2-D)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Regression Hypothesis Space] --> B[1 Feature: Best Fit Line in 2D]
    A --> C[2 Features: Best Fit Plane in 3D]
    A --> D[n Features: Best Fit Hyperplane in n+1 D]
    B --> E[Equation: y equals beta0 plus beta1 x]
    C --> F[Equation: y equals beta0 plus beta1 x1 plus beta2 x2]
    D --> G[Equation: y equals beta transpose X]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even though we call it "linear" regression, the model is linear in its **parameters (β)**, not necessarily in the input features. We can apply non-linear transformations (e.g., $x^2$, $\sqrt{x}$, $\log x$) to the inputs and the model remains a "linear regression" — this is the basis of **polynomial regression**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define **Linear Regression**. Explain the **cost function** used in linear regression with its mathematical expression.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Linear regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm used to model the **linear relationship** between a dependent variable *y* and one or more independent variables *X* by fitting a straight line (or hyperplane) that best describes the data.

The hypothesis function is $h_\beta(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$. The performance is measured using the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2}$$

The objective of training is to find the values of β that **minimize J(β)**, thereby producing the line that is closest, on average, to all training points.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between **Simple Linear Regression** and **Multiple Linear Regression**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Features** | Exactly **one** independent variable. | **Two or more** independent variables. |
| **Equation** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \beta_n x_n + \varepsilon$ |
| **Geometry** | Best-fit **straight line** in 2-D. | Best-fit **hyperplane** in (n+1)-D. |
| **Example** | Predicting sales from advertising spend. | Predicting house price from area, bedrooms, location. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B Module 2 questions typically carry 14 marks each, with internal choice between two full-length problems. Each problem contains sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The model answers below are formatted to the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation key.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method for estimating the parameters of a linear regression model. Derive the **Normal Equation** in matrix form. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Given the training data: $x = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]$ and $y = [2, 3, 4, 5, 6]$, compute the OLS estimates $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$. Also predict the value of y when $x = 10$. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) OLS Method & Normal Equation Derivation (7 Marks):**

The OLS method estimates the regression parameters β by minimizing the **sum of squared residuals** (differences between observed and predicted values).

Starting from the vectorized cost function:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\beta - y)^T (X\beta - y)$$

Expanding and using matrix calculus identity $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T A \beta) = 2A\beta$ for symmetric $A = X^T X$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{m} \left( X^T X \beta - X^T y \right) = 0$$

Solving for β (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible):

$$\boxed{\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

This is the **Normal Equation** — it gives the exact OLS estimate in closed form.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the means. $\bar{x} = 2$, $\bar{y} = 4$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ and $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$.

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (-2)(-2) + (-1)(-1) + (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (2)(2) = 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 3:** Compute slope and intercept.

$$\beta_1 = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 4 - (1.0)(2) = 2.0$$

**Step 4:** The regression equation is $\hat{y} = 2.0 + 1.0 x$.

**Step 5:** Predict for $x = 10$:

$$\hat{y} = 2.0 + 1.0 (10) = 12.0$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gradient Descent algorithm** for linear regression. Discuss the effect of the **learning rate α** on convergence. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the same data as above, perform **two iterations of batch gradient descent** starting from $\beta_0 = 0$, $\beta_1 = 0$, with learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Gradient Descent for Linear Regression (7 Marks):**

Gradient descent is an **iterative optimization** algorithm that updates parameters in the direction of the **steepest descent** of the cost function $J(\beta)$.

The update rule is:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_j}$$

For linear regression, the partial derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Effect of learning rate α:**
- **Small α**: Slow convergence, many iterations needed.
- **Optimal α**: Fast and stable convergence.
- **Large α**: May overshoot the minimum, oscillate, or diverge entirely.

**Algorithm outline:**
1. Initialize β (often to zero).
2. Compute the gradient of J(β).
3. Update β simultaneously for all j.
4. Repeat until $J(\beta)$ converges (change < ε).

**(b) Two Iterations of Gradient Descent (7 Marks):**

Given $x = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]$, $y = [2, 3, 4, 5, 6]$, $m = 5$, $\alpha = 0.01$, $\beta_0 = \beta_1 = 0$.

**Iteration 1:**
Predictions: $h(x) = 0$ for all points. Errors: $(h - y) = [-2, -3, -4, -5, -6]$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{5} \left( (-2)(0) + (-3)(1) + (-4)(2) + (-5)(3) + (-6)(4) \right) = \frac{-50}{5} = -10$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{5} (-2 - 3 - 4 - 5 - 6) = \frac{-20}{5} = -4$$

**Updates:**
$\beta_1 := 0 - 0.01 \times (-10) = 0.10$
$\beta_0 := 0 - 0.01 \times (-4) = 0.04$

**Iteration 2:**
With $\beta_0 = 0.04, \beta_1 = 0.10$, predictions: $h(x) = [0.04, 0.14, 0.24, 0.34, 0.44]$. Errors $(h - y) = [-1.96, -2.86, -3.76, -4.66, -5.56]$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{5} \left( (-1.96)(0) + (-2.86)(1) + (-3.76)(2) + (-4.66)(3) + (-5.56)(4) \right) = \frac{-49.6}{5} = -9.92$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{5} (-1.96 - 2.86 - 3.76 - 4.66 - 5.56) = \frac{-18.8}{5} = -3.76$$

**Updates:**
$\beta_1 := 0.10 - 0.01 \times (-9.92) = 0.1992$
$\beta_0 := 0.04 - 0.01 \times (-3.76) = 0.0776$

After 2 iterations: $\beta_0 \approx 0.078$, $\beta_1 \approx 0.199$ (slowly converging to the true OLS values 2.0 and 1.0).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: In gradient descent, students forget to **update β₀ and β₁ simultaneously**. Always compute both partial derivatives using the *old* values, then update.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Using **unsquared error** in MSE formulas (loses 1 mark). Always include the square.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: Confusing **R²** with MSE — R² is unitless (between 0 and 1), while MSE is in squared units of y.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting the **½** in the cost function (forgot factor — check whether the question uses MSE or the OLS form $J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (\cdot)^2$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [x] **Definition**: Linear regression models a **linear relationship** between continuous y and feature(s) X.
- [x] **Equation (SLR)**: $h(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$. (Equation MLR): $h(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \beta_n x_n$.
- [x] **Cost Function**: $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ — always non-negative, convex.
- [x] **Normal Equation (Closed-Form)**: $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ — exact, one-step, slow for large n.
- [x] **Gradient Descent Update**: $\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum (h - y) \cdot x_j$ — iterative, scales well.
- [x] **OLS Slope Formula**: $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ and intercept: $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$.
- [x] **Evaluation Metrics**: MSE, RMSE, MAE, and **R²** ($0 \le R^2 \le 1$, higher is better).
- [x] **Assumptions**: Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
- [x] **Learning Rate α**: Too small = slow; too large = divergence.
- [x] **Simultaneous Update**: In GD, all β values must be updated at the same time using old values — do not mix old and new.
- [x] **Linear in Parameters**: Linear regression is linear in β, not necessarily in X — feature transforms are allowed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression, Error Functions in Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression, Error Functions in Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Logistic Regression — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistic Regression** is a **supervised learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability that a given input instance belongs to a particular category (class). Despite its name "regression," it is a **classification algorithm** that models the probability of the default class using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**.

The output of logistic regression is a **probability value $h_\theta(x) \in [0, 1]$**, which is then thresholded (typically at **0.5**) to assign a discrete class label.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logistic Regression falls under the parametric classification family. It is a **discriminative classifier** that directly models $P(y \mid x)$ rather than joint probability $P(x, y)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sigmoid Squeeze"

Imagine you are a **medical triage officer** at a hospital entrance. For every arriving patient, you must decide: *Admit to ICU (Class 1) or send to General Ward (Class 0)*. You cannot simply say "yes" or "no" — you must estimate a **risk score** between 0% and 100%.

- A **linear regression** would give you raw scores like $-12$ or $+47$, which are meaningless as probabilities.
- A **logistic regression** *squeezes* any real number (from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$) into a clean probability interval $[0, 1]$ using a smooth "S-shaped" curve called the **sigmoid function**.

The decision rule is simple:
- If the patient's predicted probability $\geq 0.5$ → **Admit (Class 1)**
- If $< 0.5$ → **General Ward (Class 0)**

## 1.3 Error Functions in Regression — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error Functions (Loss Functions)** in regression quantify the **discrepancy between the predicted value $\hat{y}$ and the actual (true) value $y$**. The goal of training any regression model is to **minimize** this error function using optimization techniques such as **Gradient Descent**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Archer's Arrow:**
Imagine shooting arrows at a target. The **error** is the distance from each arrow to the bullseye. The **regression model** is the archer trying to minimize the average distance of all arrows to the center. Different error functions penalize errors differently:
- **Squared Error (MSE):** Punishes far-off arrows *heavily* (like a strict teacher).
- **Absolute Error (MAE):** Treats all errors equally in magnitude (like a lenient teacher).
- **Huber Loss:** A balanced hybrid (like a fair teacher).

## 1.4 Why These Topics Matter in KTU Examinations

| Concept | Why It's Exam-Critical |
|---|---|
| Sigmoid Function | Core formula, frequently asked 3-mark question |
| Cost Function (Log Loss) | Derivation is a guaranteed 14-mark question |
| Gradient Descent Update Rule | Appears in nearly every module |
| MSE / RMSE / MAE | Direct definitions tested in Part A |
| $R^2$ and Adjusted $R^2$ | Common in regression evaluation questions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z)$ maps any real input $z$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

### Key Properties:
- **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ — never reaches exactly 0 or 1
- **Domain:** $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$
- **Midpoint:** $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
- **Monotonically Increasing:** Higher $z$ → higher probability
- **Differentiable:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — a beautifully simple derivative

> [!NOTE]
> This neat derivative property is precisely why the sigmoid is mathematically preferred for gradient-based optimization.

## 2.2 Logistic Regression Hypothesis

For an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$:

$$z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n$$

$$h_\theta(x) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

> **Interpretation:** $h_\theta(x) = P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta)$ — the probability that the class label is 1 given the input and parameters.

## 2.3 Decision Boundary

The **decision boundary** is the line/surface where $h_\theta(x) = 0.5$, i.e., where $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x} = 0$:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } h_\theta(x) < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

This can be **linear** ($\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x} = 0$) or **non-linear** (e.g., polynomial: $\theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^2 + \theta_2 x_2^2 = 0$).

## 2.4 Cost Function — Why NOT MSE?

For logistic regression, using Mean Squared Error leads to a **non-convex** cost function riddled with local minima. Hence, we use the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)**:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

### Intuition Behind the Formula:
- If $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) \approx 1$ → cost $\approx 0$ (good prediction)
- If $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) \approx 0$ → cost $\to \infty$ (severe penalty)
- If $y = 0$ and $h_\theta(x) \approx 0$ → cost $\approx 0$ (good prediction)
- If $y = 0$ and $h_\theta(x) \approx 1$ → cost $\to \infty$ (severe penalty)

## 2.5 Gradient Descent Update Rule

To minimize $J(\theta)$, we update parameters in the direction of steepest descent:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

After taking the derivative (shown in Section 3), the update simplifies elegantly to:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **identical in form** to linear regression's update — but the hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ differs (sigmoid vs. linear). This is a common KTU question!

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Sigmoid (Logistic) function |
| 2 | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Derivative of sigmoid |
| 3 | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ | Hypothesis representation |
| 4 | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h)\right]$ | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss |
| 5 | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Gradient descent update |
| 6 | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 7 | $MAE = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\vert$ | Mean Absolute Error |
| 8 | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Root Mean Squared Error |
| 9 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum(y - \hat{y})^2}{\sum(y - \bar{y})^2}$ | Coefficient of Determination |
| 10 | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(m-1)}{m-n-1}$ | Adjusted R-squared |
| 11 | $\log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h_\theta(x^{(i)}))\right]$ | Log-Likelihood |
| 12 | $z = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Logit (inverse of sigmoid) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting disease presence/absence (diabetes, cancer).
- **Spam Detection:** Classifying email as spam ($y=1$) or ham ($y=0$).
- **Credit Scoring:** Banks predict loan default probability.
- **Quality Control:** Manufacturing defect detection in Industry 4.0.
- **NLP:** Sentiment analysis (positive vs. negative review).
- **Error Metrics in Engineering:** MSE is fundamental in signal processing, control systems, and time-series forecasting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function's Derivative

Starting from the definition:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$$

Taking the derivative using the chain rule:

$$\sigma'(z) = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z})$$

Simplifying:

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

Now, we algebraically manipulate this to a more elegant form. Multiply and divide by $(1 + e^{-z})$:

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} \cdot \frac{(1 + e^{-z})}{(1 + e^{-z})} = \frac{e^{-z} + e^{-2z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} + \frac{e^{-z} - e^{-2z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

$$= \frac{e^{-z}(1 + e^{-z})}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} + \frac{e^{-z} - e^{-2z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

$$= \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} + \frac{e^{-z}(1 - e^{-z})}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ and noting that $1 - \sigma(z) = \dfrac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cross-Entropy Cost Function

### Step 1: Express the model probabilistically.

For a single training example, the probability of the class label $y$ given $x$ and $\theta$ is:

$$P(y \mid x; \theta) = h_\theta(x)^y \cdot (1 - h_\theta(x))^{1-y}$$

### Step 2: Write the Likelihood over $m$ independent samples.

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h_\theta(x^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

### Step 3: Take the natural log to get Log-Likelihood.

Taking the logarithm converts products to sums, which is computationally easier and monotone (preserves optimum):

$$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

### Step 4: Convert to a minimization problem (negate the log-likelihood).

Since we want to *maximize* the likelihood, we *minimize* the negative log-likelihood:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

The factor $\dfrac{1}{m}$ averages the cost across the dataset and is conventional.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We need $\dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$. Starting from:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]$$

where $h^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ and $z^{(i)} = \theta^T x^{(i)}$.

### Step 1: Apply the chain rule.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{1}{h^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} - (1 - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - h^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} \right]$$

### Step 2: Substitute the derivative of the sigmoid.

Using $\dfrac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = h^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$ (from chain rule: $\sigma'(z) \cdot \partial z / \partial \theta_j$):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \cdot (1 - h^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)}) \cdot h^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the bracket term.

$$y^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)}) h^{(i)} = y^{(i)} - y^{(i)} h^{(i)} - h^{(i)} + y^{(i)} h^{(i)} = y^{(i)} - h^{(i)}$$

### Step 4: Final gradient expression.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 5: Gradient descent update.

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination)

$R^2$ measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the model.

**Total Sum of Squares (variance of data):**

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2$$

**Residual Sum of Squares (unexplained variance):**

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

**Definition:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2}$$

- $R^2 = 1$ → perfect prediction
- $R^2 = 0$ → model is no better than predicting the mean
- $R^2 < 0$ → model is worse than the mean (terrible fit)

## 3.5 Derivation of Adjusted $R^2$

$R^2$ has a flaw: it always increases (or stays the same) when you add more features, even if those features are useless. **Adjusted $R^2$** penalizes for adding irrelevant features:

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(m - 1)}{m - n - 1}$$

where $m$ is the number of samples and $n$ is the number of features. The denominator $m - n - 1$ represents the **degrees of freedom** — it shrinks as $n$ grows, thus penalizing extra features.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **MSE** | **MAE** | **RMSE** | **Log Loss** | **Huber Loss** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(y - \hat{y})^2$ | $\frac{1}{m}\sum\vert y - \hat{y}\vert$ | $\sqrt{MSE}$ | $-\frac{1}{m}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Quadratic if small, Linear if large |
| **Sensitivity to Outliers** | Very High | Low | High | N/A (classification) | Moderate |
| **Differentiability** | Yes | No (at 0) | Yes | Yes | Yes (for both regimes) |
| **Units** | Squared units of $y$ | Same as $y$ | Same as $y$ | Dimensionless | Same as $y$ |
| **Use Case** | Standard regression | Robust regression | Interpretable regression | Logistic Regression | Robust regression with smoothness |
| **Optimization Difficulty** | Easy (convex) | Moderate | Easy (convex) | Easy (convex) | Moderate |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given training data with $m = 3$ samples, compute the cost $J(\theta)$ for logistic regression.

| Sample | $x$ | $y$ | $h_\theta(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 1 | 0.7 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 0 | 0.4 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 1 | 0.9 |

**Solution:**

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{3} \sum_{i=1}^{3} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]$$

Sample 1: $1 \cdot \log(0.7) + 0 \cdot \log(0.3) = -0.3567$

Sample 2: $0 \cdot \log(0.4) + 1 \cdot \log(0.6) = -0.5108$

Sample 3: $1 \cdot \log(0.9) + 0 \cdot \log(0.1) = -0.1054$

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{3}(-0.3567 - 0.5108 - 0.1054) = -\frac{1}{3}(-0.9729) = 0.3243$$

The cost is **0.3243** (a small positive number → reasonably good fit).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logistic Regression Pipeline — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector x] --> B[Linear Combination z equals theta transpose x]
    B --> C[Apply Sigmoid Function sigma z]
    C --> D[Probability h theta x in 0 1]
    D --> E{Threshold 0 point 5}
    E -->|h greater than 0 point 5| F[Predicted Class y hat equals 1]
    E -->|h less than 0 point 5| G[Predicted Class y hat equals 0]
    F --> H[Compute Cross Entropy Loss]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Backpropagate Gradient]
    I --> J[Update Parameters theta]
    J --> B
```

## 4.2 Sigmoid Function Shape — Visual Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Negative Large z] -->|sigma z approx 0| B[Class 0 Region]
    C[z equals 0] -->|sigma z equals 0 point 5| D[Decision Boundary]
    E[Positive Large z] -->|sigma z approx 1| F[Class 1 Region]
    B --> D
    D --> F
```

## 4.3 Regression Error Functions — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Regression Error Functions] --> B[MSE]
    A --> C[MAE]
    A --> D[RMSE]
    A --> E[R squared]
    A --> F[Adjusted R squared]
    A --> G[Huber Loss]
    B --> B1[Penalizes Large Errors Heavily]
    C --> C1[Robust to Outliers]
    D --> D1[Same Units as Target]
    E --> E1[Explained Variance Ratio]
    F --> F1[Penalizes Extra Features]
    G --> G1[Hybrid of MSE and MAE]
```

## 4.4 Cost Function Convergence (Log Loss vs Iteration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize theta randomly] --> B[Compute J theta]
    B --> C{Converged?}
    C -->|No| D[Compute Gradient]
    D --> E[Update theta]
    E --> B
    C -->|Yes| F[Optimal theta]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Training vs Prediction Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRAINING_PHASE
        T1[Training Data x, y] --> T2[Forward Pass: Compute h theta x]
        T2 --> T3[Compute Cost J theta]
        T3 --> T4[Backward Pass: Compute Gradients]
        T4 --> T5[Update theta via Gradient Descent]
        T5 --> T2
    end
    subgraph PREDICTION_PHASE
        P1[New Input x new] --> P2[Compute h theta x new]
        P2 --> P3[Apply Threshold]
        P3 --> P4[Output y hat]
    end
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the **sigmoid function**. State and prove its key derivative property.

**Model Answer:**

The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

It maps any real-valued input $z$ to the interval $(0, 1)$, making it suitable for representing probabilities.

**Derivative Property:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$

**Proof:** (As derived in Section 3.1)

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$:

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

This property makes the sigmoid exceptionally well-suited for gradient-based optimization. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** Differentiate between **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** and **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**. When is MAE preferred over MSE?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MSE | MAE |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{1}{m}\sum(y-\hat{y})^2$ | $\frac{1}{m}\sum\vert y - \hat{y}\vert$ |
| Penalty for Outliers | Quadratic (heavy) | Linear (uniform) |
| Differentiability | Yes | No (at zero) |
| Units | Squared | Same as $y$ |

**When MAE is preferred:**
- Dataset contains **significant outliers** that should not dominate the loss.
- We want a **robust** regression model.
- The error distribution is **Laplace** (double-exponential).

**When MSE is preferred:**
- Outliers genuinely represent important variations.
- We want a **smooth, differentiable** loss for gradient-based optimization.
- Error is **Gaussian-distributed**. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Logistic Regression Derivation
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the **cost function** $J(\theta)$ for logistic regression from the **likelihood principle**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **gradient descent update rule** for logistic regression, and explain why the algorithm converges to a **global optimum**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Cost Function (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Probabilistic model.** For a single instance, we model the class label as a Bernoulli random variable:

$$P(y \mid x; \theta) = h_\theta(x)^y \cdot (1 - h_\theta(x))^{1-y}$$

where $h_\theta(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$.

**Step 2: Likelihood over $m$ independent samples.** Assuming i.i.d. samples:

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h_\theta(x^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3: Log-likelihood.** Apply $\ln(\cdot)$:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Step 4: Convert to minimization.** Negate the log-likelihood and average:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)** function. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Gradient Descent Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute partial derivative of $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta_j$.**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{1}{h^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - h^{(i)}} \cdot \left(-\frac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2: Apply chain rule** using $\dfrac{\partial h^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = h^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})h^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the bracket:**

$$y^{(i)} - y^{(i)} h^{(i)} - h^{(i)} + y^{(i)} h^{(i)} = y^{(i)} - h^{(i)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4: Update rule:**

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}}$$

**Why Global Optimum?** The cross-entropy cost function $J(\theta)$ is a **convex** function of $\theta$ (since the log-likelihood is concave). For convex functions, gradient descent is guaranteed to converge to the **global minimum** given an appropriate learning rate $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (Choice 2) — Error Functions in Regression
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the following error metrics with formulas: **MSE, RMSE, MAE, $R^2$, and Adjusted $R^2$**. Discuss their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A regression model produces the following predictions on 5 samples. Compute MSE, MAE, RMSE, and $R^2$.

| Sample | Actual $y$ | Predicted $\hat{y}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 5 | 4.8 |
| 3 | 2 | 2.2 |
| 4 | 7 | 6.0 |
| 5 | 4 | 4.5 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Error Metrics Definitions (7 marks):**

**Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average of squared differences.

$$MSE = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

- *Advantage:* Differentiable, heavily penalizes large errors.
- *Disadvantage:* Sensitive to outliers, units are squared.

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$$

- *Advantage:* Same units as $y$, easily interpretable.
- *Disadvantage:* Still outlier-sensitive.

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE):**

$$MAE = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\vert$$

- *Advantage:* Robust to outliers, intuitive interpretation (average error).
- *Disadvantage:* Not differentiable at zero → harder to optimize.

**$R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination):**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(y - \hat{y})^2}{\sum(y - \bar{y})^2}$$

- *Advantage:* Scale-independent, measures explained variance.
- *Disadvantage:* Always increases with more features (irrelevant ones too).

**Adjusted $R^2$:**

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(m - 1)}{m - n - 1}$$

- *Advantage:* Penalizes adding irrelevant features; only increases if the new feature improves the model more than expected by chance. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute errors and squared errors.**

| Sample | $y$ | $\hat{y}$ | $y - \hat{y}$ | $\vert y - \hat{y}\vert$ | $(y - \hat{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 2.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 5 | 4.8 | 0.2 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 2 | 2.2 | $-0.2$ | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 4 | 7 | 6.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.00 |
| 5 | 4 | 4.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 |

**Sums:** $\sum \vert y - \hat{y}\vert = 2.4$, $\sum(y - \hat{y})^2 = 1.58$

**Step 2: Compute MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{1.58}{5} = 0.316$$

**Step 3: Compute MAE.**

$$MAE = \frac{2.4}{5} = 0.48$$

**Step 4: Compute RMSE.**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{0.316} \approx 0.562$$

**Step 5: Compute $R^2$.**

First, compute the mean: $\bar{y} = \dfrac{3 + 5 + 2 + 7 + 4}{5} = \dfrac{21}{5} = 4.2$

Compute $SS_{tot} = \sum(y - \bar{y})^2$:

| Sample | $y$ | $y - \bar{y}$ | $(y - \bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | $-1.2$ | 1.44 |
| 2 | 5 | 0.8 | 0.64 |
| 3 | 2 | $-2.2$ | 4.84 |
| 4 | 7 | 2.8 | 7.84 |
| 5 | 4 | $-0.2$ | 0.04 |

$SS_{tot} = 14.80$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{1.58}{14.80} = 1 - 0.1068 = 0.8932$$

**Interpretation:** The model explains **89.32%** of the variance in $y$ — a strong fit. **(7 marks)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Linear and Logistic Regression Update Rules:** Both have identical update forms, but $h_\theta(x)$ is **sigmoid** in logistic, **linear** in linear regression. Writing the wrong hypothesis is a guaranteed $-1$ to $-2$ mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Log Loss:** The cost function is the **negative** log-likelihood. Dropping the minus sign (i.e., maximizing instead of minimizing) is a frequent 1-mark error.
>
> 3. **Misapplying the Sigmoid Derivative Property:** Students often write $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 + \sigma(z))$ — note the **minus** sign is critical.
>
> 4. **MSE Without the $\frac{1}{m}$ Factor:** Examiners check for the averaging factor. Forgetting it is $-0.5$ mark.
>
> 5. **$R^2$ Computation Errors:** Using $\sum \hat{y} - \bar{y}$ instead of $\sum y - \hat{y}$ in numerator. Always recheck the formula: numerator is **residual** sum of squares, not explained sum of squares.
>
> 6. **Not Justifying Convexity:** When asked why logistic regression converges to a global optimum, students often skip the **convexity argument** for the cross-entropy loss. Always state: "The log-likelihood is concave in $\theta$, hence $J(\theta)$ is convex."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Logistic Regression** is a **classification** algorithm (despite its name).
- The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ squashes real numbers to $(0, 1)$.
- Key derivative: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
- The **hypothesis** is $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$, interpreted as $P(y=1 \mid x)$.
- **Decision boundary** is where $\theta^T x = 0$.
- The **cost function** is **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)**:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h + (1 - y^{(i)})\log(1 - h)\right]$$

- Derivation path: **Bernoulli likelihood → Log-likelihood → Negate & average → Cross-Entropy**.
- **Gradient update** (identical in form to linear regression):

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$$

- **Cross-entropy is convex** → guarantees global optimum via gradient descent.
- **MSE** penalizes large errors quadratically; sensitive to outliers.
- **MAE** penalizes errors linearly; robust to outliers.
- **RMSE** is MSE in original units of $y$.
- **$R^2$** measures fraction of variance explained; $R^2 = 1$ is perfect, $R^2 = 0$ is baseline.
- **Adjusted $R^2$** penalizes extra features; preferred for multivariate models.
- **Huber Loss** is a hybrid: quadratic for small errors, linear for large ones.
- For **classification** use **Log Loss / Cross-Entropy**; for **regression** use **MSE / MAE / RMSE**.
- Always **standardize features** before gradient descent to ensure faster convergence.
