## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cross Entropy

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Cross Entropy is a fundamental loss function in information theory and machine learning that quantifies the difference between two probability distributions — typically the **true distribution** $p$ and the **predicted distribution** $q$. It measures the average number of **bits (nats or digits)** required to identify an event drawn from the true distribution $p$, when using a coding scheme optimized for the predicted distribution $q$.

For discrete probability distributions $p(x)$ and $q(x)$ over the same underlying set of events:

$$
H(p, q) = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} p(x) \log q(x)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** Cross Entropy is a non-negative scalar quantity ($H(p,q) \geq 0$), and it becomes zero **if and only if** $p \equiv q$ (the two distributions are identical). The base of the logarithm determines the unit: base-2 → **bits**, base-e → **nats**, base-10 → **digits (bans)**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster":**
Imagine a weatherman in Kerala predicting monsoon rainfall. Every day, he assigns probabilities: "80% rain, 20% sun." Cross Entropy is essentially the **"surprise score"** for his predictions. If reality matches his prediction, the surprise is low. If reality contradicts his prediction (e.g., he said 99% sun on a rainy day), the surprise — and hence the loss — is enormous. Cross Entropy accumulates this surprise across many days. A perfect forecaster would have **zero** cross entropy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Cross Entropy (Log Loss):** When the classification is binary ($y \in \{0, 1\}$), the loss simplifies to the elegant form:
>
> $$
> L = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]
> $$

**Multiclass Generalization (Categorical Cross Entropy):**
For $K$ classes, with one-hot encoded true label vector $\mathbf{y}$ and predicted probability vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$:

$$
L_{CCE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)
$$

### 1.2 Multivariate Regression

**Formal Definition:**
Multivariate (or Multiple) Linear Regression is a supervised learning algorithm that models the **linear relationship** between **two or more independent (predictor) variables** and a **single continuous dependent (response) variable**. It is the natural vector-matrix extension of simple linear regression.

$$
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Terminology Alert:** The term "multivariate" in KTU 2024 Machine Learning syllabus refers to **multiple predictors with one output** (also called *multiple linear regression*). This is distinct from *multivariate multiple regression* (multiple outputs), though the matrix formulation handles both.

**Intuitive Analogy — "House Price Predictor":**
Imagine predicting the price of a house in Kochi. Simple regression might use only the *area*. But intuitively, the price also depends on *number of bedrooms*, *distance to city center*, *age of the building*, and *floor number*. Multivariate regression combines all these "clues" into a single weighted formula to give a much sharper price estimate. Each feature gets a weight (coefficient) that says, "How much does this factor matter, holding all others constant?"

### 1.3 Naive Bayes Classifier

**Formal Definition (Bayesian Classification Foundation):**
The Naive Bayes Classifier is a family of **probabilistic classifiers** based on **Bayes' Theorem** with a **"naive" assumption of conditional independence** between features given the class label. It computes the posterior probability of each class given a feature vector and assigns the class with the **maximum posterior probability (MAP estimate)**.

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Naive" Assumption:** Despite its name, the assumption $P(x_i \mid C_k, x_j) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$ for all $i \neq j$ is rarely true in real data. Yet the classifier works remarkably well in practice — this is the central paradox that students must appreciate for KTU exams.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Email Spam Filter":**
Consider an email containing the words "FREE," "WIN," and "CASH." Naive Bayes pretends that seeing "FREE" and seeing "WIN" are **independent events** if we already know the email is spam. While this is unrealistic (spammy words cluster together), the simplification makes the math tractable and the result surprisingly accurate. The classifier essentially answers: *"Is it MORE likely that this email is spam or ham, given the words I see?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Canonical Variants of Naive Bayes (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Gaussian Naive Bayes** — for continuous features (assumes Gaussian/normal distribution)
> 2. **Multinomial Naive Bayes** — for discrete counts (popular in text classification / NLP)
> 3. **Bernoulli Naive Bayes** — for binary/boolean features (presence or absence)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cross Entropy — Operational Breakdown

**Why Cross Entropy over Mean Squared Error (MSE) for Classification?**

| Property | MSE (Regression) | Cross Entropy (Classification) |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------------|
| Convexity for sigmoid | Not convex → local minima | **Convex** → global minimum guaranteed |
| Gradient behavior | Vanishes when sigmoid saturates | **Strong gradient** when prediction is wrong |
| Probabilistic interpretation | Weak | **Direct KL divergence meaning** |
| Penalization | Quadratic | **Logarithmic — heavy penalty for confident wrong answers** |

**Operational Steps for Using Cross Entropy in a Neural Network:**
1. **Forward pass:** Compute raw scores via $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$.
2. **Softmax activation:** Convert scores to probabilities: $\hat{y}_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.
3. **Loss computation:** Apply categorical cross entropy: $L = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$.
4. **Backpropagation:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i - y_i$ (elegant gradient under softmax + cross entropy).
5. **Parameter update:** Apply gradient descent to minimize $L$.

### 2.2 Relationship: Cross Entropy ↔ KL Divergence

**The KTU-favorite connection:**

$$
H(p, q) = H(p) + D_{KL}(p \parallel q)
$$

- $H(p) = -\sum_x p(x) \log p(x)$ → **Entropy of the true distribution** (constant, irreducible).
- $D_{KL}(p \parallel q) = \sum_x p(x) \log \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ → **Kullback-Leibler divergence** (the *extra* bits needed due to using $q$ instead of $p$).

**Implication:** Minimizing cross entropy is **mathematically equivalent** to minimizing KL divergence (since $H(p)$ is constant w.r.t. model parameters $q$).

### 2.3 Multivariate Regression — Theoretical Foundation

**Why Multivariate?**
Real-world phenomena are rarely governed by a single variable. Multivariate regression captures:
- **Isolated effects** of each predictor (via partial regression coefficients).
- **Joint effects** through covariance structure.
- **Interaction awareness** (in extended models with cross terms).

**The Five Core Assumptions (Gauss-Markov Conditions):**
1. **Linearity** — relationship is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence** of error terms.
3. **Homoscedasticity** — constant variance of errors: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.
4. **No multicollinearity** — predictors are not perfectly linearly related.
5. **Normality of errors** — $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (for inference, not estimation).

### 2.4 Naive Bayes — Theoretical Pillars

**Bayes' Theorem as the Foundation:**

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Component Breakdown:**
- **Prior $P(C_k)$** — initial belief about class $k$ (computed from class frequencies).
- **Likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$** — probability of observing features given the class.
- **Evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$** — normalization constant (same for all classes, often ignored).
- **Posterior $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$** — updated belief after observing $\mathbf{x}$.

**The Naive Factorization — Independence Assumption:**

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

**MAP Decision Rule (in practice):**

$$
\hat{C} = \arg\max_{k} \; P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

(The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped since it's identical across all $k$.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log-Sum Trick for Numerical Stability:** Multiplying many small probabilities underflows in floating-point. The standard KTU answer is to take the log:
>
> $$
> \hat{C} = \arg\max_{k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]
> $$

### 2.5 Laplace (Additive) Smoothing

**Problem:** What if a feature value never appeared in training for a given class? Then $P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0$, killing the entire product (the *zero-frequency problem*).

**Solution — Laplace Smoothing (KTU Classic):**

$$
P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot V}
$$

Where $\alpha \geq 0$ is the smoothing parameter ($\alpha = 1$ → "add-one smoothing"), $V$ is the vocabulary size (or number of possible values).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Cross Entropy (general) | $H(p,q) = -\sum_x p(x) \log q(x)$ | Unit: bits (log₂) or nats (ln) |
| 2 | Binary Cross Entropy | $L = -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | $y \in \{0,1\}$, $\hat{y} \in (0,1)$ |
| 3 | Categorical Cross Entropy | $L_{CCE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | One-hot true label |
| 4 | Cross Entropy = Entropy + KL | $H(p,q) = H(p) + D_{KL}(p \parallel q)$ | Minimizing CE ≡ minimizing KL |
| 5 | Multivariate Regression Model | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$ | $p$ predictors, 1 response |
| 6 | Matrix Form | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times (p+1)$ |
| 7 | Normal Equation | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$ | OLS closed-form solution |
| 8 | Residual Sum of Squares | $RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | To be minimized |
| 9 | $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ ideally |
| 10 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C \vert \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(C)P(\mathbf{x} \vert C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Foundation of all NB |
| 11 | Naive Bayes (Posterior) | $P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)$ | Independence assumption |
| 12 | MAP Prediction | $\hat{C} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_i P(x_i \vert C_k)$ | Use log for stability |
| 13 | Gaussian NB Likelihood | $P(x_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | For continuous features |
| 14 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(x_i \vert C_k) = \dfrac{N_{x_i,C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha V}$ | Avoids zero-frequency |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Concept | Application Domain |
|---------|--------------------|
| Cross Entropy | Image classification (CNNs), medical diagnosis, fraud detection |
| Multivariate Regression | Stock price prediction, energy forecasting, demand modeling, weather prediction |
| Naive Bayes | Email spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document categorization, medical diagnosis, real-time prediction (low latency) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cross Entropy from Maximum Likelihood Estimation

**Goal:** Show that minimizing cross entropy is equivalent to Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for a Bernoulli/multinomial distribution — a KTU-favorite derivation.

**Setup:** Consider a dataset $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ where $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$ (binary classification) and the model produces $\hat{y}^{(i)} = P(y = 1 \mid x^{(i)})$.

**Step 1: Likelihood of the dataset under the model.**

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \left[\hat{y}^{(i)}\right]^{y^{(i)}} \left[1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right]^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

*Explanation:* For each sample, if $y^{(i)} = 1$, the contribution is $\hat{y}^{(i)}$; if $y^{(i)} = 0$, the contribution is $(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$. The product gives the joint probability of seeing the entire dataset.

**Step 2: Take the natural log to get the log-likelihood.**

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

*Explanation:* The logarithm transforms the product into a sum (which is easier to optimize) and is a monotonically increasing function, so maximizing $\log L$ is equivalent to maximizing $L$.

**Step 3: Convert to a minimization problem by negating and averaging.**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

*Explanation:* The negative sign converts maximization into minimization, and the $\frac{1}{m}$ normalizes to get the average loss per sample. This is precisely the **Binary Cross Entropy** loss.

**Conclusion:** Minimizing binary cross entropy $\iff$ Maximizing the log-likelihood of the data under a Bernoulli model. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Normal Equation for Multivariate Regression

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) solution $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$.

**Step 1: Express the model in matrix form.**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_{n \times 1} = \mathbf{X}_{n \times (p+1)} \boldsymbol{\beta}_{(p+1) \times 1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{n \times 1}
$$

Where $\mathbf{X}$ has rows $[1, x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \ldots, x_p^{(i)}]$ (the leading 1 absorbs the intercept $\beta_0$).

**Step 2: Write the objective — Sum of Squared Errors.**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})
$$

*Explanation:* $\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is the residual vector $\mathbf{e}$. The sum of squared errors equals the dot product of $\mathbf{e}$ with itself: $\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e}$.

**Step 3: Expand the objective function.**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{Y}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}
$$

Since $\mathbf{Y}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose: $\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$.

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y} - 2 \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and set to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} + 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}
$$

*Explanation:* Using the matrix calculus rules $\dfrac{\partial (\mathbf{a}^T \boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{a}$ and $\dfrac{\partial (\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = 2 \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric).

**Step 5: Solve for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}
$$

Pre-multiply both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$ (assuming invertibility, i.e., no perfect multicollinearity):

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Step 6: Verification — $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite.**
For any non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$: $\mathbf{v}^T (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) \mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{v})^T (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{v}) = \Vert \mathbf{X}\mathbf{v} \Vert^2 \geq 0$. Hence, when $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank, $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is positive definite, ensuring invertibility.

### 3.3 Derivation: Naive Bayes Posterior from Bayes' Theorem

**Goal:** Derive the working equation of the Naive Bayes classifier.

**Step 1: Start with Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule to the joint likelihood.**

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots
$$

**Step 3: Apply the conditional independence assumption.**

For all $i \neq j$: $\quad P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$.

The chain rule collapses dramatically:

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the MAP decision rule.**

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $k$:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k) \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Naive Bayes Classification (Text)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A training set has the following text classification counts:

| Class | Total Documents | "free" count | "money" count | "meeting" count |
|-------|----------------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Spam  | 8              | 6            | 5             | 1               |
| Ham   | 12             | 2            | 1             | 7               |

**Vocabulary $V = \{$ "free", "money", "meeting" $\}$**, $|V| = 3$. Use Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 1$.

**Classify:** A new document containing $\mathbf{x} = ($ "free", "money" $)$.

**Step 1: Compute the priors.**

$$
P(\text{Spam}) = \frac{8}{20} = 0.4
$$

$$
P(\text{Ham}) = \frac{12}{20} = 0.6
$$

**Step 2: Compute conditional probabilities with Laplace smoothing.**

For Spam, total word occurrences (per feature) $= 8$; smoothing denominator $= 8 + 1 \cdot 3 = 11$.

$$
P(\text{"free"} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{6 + 1}{8 + 3} = \frac{7}{11} \approx 0.636
$$

$$
P(\text{"money"} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{5 + 1}{8 + 3} = \frac{6}{11} \approx 0.545
$$

For Ham, denominator $= 12 + 3 = 15$.

$$
P(\text{"free"} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{2 + 1}{12 + 3} = \frac{3}{15} = 0.200
$$

$$
P(\text{"money"} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{1 + 1}{12 + 3} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.133
$$

**Step 3: Compute unnormalized posteriors.**

For Spam:
$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.4 \times 0.636 \times 0.545 \approx 0.1387
$$

For Ham:
$$
P(\text{Ham} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.6 \times 0.200 \times 0.133 \approx 0.0160
$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid \mathbf{x}) \gg P(\text{Ham} \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = \text{Spam}}
$$

**Step 5 (Optional): Normalize to get true probabilities.**

Total $= 0.1387 + 0.0160 = 0.1547$.

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.1387}{0.1547} \approx 89.7\%, \quad P(\text{Ham} \mid \mathbf{x}) \approx 10.3\%
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Multivariate Regression (Small Data)

**Problem:** Given the following data, fit a multivariate regression with two predictors.

| $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-----|
| 1   | 1     | 2     | 5   |
| 2   | 2     | 1     | 7   |
| 3   | 3     | 3     | 12  |

**Step 1: Construct matrices (with intercept column of 1's).**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$.**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 & 11 \\ 6 & 11 & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Calculation check:* Row 1 $\cdot$ Col 1: $1+1+1 = 3$. Row 1 $\cdot$ Col 2: $1+2+3 = 6$. Row 2 $\cdot$ Col 3: $2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 = 4+3+3 = 10$? Let me recompute carefully.

Row 2 of $\mathbf{X}^T$ is $[1, 2, 3]$; Col 3 of $\mathbf{X}$ is $[2, 1, 3]^T$. So $(1)(2) + (2)(1) + (3)(3) = 2 + 2 + 9 = 13$. Recheck entry (2,3) = 13, entry (3,2) = 13 (by symmetry). Corrected matrix:

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 & 13 \\ 6 & 13 & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$.**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 55 \\ 53 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Check:* Row 1: $5+7+12 = 24$. Row 2: $5+14+36 = 55$. Row 3: $10+7+36 = 53$. ✓

**Step 4: Compute $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$.**

Cofactor expansion (details omitted for brevity, but standard linear algebra):

$$
(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{42} \begin{bmatrix} 27 & -6 & -10 \\ -6 & 6 & 3 \\ -10 & 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.**

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} = \frac{1}{42} \begin{bmatrix} 27 & -6 & -10 \\ -6 & 6 & 3 \\ -10 & 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 55 \\ 53 \end{bmatrix}
$$

First row: $27(24) + (-6)(55) + (-10)(53) = 648 - 330 - 530 = -212$. Divided by 42: $\beta_0 \approx -5.05$.

Second row: $-6(24) + 6(55) + 3(53) = -144 + 330 + 159 = 345$. Divided by 42: $\beta_1 \approx 8.21$.

Third row: $-10(24) + 3(55) + 6(53) = -240 + 165 + 318 = 243$. Divided by 42: $\beta_2 \approx 5.79$.

**Final Fitted Model:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{Y} = -5.05 + 8.21 X_1 + 5.79 X_2}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross Entropy Loss Landscape (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Features x] --> B[Linear Layer: z = Wx + b]
    B --> C{Output Type?}
    C -->|Binary| D[Sigmoid Activation]
    C -->|Multiclass| E[Softmax Activation]
    D --> F[Binary Cross Entropy Loss]
    E --> G[Categorical Cross Entropy Loss]
    F --> H[Compute Gradient dL/dz]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Backpropagation Updates W, b]
    I --> J{Converged?}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[Final Probabilistic Predictions]
    
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5
    style F fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#FB8C00
    style G fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#FB8C00
    style K fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#43A047
```

### 4.2 Naive Bayes Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Observation x1, x2, ..., xn] --> B[Compute Likelihood for each class]
    P1[P(C1)] --> B
    P2[P(C2)] --> B
    PK[P(Ck)] --> B
    B --> C[Multiply: P_Ck times Product of P_xi_given_Ck]
    C --> D[Apply log for numerical stability]
    D --> E[argmax over all classes k]
    E --> F[Predicted Class Label]
    
    F1[Feature 1: P_x1_given_Ck] --> B
    F2[Feature 2: P_x2_given_Ck] --> B
    Fn[Feature n: P_xn_given_Ck] --> B
    
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style F fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style E fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#E64A19
```

### 4.3 Multivariate Regression — Data Flow & Coefficient Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data: X1, X2, ..., Xp, Y] --> B[Construct Design Matrix X with Intercept Column]
    B --> C[Compute X Transpose X]
    B --> D[Compute X Transpose Y]
    C --> E[Check Invertibility: det nonzero, low multicollinearity]
    E -->|Invertible| F[Compute Inverse of X Transpose X]
    E -->|Singular| G[Use Pseudoinverse or Regularization]
    D --> F
    F --> H[Compute beta_hat = XTX_inverse times XTY]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Predicted Output Y_hat = X times beta_hat]
    I --> J[Compute Residuals e = Y - Y_hat]
    J --> K[Evaluate R-squared, MSE, Adjusted R-squared]
    K --> L[Model Validated]
    
    style A fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style H fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00
    style L fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style G fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Loss Functions for Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classification Problem] --> B{Nature of Output}
    B -->|Two Classes| C[Binary Classification]
    B -->|Multiple Classes| D[Multiclass Classification]
    
    C --> E[Activation: Sigmoid]
    D --> F[Activation: Softmax]
    
    E --> G[Binary Cross Entropy]
    F --> H[Categorical Cross Entropy]
    
    G --> I[Loss Formula: minus y log yhat minus 1 minus y log 1 minus yhat]
    H --> J[Loss Formula: minus sum over i of yi log yhat_i]
    
    I --> K[Gradient: yhat minus y]
    J --> L[Gradient: yhat_i minus yi]
    
    K --> M[Optimizer: SGD, Adam, RMSprop]
    L --> M
    
    style A fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style M fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32
    style G fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#D84315
    style H fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#D84315
```

### 4.5 Naive Bayes — Conditional Independence Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Class C_k] --> X1[Feature X1]
    C --> X2[Feature X2]
    C --> X3[Feature X3]
    C --> Xn[Feature Xn]
    
    X1 -.-> X2
    X2 -.-> X3
    Xn -.-> X1
    
    note["Naive Assumption:<br/>X1, X2, ..., Xn are mutually<br/>independent given C_k<br/>(shown as dotted edges)"]
    
    style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style X1 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E
    style X2 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E
    style X3 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
*Define Cross Entropy loss. Derive its expression for binary classification and explain why it is preferred over Mean Squared Error (MSE) for classification tasks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Cross Entropy is a loss function that measures the dissimilarity between the true probability distribution $p$ (one-hot encoded label) and the predicted distribution $q$ (model output, e.g., from softmax/sigmoid).
- **Binary formula (1 mark):**
$$
L_{BCE} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]
$$
- **Why preferred over MSE (1 mark):** (i) MSE combined with sigmoid is **non-convex**, leading to local minima; Cross Entropy is **convex** and guarantees a global minimum. (ii) Cross Entropy produces a **stronger gradient** when the model is confidently wrong, accelerating learning. (iii) Cross Entropy has a direct **probabilistic (KL-divergence) interpretation**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
*State Bayes' Theorem. Explain how the Naive Bayes classifier uses the "naive" conditional independence assumption to compute the posterior probability of a class.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points):**

- **Bayes' Theorem (1 mark):**
$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \, P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$
- **Naive Assumption (1 mark):** Features are conditionally independent given the class: $P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$ for all $i \neq j$. This collapses the joint likelihood into a product:
$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$
- **Posterior and MAP rule (1 mark):** The classifier computes $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ for each class and selects the class with the maximum posterior (MAP estimate).

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]*

**(a) Derive the Normal Equation for Multivariate Linear Regression using the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) method. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Model Statement (1 mark):**
Multivariate linear regression models the relationship between $p$ predictors and one continuous response:
$$
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon
$$

In matrix form: $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, where $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times (p+1)$ (with a column of 1's), $\mathbf{Y}$ is $n \times 1$, $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is $(p+1) \times 1$.

**Step 2 — Assumptions (Gauss-Markov) (1 mark):**
1. Linearity in parameters
2. Independence of errors
3. Homoscedasticity (constant variance)
4. No perfect multicollinearity
5. Zero mean of errors: $E(\varepsilon_i) = 0$

**Step 3 — Objective Function (1 mark):**
Minimize the Residual Sum of Squares:
$$
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and Differentiate (2 marks):**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{Y}^T\mathbf{Y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}
$$

Setting the gradient to zero (using $\frac{\partial(\mathbf{a}^T \boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{a}$ and $\frac{\partial(\boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = 2 \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta}$):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} + 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} \implies \boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}}
$$

**Step 6 — Existence Condition (1 mark):** $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite. It is invertible (positive definite) **iff** $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank, i.e., no perfect multicollinearity. Otherwise, use the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse or regularization (Ridge regression).

---

**(b) Consider the following training data for a multivariate regression problem with two predictors: (7 marks)**

| $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-----|
| 1   | 0     | 1     | 2   |
| 2   | 1     | 1     | 4   |
| 3   | 2     | 0     | 5   |
| 4   | 3     | 2     | 9   |

**Estimate the regression coefficients using the Normal Equation. Predict $Y$ when $X_1 = 2, X_2 = 3$.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Construct matrices (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 4 \\ 6 & 14 & 7 \\ 4 & 7 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Check entry (1,1):* $1+1+1+1 = 4$. *(1,2):* $0+1+2+3 = 6$. *(1,3):* $1+1+0+2 = 4$. *(2,2):* $0+1+4+9 = 14$. *(2,3):* $0+1+0+6 = 7$. *(3,3):* $1+1+0+4 = 6$. ✓

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$ (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2+4+5+9 \\ 0+4+10+27 \\ 2+4+0+18 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 41 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$ (1.5 marks):**

Determinant: $\det(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) = 4(14 \cdot 6 - 7 \cdot 7) - 6(6 \cdot 6 - 7 \cdot 4) + 4(6 \cdot 7 - 14 \cdot 4) = 4(35) - 6(8) + 4(-14) = 140 - 48 - 56 = 36$.

Cofactor matrix computation (omitting full expansion for brevity):

$$
(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 35 & -8 & -22 \\ -8 & 8 & -4 \\ -22 & -4 & 20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Verification via identity $\mathbf{M} \mathbf{M}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$:* (omitted; trust the algebra.)

**Step 5 — Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ (1 mark):**
$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \frac{1}{36} \begin{bmatrix} 35 & -8 & -22 \\ -8 & 8 & -4 \\ -22 & -4 & 20 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 41 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- $\beta_0 = \frac{1}{36}[35(20) - 8(41) - 22(24)] = \frac{1}{36}[700 - 328 - 528] = \frac{-156}{36} = -\frac{13}{3} \approx -0.333$
- $\beta_1 = \frac{1}{36}[-8(20) + 8(41) - 4(24)] = \frac{1}{36}[-160 + 328 - 96] = \frac{72}{36} = 2$
- $\beta_2 = \frac{1}{36}[-22(20) - 4(41) + 20(24)] = \frac{1}{36}[-440 - 164 + 480] = \frac{-124}{36} \approx -0.444$

**Step 6 — Final Model and Prediction (1 mark):**
$$
\hat{Y} = -0.333 + 2 X_1 - 0.444 X_2
$$

For $X_1 = 2, X_2 = 3$:
$$
\hat{Y} = -0.333 + 2(2) - 0.444(3) = -0.333 + 4 - 1.333 = 2.333
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{Y} \approx 2.33}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Equivalent]*

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Naive Bayes classifier. Discuss its three main variants: Gaussian, Multinomial, and Bernoulli. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Naive Bayes Architecture (2 marks):**

The classifier has three functional blocks:
1. **Prior Estimation Block** — Computes $P(C_k)$ from class frequencies in training data.
2. **Likelihood Estimation Block** — For each feature $x_i$ and class $C_k$, estimates $P(x_i \mid C_k)$. The "naive" assumption factorizes the joint likelihood: $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
3. **Posterior & Decision Block** — Combines priors and likelihoods via Bayes' Theorem, then applies MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) decision rule.

A clean block diagram (already shown in Section 4.2) captures this flow.

**Step 2 — Bayes' Theorem (1 mark):**
$$
P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$
Class label assigned: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$.

**Step 3 — Three Variants (3 marks):**

| Variant | Feature Type | Likelihood Model | Equation | Use Case |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|----------|----------|
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous | Normal distribution | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | Sensor data, medical measurements |
| **Multinomial NB** | Discrete counts | Multinomial distribution | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \frac{(\sum_i x_i)!}{\prod_i x_i!} \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)^{x_i}$ | Text classification, bag-of-words |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary | Bernoulli distribution | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = p_i^{x_i}(1 - p_i)^{1 - x_i}$ | Document presence/absence, sentiment |

**Step 4 — Assumptions and Limitations (1 mark):**
- Independence assumption rarely holds in real data.
- Zero-frequency problem resolved by **Laplace smoothing**.
- Sensitive to feature scaling for continuous data (Gaussian).

---

**(b) Apply the Naive Bayes classifier to the following dataset and predict the class of a new instance $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Sunny}, \text{Cool}, \text{High}, \text{Strong})$. Use Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 1$. (7 marks)**

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No   |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No |
| 7   | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes  |
| 8   | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No   |
| 9   | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 10  | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 11  | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| 12  | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes |
| 13  | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 14  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No   |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Priors (1 mark):**
- $P(\text{Yes}) = \dfrac{9}{14} \approx 0.643$
- $P(\text{No}) = \dfrac{5}{14} \approx 0.357$

**Step 2 — Vocabulary Cardinalities (per feature) (0.5 marks):**
- Outlook: 3 values (Sunny, Overcast, Rain)
- Temperature: 3 values (Hot, Mild, Cool)
- Humidity: 2 values (High, Normal)
- Wind: 2 values (Weak, Strong)

**Step 3 — Compute Conditional Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing (3 marks):**

**Outlook given Yes (9 samples, $V=3$):**
- $P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2+1}{9+3} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.250$
- $P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3+1}{9+3} = \frac{4}{12} \approx 0.333$ (Cool appears on days 5, 6, 9 — 3 times for Yes)
- $P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3+1}{9+2} = \frac{4}{11} \approx 0.364$
- $P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3+1}{9+2} = \frac{4}{11} \approx 0.364$

**Outlook given No (5 samples):**
- $P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3+1}{5+3} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.500$
- $P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1+1}{5+3} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.250$ (Cool appears on day 6 — 1 time for No)
- $P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{4+1}{5+2} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714$
- $P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3+1}{5+2} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571$

**Step 4 — Compute Posterior Scores (1.5 marks):**

For $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Sunny}, \text{Cool}, \text{High}, \text{Strong})$:

**For Yes:**
$$
P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.643 \times 0.250 \times 0.333 \times 0.364 \times 0.364 \approx 0.00695
$$

**For No:**
$$
P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.357 \times 0.500 \times 0.250 \times 0.714 \times 0.571 \approx 0.01818
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**
$$
P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) \approx 0.01818 > P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) \approx 0.00695
$$

Normalizing: $P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) \approx 72.3\%$, $P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) \approx 27.7\%$.

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = \text{No (do not play)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing Laplace Smoothing** — If a test feature value never appears in training for a given class, $P = 0$ and the entire posterior collapses to zero. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for ignoring this. Always apply $\alpha = 1$ smoothing unless stated otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting the Denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$** — The denominator in Bayes' Theorem cancels out across classes. Students sometimes carry it through or compute it incorrectly. The decision rule uses only the **proportional** numerator: $P(C_k) \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
> 3. **Multicollinearity in Regression** — The Normal Equation fails if $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular. State the **rank condition** explicitly: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{X}) = p + 1$.
> 4. **Confusing Cross Entropy with KL Divergence** — KL divergence is **asymmetric**: $D_{KL}(p \parallel q) \neq D_{KL}(q \parallel p)$. Cross Entropy is symmetric: $H(p, q) = H(q, p)$. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Units Confusion in Cross Entropy** — Always specify whether the log is base-2 (bits) or base-e (nats). The KTU convention is typically base-e unless explicitly told otherwise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cross Entropy** quantifies the difference between two probability distributions; it is the negative log-likelihood under the assumed model, and is **convex** when used with the right activation (sigmoid/softmax), guaranteeing convergence.
- **Cross Entropy = Entropy + KL Divergence**: $H(p,q) = H(p) + D_{KL}(p \parallel q)$. Minimizing CE is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence.
- **Binary Cross Entropy**: $L = -[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ — used with sigmoid output.
- **Categorical Cross Entropy**: $L = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ — used with softmax output for multi-class problems.
- **Multivariate Regression** generalizes simple linear regression to $p$ predictors: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon$.
- The **Normal Equation** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$ provides the **closed-form OLS solution**; it requires $\mathbf{X}$ to have full column rank (no perfect multicollinearity).
- The five **Gauss-Markov assumptions** (linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, no multicollinearity, zero-mean errors) ensure OLS is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator).
- **Naive Bayes** applies Bayes' Theorem with a (often unrealistic) **conditional independence assumption**: $P(x_i \mid x_j, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
- The **MAP decision rule** is: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$; using **log-probabilities** prevents floating-point underflow.
- **Laplace Smoothing** ($+ \alpha$ in numerator, $+ \alpha V$ in denominator) prevents zero-frequency failures.
- **Three NB variants**: Gaussian (continuous), Multinomial (counts), Bernoulli (binary) — choose based on feature type.
- Naive Bayes is **fast, simple, and works well with small data** and high-dimensional sparse features (text). It is a strong baseline for text classification.
- **Naive Bayes ≠ Multinomial NB ≠ Gaussian NB** — clarify the variant in KTU exam answers; do not use generic terms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Syllabus Definition
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised, discriminative, non-probabilistic** binary classifier that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a feature space of dimension $d$ to maximize the **margin** between two classes. The optimal hyperplane is defined *entirely* by the training instances that lie closest to the decision boundary, known as **Support Vectors**. For non-linearly separable data, SVM employs the **kernel trick** to implicitly project inputs into a higher-dimensional Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) where linear separation becomes feasible.

**Classification of SVM within the ML taxonomy:**

| Attribute | Classification |
|---|---|
| Learning Paradigm | Supervised |
| Model Type | Discriminative (learns $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ boundary, not density) |
| Output Type | Binary (extended via One-vs-Rest / One-vs-One) |
| Decision Function | Geometric (distance to hyperplane) |
| Optimization | Convex Quadratic Programming (QP) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Widest Street" Intuition

> [!NOTE] Real-World Analogy
> Imagine two rival football fan groups (Class **+1** and Class **−1**) standing on a wide open field. You, as a peace officer, must draw a single straight line that separates the two crowds. **Where** you draw the line is not unique — many lines work. The SVM philosophy is: *draw the line that leaves the **widest possible empty buffer zone** (a "street") between the two groups*. The width of this street is the **margin**. Only the *boundary-most* fans — the ones actually touching the curb of this widest street — matter in deciding where the curb goes. Push them, and the street shifts. Move interior fans around freely, and the street is undisturbed. These boundary-most fans are the **support vectors**.

**Why this matters in engineering:** The widest-street hyperplane generalizes best on unseen data. Maximizing the geometric margin is mathematically equivalent to minimizing the **VC dimension** (Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension) of the classifier, providing a tighter bound on the generalization error (Structural Risk Minimization principle).

## 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary

- **Hyperplane ($\mathcal{H}$):** A $(d-1)$-dimensional affine decision surface defined by $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias term.
- **Margin ($\rho$):** The perpendicular Euclidean distance between the separating hyperplane and the nearest training sample of either class. SVM seeks to maximize $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.
- **Support Vectors:** A sparse subset of training points $\mathbf{x}_i$ that satisfy $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ — i.e., points lying precisely on the margin boundary. *All other points are mathematically irrelevant once the model is trained.*
- **Functional Margin:** $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$.
- **Geometric Margin:** $\gamma_i = \dfrac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.

> [!TIP] High-Yield Insight
> The **sparsity property** of SVM is its killer feature. Even with millions of training points, only the support vectors are stored. The model complexity in the *trained* phase scales with the number of support vectors, not the dataset size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Regimes of Linear Classification

**Regime A — Hard-Margin (Linearly Separable):**
There exists a hyperplane that perfectly classifies every training point with a non-zero margin. The constraint is strict: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$.

**Regime B — Soft-Margin (Almost Linearly Separable):**
Most points are correctly classified, but a few violations are tolerated through **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$. The trade-off is governed by the **penalty hyperparameter** $C > 0$.

**Regime C — Kernelized (Non-Linearly Separable):**
Data is mapped via $\phi: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ into a higher-dimensional feature space where linear separation is achievable. The **kernel trick** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ avoids explicit computation of $\phi$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of Hard-Margin SVM

1. **Decision Rule Hypothesis:** The classifier outputs $h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$.
2. **Geometric Margin Definition:** For a point $\mathbf{x}_i$, the perpendicular distance to $\mathcal{H}$ is $\dfrac{\lvert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \rvert}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$. The minimum over all points is the geometric margin.
3. **Rescaling Invariance:** Multiplying $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ by any non-zero scalar $k$ does not change the hyperplane geometry. We exploit this to fix the *functional margin* of the nearest point to be exactly 1, which converts an unbounded problem into a bounded one.
4. **Objective:** Minimize $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$.
5. **Dual Conversion:** Apply Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ to switch to the dual, which exposes the dot-product structure $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ — the *only* place kernels can be substituted.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Print This Table Before Every Exam
> **Table 2.1 — Complete SVM Equation Reference for KTU ESE**

| Component | Mathematical Form | KTU Exam Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Decision Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | $\mathbf{w}$ is normal to the surface |
| Signed Distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to $\mathcal{H}$ | $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ | Always signed; sign = class label |
| Geometric Margin (full dataset) | $\min_{i} \dfrac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ | The "worst-case" point defines it |
| **Hard-Margin Primal** | $\min_{\mathbf{w},b} \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \;\;\text{s.t.}\;\; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Convex QP; unique global optimum |
| **Hard-Margin Dual** | $\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | s.t. $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |
| **Soft-Margin Primal** | $\min_{\mathbf{w},b,\boldsymbol{\xi}} \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$ | s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| Slack Variable Meaning | $\xi_i = 0$ → correct & beyond margin; $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ → inside margin but correct side; $\xi_i > 1$ → misclassified | Crucial for **Part A** 3-mark questions |
| Soft-Margin Dual | $\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | Box constraint: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ |
| Recovery of $\mathbf{w}^*$ | $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Only $\alpha_i > 0$ contribute → sparsity |
| KKT Complementary Slackness | $\alpha_i^* \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \big] = 0$ | Identifies exactly which points are support vectors |
| Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | Reduces to standard linear SVM |
| Polynomial Kernel (degree $p$) | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + c)^p$ | $c \geq 0$ controls lower-order terms |
| RBF / Gaussian Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Most popular; $\sigma$ = bandwidth; equivalent to $\gamma = 1/(2\sigma^2)$ in scikit-learn |
| Sigmoid Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\kappa \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + \theta)$ | Not strictly positive semi-definite; rarely used |
| Mercer's Condition | $\int\int K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) f(\mathbf{x}) f(\mathbf{z})\, d\mathbf{x}\, d\mathbf{z} \geq 0 \;\; \forall f \in L_2$ | Necessary and sufficient for a valid kernel |
| VC-Dimension Bound (Hard Margin) | $R^2 \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \geq h$ (where $R$ = radius of enclosing sphere) | Justifies margin maximization as regularization |

> [!WARNING] Markdown Escaping Notice
> In all exam answers, the absolute value / norm $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ is rendered using the LaTeX command `\lVert` and `\rVert`. *Do not* type the literal pipe character `|` in your answer script when working in a tabular or computational context.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SVM is the workhorse for:
- **Bioinformatics:** Microarray cancer classification (high-dimensional, low-sample regime).
- **Computer Vision:** Handwritten digit recognition (historically outperformed by CNNs post-2012, still excellent on small datasets).
- **Text/NLP:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis — high-dimensional sparse bag-of-words features.
- **Geophysics & Remote Sensing:** Hyperspectral image classification.
- **Anomaly Detection:** One-Class SVM (Schölkopf et al., 2001) for outlier identification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Margin

> **Goal:** Express the perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ in closed form.

**Step 1 — Parameterize any point on the hyperplane.**
Let $\mathbf{x}_p$ be any point satisfying $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_p + b = 0$, i.e., $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_p = -b$.

**Step 2 — Express the vector from $\mathbf{x}_p$ to an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$.**
The displacement vector is $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p$.

**Step 3 — Project $\mathbf{r}$ onto the unit normal $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.**
The perpendicular distance is the projection magnitude:

$$d = \left\vert \mathbf{r} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{(\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p)^\top \mathbf{w}}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \right\vert$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_p = -b$.**

$$d = \left\vert \frac{\mathbf{x}_0^\top \mathbf{w} - (-b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \right\vert = \frac{\lvert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b \rvert}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

**Step 5 — Account for the class label.**
Multiplying by $y \in \{-1, +1\}$ (which has the same sign as $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b$ for correctly classified points) eliminates the absolute value and yields the signed geometric distance:

$$\gamma(\mathbf{x}_0) = \frac{y(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

**Step 6 — Define the dataset margin as the minimum signed distance across all training points.**

$$\rho = \min_{i \in \{1, \dots, n\}} \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Margin Bandwidth as $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$

> **Goal:** Show that the distance between the two parallel margin hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ is $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.

**Step 1 — Pick a point on the upper margin.**
Let $\mathbf{x}_+$ satisfy $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_+ + b = 1$.

**Step 2 — Pick a point on the lower margin.**
Let $\mathbf{x}_-$ satisfy $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_- + b = -1$.

**Step 3 — Compute the vector from $\mathbf{x}_-$ to $\mathbf{x}_+$.**

$$\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_- \quad \text{with norm} \quad \lVert \mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_- \rVert$$

**Step 4 — Project onto the normal direction $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.**
The bandwidth (perpendicular distance between the two parallel planes) equals the projection of $\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-$ onto the normal:

$$\text{Bandwidth} = \left\vert \frac{(\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-)^\top \mathbf{w}}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \right\vert$$

**Step 5 — Expand the numerator.**

$$(\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-)^\top \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{x}_+^\top \mathbf{w} - \mathbf{x}_-^\top \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the hyperplane constraints.**

$$= (1 - b) - (-1 - b) = 1 - b + 1 + b = 2$$

**Step 7 — Conclude.**

$$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert} \equiv \rho$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Minimizing $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ is mathematically equivalent to **maximizing** the bandwidth $\rho$. This is the core reason SVMs choose the *widest street*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Dual Problem (Lagrangian Dual)

> **Goal:** Convert the constrained hard-margin primal into its dual, exposing the dot-product structure suitable for kernel substitution.

**Step 1 — State the primal.**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; i = 1, \dots, n$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian.**
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each inequality constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivatives and set to zero (stationarity conditions).**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute into the Lagrangian to obtain the dual.**
Plug $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ back into $\mathcal{L}$. First, expand the squared norm:

$$\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 5 — Combine the terms.**

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) - \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i$$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 7 — State the dual problem.**

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

**Step 8 — The Kernel Trick.**
Replace every dot product $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with a kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

> [!NOTE] Why this is the *trick*
> The data never appears as $\mathbf{x}_i$ in isolation — only as pairwise inner products $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. We can therefore replace the inner product with a kernel function that *implicitly* computes the inner product in a (potentially infinite-dimensional) feature space, without ever computing the feature vectors $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ explicitly. This is called the *kernel trick*.

---

## 3.4 Recovery of Bias $b$ and the KKT Support-Vector Condition

Once $\boldsymbol{\alpha}^*$ is found, $\mathbf{w}^*$ is recovered as $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$. To recover $b^*$, use any support vector $\mathbf{x}_s$ for which $0 < \alpha_s^* < C$ (in the soft-margin case) and $y_s(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_s + b^*) = 1$:

$$b^* = y_s - \mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_s = y_s - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i^* y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_s)$$

The **KKT complementary slackness** condition $\alpha_i^* \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \big] = 0$ guarantees that only points with $\alpha_i^* > 0$ contribute to $\mathbf{w}^*$ — these are, by definition, the **support vectors**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Variant | Objective | Constraint on $\alpha_i$ | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard-Margin SVM | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | Perfectly separable, noise-free |
| Soft-Margin SVM (C-SVM) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Overlapping, noisy, real-world |
| $\nu$-SVM | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \nu \rho + \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \xi_i$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq 1/n$, $\sum \alpha_i \geq \nu$ | Fraction $\nu$ of margin errors bounded |
| Least-Squares SVM (LS-SVM) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + \tfrac{C}{2} \sum_i e_i^2$ | Equality constraints (no sparsity) | Faster, but loses sparsity |
| One-Class SVM | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \rho + \tfrac{1}{\nu n} \sum_i \xi_i$ | $\alpha_i \in [0, 1/(\nu n)]$ | Anomaly / novelty detection |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Linear SVM Decision Geometry

This diagram illustrates the geometric structure of a trained linear SVM, identifying the hyperplane, the two margin boundaries, the support vectors, and the regular non-support training points.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Training Dataset of N Points in R^d] --> B[Linear Separability Check]
    B --> C{Is a Hyperplane wT x + b = 0 Achievable?}
    C -- YES, Strict Margin --> D[Hard-Margin SVM Primal]
    C -- NO, Overlapping Classes --> E[Soft-Margin SVM with Slack xi and Penalty C]
    D --> F[Solve QP: minimize 1/2 of norm of w squared]
    E --> F2[Solve QP: minimize 1/2 of norm of w squared + C times sum of xi]
    F --> G[Dual via Lagrangian Multipliers alpha]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{Data Linearly Separable in Feature Space?}
    H -- YES --> I[Use Linear Kernel K of x_i and x_j equals dot product]
    H -- NO --> J[Use RBF or Polynomial Kernel via Kernel Trick]
    I --> K[Recover w and b from alpha and Support Vectors]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output Classifier h of x equals sign of wT x + b]
    L --> M[Predict Class Label for New Query x_query]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — SVM Prediction Pipeline (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input Vector x in R^d] --> feat{Featurization Step}
    feat --> kern[Kernel Function K]
    kern --> sv[Identify Support Vectors x_s]
    sv --> alpha[Retrieve Learned Lagrange Multipliers alpha_s]
    alpha --> dot[Compute Weighted Sum sum of alpha_s y_s K of x_s and x]
    dot --> bia[Add Bias Term b]
    bia --> sig[Apply Sign Function]
    sig --> out[Output Predicted Class y_pred in minus 1 and plus 1]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Support Vector Localisation (Textual)

| Region | Geometric Condition | $\alpha_i$ Value | Role in Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Beyond Upper Margin** | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) > 1$ | $\alpha_i = 0$ | Inactive; ignored after training |
| **On Upper Margin (Class +1)** | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ | $\alpha_i > 0$ | **Support Vector** |
| **Inside the Band (Correct Side)** | $0 \leq y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 1$ | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ (soft margin) | **Support Vector** (on margin in hard margin) |
| **Misclassified Side** | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) < 0$ | $\alpha_i = C$ (soft margin) | **Support Vector** with $\xi_i > 1$ |

## 4.4 Effect of the Hyperparameter $C$ — Visual Intuition

- **Large $C$ (e.g., $C \to \infty$):** The penalty for slack is severe. The model tolerates almost no misclassification → *narrow* effective margin, low bias, **high variance** (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$ (e.g., $C \to 0$):** Slack is cheap. The model tolerates many violations → *wider* effective margin, **high bias**, low variance (risk of underfitting).
- **Moderate $C$:** Bias-variance trade-off is balanced. Selected via cross-validation or grid search.

> [!TIP] KTU Examiner's Heuristic
> When asked to "explain the effect of $C$," always frame it as a **regularization parameter inverse** — small $C$ = strong regularization, large $C$ = weak regularization. Many students invert this in exams and lose marks.
