# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reference (Module 2 portion):**
> - Part A: 3 marks each, ~1 to 2 questions per module, 50–80 word answers.
> - Part B: 14 marks each with internal choice, sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.
> - Module 2 weightage ≈ 20–25% of full paper.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define a Support Vector Machine. List any two advantages and one limitation of using SVM for classification.**

**Model Answer (target: ~60 words):**

A Support Vector Machine is a supervised binary classifier that finds the hyperplane in feature space which **maximizes the geometric margin** between two classes. The hyperplane is defined solely by the **support vectors** — training points closest to the decision boundary.

**Advantages:**
1. Effective in **high-dimensional spaces** (e.g., text classification) where number of features $d$ exceeds sample size $n$.
2. Memory-efficient at prediction time — only support vectors are stored.

**Limitation:**
1. **Poor scalability** to very large datasets ($n > 10^5$) due to QP solver complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$.
2. Does not directly provide probability estimates (requires Platt scaling).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Explain the role of the kernel function in SVM. Name any two kernel functions with their mathematical forms.**

**Model Answer (target: ~60 words):**

The **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ enables SVM to learn non-linear decision boundaries by **implicitly** mapping data into a higher-dimensional feature space via $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and computing inner products there *without* explicitly computing $\phi$. This is the **kernel trick**.

**Two standard kernels:**

1. **Polynomial Kernel (degree $p$):** $\;\;K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + c)^p$, where $c \geq 0$.
2. **Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel:** $\;\;K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a) Formulate the optimization problem for the linearly separable (hard-margin) SVM. Clearly state the objective function, the constraints, and the geometric interpretation of the margin. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let the training set be $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$. The decision hyperplane is $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ with normal vector $\mathbf{w}$ and bias $b$.

**Step 2 — Geometric margin derivation.**
The perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{\lvert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \rvert}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$. For a correctly classified point, multiplying by $y_i$ removes the absolute value.

**Step 3 — Canonical form.**
By the rescaling invariance of the hyperplane, we constrain the *functional* margin of the closest point to equal 1. This yields the canonical form constraints:

$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$$

**Step 4 — Objective.**
Maximize the geometric margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$ ↔ minimize $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ ↔ minimize $\dfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ (the squared form gives a quadratic programming problem with a unique global minimum).

**Step 5 — Primal formulation.**

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The constraints force every training point to lie on the correct side of the two margin hyperplanes ($\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$). The objective maximizes the perpendicular distance between these two parallel hyperplanes, equal to $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$.

---

**(b) Derive the dual formulation using Lagrange multipliers. Explain how this dual leads to the concept of support vectors. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lagrangian.**
Introduce $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each inequality constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \big]$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \implies \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substituting back yields the dual.**

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 4 — Support vector emergence.**
By **KKT complementary slackness**, $\alpha_i^* \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \big] = 0$. This implies:
- If $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) > 1$ (point is beyond the margin, not on the boundary), then $\alpha_i^* = 0$. Such a point does **not** contribute to $\mathbf{w}^*$.
- If $\alpha_i^* > 0$, then the point must satisfy $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) = 1$ — it lies *exactly* on the margin boundary. These are the **support vectors**.

Hence, $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the support set. The model is fully determined by $|\mathcal{S}|$ points.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

**(a) Discuss the limitations of hard-margin SVM. How does the soft-margin formulation address these issues? Formulate the soft-margin primal and explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Limitation of hard-margin SVM.**
Hard-margin SVM requires the data to be **perfectly linearly separable** with zero margin violations. Real-world data contains:
- Class overlap (Bayes error > 0).
- Noise and outliers that make strict separation impossible.
- In such cases, the hard-margin problem is **infeasible** — no $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ satisfies all constraints.

**Step 2 — Introduction of slack variables.**
Cortes and Vapnik (1995) introduced **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ that relax the constraints:

$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

The quantity $\xi_i$ measures the degree of violation of the $i$-th training point:
- $\xi_i = 0$: point is correctly classified and outside the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$: point is inside the margin but on the correct side.
- $\xi_i > 1$: point is misclassified.

**Step 3 — Soft-margin primal formulation.**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$

subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Step 4 — Role of $C$.**
The hyperparameter $C > 0$ is the **regularization parameter** that controls the trade-off:
- **Large $C$:** Heavy penalty for margin violations → narrow margin, low bias, high variance (overfitting).
- **Small $C$:** Lenient penalty → wide margin, high bias, low variance (underfitting).
- $C$ is selected via cross-validation.

**Step 5 — Soft-margin dual (for completeness).**
The dual is identical in form to the hard-margin dual, except the constraint becomes a **box constraint**:

$$0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

---

**(b) Explain the kernel trick in SVM. Why is it computationally advantageous? Define the RBF kernel and explain the role of the parameter $\sigma$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation.**
Many real datasets are not linearly separable in their original input space. The classical remedy is to map inputs to a higher-dimensional feature space via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^D$ (where $D \gg d$) where a linear separator may exist.

**Step 2 — Computational bottleneck.**
Direct mapping is expensive: $D$ can be infinite (e.g., RBF). The dual problem, however, contains the data only inside inner products $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$.

**Step 3 — The kernel trick.**
Define a kernel function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ that equals the inner product in feature space without explicitly computing $\phi$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

Replace every occurrence of $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ in the dual objective with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. The optimization and prediction are carried out in the original space, but the resulting classifier is *equivalent* to a linear classifier in feature space.

**Step 4 — Computational advantage.**
For instance, polynomial kernel of degree $p$ in $d$ dimensions corresponds to a feature space of dimension $\binom{d+p}{p}$. Direct computation is $\mathcal{O}(d^p)$; the kernel trick reduces it to $\mathcal{O}(d)$.

**Step 5 — RBF kernel definition.**

$$K_{\text{RBF}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Role of $\sigma$.**
$\sigma$ is the **bandwidth** parameter that controls the locality of the kernel:
- **Small $\sigma$:** $K \to 0$ rapidly with distance → highly localized influence → complex, wiggly decision boundary → risk of overfitting.
- **Large $\sigma$:** $K \to 1$ for most pairs → smoother, more global decision boundary → risk of underfitting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Deduction Traps in SVM Questions
> 1. **Mixing up $C$ and $\lambda$:** $C$ in soft-margin SVM is the *inverse* of regularization strength. Students often describe $C$ as "regularization strength" — this is **wrong**. Say instead: "$C$ is inversely proportional to regularization strength."
> 2. **Forgetting the bias constraint:** In the dual derivation, the condition $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ is **mandatory**. Writing only $\alpha_i \geq 0$ will cost 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing the geometric and functional margins:** Geometric margin has $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ in the denominator; functional margin does not. Examiners will deduct marks if you use the two interchangeably.
> 4. **Writing $\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j$ as $\mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_j$** in the dual — this is ambiguous. Always use the transpose form $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$.
> 5. **Mis-stating Mercer's condition:** Mercer's theorem requires $K$ to be *symmetric and positive semi-definite* for a valid kernel. "Symmetric and continuous" is **incomplete**.
> 6. **Hard-margin feasibility assumption:** You must explicitly state the assumption that the data is linearly separable before writing the hard-margin primal.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] Rapid Revision Checklist — SVM

- [x] **SVM = Maximal Margin Classifier** in feature space; defined by **support vectors only**.
- [x] **Decision rule:** $h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$.
- [x] **Margin bandwidth** = $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$; objective = minimize $\dfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$.
- [x] **Hard-margin primal:** $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
- [x] **Soft-margin primal:** $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.
- [x] **Dual objective:** $\max \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
- [x] **Hard-margin dual constraint:** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- [x] **Soft-margin dual constraint:** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- [x] **Weight vector recovery:** $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ (sparse — only $\alpha_i > 0$ contribute).
- [x] **Bias recovery:** Use any $i$ with $0 < \alpha_i < C$: $b^* = y_i - \sum_j \alpha_j^* y_j K(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$.
- [x] **KKT complementary slackness** is the *defining* property that isolates support vectors.
- [x] **Kernel trick** replaces inner products with $K(\cdot, \cdot)$; the classifier remains linear in feature space.
- [x] **RBF kernel** is the most general purpose; $\sigma$ controls smoothness.
- [x] **Mercer's condition:** $K$ must be symmetric and positive semi-definite to be a valid kernel.
- [x] **Hyperparameter $C$** = inverse regularization; small $C$ = wide margin (more bias), large $C$ = narrow margin (less bias).
- [x] **SVM is a convex optimization problem** — global optimum is guaranteed; no local minima issue (unlike neural networks).
- [x] **SVMs do not natively output probabilities**; use Platt scaling for calibrated probabilities.
- [x] **Multiclass extensions:** One-vs-Rest (OvR) and One-vs-One (OvO).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Trees - Random Forests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees - Random Forests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Decision Tree — Formal Definition

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both **classification** and **regression** tasks. It learns a hierarchical, tree-structured model of decisions by recursively partitioning the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions, where each internal node represents a test on a feature, each branch represents the outcome of that test, and each leaf node represents a class label (or a continuous value in regression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A Decision Tree is a flowchart-like structure in which each internal node denotes a test on an attribute, each branch represents the outcome of the test, and each leaf node holds a class label. The topmost node is called the **root node**.*

Mathematically, a decision tree partitions the input space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ into axis-aligned rectangular regions $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_M$, where each region $R_m$ is assigned a predicted output $\hat{y}_m$. The model is:

$$\hat{f}(x) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \hat{y}_m \cdot \mathbb{I}(x \in R_m)$$

where $\mathbb{I}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Root Node** | The topmost node representing the entire dataset; the first split |
| **Internal Node** | A node that performs a decision test on a feature |
| **Branch / Edge** | The outcome of a decision test, leading to a child node |
| **Leaf / Terminal Node** | A node with no children; holds the final class or value |
| **Splitting** | The process of dividing a node into two or more sub-nodes |
| **Pruning** | The process of removing sub-nodes to reduce overfitting |

> [!NOTE]
> Decision Trees belong to the family of **white-box models** — their decision logic is fully human-interpretable, unlike black-box models such as neural networks or SVMs.

---

## 1.2 Random Forest — Formal Definition

A **Random Forest** is an **ensemble learning** algorithm that constructs a multitude of decision trees during training and outputs the **mode of the classes** (for classification) or **mean prediction** (for regression) of the individual trees. It combines the principles of **bagging (Bootstrap AGGregatING)** and **random feature selection** to produce a robust, low-variance predictor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Random Forest is a meta-estimator that fits a number of decision tree classifiers on various sub-samples of the dataset and uses averaging to improve the predictive accuracy and control over-fitting.*

---

## 1.3 🌱 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

### Decision Tree Analogy: "The 20-Questions Game"

Imagine you are playing the **"20 Questions"** game with a friend. Your goal is to guess an object (say, a *mango*). You ask strategic yes/no questions:

1. *"Is it a fruit?"* → **Yes** (splits the world of all objects into *fruits* vs *non-fruits*)
2. *"Is it tropical?"* → **Yes** (narrows down to *mango, pineapple, papaya, banana...*)
3. *"Is its skin green when ripe?"* → **No** (eliminates some)
4. *"Is it larger than a fist?"* → **Yes** → *You guess: MANGO!* 🍋

Each question **splits the remaining possibilities** based on the **most informative feature**. The final answer is the leaf. **This is exactly how a Decision Tree learns!**

> [!NOTE]
> The art of building a good decision tree is asking the **most informative question first** — this is measured by **Information Gain** or **Gini Index reduction**.

### Random Forest Analogy: "The Wisdom of the Crowd"

Now suppose you ask **100 different friends** independently, and each friend asks their own 20 questions (possibly different ones) to arrive at a guess. You then take the **majority vote**. This crowd-sourced wisdom is far more accurate than any single friend's guess because:

- Each friend sees a slightly different subset of training examples (bootstrap sampling).
- Each friend considers a random subset of features at each question (random subspace method).
- Errors of individual trees cancel out in the ensemble.

**This is Random Forest! 🌳🌳🌳**

---

## 1.4 Why Decision Trees & Random Forests Matter in Engineering

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|--------------------|----------|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Classifying tumors as benign/malignant |
| **Credit Scoring** | Loan approval based on applicant features |
| **Fault Detection** | Predicting machinery failure in Industry 4.0 |
| **Remote Sensing** | Land-cover classification from satellite imagery |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression analysis |
| **Customer Churn** | Telecom/retail predictive analytics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes Decision Trees because they are **interpretable**, **handle mixed data types** (numerical + categorical), **require little data preprocessing**, and form the **backbone of powerful ensembles** like Random Forests, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosted Trees.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Does a Decision Tree Learn? — The Recursive Partitioning Algorithm

A decision tree is built using a **top-down, greedy approach** known as **recursive binary splitting** (or Hunt's algorithm in its general form). The procedure is:

1. **Start** at the root with the entire training dataset.
2. **Select the best feature** $X_j$ and split point $s$ that produces the most "pure" child nodes.
3. **Partition** the data into two (or more) subsets based on the test $X_j \leq s$.
4. **Recurse** on each child subset until a stopping criterion is met (e.g., maximum depth, minimum samples, pure node).
5. **Assign** a class label to each leaf (majority class) or a regression value (mean).

The "best" split is determined by an **impurity measure** — the most common being **Entropy**, **Gini Index**, and **Classification Error**.

---

## 2.2 Impurity Measures — The Heart of the Algorithm

### 2.2.1 Entropy (Information-Theoretic Measure)

**Entropy** quantifies the amount of **disorder or randomness** in a node. For a node with class distribution $(p_0, p_1, \dots, p_{K-1})$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

- $H(S) = 0$ → **Pure node** (all samples belong to one class)
- $H(S) = \log_2 K$ → **Maximum disorder** (uniform distribution)

> [!NOTE]
> By convention, $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ (since $\lim_{p \to 0^+} p \log_2 p = 0$).

### 2.2.2 Information Gain

**Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting the dataset $S$ on feature $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ where feature $A = v$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The feature with the HIGHEST Information Gain is chosen for splitting at each node.** This is the **ID3 algorithm** (Quinlan, 1986).

### 2.2.3 Gini Index (CART Algorithm)

The **Gini Index** measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen element if it were labeled according to the class distribution in the node:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} p_i^2$$

- $\text{Gini} = 0$ → Pure node
- $\text{Gini} = 1 - \frac{1}{K}$ → Maximum impurity (uniform distribution)

The Gini gain for a split is:

$$\Delta \text{Gini}(S, A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **CART (Classification and Regression Trees)** algorithm uses the **Gini Index** by default, while **ID3** uses **Entropy/Information Gain**, and **C4.5** uses the **Gain Ratio** (a normalized version of Information Gain).

### 2.2.4 Gain Ratio (C4.5 Algorithm)

Information Gain is biased toward features with many distinct values. The **Gain Ratio** normalizes IG by the **intrinsic value (split information)**:

$$\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$$

where

$$\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \left( \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \right)$$

### 2.2.5 Classification Error

Used mainly in **CART pruning** (not splitting):

$$\text{Error}(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$$

---

## 2.3 Pruning — Controlling Overfitting

Decision trees are prone to **high variance / overfitting** if grown to full depth. **Pruning** mitigates this:

| Type | Method | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Pre-pruning (Early Stopping)** | Set constraints *during* growth | E.g., `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf` |
| **Post-pruning (Reduced Error / Cost-Complexity)** | Grow full tree, then *prune back* | Replace sub-trees with leaves if validation error decreases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost-Complexity Pruning (Weakest Link Pruning):** The pruning parameter $\alpha \geq 0$ balances tree size vs. fit:
> $$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$
> where $R(T)$ is the total misclassification rate and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of terminal nodes.

---

## 2.4 🌲 Random Forest — The Ensemble Mechanism

A Random Forest combines two powerful randomization techniques:

### 2.4.1 Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregation)

For each of the $B$ trees:
1. **Sample with replacement** a bootstrap dataset of size $N$ from the original training set of size $N$. On average, ~**63.2%** of unique samples appear in each bootstrap sample.
2. Train an unpruned decision tree on this bootstrap sample.

The final prediction is:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{RF}}(x) = \text{mode}\{\hat{y}_b(x)\}_{b=1}^{B} \quad \text{(classification)}$$

$$\hat{y}_{\text{RF}}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{y}_b(x) \quad \text{(regression)}$$

### 2.4.2 Random Feature Selection (Random Subspace Method)

At each split in each tree, only a random subset of $m$ features is considered (instead of all $d$ features). Common choices:

$$m = \sqrt{d} \quad \text{(classification, default in many libraries)}$$

$$m = \left\lfloor \frac{d}{3} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(regression)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **decorrelates the trees** — a strong predictor feature would not dominate every tree, reducing correlation between trees and lowering ensemble variance.

### 2.4.3 Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error

Since each bootstrap sample omits ~**36.8%** of the training data on average, these "left-out" samples form a natural **validation set** for each tree. The **OOB error** is the aggregated error on these held-out predictions:

$$\text{OOB Error} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}(x_i)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> OOB error provides an **unbiased estimate of test error** without needing a separate cross-validation loop — a major practical advantage of Random Forests.

### 2.4.4 Feature Importance

Two standard measures:

**(a) Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) — Gini Importance:**

$$\text{Imp}(X_j) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \sum_{t \in \text{splits on } X_j} w_t \cdot \Delta \text{Gini}_t$$

**(b) Mean Decrease in Accuracy (MDA) — Permutation Importance:**
Randomly permute feature $X_j$ in the OOB samples and measure the drop in accuracy.

---

## 2.5 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — All Key Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Range: $[0, \log_2 K]$ |
| 2 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Used in ID3 |
| 3 | **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i} p_i^2$ | Used in CART |
| 4 | **Gini Gain** | $\Delta\text{Gini}(S,A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$ | Maximize |
| 5 | **Gain Ratio** | $\text{GR}(S,A) = \frac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)}$ | Used in C4.5 |
| 6 | **Split Information** | $\text{SplitInfo}(S,A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Intrinsic value |
| 7 | **Classification Error** | $\text{Error}(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | Used in pruning |
| 8 | **Cost-Complexity** | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | Pruning criterion |
| 9 | **Bootstrap Fraction** | $P(\text{in-bag}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N \approx 0.632$ | As $N \to \infty$ |
| 10 | **Random Subspace Size** | $m = \sqrt{d}$ (classification), $m = d/3$ (regression) | Default heuristic |
| 11 | **RF Prediction (Class.)** | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{\hat{y}_1, \dots, \hat{y}_B\}$ | Majority vote |
| 12 | **RF Prediction (Reg.)** | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{y}_b$ | Averaging |
| 13 | **OOB Error** | $\text{OOB} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \mathbb{I}(y_i \neq \hat{y}_{OOB,i})$ | Unbiased test estimate |
| 14 | **Bagging Variance Reduction** | $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{B} + \frac{B-1}{B} \rho \sigma^2$ | $\rho$ = tree correlation |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Random Forests power diagnostic systems where interpretability and accuracy both matter.
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection using ensemble tree models.
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance — sensor data classification for fault prediction.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Flood/landslide susceptibility mapping.
- **Finance:** Fraud detection and credit risk modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Information Gain Calculation (KTU Exam Favorite)

### Problem Statement

Given the following training dataset, build a decision tree using **Information Gain (ID3)**. Predict the class label for the tuple: `(Outlook=Sunny, Temperature=Hot, Humidity=High, Wind=Weak)`.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------------|
| D1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No         |
| D2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No       |
| D3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| D4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes        |
| D5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No       |
| D7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| D8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No         |
| D9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| D12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes      |
| D13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes        |
| D14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No        |

**Total = 14 samples; Yes = 9, No = 5**

---

### Step 1: Compute Entropy of the Full Dataset $S$

$$H(S) = -p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} - p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}}$$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

Computing the logarithms:

$$\log_2 \frac{9}{14} = \log_2 9 - \log_2 14 = 3.1699 - 3.8074 = -0.6375$$

$$\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = \log_2 5 - \log_2 14 = 2.3219 - 3.8074 = -1.4855$$

Substituting:

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}(-0.6375) - \frac{5}{14}(-1.4855)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4097 + 0.5305 = \boxed{0.9403 \text{ bits}}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Information Gain for Each Feature

#### 2A. Information Gain for `Outlook`

**Subsets:**
- `Sunny` → {D1, D2, D8, D9, D11} → Yes=2, No=3 → $H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = ?$
- `Overcast` → {D3, D7, D12, D13} → Yes=4, No=0 → $H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = ?$
- `Rain` → {D4, D5, D6, D10, D14} → Yes=3, No=2 → $H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = ?$

**Sub-entropy calculations:**

For `Sunny` (5 samples: 2 Yes, 3 No):

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5}$$

$$\log_2 0.4 = -1.3219, \quad \log_2 0.6 = -0.7370$$

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -0.4(-1.3219) - 0.6(-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

For `Overcast` (4 samples, all Yes — pure):

$$H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 = \boxed{0}$$

For `Rain` (5 samples: 3 Yes, 2 No):

$$H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5}$$

$$H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = -0.6(-0.7370) - 0.4(-1.3219) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$$

**Weighted average entropy after split:**

$$H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936$$

**Information Gain for `Outlook`:**

$$IG(S, \text{Outlook}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = \boxed{0.2467}$$

---

#### 2B. Information Gain for `Temperature`

**Subsets:**
- `Hot` → {D1, D2, D3, D13} → Yes=2, No=2 → $H = 1.0$ (perfectly balanced)
- `Mild` → {D4, D8, D10, D11, D12, D14} → Yes=4, No=2
- `Cool` → {D5, D6, D7, D9} → Yes=3, No=1

**Sub-entropies:**

$$H_{\text{Hot}} = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} = 0.5(1) + 0.5(1) = 1.0000$$

$$H_{\text{Mild}} = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6}$$

$$= -\frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3} - \frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3}$$

$$\log_2(2/3) = -0.5850, \quad \log_2(1/3) = -1.5850$$

$$H_{\text{Mild}} = 0.6667(0.5850) + 0.3333(1.5850) = 0.3900 + 0.5283 = 0.9183$$

$$H_{\text{Cool}} = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4}$$

$$\log_2(0.75) = -0.4150, \quad \log_2(0.25) = -2.0000$$

$$H_{\text{Cool}} = 0.75(0.4150) + 0.25(2.0000) = 0.3113 + 0.5000 = 0.8113$$

**Weighted average:**

$$H(S \mid \text{Temp}) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0000) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$= 0.2857 + 0.3936 + 0.2318 = 0.9111$$

$$IG(S, \text{Temp}) = 0.9403 - 0.9111 = \boxed{0.0292}$$

---

#### 2C. Information Gain for `Humidity`

**Subsets:**
- `High` → {D1, D2, D3, D4, D8, D12, D14} → Yes=3, No=4
- `Normal` → {D5, D6, D7, D9, D10, D11, D13} → Yes=6, No=1

$$H_{\text{High}} = -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\frac{4}{7}$$

$$\log_2(3/7) = \log_2 3 - \log_2 7 = 1.5850 - 2.8074 = -1.2224$$

$$\log_2(4/7) = 2.0000 - 2.8074 = -0.8074$$

$$H_{\text{High}} = 0.4286(1.2224) + 0.5714(0.8074) = 0.5239 + 0.4613 = 0.9852$$

$$H_{\text{Normal}} = -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7}$$

$$\log_2(6/7) = 2.5850 - 2.8074 = -0.2224$$

$$\log_2(1/7) = -2.8074$$

$$H_{\text{Normal}} = 0.8571(0.2224) + 0.1429(2.8074) = 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917$$

**Weighted average:**

$$H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) = 0.4926 + 0.2959 = 0.7885$$

$$IG(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = \boxed{0.1518}$$

---

#### 2D. Information Gain for `Wind`

**Subsets:**
- `Weak` → {D1, D3, D4, D5, D8, D9, D10, D13} → Yes=6, No=2
- `Strong` → {D2, D6, D7, D11, D12, D14} → Yes=3, No=3

$$H_{\text{Weak}} = -\frac{6}{8}\log_2\frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8}\log_2\frac{2}{8}$$

$$= -0.75 \cdot \log_2 0.75 - 0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 = 0.75(0.4150) + 0.25(2.0000) = 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113$$

$$H_{\text{Strong}} = -\frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} = 0.5(1) + 0.5(1) = 1.0000$$

**Weighted average:**

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0000) = 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922$$

$$IG(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = \boxed{0.0481}$$

---

### Step 3: Choose the Root Node — Information Gain Comparison

| Feature | Information Gain |
|---------|-----------------|
| **Outlook** | **0.2467** ✅ |
| Humidity | 0.1518 |
| Wind | 0.0481 |
| Temperature | 0.0292 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`Outlook` is chosen as the root node** because it has the **highest Information Gain** (0.2467 bits).

---

### Step 4: Recurse on the Branches

The `Overcast` branch is **pure** (all Yes) → leaf node = **Yes** ✓

For `Sunny` branch ({D1, D2, D8, D9, D11}, Yes=2, No=3):

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 0.9710$$

Compute IG for remaining features (Humidity, Wind, Temperature) on this subset. Continuing this recursively builds the complete tree.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Gini Index Computation

For a node with class distribution $(p_0, p_1)$:

$$\text{Gini} = 1 - p_0^2 - p_1^2$$

**Numerical example:** Node with 30 Yes and 70 No:

$$\text{Gini} = 1 - (0.3)^2 - (0.7)^2 = 1 - 0.09 - 0.49 = 0.42$$

For a binary split producing child nodes with 30 samples (20 Yes, 10 No) and 70 samples (10 Yes, 60 No):

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{parent}} = 1 - 0.3^2 - 0.7^2 = 0.42$$

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{left}} = 1 - \left(\frac{20}{30}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{10}{30}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.4444 - 0.1111 = 0.4444$$

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{right}} = 1 - \left(\frac{10}{70}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{60}{70}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.0204 - 0.7347 = 0.2449$$

$$\text{Weighted Gini after split} = \frac{30}{100}(0.4444) + \frac{70}{100}(0.2449) = 0.1333 + 0.1714 = 0.3048$$

$$\text{Gini Gain} = 0.42 - 0.3048 = \boxed{0.1152}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Random Forest OOB Error

Suppose we have $N=10$ training samples and $B=3$ trees. The bootstrap samples and OOB samples are:

| Tree | Bootstrap Sample (in-bag) | OOB Samples |
|------|---------------------------|-------------|
| T1   | {1, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10} (size 8) | {2, 6} |
| T2   | {2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10} | {1, 5} |
| T3   | {1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10} | {4, 7} |

For each sample $i$, collect predictions from trees for which it was OOB:

- Sample 1: predicted by T2, T3 → majority vote
- Sample 2: predicted by T1, T3 → majority vote
- ... and so on.

The OOB error is then:

$$\text{OOB Error} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_{\text{OOB},i}\right)$$

This gives an **unbiased** estimate of generalization error.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Decision Tree | Random Forest |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Bias** | Low | Slightly higher |
| **Variance** | **High** | **Low** (averaging reduces it) |
| **Overfitting** | Prone | Resistant |
| **Interpretability** | **High** (white-box) | Low (black-box ensemble) |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Slower (B trees) |
| **Prediction Speed** | Fast | Slower (B forward passes) |
| **Handles Missing Data** | Limited | Better (proximity) |
| **Feature Importance** | Yes | Yes (MDI, MDA) |
| **Hyperparameters** | depth, min_samples | n_estimators, max_features |
| **Tuning Effort** | Low | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.3 Decision Tree Algorithm Pseudocode (Hunt's Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Function BuildTree S, Features"]
    s2["If all samples in S have same class: return Leaf class"]
    s3["If Features is empty or stopping criterion met: return Leaf majority class"]
    s4["For each feature f in Features: compute impurity reduction IG or Gini"]
    s5["Select best feature f_star with max impurity reduction"]
    s6["Partition S into S1, S2, ..., Sk on f_star"]
    s7["Recurse: BuildTree S1, Features minus f_star"]
    s8["Recurse: BuildTree S2, Features minus f_star"]
    s9["Return Internal node test on f_star with children"]
    s1 --> s2
    s2 -->|No| s3
    s2 -->|Yes| s4
    s3 -->|No| s4
    s3 -->|Yes| s5
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s6 --> s8
    s7 --> s9
    s8 --> s9
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Decision Tree. Explain the role of entropy in decision tree construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Decision Tree** is a tree-structured supervised learning model used for classification and regression. Each internal node tests a feature, each branch represents a test outcome, and each leaf node represents a class label or predicted value.

**Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures the impurity (disorder) of a node. A pure node has $H=0$; a 50-50 binary split has $H=1$.

**Role in DT construction:** At each node, the algorithm selects the feature that **maximizes Information Gain** $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$, i.e., the feature that **most reduces entropy** after the split. This greedy choice yields the most informative first question (root node).

**(1 mark)** — definition of decision tree
**(1 mark)** — entropy formula with intuition
**(1 mark)** — IG and how it drives split selection

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Bagging and Random Forest.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bagging | Random Forest |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Feature subset per split** | Considers **all** $d$ features | Considers **random subset** of $m < d$ features |
| **Tree correlation** | **High** (same strong features at root) | **Low** (randomization decorrelates) |
| **Variance reduction** | Moderate | Superior |
| **Default base learner** | Any (often unpruned DT) | Unpruned decision trees |
| **Hyperparameter $m$** | $m = d$ (full) | $m = \sqrt{d}$ (class.), $d/3$ (reg.) |

**(1 mark)** — Bagging definition
**(1 mark)** — RF definition with feature randomization
**(1 mark)** — Correct differentiation (table or key points)

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** for building a decision tree. Describe the **Information Gain** criterion with a suitable formula. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Consider the following dataset. Compute the **Entropy**, **Information Gain for each attribute**, and identify the **root node** attribute. *(7 marks)*

| Day | Outlook | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|----------|------|------|
| 1   | Sunny   | High     | Weak | No   |
| 2   | Sunny   | High     | Strong | No |
| 3   | Overcast| High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 4   | Rain    | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| 5   | Rain    | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| 6   | Rain    | High     | Strong | No |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — ID3 Algorithm (7 marks)

**ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy, top-down decision tree induction algorithm developed by Ross Quinlan (1986). It uses **Information Gain** as the splitting criterion.

**Algorithm steps (write in exam):**

1. Compute the entropy $H(S)$ of the current node $S$.
2. For each attribute $A$, compute the weighted average entropy after splitting on $A$.
3. Compute $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ for every $A$.
4. Choose the attribute $A^*$ with the **highest IG** as the splitting attribute.
5. Partition the dataset by $A^*$ and recurse on each subset.
6. Stop when:
   - All samples in a node belong to one class (pure), or
   - No more attributes, or
   - Information gain of all candidates is below threshold, or
   - A stopping criterion (max depth, min samples) is met.

**Formulas:**

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Advantages of ID3:** simple, interpretable, handles categorical features.
**Limitations:** biased toward high-cardinality features (solved by C4.5's Gain Ratio), only handles categorical features natively, prone to overfitting (solved by pruning).

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Class Distribution**
Total = 6 samples, **Yes = 3, No = 3**

**Step 2: Entropy of S**

$$H(S) = -\frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6}\log_2\frac{3}{6} = 0.5(1) + 0.5(1) = 1.0000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Information Gain for `Outlook`**

- `Sunny` → {1, 2} → Yes=0, No=2 → $H = 0$
- `Overcast` → {3} → Yes=1, No=0 → $H = 0$
- `Rain` → {4, 5, 6} → Yes=2, No=1 → $H = -\frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3} - \frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3} = 0.6667(0.5850) + 0.3333(1.5850) = 0.9183$

$$H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{2}{6}(0) + \frac{1}{6}(0) + \frac{3}{6}(0.9183) = 0.4592$$

$$IG(S, \text{Outlook}) = 1.0000 - 0.4592 = \boxed{0.5408}$$

**Step 4: Information Gain for `Humidity`**

- `High` → {1, 2, 3, 4, 6} → Yes=2, No=3
- `Normal` → {5} → Yes=1, No=0 → $H = 0$

$$H_{\text{High}} = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} = 0.4(1.3219) + 0.6(0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{5}{6}(0.9710) + \frac{1}{6}(0) = 0.8092$$

$$IG(S, \text{Humidity}) = 1.0000 - 0.8092 = \boxed{0.1908}$$

**Step 5: Information Gain for `Wind`**

- `Weak` → {1, 3, 4, 5} → Yes=3, No=1
- `Strong` → {2, 6} → Yes=0, No=2 → $H = 0$

$$H_{\text{Weak}} = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} = 0.75(0.4150) + 0.25(2.0000) = 0.8113$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{4}{6}(0.8113) + \frac{2}{6}(0) = 0.5409$$

$$IG(S, \text{Wind}) = 1.0000 - 0.5409 = \boxed{0.4591}$$

**Step 6: Comparison & Root Selection**

| Attribute | Information Gain |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **Outlook** | **0.5408** ✅ |
| Wind | 0.4591 |
| Humidity | 0.1908 |

**➡️ Root Node = `Outlook`** (highest IG).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Random Forest** algorithm in detail. Discuss how **bagging** and **random feature selection** help reduce variance and decorrelate trees. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Gini Index** splitting criterion. For a binary classification node with 40 samples (30 in class 0, 10 in class 1) split into two children of sizes 25 and 15 with class distributions (20, 5) and (10, 5) respectively, compute the **parent Gini**, **children Gini**, and the **Gini Gain**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Random Forest Algorithm (7 marks)

**Random Forest** is an ensemble learning algorithm proposed by **Leo Breiman (2001)**. It constructs multiple decision trees and aggregates their predictions to improve accuracy and control overfitting.

**Algorithm:**

**Input:** Training set $S$ of size $N$, number of trees $B$, feature subspace size $m$.

**For $b = 1$ to $B$:**

1. Draw a **bootstrap sample** $S_b$ of size $N$ by sampling from $S$ **with replacement**.
2. Grow an **unpruned decision tree** $T_b$ on $S_b$:
   - At each node, select a **random subset of $m$ features** (from $d$ total) and pick the best split among them.
   - Grow the tree to maximum depth (no pruning).
3. Output the ensemble $\{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_B\}$.

**Prediction:**

- Classification: $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \dots, \hat{y}_B\}$
- Regression: $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{y}_b$

**Role of Bagging:**

Bagging creates **diversity** among trees by training each on a slightly different dataset. Since each bootstrap sample omits ~36.8% of original samples, trees see different data → reduce **variance** through averaging.

**Variance reduction (intuition):**

For an ensemble of $B$ i.i.d. predictors with variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$:

$$\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b\right) = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$$

As $B \to \infty$, the variance approaches $\rho \sigma^2$. So **reducing correlation $\rho$** is the key. This is precisely what **random feature selection** does:

- At each split, only $m = \sqrt{d}$ (classification) or $d/3$ (regression) features are considered.
- A strong predictor no longer dominates every tree → trees become **decorrelated** → lower $\rho$ → lower ensemble variance.

**Additional benefits:**
- Built-in **OOB error** estimation.
- Robust to noise and outliers.
- Handles high-dimensional data well.
- Provides **feature importance** scores.

---

### Part (b) — Gini Index Computation (7 marks)

**Gini Index Definition (2 marks):**

The **Gini Index** for a node with class probabilities $(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_K)$ is:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i^2$$

It measures the probability of misclassification if a label is randomly drawn from the class distribution. Range: $[0, 1 - 1/K]$.

**CART** uses Gini for split selection — the split with the **largest reduction in Gini** (Gini Gain) is chosen.

**Numerical Computation (5 marks):**

**Parent node:** 40 samples, class 0 = 30, class 1 = 10

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{parent}} = 1 - \left(\frac{30}{40}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{10}{40}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.75)^2 - (0.25)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.5625 - 0.0625 = \boxed{0.3750}$$

**Left child (n=25):** class 0 = 20, class 1 = 5

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{left}} = 1 - \left(\frac{20}{25}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{5}{25}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.8)^2 - (0.2)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.64 - 0.04 = 0.3200$$

**Right child (n=15):** class 0 = 10, class 1 = 5

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{right}} = 1 - \left(\frac{10}{15}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{5}{15}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.6667)^2 - (0.3333)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.4444 - 0.1111 = 0.4444$$

**Weighted children Gini:**

$$\text{Gini}_{\text{children}} = \frac{25}{40}(0.3200) + \frac{15}{40}(0.4444)$$

$$= 0.6250 \times 0.3200 + 0.3750 \times 0.4444$$

$$= 0.2000 + 0.1667 = 0.3667$$

**Gini Gain:**

$$\Delta \text{Gini} = \text{Gini}_{\text{parent}} - \text{Gini}_{\text{children}} = 0.3750 - 0.3667 = \boxed{0.0083}$$

**Interpretation:** This split provides only a small reduction in impurity (0.0083), suggesting it is a **weak split** and may not be worthwhile.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't forget the weighted averaging** when computing $H(S\mid A)$ or weighted Gini — students often sum unweighted sub-entropies.
> 2. **Use $\log_2$** for entropy (not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$) — this is the KTU convention for Information Gain in bits.
> 3. **Handle $0 \log 0$** properly: by convention, $0 \log 0 = 0$ (the limit is zero).
> 4. **Do NOT confuse** Gini Gain with Classification Error — error is rarely used for splitting, only for CART pruning.
> 5. **Random Forest ≠ Bagging** — RF adds the second layer of randomization (feature subsets). This is a **favourite KTU trap question**.
> 6. **Always state the units** (bits) and **justify** the root-node choice in 1 line.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Decision Trees & Random Forests

- ✅ **Decision Tree** = hierarchical model; root → internal nodes → leaves. Built **top-down, greedy**.
- ✅ **Entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ → measures **disorder**; **IG** = reduction in entropy after split.
- ✅ **Gini Index** $G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ → used in **CART**; faster than entropy (no log).
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** = IG / SplitInfo → used in **C4.5**; normalizes for high-cardinality features.
- ✅ **ID3** = Information Gain; **C4.5** = Gain Ratio; **CART** = Gini Index + binary splits.
- ✅ **Pre-pruning**: stop early (max_depth, min_samples). **Post-pruning**: grow fully, then prune using cost-complexity $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$.
- ✅ **Random Forest** = Bagging + Random Subspace Method → **decorrelates trees** → lower variance.
- ✅ **OOB Error** ≈ 36.8% samples held out per tree → unbiased test error estimate.
- ✅ **Random subspace size**: $m = \sqrt{d}$ (classification), $m = d/3$ (regression).
- ✅ **Feature Importance**: MDI (impurity-based) and MDA (permutation-based).
- ✅ **DT = low bias, high variance**; **RF = low bias, low variance**.
- ✅ KTU exam loves: IG computation, Gini calculation, root node selection, ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART comparison, Bagging vs RF distinction, OOB error explanation.
- ✅ Mention **interpretability** advantage of DT and **variance reduction** advantage of RF in every comparison answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"High IG wins the root."** 🏆
> - **"Bagging = bootstrap, Forest = bootstrap + feature random."** 🌳
> - **"OOB is the free cross-validation of Random Forest."** 🎁

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods: Boosting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods: Boosting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boosting** is a sequential **ensemble learning** technique that combines a set of *weak learners* (models that perform only slightly better than random guessing) into a single *strong learner* by training each new model specifically to correct the errors made by its predecessors. Every successive learner focuses more (through re-weighting or residual fitting) on the data points that previous learners misclassified.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, boosting is treated as a **bias-reduction** strategy (as opposed to bagging, which is primarily a *variance-reduction* strategy). The two canonical instantiations a student **must** master are:

1. **AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)** — re-weights misclassified instances.
2. **Gradient Boosting** — fits new learners to the *negative gradient* of the loss (residuals).

Key symbolic building blocks used throughout the syllabus:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $h_t(x)$ | Weak hypothesis produced at round $t$ |
| $w_i^{(t)}$ | Weight assigned to training instance $i$ at round $t$ |
| $\varepsilon_t$ | Weighted training error of $h_t$ |
| $\alpha_t$ | Confidence / voting power assigned to $h_t$ |
| $H(x)$ | Final boosted strong classifier |
| $F_M(x)$ | Final boosted regression function after $M$ rounds |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Panel of Tutors"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a student preparing for an exam by consulting a *panel of tutors*, one after another. After every session, the student marks the *questions the previous tutor got wrong* in red ink. The next tutor is forced to spend **more time on the red questions** and less on the easy ones. By the end, even though every individual tutor is "just average" (a weak learner), the *combined wisdom* (the strong learner) crushes the exam.

This is exactly what boosting does with data points:
- The "red ink" = higher sample weights $w_i$.
- Each "tutor" = one weak learner $h_t$.
- The "combined wisdom" = weighted majority vote $H(x)$.

## 1.3 Why Boosting Works — The Bias View

Bagging reduces *variance* by averaging parallel, decorrelated models. Boosting reduces *bias* by **sequentially chasing the residual error** of the ensemble so far. Because each learner is forced to look where the ensemble is weak, the **combined decision boundary** becomes more expressive and tightly fits (sometimes over-fits) the data.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for clearly distinguishing **Bagging = parallel + variance ↓** from **Boosting = sequential + bias ↓**. Confusing the two is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Boosting Pipeline (Operational Steps)

1. **Initialize** uniform sample weights: $w_i^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$.
2. **For** $t = 1, 2, \dots, M$ **do**:
   - Train weak learner $h_t(x)$ on the *weighted* training set $\{(x_i, y_i, w_i^{(t)})\}$.
   - Compute its *weighted* error $\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i : h_t(x_i) \ne y_i} w_i^{(t)}$.
   - Compute the learner's voting weight $\alpha_t$.
   - Update every instance weight so that *misclassified* points gain mass.
   - Re-normalize weights so they sum to **1**.
3. **Output** the strong classifier $H(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{M}\alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)$.

The "**why**" of each step:
- Step 1 → no prior bias; every point starts equally important.
- Step 2a → each learner specializes on a weighted view of data.
- Step 2b → measuring *weighted* misclassification tells us where the ensemble is currently weak.
- Step 2c → better learners earn higher $\alpha_t$ (more voting power).
- Step 2d-e → amplifies hard examples so the next learner is forced to focus on them.
- Step 3 → weighted majority vote of all weak learners.

## 2.2 AdaBoost (Discrete / SAMME for binary case)

AdaBoost is the **textbook** boosting algorithm in the KTU syllabus. For binary labels $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

### 2.2.1 Weighted Error

$$
\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[h_t(x_i) \ne y_i\right]
$$

The condition $\varepsilon_t < 0.5$ **must** hold; otherwise $\alpha_t$ becomes non-positive and boosting collapses.

### 2.2.2 Learner Confidence $\alpha_t$

$$
\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)
$$

Interpretation: when $\varepsilon_t \to 0$, $\alpha_t \to +\infty$ (the learner is nearly perfect and gets enormous say). When $\varepsilon_t \to 0.5$, $\alpha_t \to 0$ (the learner is no better than random — ignore it).

### 2.2.3 Weight Update Rule

$$
w_i^{(t+1)} \;=\; \frac{w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\big)}{Z_t}
$$

where $Z_t$ is the normalization constant:

$$
Z_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\big)
$$

The factor $\exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t)$ produces two distinct cases:

| Case | $y_i h_t(x_i)$ | Multiplier | Effect on weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Correctly classified | $+1$ | $e^{-\alpha_t} < 1$ | Weight **shrinks** |
| Misclassified | $-1$ | $e^{+\alpha_t} > 1$ | Weight **grows** |

### 2.2.4 Final Strong Classifier

$$
H(x) \;=\; \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{M}\alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)
$$

The **training-error bound** (famous Schapire–Singer result) is:

$$
\mathrm{err}_{\mathrm{train}}(H) \;\le\; \prod_{t=1}^{M} Z_t \;=\; \prod_{t=1}^{M} 2\sqrt{\varepsilon_t(1-\varepsilon_t)}
$$

If every $\varepsilon_t \le 0.5 - \gamma$, training error drops **exponentially** with $M$.

## 2.3 Gradient Boosting — The Modern Generalization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Gradient Boosting recasts boosting as **gradient descent in function space**. Instead of tweaking sample weights, each new learner is fit to the *negative gradient* (residuals) of a chosen loss function $L(y, F(x))$.

### 2.3.1 Additive Model

$$
F_M(x) \;=\; F_0(x) \;+\; \sum_{m=1}^{M}\gamma_m \, h_m(x)
$$

with constant initialization $F_0(x) = \arg\min_{\gamma}\sum_i L(y_i, \gamma)$.

### 2.3.2 Negative-Gradient (Pseudo-Residual) Target

$$
r_{i,m} \;=\; -\!\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}}
$$

For **squared-error loss** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - F)^2$, the pseudo-residual collapses to the ordinary residual:

$$
r_{i,m} \;=\; y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)
$$

### 2.3.3 Line Search for Step Size

$$
\gamma_m \;=\; \arg\min_{\gamma}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L\!\left(y_i,\; F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma \, h_m(x_i)\right)
$$

### 2.3.4 Shrinkage (Learning Rate) Regularization

$$
F_m(x) \;=\; F_{m-1}(x) \;+\; \nu \,\gamma_m \, h_m(x), \quad 0 < \nu \le 1
$$

Smaller $\nu$ → slower learning → better generalization → need more trees $M$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Concept | Equation | Where Used | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial weights | $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$ | AdaBoost step 1 | Must be uniform |
| Weighted error | $\varepsilon_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)}\mathbb{1}[h_t(x_i)\ne y_i]$ | AdaBoost | Must be $<0.5$ |
| Confidence | $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$ | AdaBoost | Flip sign for SAMME multi-class |
| Weight update (raw) | $w_i^{(t)} e^{-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)}$ | AdaBoost | Forget $-y_i h_t$ → 0 |
| Normalizer | $Z_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} e^{-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)}$ | AdaBoost | Always include |
| Strong classifier | $H(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\left(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$ | AdaBoost | Use sign only for binary |
| Pseudo-residual | $r_{im} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$ (L2) | Gradient Boost | Only true for L2 loss |
| Update | $F_m = F_{m-1} + \nu \gamma_m h_m$ | Gradient Boost | $\nu$ is *shrinkage* |
| Bound | $\mathrm{err}\le\prod_t 2\sqrt{\varepsilon_t(1-\varepsilon_t)}$ | Theory | Stops being tight with noise |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fraud detection** in banking (XGBoost on transactional features).
- **Medical diagnosis** (boosted decision trees on tabular clinical data).
- **Click-through-rate prediction** in ad-tech.
- **Ranking** (LambdaMART = Gradient Boosting with NDCG loss, used in search engines).
- **Bioinformatics** — gene-expression classification.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 module outcomes CO2 & CO3 directly map to *understanding* and *applying* ensemble techniques. Be prepared to justify the choice of boosting over bagging in a low-bias, high-variance regime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$?

We want to choose $\alpha_t$ to **minimize the upper bound on the training error** $ \prod_{t=1}^{M} Z_t$, which means minimizing each $Z_t$ individually.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_t &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\big) \\
&= \sum_{i : y_i = h_t(x_i)} w_i^{(t)} e^{-\alpha_t} \;+\; \sum_{i : y_i \ne h_t(x_i)} w_i^{(t)} e^{+\alpha_t} \\
&= (1-\varepsilon_t)\,e^{-\alpha_t} \;+\; \varepsilon_t\,e^{+\alpha_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take the derivative with respect to $\alpha_t$ and set it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dZ_t}{d\alpha_t} &= -(1-\varepsilon_t)e^{-\alpha_t} + \varepsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t} \;=\; 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_t e^{\alpha_t} &= (1-\varepsilon_t)e^{-\alpha_t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad e^{2\alpha_t} &= \frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \alpha_t &= \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the formula you write in the exam. The derivation earns the **"Understand" / "Apply"** marks under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 3.2 Full Numerical Walk-Through — AdaBoost on 5 Points

### 3.2.1 The Dataset

| Index $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $+1$ |
| 2 | 2 | $+1$ |
| 3 | 3 | $-1$ |
| 4 | 4 | $-1$ |
| 5 | 5 | $+1$ |

### 3.2.2 Initialization

$$
w_i^{(1)} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \;=\; \frac{1}{5} \;=\; 0.2 \quad \forall i
$$

### 3.2.3 Round 1 ($t = 1$)

Choose a *threshold stump* — the simplest weak learner:
$$
h_1(x) = \begin{cases} +1, & x \le 2.5 \\ -1, & x > 2.5 \end{cases}
$$

Predictions: $\hat{y} = [+1, +1, -1, -1, -1]$. Compare with $y = [+1, +1, -1, -1, +1]$.

Misclassified points: only $i = 5$.

$$
\varepsilon_1 \;=\; \sum_{i: \text{miscl}} w_i^{(1)} \;=\; 0.2
$$

Learner confidence:

$$
\alpha_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-0.2}{0.2}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\ln(4) \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.3863 \;=\; 0.6931
$$

Raw updated weights (before normalization):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_i^{(2)} &\propto w_i^{(1)} \exp\!\big(-\alpha_1 y_i h_1(x_i)\big) \\
\text{Correct } (i=1,2,3,4):\quad & 0.2 \times e^{-0.6931} = 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.1 \\
\text{Wrong   } (i=5):\quad & 0.2 \times e^{+0.6931} = 0.2 \times 2.0 = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalization constant:

$$
Z_1 \;=\; 4(0.1) + 0.4 \;=\; 0.8
$$

Normalized weights after round 1:

$$
w^{(2)} \;=\; \left[0.125,\; 0.125,\; 0.125,\; 0.125,\; 0.500\right]
$$

Notice that the misclassified point's weight has jumped from $0.2$ to $0.5$ — the next learner will be forced to **focus on it**.

### 3.2.4 Round 2 ($t = 2$)

Train another stump on the re-weighted data. A reasonable choice:

$$
h_2(x) = \begin{cases} -1, & x < 4.5 \\ +1, & x \ge 4.5 \end{cases}
$$

Predictions: $\hat{y} = [-1, -1, -1, -1, +1]$. Compare with $y = [+1, +1, -1, -1, +1]$.

Misclassified: $i = 1, 2$.

$$
\varepsilon_2 \;=\; w_1^{(2)} + w_2^{(2)} \;=\; 0.125 + 0.125 \;=\; 0.25
$$

$$
\alpha_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.75}{0.25}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\ln(3) \;=\; 0.5493
$$

### 3.2.5 Final Strong Classifier (after 2 rounds)

$$
H(x) \;=\; \mathrm{sign}\!\Big(\alpha_1 h_1(x) + \alpha_2 h_2(x)\Big) \;=\; \mathrm{sign}\!\big(0.6931\,h_1(x) + 0.5493\,h_2(x)\big)
$$

Evaluate on every $x_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x=1: &\;\; 0.6931(+1) + 0.5493(-1) = +0.1438 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat y = +1 \quad \checkmark \\
x=2: &\;\; 0.6931(+1) + 0.5493(-1) = +0.1438 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat y = +1 \quad \checkmark \\
x=3: &\;\; 0.6931(-1) + 0.5493(-1) = -1.2424 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat y = -1 \quad \checkmark \\
x=4: &\;\; 0.6931(-1) + 0.5493(-1) = -1.2424 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat y = -1 \quad \checkmark \\
x=5: &\;\; 0.6931(-1) + 0.5493(+1) = -0.1438 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat y = -1 \quad \mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Training accuracy after 2 rounds = **80%**. A third round would push point 5 the right way. This walk-through is the *exact* style KTU wants for a 14-marker.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bagging vs Boosting

| Dimension | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| Training style | Parallel | Sequential |
| Primary effect | Variance ↓ | Bias ↓ |
| Weight on instances | Equal for all trees | Updated each round |
| Best weak learner | High-variance, low-bias (deep tree) | Low-variance, high-bias (stump) |
| Over-fitting risk | Low | High if $M$ too large |
| Final combiner | Equal-weight average / vote | Weighted vote by $\alpha_t$ |
| Famous algorithm | Random Forest | AdaBoost, GBM, XGBoost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AdaBoost End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data X Y] --> B[Initialize Uniform Weights w1 equals 1 over N]
    B --> C[Train Weak Learner h1]
    C --> D[Compute Weighted Error epsilon1]
    D --> E[Compute Confidence alpha1 equals half ln of 1 minus epsilon over epsilon]
    E --> F[Update Weights w2 using exp minus alpha y h]
    F --> G{Stop Condition Met}
    G -- No --> H[Train Next Weak Learner ht]
    H --> I[Compute epsilon t and alpha t]
    I --> J[Update Weights w t plus 1]
    J --> G
    G -- Yes --> K[Final Strong Classifier H of x equals sign of sum alpha t h t x]
```

## 4.2 Gradient Boosting — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Model F0 equals constant] --> B[Compute Pseudo Residuals r1]
    B --> C[Fit Weak Learner h1 to r1]
    C --> D[Line Search gamma1]
    D --> E[Update F1 equals F0 plus nu gamma1 h1]
    E --> F[Compute Residuals r2]
    F --> G[Fit Learner h2 to r2]
    G --> H[Update F2 equals F1 plus nu gamma2 h2]
    H --> I{Iteration less than M}
    I -- Yes --> F
    I -- No --> J[Output FM as Final Strong Predictor]
```

## 4.3 Data-Weight Evolution Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Round 1 All Weights Equal 0.2] --> B[Identify Misclassified Point]
    B --> C[Boost its Weight to 0.5]
    C --> D[Shrink Correct Weights to 0.125]
    D --> E[Round 2 Learner Focuses on Boosted Point]
    E --> F[New Errors Get Boosted Further]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** *Differentiate between Bagging and Boosting. Which one primarily reduces bias?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Bagging** trains multiple models **in parallel** on bootstrap samples and combines them by **equal-weight voting/averaging** — it primarily reduces **variance** *(1 mark)*.
> - **Boosting** trains weak learners **sequentially**, with each new learner focusing on the errors of the previous ones, combined by **confidence-weighted vote** — it primarily reduces **bias** *(1 mark)*.
> - Therefore, **Boosting is the bias-reducing technique** *(1 mark)*.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** *State the AdaBoost weight-update rule and explain the role of the normalizer $Z_t$.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - The weight-update rule is $w_i^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\big)}{Z_t}$ *(1 mark)*.
> - The exponential factor is $e^{-\alpha_t}$ for correctly classified points and $e^{+\alpha_t}$ for misclassified ones, so **misclassified points receive larger weights** in the next round *(1 mark)*.
> - $Z_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t)$ rescales weights so they form a valid probability distribution summing to **1** *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive (Module-Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Derive the AdaBoost expression for the confidence parameter $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$ from the minimization of the normalization constant $Z_t$. Show all algebraic steps. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Given the dataset $\{(1,+1),(2,+1),(3,-1),(4,-1),(5,+1)\}$, perform **two complete rounds** of AdaBoost using decision stumps. Show weight initialization, error calculation, $\alpha_t$ computation, and weight updates at each round. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Write the normalization constant separating correctly and incorrectly classified points:

$$
Z_t = (1-\varepsilon_t)\,e^{-\alpha_t} + \varepsilon_t\,e^{+\alpha_t}
$$

*(1 mark for setup)*. Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha_t$:

$$
\frac{dZ_t}{d\alpha_t} = -(1-\varepsilon_t)e^{-\alpha_t} + \varepsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t}
$$

*(1 mark)*. Set to zero and solve:

$$
\varepsilon_t e^{\alpha_t} = (1-\varepsilon_t)e^{-\alpha_t} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{2\alpha_t} = \frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}
$$

*(2 marks)*. Take natural log:

$$
2\alpha_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)
$$

*(2 marks)*. State the condition $\varepsilon_t < 0.5$ to ensure $\alpha_t > 0$ *(1 mark)*.

**(b) Numerical Rounds (7 marks):**

- Initial weights $w_i^{(1)} = 0.2$ *(1 mark)*.
- Round 1: $h_1$ is $x \le 2.5 \Rightarrow +1$, gives $\varepsilon_1 = 0.2$, $\alpha_1 = 0.693$ *(2 marks)*.
- Update weights to $[0.125, 0.125, 0.125, 0.125, 0.5]$ after normalization by $Z_1 = 0.8$ *(2 marks)*.
- Round 2: $h_2$ is $x \ge 4.5 \Rightarrow +1$, gives $\varepsilon_2 = 0.25$, $\alpha_2 = 0.549$ *(2 marks)*.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Gradient Boosting** as gradient descent in function space. Define pseudo-residuals and derive the update rule for squared-error loss. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare Bagging and Boosting along **five dimensions** (training style, primary reduction, instance weighting, over-fitting risk, and a typical algorithm). State the Schapire bound on the training error of AdaBoost. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gradient Boosting (7 marks):**

- Boosting can be viewed as **empirical risk minimization in function space** using steepest descent *(1 mark)*.
- The ensemble $F_M(x) = F_0 + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \gamma_m h_m(x)$ is built by **stage-wise** additive expansion *(1 mark)*.
- The negative gradient of loss $L$ w.r.t. current prediction gives the pseudo-residual:

$$
r_{im} = -\left.\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right|_{F = F_{m-1}}
$$

*(2 marks)*. For squared error $L = \tfrac{1}{2}(y-F)^2$, the derivative gives $r_{im} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$, i.e., the **ordinary residual** *(2 marks)*. The new learner $h_m$ is fit to these residuals, and $F_m = F_{m-1} + \nu \gamma_m h_m$ with shrinkage $\nu$ *(1 mark)*.

**(b) Comparison and Bound (7 marks):**

- Five-dimension comparison table *(5 marks — 1 mark per correct, well-explained dimension)*.
- Schapire bound: $\mathrm{err}_{\mathrm{train}}(H) \le \prod_{t=1}^{M} 2\sqrt{\varepsilon_t(1-\varepsilon_t)}$ *(1 mark)*.
- Note that if every $\varepsilon_t \le 0.5 - \gamma$, the error drops **exponentially** in $M$ *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Boosting questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $Z_t$** in the weight-update equation — *−1 mark per occurrence*.
> 2. **Confusing $-y_i h_t$ with $-(y_i + h_t)$** in the exponent — *−1 mark*.
> 3. **Using average error instead of weighted error** for $\varepsilon_t$ — *−2 marks*; this destroys the entire algorithm logic.
> 4. **Stating "Boosting reduces variance"** — examiners reject this outright; boosting reduces **bias**.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps** in part (a) of a 14-marker — even if the final formula is right, you lose process marks.
> 6. **Not normalizing weights** — the final $H(x)$ formula breaks down.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Boosting = sequential ensemble + bias reduction** (Bagging = parallel + variance reduction).
- **Weak learner** must be *slightly* better than random ($\varepsilon_t < 0.5$).
- **AdaBoost initialization:** $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$.
- **Weighted error:** $\varepsilon_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \mathbb{1}[h_t(x_i) \ne y_i]$.
- **Confidence:** $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln[(1-\varepsilon_t)/\varepsilon_t]$ — derived by minimizing $Z_t$.
- **Weight update (with normalizer):** $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))/Z_t$.
- **Final classifier:** $H(x) = \mathrm{sign}\!\big(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)\big)$.
- **Schapire bound:** $\mathrm{err} \le \prod_t 2\sqrt{\varepsilon_t(1-\varepsilon_t)}$ — exponential decay.
- **Gradient Boosting = gradient descent in function space**; pseudo-residuals = $-\partial L/\partial F$.
- **Squared loss pseudo-residual** = ordinary residual $y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$.
- **Shrinkage** $\nu$ is a regularization hyper-parameter (typical $\nu = 0.01$ to $0.1$).
- **Famous Gradient-Boosted families:** GBM, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost.
- **Over-fitting guard:** cap $M$, use shrinkage, use subsample fraction, use shallow trees.
- **Bagging vs Boosting comparison** is a *guaranteed* 7-marker every KTU cycle — memorize the matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging (Bootstrap AGGregatING)** is an *ensemble meta-algorithm* introduced by **Leo Breiman (1996)** designed to improve the stability and accuracy of machine learning algorithms by combining the predictions of multiple base models trained on different bootstrap samples of the same training dataset.

Formally, given a training set $D$ of size $N$, Bagging generates $B$ new training sets $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_B$ by sampling from $D$ **uniformly and with replacement**. Each $D_b$ has the same size $N$ as the original dataset. A base learner (e.g., Decision Tree) is then trained on each $D_b$, and the final prediction is obtained through an aggregation function:

$$
\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x) \quad \text{(Regression)}
$$

$$
\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{I}\!\left(\hat{f}_b(x) = y\right) \quad \text{(Classification - Majority Voting)}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Jury of Doctors"**
> Imagine you have a rare medical condition and visit **one doctor**. That single doctor might misdiagnose you. Now imagine you visit **100 doctors independently**, each reviewing your reports with their own slight bias. You then take a **majority vote** of their diagnoses. The collective opinion is far more reliable than any individual. That is Bagging — instead of relying on one model (one doctor), it trains many models on slightly different versions of the data and "averages out" their individual errors.

> Each model sees a *slightly different world* (because each bootstrap sample is different), so they make *different mistakes*. When combined, these independent mistakes cancel out, and the **common correct signal** is amplified.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Bootstrap Sample** | A dataset of size $N$ drawn *with replacement* from the original training set of size $N$. |
| **Base Learner** | The underlying model (e.g., unpruned Decision Tree) trained on each bootstrap sample. |
| **Aggregation** | The strategy used to combine predictions (averaging for regression, voting for classification). |
| **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Samples** | The ~**36.8%** of training instances *not* selected in a particular bootstrap sample. |
| **Ensemble** | A composite model whose predictions are the aggregate of multiple base models. |
| **Variance Reduction** | Bagging's primary effect — it reduces model variance without substantially increasing bias. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The Bagging algorithm executes in **four principal stages**:

1. **Bootstrap Sampling Phase**
   - From the original dataset $D$ of size $N$, draw $B$ independent bootstrap samples $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_B$.
   - Each sample $D_b$ is created by sampling $N$ instances *with replacement*.
   - Because sampling is with replacement, on average **~63.2%** of the original instances appear at least once in $D_b$; the remaining **~36.8%** are *Out-of-Bag* (OOB).

2. **Parallel Base-Model Training Phase**
   - A base learner (typically an unpruned Decision Tree, since trees are *high-variance low-bias* learners) is trained on each $D_b$, producing predictors $\hat{f}_1, \hat{f}_2, \dots, \hat{f}_B$.
   - Training is *independent* across samples, making Bagging naturally **parallelizable**.

3. **Aggregation Phase**
   - For **regression** problems: take the arithmetic mean of all $\hat{f}_b(x)$ predictions.
   - For **classification** problems: take the *majority vote* (mode of class labels).

4. **OOB Error Estimation (Validation)**
   - For each training instance $x_i$, predict using only the models for which $x_i$ was OOB.
   - This gives an unbiased estimate of the test error **without needing a separate validation set**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Bagging — Bias-Variance Lens

- **Decision Trees (unpruned)** have **low bias** but **high variance** — small changes in data produce very different trees.
- Bagging keeps the **bias approximately the same** (since averaging does not change the expected value of unbiased estimators significantly) but **drastically reduces variance**.
- This is the *fundamental reason* Bagging is most effective with **unstable learners** (Trees, Neural Nets) and *not useful* with stable learners (k-NN, Naive Bayes).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 — Memorize These**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Probability a sample is selected in one draw | $p = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Uniform sampling probability. |
| Probability a sample is **not** selected in $N$ draws | $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N} \;\xrightarrow{N \to \infty}\; \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | Fraction of OOB samples. |
| Expected unique samples in a bootstrap sample | $N \cdot \left(1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N}\right) \approx 0.632N$ | ~63.2% of unique instances. |
| Bagged Prediction (Regression) | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{B}\displaystyle\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x)$ | Arithmetic mean. |
| Bagged Prediction (Classification) | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_{y} \displaystyle\sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{I}\!\left(\hat{f}_b(x) = y\right)$ | Majority vote. |
| Variance of Bagged Mean | $\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \rho \sigma^{2} + \dfrac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^{2}$ | $\rho$ = pairwise correlation of base learners. |
| Variance Reduction Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}})}{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_b)} = \rho + \dfrac{1-\rho}{B}$ | Approaches $\rho$ as $B \to \infty$. |
| OOB Error Estimate | $\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\!\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{\,\text{OOB}}\right)$ | Unbiased test error estimate. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Bagging is the foundation of high-stakes ML systems — **Random Forests** (used in medical diagnosis, credit scoring, fraud detection) and **Extra Trees** are direct extensions of Bagging applied to decision trees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Averaging Reduces Variance (The Heart of Bagging)

We need to show mathematically why $\text{Var}(\text{mean of estimators}) \le \text{Var}(\text{single estimator})$.

**Step 1 — Setup the Aggregation**

Let the $B$ base learners trained on bootstrap samples produce predictions $\hat{f}_1(x), \hat{f}_2(x), \dots, \hat{f}_B(x)$ for a fixed input $x$. The bagged prediction is:

$$
\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Variance of the Bagged Estimator**

Applying the variance operator to a sum:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \sum_{b'=1}^{B} \text{Cov}\!\left(\hat{f}_b, \hat{f}_{b'}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose Diagonal and Off-Diagonal Terms**

The double sum has $B$ diagonal terms (where $b = b'$) and $B^{2} - B$ off-diagonal terms (where $b \ne b'$):

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ B \cdot \text{Var}(\hat{f}_b) + \sum_{b \neq b'} \text{Cov}(\hat{f}_b, \hat{f}_{b'}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Assume Identical Marginal Variance and Pairwise Correlation $\rho$**

Since each $\hat{f}_b$ is trained on a different bootstrap sample, they are *identically distributed* with variance $\sigma^{2}$. The covariance between any two distinct learners is $\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_b, \hat{f}_{b'}) = \rho \sigma^{2}$, where $\rho$ is the **pairwise correlation coefficient**.

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ B \sigma^{2} + (B^{2} - B) \rho \sigma^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Expression**

Factor out $\sigma^{2}$ and simplify:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{B^{2}} \left[ B + B^{2}\rho - B\rho \right] = \sigma^{2} \left[ \frac{1}{B} + \rho - \frac{\rho}{B} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \rho \sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{B}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Result**

- As $B \to \infty$, the second term vanishes and $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) \to \rho \sigma^{2}$.
- If learners were **independent** ($\rho = 0$), variance would shrink to $\sigma^{2}/B$ — a $B$-fold reduction.
- In practice, $\rho > 0$ (since all learners train on overlapping data), so the reduction is *less* than $B$-fold but still substantial.
- **Bias remains unchanged** because $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_b]$ (averaging unbiased estimators yields an unbiased estimator).
