## 3.2 Derivation: Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error Estimate

**Step 1 — Probability a Sample is OOB for a Given Bootstrap**

For a given sample $x_i$, the probability it is *not* chosen in a single draw from $N$ samples is $(1 - 1/N)$. Since $D_b$ has $N$ draws, the probability $x_i$ is never drawn is:

$$
P(\text{OOB}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Limit as $N \to \infty$**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679
$$

So approximately **36.8%** of training samples are OOB for any given $D_b$.

**Step 3 — Construct the OOB Prediction**

For each $x_i$, identify the subset of base learners $S_i = \{ b : x_i \notin D_b \}$. Predict using only these:

$$
\hat{y}_i^{\,\text{OOB}} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{b \in S_i} \mathbb{I}\!\left(\hat{f}_b(x_i) = y\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the OOB Error**

$$
\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\!\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{\,\text{OOB}}\right)
$$

This estimate is **asymptotically equivalent to Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)** but computationally far cheaper.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bagging vs Boosting vs Single Model

| Property | Single Tree | Bagging | Boosting (AdaBoost) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bias** | Low | Low (~same) | Very Low |
| **Variance** | High | **Reduced** | May Increase |
| **Base Learner** | Deep Tree | Deep Tree | Shallow Stump |
| **Sampling** | Single set | Bootstrap | Reweighted |
| **Training** | Single pass | Parallel | Sequential |
| **Weights on Models** | N/A | Equal | Unequal (by accuracy) |
| **Focus on Outliers** | None | None | **High** |
| **Overfitting Risk** | High | Low | Moderate-High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Bagging Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Training Dataset D, size N] --> B[For b = 1 to B, do]
    B --> C[Draw Bootstrap Sample Db: Sample N instances with replacement from D]
    C --> D[Train Base Learner fb on Db, e.g., Unpruned Decision Tree]
    D --> E[Store Trained Model fb in Ensemble Pool]
    E --> F{More iterations?}
    F -- Yes --> B
    F -- No --> G[Ensemble of B Models: f1, f2, ..., fB]
    G --> H{Problem Type?}
    H -- Regression --> I[Average Predictions: fbag = mean of fb]
    H -- Classification --> J[Majority Voting: fbag = mode of fb]
    I --> K[Final Aggregated Prediction]
    J --> K
    C -. OOB Samples .-> L[OOB Samples: ~36.8% not in Db]
    L --> M[Compute OOB Error Estimate]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — OOB Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Training Instance xi] --> B{Is xi in Bootstrap Sample Db?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Skip - xi was in-sample for fb]
    B -- No --> D[Use fb to predict xi]
    D --> E[Aggregate OOB Predictions across all b where xi was OOB]
    E --> F[Compute OOB Error: Compare with true yi]
    F --> G[Final OOB Error Estimate ~= Test Error]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The first flowchart shows the *training-time ensemble construction* — note the parallel branches and the central aggregation hub. The second flowchart illustrates the *OOB validation mechanism* — a free "bonus" test set that emerges naturally from bootstrap sampling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Bagging. List any two advantages of Bagging. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) is an ensemble learning technique proposed by Leo Breiman (1996) that combines the predictions of multiple base models, each trained on a different bootstrap sample (sampling with replacement) of the original training set. The final prediction is made by averaging (regression) or majority voting (classification).

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. Reduces **variance** of unstable learners (like decision trees) without significantly increasing bias.
2. Provides a built-in **OOB error estimate**, eliminating the need for a separate validation set.
3. Easily **parallelizable** — each base model trains independently.

---

### **Q2. What is a bootstrap sample? What is the expected fraction of unique instances in a bootstrap sample of size $N$? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A bootstrap sample is a dataset of size $N$ created by sampling $N$ instances from the original training set $D$ of size $N$ **with replacement** (i.e., the same instance can be drawn multiple times).

**Expected fraction (1.5 marks):** The probability that a specific instance is *not* chosen in a single draw is $(1 - 1/N)$. After $N$ independent draws, the probability it is never chosen is $(1 - 1/N)^N \to 1/e \approx 0.368$. Hence the probability it *is* chosen at least once is $1 - 1/e \approx 0.632$. So the expected fraction of unique instances in a bootstrap sample is approximately **63.2%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

**(a) Explain the Bagging algorithm in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss how Bagging reduces variance of base learners. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Input**: Training set $D = \{(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_N, y_N)\}$, base learning algorithm $\mathcal{L}$, number of bootstrap samples $B$.

2. **For** $b = 1$ to $B$:
   - Generate a bootstrap sample $D_b$ by sampling $N$ instances from $D$ with replacement.
   - Train a base learner $\hat{f}_b = \mathcal{L}(D_b)$.

3. **Output** the ensemble $\{\hat{f}_1, \dots, \hat{f}_B\}$.

4. **Prediction** for a new instance $x$:
   - Regression: $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}_b(x)$
   - Classification: $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_y \sum_b \mathbb{I}(\hat{f}_b(x) = y)$

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above.

**Variance Reduction Explanation (2 marks):**

Bagging reduces variance because averaging independent (or weakly correlated) random variables reduces variance. If base learners are independent with variance $\sigma^2$, the bagged estimator has variance $\sigma^2/B$. In practice, since base learners trained on overlapping bootstrap samples have correlation $\rho$, the variance is $\rho\sigma^2 + (1-\rho)\sigma^2/B$, which is still substantially less than $\sigma^2$. Critically, the **bias is preserved** because $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_b]$.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, explain the concept of Out-of-Bag (OOB) error estimation. Show mathematically that the probability of an instance being OOB converges to $1/e$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**OOB Concept with Example (3 marks):**

Suppose $N = 100$ and we draw a bootstrap sample $D_b$ of size 100 with replacement. Some instances appear multiple times, and on average, ~37 of the original 100 instances are **never selected** — these are the OOB samples for $D_b$.

**Example:** If instance $x_{42}$ is OOB for models $\hat{f}_3, \hat{f}_{7}, \hat{f}_{15}$, then the OOB prediction for $x_{42}$ is:
$\hat{y}_{42}^{\text{OOB}} = \arg\max_y \sum_{b \in \{3,7,15\}} \mathbb{I}(\hat{f}_b(x_{42}) = y)$

The OOB error is the misclassification rate over all training instances using only their OOB models.

**Mathematical Proof (4 marks):**

Probability a given instance $x_i$ is **not** selected in a single draw from $N$ samples:
$P(\text{not chosen in one draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{N} = \frac{N-1}{N}$

Since $D_b$ is formed by $N$ independent draws, the probability $x_i$ is **never** selected in all $N$ draws:
$P(\text{OOB}) = \left(\frac{N-1}{N}\right)^{N} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N}$

Taking the limit as $N \to \infty$:
$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N} = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679
$$

Hence, asymptotically, **~36.8%** of training samples are OOB for any bootstrap sample, providing a free validation set.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative): `[KTU Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) Compare Bagging and Boosting. Explain with diagrams how each handles the bias-variance trade-off. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Bagging | Boosting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Goal | **Reduce Variance** | **Reduce Bias** |
| Base Learners | Trained in **parallel** | Trained **sequentially** |
| Sample Weighting | Uniform bootstrap | Reweighted (focus on errors) |
| Model Combination | Equal weight (vote/avg) | Weighted by accuracy |
| Sensitivity to Noise | **Robust** | **Sensitive** (overfits) |
| Overfitting | Low risk | Higher risk |
| Example Algorithm | Random Forest | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting |

**Bias-Variance Discussion (3 marks):**

- **Bagging** uses *unpruned trees* (already low-bias, high-variance). Averaging many such trees reduces variance dramatically while keeping bias the same. Result: **lower total error**.
- **Boosting** uses *weak learners* (high-bias, low-variance) like decision stumps. Each subsequent model focuses on the errors of the previous ones, **sequentially reducing bias**. Variance may increase slightly but overall error decreases.

**Diagrams:** Refer to Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix.

---

**(b) Consider a Bagging ensemble with $B = 25$ base learners, each having variance $\sigma^{2} = 100$ and pairwise correlation $\rho = 0.3$. Compute (i) the variance of a single base learner, (ii) the variance of the bagged estimator, and (iii) the percentage reduction in variance achieved by Bagging. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Variance of a single base learner (1 mark):**
$\text{Var}(\hat{f}_b) = \sigma^{2} = 100$

**(ii) Variance of the bagged estimator (3 marks):**

Using the derived formula:
$$
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho \sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{B}
$$

Substituting $\rho = 0.3$, $\sigma^{2} = 100$, $B = 25$:
$$
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = (0.3)(100) + \frac{(1 - 0.3)(100)}{25}
$$

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = 30 + \frac{(0.7)(100)}{25} = 30 + \frac{70}{25} = 30 + 2.8
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = 32.8\;}
$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_b) - \text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}})}{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_b)} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{100 - 32.8}{100} \times 100\% = \frac{67.2}{100} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Variance Reduction} = 67.2\%\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Students frequently make three critical mistakes on Bagging numerical questions:
> 1. **Confusing OOB probability** — it is $(1 - 1/N)^N$, **not** $(1 - 1/N)$. Don't drop the exponent.
> 2. **Forgetting the correlation term** $\rho$ — when $\rho = 0$ the variance is $\sigma^2/B$, but when $\rho > 0$ the *second term alone* does not give total variance. Use the **full formula**.
> 3. **Mixing up the aggregation rule** — regression uses **mean**, classification uses **majority vote (mode)**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Bagging vs Boosting confusion** — saying "Bagging reduces bias" is a textbook error. It **reduces variance**. Bias stays nearly the same.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bagging**

- ⭐ **Full Form**: Bootstrap AGGregatING (Breiman, 1996).
- ⭐ **Core Idea**: Train $B$ models on $B$ bootstrap samples → aggregate predictions.
- ⭐ **Bootstrap sample** = sampling $N$ instances from $N$ with replacement.
- ⭐ **~63.2%** unique samples in each bootstrap; **~36.8%** are OOB.
- ⭐ **Aggregation**: Mean (regression) / Majority vote (classification).
- ⭐ **Primary effect**: **Variance reduction**; bias approximately unchanged.
- ⭐ **Best with**: Unstable, high-variance learners (unpruned Decision Trees, Neural Nets).
- ⭐ **Not useful with**: Stable learners (k-NN, Naive Bayes, Linear Regression).
- ⭐ **OOB Error**: Free validation estimate, asymptotically equivalent to LOOCV.
- ⭐ **Variance Formula**: $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{B}$.
- ⭐ **Limitation**: Loss of interpretability (a single deep tree is interpretable, a 500-tree bagged ensemble is not).
- ⭐ **Foundation for**: Random Forest (Bagging + random feature subsets at each split).
- ⭐ **Bagging ≠ Boosting**: Bagging = parallel/variance-reduction; Boosting = sequential/bias-reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering - K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Clustering - K-Means # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **K-Means Clustering** is an unsupervised, prototype-based, partitional clustering algorithm that aims to partition a dataset of $N$ observations into $K$ pre-defined, non-overlapping clusters, where each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean (centroid), serving as a prototype of the cluster. Mathematically, given a dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the K-Means algorithm minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also known as the **inertia** or **distortion function**: $$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$ where: - $C_k$ is the $k^{th}$ cluster - $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$ - $\Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ is the squared Euclidean distance between point $x_i$ and centroid $\mu_k$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** K-Means belongs to the family of **Prototype-Based Clustering** methods and is the foundational algorithm for understanding partitional, centroid-based clustering. It is a *hard clustering* technique (each point belongs to exactly one cluster), distinguishing it from soft methods like Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up Imagine you are a **school teacher organizing 60 students into 3 project groups** based on their performance in Mathematics and Physics. You don't have predefined groups — you just want students with similar scores to work together. **Step 1:** You randomly pick 3 students as "group leaders" (initial centroids). **Step 2:** Every other student joins the group whose leader has the most similar marks (distance calculation). **Step 3:** Once groups are formed, you recalculate the "average" performance of each group and appoint the student closest to this average as the new leader (centroid update). **Step 4:** Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until no student wants to switch groups — i.e., groups stabilize. **That's K-Means in a nutshell!** 🎯 ## 1.3 Key Characteristics (Must-Know for KTU) | Property | Description | |----------|-------------| | **Type** | Unsupervised Learning | | **Clustering Style** | Hard, Partitional, Prototype-Based | | **Input Required** | Number of clusters $K$ | | **Distance Metric** | Squared Euclidean Distance (default) | | **Output** | $K$ centroids + cluster assignments | | **Complexity** | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ where $I$ = iterations | | **Determinism** | Non-deterministic (depends on initialization) | | **Scalability** | Highly scalable for large datasets | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** K-Means minimizes **intra-cluster variance** (compactness) but does **NOT** explicitly maximize inter-cluster distance (separation). It uses a *greedy, iterative refinement* strategy with no global optimality guarantee. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The K-Means Algorithm — Operational Breakdown The standard **Lloyd's Algorithm** (the most common K-Means implementation) follows a 4-step iterative procedure: ### **Step 1: Initialization** - Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ - Methods: Random sampling, K-Means++, or Forgy initialization ### **Step 2: Assignment Step (E-Step equivalent)** Assign each data point $x_i$ to the nearest centroid: $$C_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2$$ ### **Step 3: Update Step (M-Step equivalent)** Recalculate each centroid as the **mean** of all points assigned to it: $$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$ ### **Step 4: Convergence Check** Stop when either: - Centroids no longer change: $\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \mu_k^{(t)}$ - Assignments no longer change - Maximum iterations reached - Change in objective $J$ falls below threshold $\epsilon$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this work?** Lloyd's algorithm is essentially coordinate descent on $J$. The assignment step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. $C$ (fixing $\mu$), and the update step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. $\mu$ (fixing $C$). Each step never increases $J$, guaranteeing convergence to a **local minimum**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Conditions | |---|-------------------|------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Objective Function (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | To be minimized | | 2 | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x_i, \mu_k) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2}$ | Default metric | | 3 | **Squared Distance** (used in computation) | $d^2(x_i, \mu_k) = \sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$ | Avoids square root | | 4 | **Centroid Update Rule** | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | $N_k$ = points in cluster $k$ | | 5 | **Total Variance Decomposition** | $T = W(C) + B(C)$ | $T$ = total, $W$ = within, $B$ = between | | 6 | **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ | | 7 | **Elbow Method (WCSS vs K)** | Plot $J(K)$ for $K = 1, 2, \ldots$ | Find "elbow" point | | 8 | **Time Complexity** | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | Per iteration | | 9 | **Manhattan Distance** (alt) | $d_1 = \sum_j \vert x_{ij} - \mu_{kj} \vert$ | Use \vert for abs value | | 10 | **Cluster Cohesion** | $cohesion(C_k) = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert$ | Lower is better | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** K-Means is the workhorse for **customer segmentation** (marketing), **image compression** (vector quantization), **document clustering** (text mining), **anomaly detection** (points far from any centroid), and **sensor data analysis** in IoT systems. ## 2.3 Choosing K — The Critical Hyperparameter Problem Since K-Means requires $K$ as input, several methods exist: ### **A. Elbow Method** - Plot WCSS $J$ against $K$ - Look for the "elbow" where the rate of decrease sharply drops - That point is the optimal $K$ ### **B. Silhouette Analysis** For each point $i$: - $a(i)$ = average distance to other points in same cluster (cohesion) - $b(i)$ = minimum average distance to points in next nearest cluster (separation) $$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$ - $s(i) \approx 1$: well-clustered - $s(i) \approx 0$: on boundary - $s(i) < 0$: possibly in wrong cluster ### **C. Gap Statistic** Compares WCSS to that expected under a null reference distribution. ## 2.4 K-Means++ Initialization (Smart Seeding) To address poor random initialization, **K-Means++** selects initial centroids with probability proportional to squared distance from existing centroids: 1. Choose first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random 2. For each point $x_i$, compute $D(x_i)^2 = \min_{k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ 3. Select next $\mu_k$ with probability $\frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_j D(x_j)^2}$ 4. Repeat until $K$ centroids are chosen 5. Proceed with standard K-Means **Guarantee:** $J_{K\text{-means++}} \leq O(\log K) \cdot J_{optimal}$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Optimal Centroid is the Mean **Claim:** Given a fixed cluster assignment $C_k$, the centroid $\mu_k$ that minimizes $J$ is the arithmetic mean of points in $C_k$. **Proof by Calculus:** $$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$$ To find the optimal $\mu_k$, take the partial derivative with respect to $\mu_{kj}$ and set to zero: $$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_{kj}} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) = 0$$ Solving: $$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_{ij} - N_k \cdot \mu_{kj} = 0$$ $$\mu_{kj} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_{ij}$$ Since this holds for every dimension $j \in \{1, \ldots, d\}$: $$\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i \qquad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Significance:** This derivation is a **favourite exam question** in Module 3. You may be asked to "prove that the K-Means centroid is the mean" or "derive the update rule." Master this proof. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Apply K-Means with $K = 2$ on the following 2D points: $A = (1, 1), \quad B = (1, 2), \quad C = (2, 1), \quad D = (8, 8), \quad E = (9, 9), \quad F = (8, 9)$ **Initial centroids:** $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (8, 8)$ (Forgy initialization) ### **Iteration 1** **Step A — Assignment:** Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids. | Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2 = (8,8)$ | Assigned Cluster | |-------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------| | $A(1,1)$ | $0$ | $98$ | $C_1$ | | $B(1,2)$ | $1$ | $85$ | $C_1$ | | $C(2,1)$ | $1$ | $85$ | $C_1$ | | $D(8,8)$ | $98$ | $0$ | $C_2$ | | $E(9,9)$ | $128$ | $2$ | $C_2$ | | $F(8,9)$ | $112$ | $1$ | $C_2$ | **Detailed calculation for $B(1,2)$:** $$d^2(B, \mu_1) = (1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 0 + 1 = 1$$ $$d^2(B, \mu_2) = (1-8)^2 + (2-8)^2 = 49 + 36 = 85$$ So $B \in C_1$. ✓ **Step B — Update Centroids:** $$\mu_1^{new} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(4, 4) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$$ $$\mu_2^{new} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8) + (9,9) + (8,9)\right] = \frac{1}{3}(25, 26) = \left(\frac{25}{3}, \frac{26}{3}\right)$$ **Step C — Compute Objective $J^{(1)}$:** $$J^{(1)} = \sum_{x \in C_1} \Vert x - \mu_1^{new} \Vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - \mu_2^{new} \Vert^2$$ **For $C_1$:** $$\Vert A - \mu_1^{new} \Vert^2 = (1 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (1 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{2}{9}$$ $$\Vert B - \mu_1^{new} \Vert^2 = (1 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (2 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9}$$ $$\Vert C - \mu_1^{new} \Vert^2 = (2 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 + (1 - \tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{4}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9}$$ $$W(C_1) = \tfrac{2}{9} + \tfrac{5}{9} + \tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{12}{9} = \tfrac{4}{3}$$ **For $C_2$:** $$\Vert D - \mu_2^{new} \Vert^2 = (8 - \tfrac{25}{3})^2 + (8 - \tfrac{26}{3})^2 = (-\tfrac{1}{3})^2 + (-\tfrac{2}{3})^2 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9}$$ $$\Vert E - \mu_2^{new} \Vert^2 = (9 - \tfrac{25}{3})^2 + (9 - \tfrac{26}{3})^2 = (\tfrac{2}{3})^2 + (\tfrac{1}{3})^2 = \tfrac{4}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{5}{9}$$ $$\Vert F - \mu_2^{new} \Vert^2 = (8 - \tfrac{25}{3})^2 + (9 - \tfrac{26}{3})^2 = (-\tfrac{1}{3})^2 + (\tfrac{1}{3})^2 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{2}{9}$$ $$W(C_2) = \tfrac{5}{9} + \tfrac{5}{9} + \tfrac{2}{9} = \tfrac{12}{9} = \tfrac{4}{3}$$ $$J^{(1)} = \tfrac{4}{3} + \tfrac{4}{3} = \tfrac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$$ ### **Iteration 2 — Check Convergence** Recompute assignments with new centroids. Since clusters remain identical (all $C_1$ points still closer to $\mu_1^{new}$ than to $\mu_2^{new}$, and vice versa), the algorithm **converges**. **Final Result:** - $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$ with centroid $(4/3, 4/3)$ - $C_2 = \{D, E, F\}$ with centroid $(25/3, 26/3)$ - Final WCSS = $8/3$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked for "convergence after how many iterations" — the answer is the iteration where cluster assignments become stable, NOT when $J$ exactly reaches 0. Always show the full iteration table with distances. ## 3.3 Derivation: Convergence of Lloyd's Algorithm **Claim:** Lloyd's algorithm converges in a finite number of steps. **Proof Sketch:** 1. There are **at most $K^N$** possible cluster assignments (finite). 2. Each iteration produces a **strict decrease** in $J$ unless convergence: $$J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$$ 3. Since $J$ is bounded below (by $0$), and the number of possible $J$ values over the finite assignment space is finite, the algorithm must terminate. $$\therefore \text{Convergence is guaranteed in finite iterations} \qquad \blacksquare$$ > [!WARNING] > **Local Optimum Trap:** Convergence ≠ Global Optimum. Bad initialization can lead to poor local minima. This is why **K-Means++** and **multiple restarts** are recommended in practice. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 K-Means Algorithm Flowchart (Lloyd's Algorithm) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Input dataset X and K] --> B[Initialize K centroids
Random or K-Means++] B --> C[Assignment Step:
Assign each point to nearest centroid] C --> D[Update Step:
Recalculate centroid as mean of cluster] D --> E{Convergence Check:
Centroids stable or
max iterations reached?} E -- No --> C E -- Yes --> F[Output:
Final Centroids and
Cluster Assignments] F --> G[End] ``` ## 4.2 Convergence Behavior — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Iteration1[Iteration 1 - Initial State] I1A[Random Initial
Centroids] --> I1B[Initial Assignment
Poor Quality] end subgraph Iteration2[Iteration 2] I2A[Updated Centroids
Mean of Cluster] --> I2B[Better Assignment
J Decreases] end subgraph IterationN[Iteration N - Converged] INA[Stable Centroids] --> INB[Stable Assignments
J Minimized] end Iteration1 --> Iteration2 Iteration2 --> IterationN ``` ## 4.3 K-Means vs Alternative Methods (Conceptual Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Unsupervised Clustering] --> B[Partitional Methods] A --> C[Hierarchical Methods] A --> D[Density-Based Methods] A --> E[Model-Based Methods] B --> B1[K-Means
K-Medoids
CLARANS] C --> C1[Agglomerative
Divisive] D --> D1[DBSCAN
OPTICS] E --> E1[Gaussian Mixture
Models GMM] ``` ## 4.4 Centroid Update — Block Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cluster C_k contains
N_k data points] --> B[Sum all vectors
in C_k] B --> C[Compute arithmetic
mean of vectors] C --> D[Set new centroid
mu_k = mean] D --> E[Repeat for all
k = 1 to K] E --> F[Check convergence
via J function] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Note:** For high-dimensional data, K-Means results are often visualized using **PCA** (2D projection) or **t-SNE**. The final centroids can be plotted on top of the clusters to show cluster compactness. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define clustering. List any two differences between supervised and unsupervised learning with respect to clustering.** **Model Answer:** **Clustering** is the process of grouping a set of objects into clusters such that objects in the same cluster are more similar to each other than to those in other clusters, based on some similarity measure (e.g., Euclidean distance). **Differences:** | Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning (Clustering) | |--------|---------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Labels** | Uses labeled training data | No labels provided | | **Goal** | Learn input → output mapping | Discover hidden structure / groups | | **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall | Silhouette score, WCSS, visual inspection | | **Example** | Classification, Regression | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical | > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 2 differences (1 mark each). --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **Q: Explain the role of the objective function $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ in K-Means clustering.** **Model Answer:** The objective function $J$ is known as the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** or **inertia**. It serves three critical roles: 1. **Quantifies Cluster Quality:** $J$ measures the total squared distance of all points from their respective cluster centroids. A **smaller $J$** indicates **tighter, more compact** clusters. 2. **Optimization Target:** K-Means iteratively **minimizes** $J$ through: - **Assignment step:** Fix centroids, minimize $J$ w.r.t. cluster assignments - **Update step:** Fix assignments, minimize $J$ w.r.t. centroids (closed-form mean) 3. **Model Selection Tool:** $J$ is used in the **Elbow Method** to determine the optimal number of clusters $K$ by plotting $J$ vs $K$ and identifying the "elbow." **Limitations:** $J$ is non-convex, has no global minimum guarantee, and tends to **decrease monotonically** with increasing $K$ (reaching 0 when $K = N$). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with its objective function. Discuss the steps involved in the iterative procedure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following 2D data points: $P_1 = (2, 10), P_2 = (2, 6), P_3 = (8, 4), P_4 = (5, 8), P_5 = (7, 5), P_6 = (6, 4), P_7 = (1, 2), P_8 = (4, 9)$, perform K-Means clustering with $K = 3$. Initial centroids are $C_1 = (2, 10), C_2 = (5, 8), C_3 = (1, 2)$. Show all iterations until convergence. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution (a) — 7 marks** **K-Means Clustering Algorithm:** K-Means is a prototype-based, partitional clustering algorithm that partitions $N$ data points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **WCSS objective**: $$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$ **Iterative Procedure (Lloyd's Algorithm):** **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ via random selection or K-Means++. **Step 2 — Assignment:** For each point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest (minimum squared Euclidean distance): $$C_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2$$ **Step 3 — Update:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster: $$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$ **Step 4 — Convergence:** Stop when cluster assignments no longer change, or when $\Vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$, or after maximum iterations. **Convergence Guarantee:** $J$ is monotonically non-increasing, and there are finitely many possible cluster assignments, so the algorithm terminates in finite steps (though only at a local minimum). **Key Properties:** - Time complexity: $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ - Sensitive to initialization (use K-Means++) - Requires specifying $K$ in advance --- #### **Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Iteration 1: Initial Centroids** $\mu_1 = (2,10), \mu_2 = (5,8), \mu_3 = (1,2)$ **Distance Calculations (Squared Euclidean):** For $P_1 = (2,10)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = (2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2 = 0$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = (2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$ - $d^2(\mu_3) = (2-1)^2 + (10-2)^2 = 1 + 64 = 65$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_1 \to C_1$ For $P_2 = (2,6)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 0 + 16 = 16$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 9 + 4 = 13$ ✓ minimum - $d^2(\mu_3) = 1 + 16 = 17$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_2 \to C_2$ For $P_3 = (8,4)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 36 + 36 = 72$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 9 + 16 = 25$ ✓ - $d^2(\mu_3) = 49 + 4 = 53$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_3 \to C_2$ For $P_4 = (5,8)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 9 + 4 = 13$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ - $d^2(\mu_3) = 16 + 36 = 52$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_4 \to C_2$ For $P_5 = (7,5)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 25 + 25 = 50$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 4 + 9 = 13$ ✓ - $d^2(\mu_3) = 36 + 9 = 45$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_5 \to C_2$ For $P_6 = (6,4)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 16 + 36 = 52$ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 1 + 16 = 17$ ✓ - $d^2(\mu_3) = 25 + 4 = 29$ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_6 \to C_2$ For $P_7 = (1,2)$: - $d^2(\mu_3) = 0$ ✓ - $\Rightarrow$ Assign $P_7 \to C_3$ For $P_8 = (4,9)$: - $d^2(\mu_1) = 4 + 1 = 5$ ✓ - $d^2(\mu_2) = 1 + 1 = 2$ (Actually minimum!) **Correction:** $P_8$ should go to $C_2$ since $d^2 = 2 < 5$. | Point | $d^2(\mu_1)$ | $d^2(\mu_2)$ | $d^2(\mu_3)$ | Assignment | |-------|--------------|--------------|--------------|------------| | $P_1$ | 0 | 13 | 65 | $C_1$ | | $P_2$ | 16 | 13 | 17 | $C_2$ | | $P_3$ | 72 | 25 | 53 | $C_2$ | | $P_4$ | 13 | 0 | 52 | $C_2$ | | $P_5$ | 50 | 13 | 45 | $C_2$ | | $P_6$ | 52 | 17 | 29 | $C_2$ | | $P_7$ | 66 | 52 | 0 | $C_3$ | | $P_8$ | 5 | 2 | 52 | $C_2$ | **New Centroids:** $$\mu_1^{new} = (2, 10) \quad [P_1 \text{ only}]$$ $$\mu_2^{new} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6+4}{6}, \frac{6+4+8+5+4+9}{6}\right) = \left(\frac{32}{6}, \frac{36}{6}\right) = (5.33, 6)$$ $$\mu_3^{new} = (1, 2) \quad [P_7 \text{ only}]$$ **Iteration 2:** Recompute distances with new centroids. If cluster assignments remain stable, **algorithm converges**. **Final Result:** $C_1 = \{P_1\}, C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_8\}, C_3 = \{P_7\}$ > [!NOTE] > This example shows that bad initialization can lead to a **degenerate cluster** (singleton). In practice, multiple initializations or K-Means++ is used to avoid this. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain the K-Means++ initialization algorithm. How does it improve upon random initialization? Discuss its convergence guarantee. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the Elbow Method and Silhouette Analysis for determining the optimal number of clusters in K-Means. Compare both approaches. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution (a) — 7 marks** **K-Means++ Initialization Algorithm:** K-Means++ is a smart seeding technique that probabilistically selects initial centroids to be **well-separated**, addressing the local optimum problem of random initialization. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. **Choose first centroid** $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the dataset $X$. 2. **Compute distances:** For each data point $x_i$, calculate the squared distance to the nearest already-chosen centroid: $$D(x_i)^2 = \min_{\mu_j \in \text{chosen}} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$ 3. **Select next centroid** $\mu_k$ with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^2$: $$P(\mu_k = x_i) = \frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} D(x_j)^2}$$ 4. **Repeat** Steps 2–3 until $K$ centroids are selected. 5. Proceed with standard Lloyd's K-Means. **Improvements over Random Initialization:** | Aspect | Random Init | K-Means++ | |--------|-------------|-----------| | **Spread** | May cluster initial centroids | Spreads centroids across data | | **Final WCSS** | Higher, unstable | Lower, more consistent | | **Convergence Speed** | Slower | Faster (fewer iterations) | | **Cluster Quality** | Often poor local minima | Near-optimal in practice | **Convergence Guarantee (Theoretical):** Arthur and Vassilvitskii (2007) proved: $$\mathbb{E}[J_{K\text{-means++}}] \leq 8(\ln K + 2) \cdot J_{OPT}$$ This is an **$O(\log K)$** approximation guarantee — K-Means++ produces solutions within a logarithmic factor of the optimal clustering, with high probability. --- #### **Solution (b) — 7 marks** **A. Elbow Method:** **Procedure:** 1. Run K-Means for $K = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, K_{max}$ (typically $K_{max} = 10$) 2. Record WCSS $J(K)$ for each $K$ 3. Plot $J(K)$ vs $K$ 4. Identify the "**elbow**" — the point where the rate of WCSS decrease sharply drops **Mathematical Intuition:** The marginal gain in compactness diminishes after the elbow. Adding more clusters beyond this point leads to **overfitting**. **Advantages:** - Simple, visual, computationally cheap - Effective when clusters are well-separated **Disadvantages:** - Subjective — "elbow" is not always clearly visible - May give ambiguous results for overlapping clusters **B. Silhouette Analysis:** **Procedure:** 1. Run K-Means for different $K$ values 2. For each point $i$, compute: - $a(i)$ = mean distance to other points in same cluster (cohesion) - $b(i)$ = mean distance to points in next nearest cluster (separation) 3. Compute Silhouette coefficient: $$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$ 4. Average $s(i)$ over all points: $\bar{S}(K) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i s(i)$ 5. Choose $K$ that maximizes $\bar{S}(K)$ **Range of $s(i)$:** - $s(i) \approx 1$: point well-clustered - $s(i) \approx 0$: point on cluster boundary - $s(i) < 0$: point may be in wrong cluster **Comparison:** | Criterion | Elbow Method | Silhouette Analysis | |-----------|--------------|---------------------| | **Metric** | WCSS (within-cluster compactness) | Cohesion vs Separation | | **Output** | Visual elbow | Numerical score in $[-1, 1]$ | | **Objectivity** | Subjective | More objective | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher (needs pairwise distances) | | **Best For** | Quick estimation | Precise cluster validity | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > ⚠️ **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Elbow with Silhouette:** Students often mix up the two methods. The Elbow Method uses WCSS; Silhouette uses pairwise distance ratios. > > 2. **Forgetting to mention K-Means++:** If asked about "improving K-Means," always mention initialization improvements — K-Means++ is a **guaranteed scoring method**. > > 3. **In numerical problems, students forget to recompute centroids** after the assignment step, leading to a 2-mark deduction. > > 4. **Not showing distance calculations explicitly** — KTU examiners expect a clear distance table for every iteration. > > 5. **Stating "K-Means gives global optimum"** — this is FALSE. K-Means converges to a local minimum. Always clarify this distinction. > > 6. **In derivation questions, skipping the partial derivative step** when proving the centroid is the mean — show $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = 0$ explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — K-Means (Module 3)** - ✅ **K-Means** = unsupervised, **partitional**, prototype-based, hard clustering. - ✅ **Objective:** Minimize WCSS $J = \sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$. - ✅ **Algorithm:** Lloyd's 4-step process — Initialize → Assign → Update → Check Convergence. - ✅ **Centroid formula:** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ (proved via calculus). - ✅ **Distance metric:** Default is **squared Euclidean**; Manhattan is an alternative. - ✅ **Convergence:** Guaranteed in finite steps but only to a **local minimum**. - ✅ **K-Means++:** Probabilistic seeding, $O(\log K)$ approximation guarantee. - ✅ **Choosing K:** Elbow Method (WCSS plot) or Silhouette Analysis ($s(i) \in [-1, 1]$). - ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$. - ✅ **Limitations:** Requires $K$ upfront, sensitive to outliers and initialization, assumes spherical clusters of similar size. - ✅ **Applications:** Customer segmentation, image compression, document clustering, anomaly detection. - ✅ **Variants:** K-Medoids (uses actual points), Mini-Batch K-Means (scalable), Bisecting K-Means. - ✅ **KTU Favourite Questions:** (1) Derive centroid as mean, (2) Numerical iteration table, (3) Compare K-Means++ vs random init, (4) Elbow vs Silhouette.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** High

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Machine Learning under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the term **"High"** when used as a qualitative descriptor of model behavior refers to two critical diagnostic properties of a learning algorithm's generalization performance:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH BIAS** — A model exhibits **High Bias** when it makes systematically erroneous assumptions about the underlying data distribution, leading to **underfitting**. The model is too simple to capture the true relationship between features and the target variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH VARIANCE** — A model exhibits **High Variance** when it is excessively sensitive to the specific training data, leading to **overfitting**. Small fluctuations in the training set cause large changes in the learned function.

Mathematically, we decompose the **expected prediction error** of a model at point $x$ as:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Exam Preparation" Metaphor**

Imagine two students preparing for an examination:

- **Student A (High Bias):** Memorizes only **3 basic formulas** and ignores the rest of the syllabus. On *any* question paper — easy or hard — he applies those 3 formulas. He consistently scores around **40/100** regardless of which sample paper he practices. He is **systematically wrong** but **consistent**.

- **Student B (High Variance):** Memorizes **the entire answer key of last year's paper** word-for-word. If a similar question appears, he scores **95/100**; if even a slight variation appears, he scores **20/100**. He is **highly accurate on training-like data** but **wildly inconsistent on new data**.

| Property | Student A (High Bias) | Student B (High Variance) |
|---|---|---|
| Training Score | Low (≈40) | Very High (≈99) |
| Test Score | Low (≈40) | Very Low (≈30) |
| Consistency | High | Low |
| Remedy | Add more features / use complex model | Regularize / use more data |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

- **Bias Squared**: Measured in $(error)^2$ units. **Typical range: $0 \to \infty$**.
- **Variance**: Measured in $(error)^2$ units. **Typical range: $0 \to \infty$**.
- **Irreducible Error** $\sigma^2$: A constant lower bound determined by **noise in the data**, not by the model. **Cannot be reduced** by any algorithm.
- **Model Complexity** parameter (e.g., polynomial degree $d$, tree depth $D$): A tuning knob whose increase typically **reduces bias but inflates variance**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **CO Mapping for PEECT413 (KTU 2024)**, this topic directly addresses **CO3**: *“Analyze the bias-variance tradeoff and apply regularization techniques to optimize model generalization.”* It is a **favourite 14-mark ESE question** and routinely appears as a **3-mark Part A conceptual question**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Bias-Variance Mechanics

The decomposition of expected error into its three constituent components is the **backbone of all diagnostic reasoning** in supervised learning. Let us dissect each component:

### 2.1.1 Bias (Systematic Error)

- **Definition**: The difference between the **expected prediction of the model** and the **true function value**.
- **Cause**: Oversimplification — e.g., fitting a **linear model** to data that follows a **quadratic trend**.
- **Symptom**: Both training error and test error are **high** and nearly equal.
- **Mathematical Statement**:

$$
\text{Bias}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] - f(x)
$$

- **Direction of Effect**: As **model complexity increases** $\Rightarrow$ **Bias decreases monotonically**.

### 2.1.2 Variance (Sensitivity to Training Data)

- **Definition**: The amount by which $\hat{f}(x)$ would change if we estimated it using a **different training dataset**.
- **Cause**: Overfitting — model captures noise as if it were signal.
- **Symptom**: Training error is **very low**, but test error is **much higher** (large generalization gap).
- **Mathematical Statement**:

$$
\text{Var}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right]
$$

- **Direction of Effect**: As **model complexity increases** $\Rightarrow$ **Variance increases monotonically**.

### 2.1.3 Irreducible Error (Bayes Error / Noise)

- **Definition**: The variance of the **noise term** $\varepsilon$ inherent in the data generation process:

$$
y = f(x) + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)
$$

- **Property**: **No model can reduce this below $\sigma^2$**.
- **Engineering Implication**: Even a *perfect* oracle has non-zero error.

## 2.2 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (The Central Dogma)

The fundamental tension in Machine Learning:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tradeoff**: Reducing bias by increasing model complexity will simultaneously increase variance. The optimal model is the one that **minimizes the sum of bias² + variance**, not either individually.

This is formalized as:

$$
\text{Total Expected Error}(x) = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias of estimator | $\text{Bias} = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ | Diagnose underfitting | (output units) |
| 2 | Variance of estimator | $\text{Var} = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$ | Diagnose overfitting | $(output)^2$ |
| 3 | MSE Decomposition | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Full error diagnosis | $(output)^2$ |
| 4 | Underfitting signature | $\text{Train Error} \approx \text{Test Error} \approx \text{High}$ | Detect High Bias | dimensionless |
| 5 | Overfitting signature | $\text{Train Error} \ll \text{Test Error}$ | Detect High Variance | dimensionless |
| 6 | Regularization (L2) | $\min_\theta \sum L(y, \hat{y}) + \lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ | Combat High Variance | objective units |
| 7 | Regularization (L1) | $\min_\theta \sum L(y, \hat{y}) + \lambda \sum \vert\theta_i\vert$ | Feature selection | objective units |
| 8 | Optimal complexity | $\dfrac{d}{d\text{complexity}}\left(\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}\right) = 0$ | Tune hyperparameters | — |
| 9 | Irreducible noise floor | $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\varepsilon)$ | Lower bound of error | $(output)^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax**: Per KTU protocol, all absolute values (e.g., $\vert\theta_i\vert$) use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Diagnostics**: A **High Bias** model may miss cancer cases (false negatives), causing fatal outcomes. A **High Variance** model may produce wildly different predictions across hospitals.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: **High Variance** in object detection leads to dangerous inconsistencies in novel environments.
- **Financial Forecasting**: **High Bias** in credit scoring systematically rejects worthy applicants.
- **NLP & Computer Vision**: Large neural networks typically exhibit **High Variance**; hence the universal use of **dropout, weight decay, and data augmentation** as variance-reduction techniques.

---

## 2.5 Diagnostic Decision Tree

1. **Is training error HIGH?**
   - **YES** $\Rightarrow$ **High Bias (Underfitting)** → *Increase model complexity*.
2. **Is training error LOW but test error HIGH?**
   - **YES** $\Rightarrow$ **High Variance (Overfitting)** → *Add regularization / gather more data*.
3. **Are both errors LOW and close?**
   - **YES** $\Rightarrow$ **Ideal regime** → Stop tuning, deploy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: The Bias-Variance Decomposition of MSE

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 ESE. We derive it from first principles with **every algebraic step explicitly shown**.

### Step 1: Define the Setup

We have a target function:

$$
y = f(x) + \varepsilon
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is random noise with:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2
$$

The model produces a prediction:

$$
\hat{y} = \hat{f}(x)
$$

### Step 2: Express the Expected Squared Error at Point $x$

The expected squared error at a fixed $x$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]
$$

### Step 3: Expand the Square Algebraically

We expand the square using the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$
(y - \hat{f}(x))^2 = y^2 - 2y\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2
$$

Applying expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \mathbb{E}[y^2] - 2\mathbb{E}[y\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}^2]
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$

We expand each term considering $f(x)$ is a constant w.r.t. the noise:

**Term A:** $\mathbb{E}[y^2]$

$$
\mathbb{E}[y^2] = \mathbb{E}[(f + \varepsilon)^2] = \mathbb{E}[f^2 + 2f\varepsilon + \varepsilon^2] = f^2 + 2f\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = f^2 + 0 + \sigma^2
$$

So $\mathbb{E}[y^2] = f^2 + \sigma^2$.

**Term B:** $\mathbb{E}[y\hat{f}]$

$$
\mathbb{E}[y\hat{f}] = \mathbb{E}[(f + \varepsilon)\hat{f}] = f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] = f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]
$$

**Term C:** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}^2]$

We leave this as $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}^2]$ for now.

### Step 5: Combine All Three Terms

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = (f^2 + \sigma^2) - 2f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}^2]
$$

### Step 6: Apply the Add-and-Subtract Trick

We introduce $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$ strategically. Using the identity:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}^2] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f})
$$

We also note:

$$
f^2 - 2f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]^2 = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}) + \sigma^2
$$

### Step 7: Final Clean Form

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{sensitivity error}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{noise floor}}\;}
$$

### Step 8: Interpretation of Each Component

- **Bias² term** $\bigl(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]\bigr)^2$: How far off the **average** prediction is from the truth.
- **Variance term** $\text{Var}[\hat{f}]$: How much the prediction **fluctuates** across training sets.
- **$\sigma^2$**: Inherent randomness; the **irreducible** minimum error.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (High-Yield KTU Style)

> **Problem**: Three different models are trained on the same dataset. The squared bias, variance, and irreducible noise values are given. Compute the total expected MSE for each and identify which model has High Bias and which has High Variance.

| Model | Bias² | Variance | $\sigma^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model A (Linear) | 100 | 10 | 36 |
| Model B (Deep NN, no regularization) | 4 | 250 | 36 |
| Model C (Regularized) | 25 | 25 | 36 |

**Solution:**

**Model A:**
$$
\text{MSE}_A = 100 + 10 + 36 = 146
$$

**Model B:**
$$
\text{MSE}_B = 4 + 250 + 36 = 290
$$

**Model C:**
$$
\text{MSE}_C = 25 + 25 + 36 = 86
$$

**Diagnosis:**
- Model A has **High Bias** (100 dominates) → **Underfitting**.
- Model B has **High Variance** (250 dominates) → **Overfitting**.
- **Model C is optimal** — balanced and lowest total error.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Remedies for High Bias vs High Variance

| Symptom | Diagnosis | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Train error high, Test error high | High Bias | Add more features, Increase model complexity, Decrease regularization $\lambda$, Train longer |
| Train error low, Test error high | High Variance | Get more training data, Reduce features, Increase regularization $\lambda$, Use Dropout, Use Early stopping, Ensemble methods (Bagging) |
| Train error low, Test error low | Good Fit | Stop, deploy the model |
| Train error high, Test error lower (rare) | Data leakage / bug | Inspect data pipeline |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Bias-Variance Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Expected MSE at point x] --> B[Add and Subtract Trick]
    B --> C[Bias Squared Term]
    B --> D[Variance Term]
    B --> E[Irreducible Noise sigma squared]
    C --> F[How far average prediction is from truth]
    D --> G[How much prediction fluctuates across datasets]
    E --> H[Lower bound set by data noise]
    F --> I[Model underfits if HIGH]
    G --> J[Model overfits if HIGH]
    I --> K[Remedy: increase complexity]
    J --> L[Remedy: regularization or more data]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Model Complexity vs Error Tradeoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Complexity] --> B[High Bias Low Variance]
    B --> C[Underfitting Region]
    D[High Complexity] --> E[Low Bias High Variance]
    E --> F[Overfitting Region]
    G[Optimal Complexity Sweet Spot] --> H[Balanced Bias and Variance]
    H --> I[Minimum Total Error]
    C --> I
    F --> I
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a High-Variance Mitigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawData[Raw Training Data] --> Split[Train Validation Test Split]
    Split --> TrainSet[Training Set]
    Split --> ValSet[Validation Set]
    Split --> TestSet[Test Set]
    TrainSet --> ModelFit[Model Fitting Engine]
    ModelFit --> PredTrain[Predictions on Train]
    ValSet --> HyperTune[Hyperparameter Tuning Loop]
    HyperTune --> Reg[Regularization Layer L1 or L2 or Dropout]
    Reg --> ModelFit
    PredTrain --> BiasEval[Bias Estimator]
    BiasEval --> Diag[Diagnostic Module]
    TestSet --> PredTest[Predictions on Test]
    PredTest --> VarEval[Variance Estimator]
    VarEval --> Diag
    Diag --> Output{High Bias or High Variance}
    Output --> Fix1[Increase Complexity]
    Output --> Fix2[Increase Data or Regularize]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Diagnostic Decision Tree (Exhaustive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Train Error High}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A[High Bias Underfitting]
    A --> A1[Add Features]
    A --> A2[Increase Complexity]
    A --> A3[Decrease Regularization]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Test Error Much Higher than Train}
    Q2 -- Yes --> B[High Variance Overfitting]
    B --> B1[Collect More Data]
    B --> B2[Regularize]
    B --> B3[Use Bagging or Dropout]
    Q2 -- No --> C[Good Fit Deploy Model]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define High Bias and High Variance in the context of supervised learning. How do they affect model performance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **High Bias** refers to the situation where a model is **too simple** to capture the underlying pattern in the data, leading to **underfitting**. Mathematically, $\text{Bias} = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$. The model makes **systematic errors** regardless of the training data used.

> **High Variance** refers to the situation where a model is **overly sensitive** to the specific training data, leading to **overfitting**. Mathematically, $\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$. Small changes in training data cause large changes in the learned function.

**Effect on Performance:**
- High Bias: **Both training and test errors are high and similar**.
- High Variance: **Training error is very low, but test error is significantly higher** (large generalization gap).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Bias-Variance Tradeoff? Why is irreducible error important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is the fundamental tension in machine learning whereby reducing bias (by increasing model complexity) increases variance, and vice versa. The optimal model is the one that **minimizes the sum** of bias² and variance.

The decomposition is:

$$
\text{Expected Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2
$$

> **Irreducible error** ($\sigma^2$) represents the **inherent noise** in the data. It is a **lower bound** on prediction error that **no model can overcome**, regardless of its complexity or training. It arises from factors such as measurement errors, missing variables, or inherent stochasticity in the data-generation process.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **(a) Derive the Bias-Variance decomposition of Mean Squared Error. Clearly identify each component. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Setup.** We model the true relationship as $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ and $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$.

**Step 2: Expand the squared error.**

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \mathbb{E}[y^2] - 2\mathbb{E}[y\hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)^2]
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$.**

For $\mathbb{E}[y^2]$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(f + \varepsilon)^2] = f^2 + 2f\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = f^2 + \sigma^2
$$

For $\mathbb{E}[y\hat{f}]$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(f + \varepsilon)\hat{f}] = f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] = f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]
$$

**Step 4: Combine and apply add-and-subtract.**

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = (f^2 + \sigma^2) - 2f\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]^2 + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]^2
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}) + \sigma^2
$$

**Step 5: Final decomposition.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MSE} = \underbrace{(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f})}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}\;}
$$

**Component Identification:**
1. **Bias²**: Systematic error due to wrong model assumptions.
2. **Variance**: Sensitivity to training set fluctuations.
3. $\sigma^2$: Irreducible noise — cannot be eliminated.

---

#### **(b) For three models trained on the same dataset, compute total expected MSE and identify which has High Bias, High Variance, or is optimal. Justify remedies. (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Model | Bias² | Variance | $\sigma^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 (Linear Regression) | 150 | 20 | 30 |
| M2 (5th-Degree Polynomial) | 10 | 280 | 30 |
| M3 (3rd-Degree Polynomial with Regularization) | 30 | 35 | 30 |

**Model Solution:**

**Model M1:**
$$
\text{MSE}_{M1} = 150 + 20 + 30 = 200
$$

**Model M2:**
$$
\text{MSE}_{M2} = 10 + 280 + 30 = 320
$$

**Model M3:**
$$
\text{MSE}_{M3} = 30 + 35 + 30 = 95
$$

**Diagnosis:**
- **M1 has High Bias (150 dominates)** → Underfitting → **Remedy**: Increase complexity (e.g., polynomial features, kernel methods).
- **M2 has High Variance (280 dominates)** → Overfitting → **Remedy**: Increase regularization $\lambda$, gather more data, use cross-validation.
- **M3 is optimal** — lowest MSE = 95 with balanced bias and variance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the Bias-Variance Tradeoff with a suitable diagram. Describe how model complexity influences bias and variance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** describes the inverse relationship between a model's ability to minimize bias (systematic error) and its tendency to exhibit variance (sensitivity to training data). As model complexity increases:

- **Bias decreases monotonically** — the model captures more patterns.
- **Variance increases monotonically** — the model fits noise.
- **Total error** has a U-shape with an **optimal point** at the minimum.

```
Error
   |  \                    /
   |   \  Variance      /
   |    \              /
   |     \            /
   |      \__________/   <-- Total Error (minimum at *)
   |      /          \
   |     /  Bias^2   \
   |    /             \
   |___/__________________\___ Model Complexity
            *
       OPTIMAL POINT
```

**Influence of Complexity:**
- **Low Complexity (left)**: High Bias dominates → Underfitting.
- **High Complexity (right)**: High Variance dominates → Overfitting.
- **Optimal Point (*)**: Bias² + Variance minimized.

**Practical Tuning Strategies:**
1. Use **cross-validation** (k-fold) to detect over/underfitting.
2. Tune hyperparameters like polynomial degree, tree depth, $\lambda$.
3. Apply **regularization** to control complexity.

---

#### **(b) Discuss in detail techniques to handle High Bias and High Variance. Provide at least three techniques for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### **Techniques to Handle High Bias (Underfitting):**

1. **Increase Model Complexity**: Move from linear to polynomial, or from shallow to deep networks.
2. **Add More Features**: Use feature engineering, polynomial features, or interaction terms.
3. **Decrease Regularization**: Reduce $\lambda$ in L1/L2 regularization, or remove dropout layers.
4. **Train Longer**: Allow the model more epochs to converge to a better solution.
5. **Use a More Powerful Model Class**: Switch from linear regression to Random Forest or Neural Networks.

### **Techniques to Handle High Variance (Overfitting):**

1. **Gather More Training Data**: The single most effective remedy.
2. **Apply Regularization**:
   - **L2 (Ridge)**: Adds $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ penalty.
   - **L1 (Lasso)**: Adds $\lambda \sum \vert\theta_i\vert$ penalty — induces sparsity.
3. **Use Dropout** (in Neural Networks): Randomly deactivates neurons during training.
4. **Early Stopping**: Halt training when validation error starts rising.
5. **Ensemble Methods**:
   - **Bagging**: Reduces variance by averaging multiple models (e.g., Random Forest).
   - **Boosting**: Reduces bias by sequential weak learners (e.g., AdaBoost, XGBoost).
6. **Reduce Features**: Use PCA or feature selection to eliminate noisy features.
7. **Cross-Validation**: Use k-fold CV for robust performance estimation.

### **Summary Table:**

| Problem | Techniques |
|---|---|
| High Bias | ↑ Complexity, ↑ Features, ↓ Regularization, Train longer |
| High Variance | ↑ Data, ↑ Regularization, Dropout, Early Stopping, Bagging, ↓ Features |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Bias² with Bias**: Examiners award 1 mark specifically for **squaring** the bias term. Writing $\text{Bias}$ instead of $\text{Bias}^2$ costs a mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sigma^2$ term**: Many students derive only two components. The **irreducible error** $\sigma^2$ is a **mandatory third term** worth 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Wrong direction of effect**: Students often state *"increasing complexity increases bias"* — this is **incorrect**. Increasing complexity **decreases** bias but **increases** variance.
> 
> 4. **No justification of remedies**: When asked to "fix" High Bias/Variance, students list techniques but don't justify *why*. Examiners expect a 1-line justification (e.g., *"Regularization penalizes large weights, preventing the model from memorizing noise"*).
> 
> 5. **Skipping the derivation**: In 14-mark questions, the **derivation is worth 5–7 marks**. Skipping algebraic steps leads to heavy deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- ✅ **Bias-Variance Decomposition**: $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$
- ✅ **High Bias** = Underfitting = **Simple model** = Train & Test error **both high**
- ✅ **High Variance** = Overfitting = **Complex model** = Train error **low**, Test error **high**
- ✅ **Tradeoff**: ↑ Complexity → ↓ Bias, ↑ Variance
- ✅ **Irreducible error** $\sigma^2$ **cannot be removed** by any algorithm
- ✅ **High Bias remedy**: ↑ Complexity, ↑ Features, ↓ Regularization
- ✅ **High Variance remedy**: ↑ Data, ↑ Regularization, Dropout, Bagging, Early Stopping
- ✅ **L1 Regularization** induces **sparsity** (feature selection effect)
- ✅ **L2 Regularization** shrinks weights toward zero (ridge effect)
- ✅ **k-fold Cross-Validation** is the standard tool to detect bias/variance issues
- ✅ **Ensemble Bagging** reduces **variance**; **Boosting** reduces **bias**
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark Qs: **always derive** the decomposition — 5+ marks depend on it
- ✅ **Bias and Variance are computed at each input point $x$** — not globally scalar (advanced nuance)
- ✅ Total expected error is a **sum**, not a product — students frequently confuse this

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hierarchical Clustering** is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that builds a nested hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging smaller clusters into larger ones (agglomerative approach) or recursively splitting a single cluster into smaller sub-clusters (divisive approach). The output is conventionally represented as a tree-structured diagram known as a **dendrogram**, which encodes the entire merging/splitting history and the inter-cluster distances at which clusters were combined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hierarchical Clustering is a **deterministic** algorithm (no random initialization) and forms a core part of Module 3 alongside Partitioning methods (K-Means), Density-based methods (DBSCAN), and Grid-based methods. Unlike K-Means, it does **not** require pre-specifying the number of clusters $K$.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, hierarchical clustering constructs a sequence of partitions $\mathcal{P}_0, \mathcal{P}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{P}_{n-1}$ where:
- $\mathcal{P}_0 = \{\{x_1\}, \{x_2\}, \ldots, \{x_n\}\}$ (singleton clusters)
- $\mathcal{P}_{n-1} = \{\mathcal{D}\}$ (single universal cluster)
- Each successive partition is obtained by merging the two closest clusters from the previous partition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine organizing a **genealogy tree of a royal family**:
- At the bottom (leaves), you have **individual family members** (data points).
- As you move up the tree, **siblings are grouped** under parents (small clusters merging).
- Further up, **families are grouped** into clans (medium clusters merging).
- At the very top, the **entire royal lineage** sits as one root (single cluster).

**Key Analogy Insight:** The **height** at which two branches join in the tree tells you *how similar* (or distant) those family members originally were. Cut the tree horizontally at any height, and the vertical branches you slice through become your final clusters — very similar to how a dendrogram is "cut" to obtain $K$ clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Contrast:** Think of K-Means as *building a city* where you decide in advance how many neighborhoods to create. Hierarchical Clustering is like *growing a tree* where the structure naturally reveals the optimal groupings as you observe the branching pattern.

## 1.3 Types of Hierarchical Clustering

| Type | Strategy | Complexity | KTU Weightage |
|------|----------|------------|---------------|
| **Agglomerative** (Bottom-Up) | Start with $n$ singletons → merge closest pair repeatedly | $O(n^3)$ time, $O(n^2)$ space | **High (most asked)** |
| **Divisive** (Top-Down) | Start with one cluster → split recursively | $O(2^n)$ theoretically | Moderate |

**Agglomerative** is the industry and KTU standard because it is computationally tractable and conceptually simpler.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering — Operational Logic

The algorithm follows a strict greedy bottom-up merging procedure:

1. **Initialization:** Treat each of the $n$ data points as a singleton cluster → total $n$ clusters.
2. **Distance Matrix Construction:** Compute the pairwise distance matrix $\mathbf{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ using a chosen distance metric.
3. **Merge Step:** Identify the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ with the **minimum inter-cluster distance** according to the chosen **linkage criterion**.
4. **Update:** Merge $C_i$ and $C_j$ into a new cluster $C_{ij}$. Reduce cluster count by 1.
5. **Recompute Distances:** Update the distance matrix using the **Lance-Williams recurrence formula** (or direct linkage formula).
6. **Termination Check:** Repeat steps 3–5 until only one cluster remains (or a stopping criterion like $K$ clusters is reached).
7. **Visualization:** Cut the resulting dendrogram at the desired height to obtain $K$ flat clusters.

## 2.2 The Lance-Williams Recurrence Formula (KTU High-Yield)

When two clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ are merged into $C_{i \cup j}$, the distance from the new cluster to any existing cluster $C_k$ is computed as:

$$d(C_{i \cup j}, C_k) = \alpha_i \, d(C_i, C_k) + \alpha_j \, d(C_j, C_k) + \beta \, d(C_i, C_j) + \gamma \, \vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \vert$$

The coefficients $(\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma)$ uniquely determine the linkage type. This unified formula is exam-critical because it shows how **all four standard linkages are special cases of a single recurrence**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Linkage Method | Formula for $d(C_i, C_j)$ | Lance-Williams Coefficients | Best Use Case |
|----------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **Single Linkage** (MIN) | $\displaystyle\min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\ \beta = 0,\ \gamma = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Elongated / chain-shaped clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** (MAX) | $\displaystyle\max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\ \beta = 0,\ \gamma = +\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Compact, spherical clusters |
| **Average Linkage** (UPGMA) | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{\vert C_i\vert \vert C_j\vert}\sum_{x \in C_i}\sum_{y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | $\alpha_i = \tfrac{\vert C_i\vert}{\vert C_i\vert+\vert C_j\vert},\ \alpha_j = \tfrac{\vert C_j\vert}{\vert C_i\vert+\vert C_j\vert},\ \beta = \gamma = 0$ | Balanced, robust to outliers |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $\Delta(A,B) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\vert C_i\vert \vert C_j\vert}{\vert C_i\vert+\vert C_j\vert}} \cdot \Vert \mu_i - \mu_j \Vert_2$ | Uses increase in **within-cluster SSE** | Minimize intra-cluster variance |

### Standard Distance Metrics Used to Populate the Initial Matrix

| Metric | Formula | Range Behavior |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| **Euclidean ($L_2$)** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(x_k - y_k)^2}$ | Most common, isotropic |
| **Manhattan ($L_1$)** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_k - y_k \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Minkowski ($L_p$)** | $d(x,y) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_k - y_k \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization of $L_1$ and $L_2$ |
| **Cosine Distance** | $d(x,y) = 1 - \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x\Vert \Vert y\Vert}$ | Text / high-dimensional sparse data |
| **Mahalanobis** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-y)}$ | Correlated features |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hierarchical clustering is heavily used in **phylogenetic tree construction** (biology), **customer segmentation** (marketing), **document/topic taxonomy** (NLP), and **image segmentation** (computer vision) where the *nested grouping structure* itself is informative, not just the flat partition.

## 2.4 Complexity Analysis (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Phase | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|-------|---------------|----------|
| Time | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ with priority queue | $O(2^n)$ worst case |
| Space | $O(n^2)$ for distance matrix | $O(n)$ to $O(2^n)$ |
| Scalability | Poor for $n > 10{,}000$ | Very poor |

This is why **BIRCH** and **CLINK** algorithms exist as scalable variants — but they are out of KTU 2024 syllabus scope.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked Example: Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage (KTU Favorite)

Consider **5 one-dimensional points**: $A=2,\ B=4,\ C=6,\ D=8,\ E=10$. We will cluster them using **Single Linkage** with **Euclidean distance**.

### Step 1: Initial Distance Matrix

Construct $\mathbf{D}^{(0)}$ for all $n=5$ points (symmetric, diagonal = 0):

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D & E \\
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
D & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
E & 8 & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: First Merge — Find Minimum Off-Diagonal Entry

The minimum off-diagonal value is **2**, occurring at multiple pairs: $(A,B)$, $(B,C)$, $(C,D)$, $(D,E)$.

By tie-breaking convention (lexicographic order), merge **$A$ and $B$** → form cluster $C_1 = \{A, B\}$.

Remaining clusters: $C_1=\{2,4\},\ C_2=\{C\},\ C_3=\{D\},\ C_4=\{E\}$.

### Step 3: Recompute Distances Using Single Linkage

For single linkage, the distance between two clusters is the **minimum pairwise distance** between their members. We use the Lance-Williams formula with $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\ \gamma = -\tfrac{1}{2},\ \beta = 0$:

$$
d(C_{1 \cup j}, C_k) = \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_i, C_k) + \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_j, C_k) - \tfrac{1}{2} \vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \vert
$$

This is algebraically equal to $\min(d(C_i, C_k), d(C_j, C_k))$.

Compute new distances from $C_1 = \{A, B\}$:
- $d(C_1, C) = \min(d(A,C), d(B,C)) = \min(4, 2) = 2$
- $d(C_1, D) = \min(d(A,D), d(B,D)) = \min(6, 4) = 4$
- $d(C_1, E) = \min(d(A,E), d(B,E)) = \min(8, 6) = 6$

### Step 4: Updated Distance Matrix $\mathbf{D}^{(1)}$

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_1 & C & D & E \\
C_1 & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 2 & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
D & 4 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
E & 6 & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Second Merge

Minimum value = **2**, occurs at $(C_1, C)$ and $(C, D)$. By convention, merge **$C_1$ and $C$** → form $C_2 = \{A, B, C\}$.

Using single linkage, distances from $C_2$:
- $d(C_2, D) = \min(d(C_1,D), d(C,D)) = \min(4, 2) = 2$
- $d(C_2, E) = \min(d(C_1,E), d(C,E)) = \min(6, 4) = 4$

### Step 6: Updated Matrix $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_2 & D & E \\
C_2 & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
D & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
E & 4 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7: Third Merge

Minimum = 2 at $(C_2, D)$ and $(D, E)$. Merge **$C_2$ and $D$** → $C_3 = \{A, B, C, D\}$.

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_3 & E \\
C_3 & 0 & 2 \\
E & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 8: Final Merge

Merge $C_3$ and $E$ into the **root cluster** $C_4 = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ at height **2**.

### Step 9: Dendrogram Reconstruction

```
Height: 2 ┤         ┌─────────┐
          │         │         │
Height: 2 ┤    ┌────┴────┐    │
          │    │         │    │
Height: 2 ┤ ┌──┴──┐      │    │
          │ │     │      │    │
Height: 0 ┴─┴─────┴──────┴────┴──
           A  B   C      D    E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In this evenly-spaced 1D example, all merges happen at the *same height* (2 units). This is a signature of a *well-separated, chain-like* dataset where single linkage works perfectly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Complete Linkage Penalizes Outliers

For a cluster $C_i$ and a far-away outlier cluster $C_j$, complete linkage uses:
$$d_{\text{complete}}(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i,\, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

The max operator is **highly sensitive to noise**: a single distant outlier point $y^*$ inside $C_j$ inflates the entire inter-cluster distance, preventing premature merging. This is the mathematical reason why complete linkage produces more compact, balanced clusters compared to single linkage's tendency to create "chaining" artifacts.

## 3.3 Ward's Linkage — Derivation of the Objective

Ward's method minimizes the **increase in total within-cluster sum of squared errors (SSE)** when two clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ are merged:

$$
\Delta(C_i, C_j) = \frac{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert} \cdot \Vert \mu_i - \mu_j \Vert_2^2
$$

where $\mu_i, \mu_j$ are the centroids. The derivation begins with the definition:

$$
\text{SSE}(C) = \sum_{x \in C} \Vert x - \mu_C \Vert_2^2
$$

When $C_i$ and $C_j$ merge into $C_{i \cup j}$:

$$
\Delta = \text{SSE}(C_{i \cup j}) - \text{SSE}(C_i) - \text{SSE}(C_j)
$$

Using the **parallel axis theorem** from physics/mechanics, one can prove:

$$
\text{SSE}(C_{i \cup j}) = \text{SSE}(C_i) + \text{SSE}(C_j) + \frac{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert} \cdot \Vert \mu_i - \mu_j \Vert_2^2
$$

The third term is exactly $\Delta(C_i, C_j)$. Ward's linkage therefore greedily chooses the merge that **adds the least variance** to the system, which is why it is the default choice in **scikit-learn's `AgglomerativeClustering`**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Agglomerative)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Load Dataset of n points] --> initA[Initialize n singleton clusters]
    initA --> distA[Compute n x n pairwise distance matrix]
    distA --> checkA{Stop criterion met?}
    checkA -- No --> findA[Find closest pair Ci, Cj]
    findA --> mergeA[Merge Ci and Cj into Cij]
    mergeA --> updA[Update distance matrix using Lance-Williams]
    updA --> logA[Record merge height in dendrogram]
    logA --> checkA
    checkA -- Yes --> cutA[Cut dendrogram at chosen height]
    cutA --> outA[Output K flat clusters]
    outA --> stopA[End]

    style startA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style stopA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style mergeA fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style cutA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataA[Raw Dataset D] --> preA[Preprocessing: Normalization / Scaling]
    preA --> metricA[Distance Metric Module: Euclidean / Manhattan / Cosine]
    metricA --> matrixA[Distance Matrix Builder]
    matrixA --> linkageA[Linkage Strategy Selector]
    linkageA --> singleA[Single Linkage]
    linkageA --> completeA[Complete Linkage]
    linkageA --> averageA[Average Linkage UPGMA]
    linkageA --> wardA[Ward's Minimum Variance]
    singleA --> engineA[Cluster Merge Engine]
    completeA --> engineA
    averageA --> engineA
    wardA --> engineA
    engineA --> dendroA[Dendrogram Constructor]
    dendroA --> cutA[Horizontal Cut Selector: K or threshold]
    cutA --> finalA[Final Cluster Assignment]
    finalA --> evalA[Validation: Silhouette / Cophenetic Correlation]

    style dataA fill:#bbdefb
    style finalA fill:#c8e6c9
    style evalA fill:#ffccbc
```

## 4.3 Linkage Method Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Hierarchical Clustering Linkage Methods]
    rootA --> sA[Single Linkage]
    rootA --> cA[Complete Linkage]
    rootA --> avA[Average Linkage]
    rootA --> wA[Ward's Linkage]
    sA --> sDesc[Uses MIN distance. Tendency: chaining. Strength: detects elongated shapes.]
    cA --> cDesc[Uses MAX distance. Tendency: compact clusters. Strength: outlier robust.]
    avA --> avDesc[Uses MEAN pairwise distance. Compromise between MIN and MAX.]
    wA --> wDesc[Minimizes SSE increase. Produces equal-sized clusters. Default in sklearn.]

    style rootA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style sA fill:#ffcdd2
    style cA fill:#c8e6c9
    style avA fill:#bbdefb
    style wA fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.4 Dendrogram Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    h0[Height Scale 0 to Max]
    h0 --> lowA[Low Merge Heights: Highly similar points]
    h0 --> midA[Mid Merge Heights: Moderate cluster similarity]
    h0 --> highA[High Merge Heights: Outliers or last merges]
    lowA --> p1[Leaf nodes: individual data points]
    midA --> p2[Internal nodes: cluster fusions]
```
