# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the ReLU activation function. State its mathematical form and range.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)** is a non-linear activation function defined as:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x)$$

It outputs the input directly for all positive values and outputs zero for all non-positive values.

**Key Properties:**
- **Range:** $[0, +\infty)$ — the output is always non-negative.
- **Linearity:** Piecewise linear; it is linear for $x > 0$ and constant (zero) for $x \leq 0$.
- **Differentiability:** Differentiable everywhere except at the origin $x = 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the mathematical form, 1 mark for the range and one property.

---

### Question 2: Conceptual Comparison `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is ReLU preferred over the Sigmoid activation function in deep neural networks? List any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

ReLU is preferred over Sigmoid in deep networks for the following reasons:

1. **Mitigates the Vanishing Gradient Problem:** ReLU's gradient is exactly 1 for all positive inputs, so gradients do not shrink during backpropagation. In contrast, Sigmoid's maximum gradient is only 0.25, causing gradients to vanish in deep networks.

2. **Computational Efficiency:** ReLU requires only a simple thresholding operation ($\max(0, x)$), whereas Sigmoid requires an expensive exponential computation ($\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$). This makes ReLU faster to evaluate and easier to parallelize on GPUs.

3. **Induces Sparsity:** ReLU outputs exactly zero for negative inputs, leading to sparse activations that often improve generalization.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for each well-explained reason, 1 mark for an additional supporting point.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the ReLU activation function in detail. Derive its derivative and discuss the dying ReLU problem.

**(b)** Compare ReLU, Leaky ReLU, and ELU activation functions based on range, gradient behavior, and ability to mitigate the vanishing gradient problem.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) is a piecewise linear activation function defined as:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } x \leq 0 \\ x, & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}$$

**Derivative Derivation (3 Marks):**
Differentiating the piecewise definition branch by branch:

For $x < 0$: $f(x) = 0 \implies f'(x) = 0$

For $x > 0$: $f(x) = x \implies f'(x) = 1$

Therefore, the gradient is:

$$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } x < 0 \\ 1, & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}$$

At $x = 0$, the derivative is undefined in the classical sense. The left-hand limit of the derivative is $0$ and the right-hand limit is $1$. In practice, a sub-gradient of $0$ (or $1$) is used at this point.

**Dying ReLU Problem (2 Marks):**
A neuron is said to be "dying" if its pre-activation is negative for all training examples. In that case, the neuron always outputs $0$, and its gradient is always $0$. Consequently, the weights feeding into this neuron never update during training, and the neuron becomes permanently inactive. This is known as the **Dying ReLU Problem**, and it reduces the effective capacity of the network.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Property | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | ELU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | $\max(0, x)$ | $\max(\alpha x, x)$, $\alpha=0.01$ | $x$ if $x \geq 0$, else $\alpha(e^x - 1)$ |
| **Range** | $[0, +\infty)$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $(-\alpha, +\infty)$ |
| **Gradient at $x > 0$** | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| **Gradient at $x < 0$** | $0$ | $\alpha$ (small positive) | $\alpha e^x$ (small positive) |
| **Mitigates Vanishing Gradient** | Yes (for $x > 0$) | Yes (fully) | Yes (fully) |
| **Suffers from Dying Neurons** | Yes | No | No |
| **Computational Cost** | Very low | Low | Moderate (uses exponential) |

**Analysis (3 Marks):**
- **ReLU** is the simplest and fastest, but suffers from dying neurons because its gradient is exactly $0$ for negative pre-activations.
- **Leaky ReLU** fixes this by allowing a small positive gradient ($\alpha = 0.01$) for negative inputs, ensuring all neurons remain trainable.
- **ELU** goes further by using an exponential form for negative inputs, which produces a smoother, more negative output that is centered closer to zero. This often leads to faster learning, but at the cost of additional exponential computation.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat mathematical formulation, explain the working of the ReLU activation function. Plot its graph and show why it is non-linear despite being piecewise linear.

**(b)** Discuss the role of ReLU in addressing the vanishing gradient problem. How does it compare with Sigmoid and Tanh in this regard? Mention two variants of ReLU and explain how they overcome its limitations.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**
The ReLU activation function is defined as:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } x \leq 0 \\ x, & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}$$

It is a **piecewise linear** function that outputs $0$ for all non-positive inputs and passes through the input unchanged for all positive inputs.

**Graph Description (2 Marks):**
- For $x \leq 0$: The graph is a horizontal line along the x-axis at $y = 0$.
- For $x > 0$: The graph is a straight line with slope $1$, passing through the origin.
- The function has a "kink" or non-differentiable point at $x = 0$.

**Why It Is Non-Linear (2 Marks):**
Although ReLU is linear within each of its two branches, the **composition of two linear pieces joined at a kink at the origin makes the function globally non-linear**. A truly linear function must satisfy $f(ax + by) = af(x) + bf(y)$ for all $a, b$. ReLU fails this test because, for example:

$$\text{ReLU}(1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1)) = \text{ReLU}(0) = 0$$

But:

$$1 \cdot \text{ReLU}(1) + 1 \cdot \text{ReLU}(-1) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

Since $0 \neq 1$, ReLU is non-linear. This non-linearity is what allows neural networks with ReLU activations to approximate complex, non-linear functions.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Vanishing Gradient Problem (2 Marks):**
The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients become exponentially small as they propagate backward through deep networks. For Sigmoid, the maximum gradient is $0.25$ (at $x = 0$). For Tanh, the maximum gradient is $1$ (at $x = 0$) but falls rapidly toward $0$ for large $|x|$. In both cases, multiplying many such small gradients together causes the gradient at early layers to be effectively zero, preventing those layers from learning.

**Why ReLU Solves This (2 Marks):**
ReLU's gradient is exactly $1$ for all positive inputs. Therefore, when a neuron is active ($x > 0$), the gradient passes through unchanged during backpropagation. There is no multiplicative decay, so deep networks can be trained effectively.

**Two Variants of ReLU (3 Marks):**

1. **Leaky ReLU:** Defined as $f(x) = \max(\alpha x, x)$ with $\alpha = 0.01$. It allows a small, non-zero gradient for negative inputs, ensuring no neuron ever becomes permanently "dead." It overcomes the dying ReLU problem.

2. **Parametric ReLU (PReLU):** Same as Leaky ReLU, but $\alpha$ is a learnable parameter that is updated via backpropagation. It is more flexible but introduces extra parameters per neuron.

3. **Exponential Linear Unit (ELU):** Defined as $f(x) = x$ for $x \geq 0$ and $f(x) = \alpha(e^x - 1)$ for $x < 0$. ELU produces negative outputs that push the mean activation closer to zero, which often speeds up learning. It overcomes both dying ReLU and the bias-shift problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary case:** Many students write the ReLU derivative as $f'(x) = 1$ for all $x$. **Lose 1 mark** if you do not address the $x \leq 0$ case.
> 2. **Claiming ReLU is fully linear:** ReLU is piecewise linear, not globally linear. State explicitly that the kink at the origin makes it non-linear. **Lose 1 mark** if you call it "linear."
> 3. **Not mentioning the dying ReLU problem:** This is the most important drawback and a board-exam favorite. **Lose 2 marks** if omitted.
> 4. **Confusing Leaky ReLU with Parametric ReLU:** In Leaky ReLU, $\alpha$ is fixed (typically $0.01$). In PReLU, $\alpha$ is learned. Markers **deduct 1 mark** for this confusion.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare with Sigmoid/Tanh:** A KTU question asking "why ReLU" expects a comparative answer. Merely listing ReLU's properties without comparison **loses 1-2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ReLU (Module 4, PEECT413)**

- **Definition:** $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ — outputs $x$ if $x > 0$, else $0$.
- **Range:** $[0, +\infty)$ — strictly non-negative outputs.
- **Derivative:** $f'(x) = 1$ if $x > 0$, else $0$. Undefined at $x=0$.
- **Why preferred:** Non-saturating for positive inputs, computationally cheap, induces sparsity, faster convergence.
- **Vanishing gradient fix:** Gradient is exactly $1$ for active neurons — no multiplicative decay.
- **Dying ReLU problem:** A neuron with always-negative pre-activation outputs $0$ forever, never updates.
- **Key Variants to Memorize:**
   - **Leaky ReLU:** $f(x) = \max(0.01x, x)$ — fixes dying neurons.
   - **PReLU:** Leaky ReLU with learnable $\alpha$.
   - **ELU:** Exponential form for negative inputs, mean activation closer to zero.
   - **Swish:** $f(x) = x \cdot \sigma(\beta x)$ — non-monotonic, used in transformers.
   - **GELU:** Gaussian Error Linear Unit — used in BERT and GPT.
- **ReLU is NOT linear globally** — it is non-linear due to the kink at $x=0$.
- **ReLU is NOT zero-centered** — this is a minor drawback.
- **Sigmoid and Tanh are saturating** — they suffer from vanishing gradients; ReLU does not.
- **Default activation for hidden layers** in modern CNNs (ResNet, VGG, etc.) and feed-forward networks.
- **For binary classification output:** Use Sigmoid. For multi-class: Use Softmax. For hidden layers: Use ReLU.
- **KTU Board Favorite Questions:** (1) Derivation of ReLU derivative, (2) Comparison of ReLU with Sigmoid/Tanh, (3) Dying ReLU problem and Leaky ReLU solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always end your answer with a comparative table and a real-world application example. KTU examiners award up to **1 bonus mark** for demonstrating applied knowledge (e.g., "ReLU is used in ResNet, which won the 2015 ImageNet competition").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tanh. Backpropagation - Chain Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tanh. Backpropagation - Chain Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent) Activation Function

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **hyperbolic tangent (tanh)** is a smooth, non-linear, zero-centered, S-shaped (sigmoidal) activation function used in artificial neural networks. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of hyperbolic sine to hyperbolic cosine, and maps any real-valued input into the bounded interval $\vert \text{output} \vert < 1$.

$$
\tanh(x) = \frac{\sinh(x)}{\cosh(x)} = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}
$$

**Range:** $\tanh(x) \in (-1, +1)$ — strictly bounded between negative and positive unity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Property — Zero-Centred Output:**
> Unlike the sigmoid function (range $0$ to $1$), tanh produces outputs that are symmetric around zero. This is a critical property because it causes the *gradient updates during backpropagation to follow the natural sign of the error*, leading to faster and more stable convergence of gradient descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Tanh over Sigmoid?**
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. Tanh is preferred in **hidden layers** because (i) it is zero-centred, and (ii) its maximum derivative is **1.0** (compared to sigmoid's maximum of **0.25**), allowing stronger gradient flow and reduced vanishing-gradient severity in shallow networks.

---

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Smooth Steering Wheel":**
Imagine driving a car where the steering wheel turns from fully-left to fully-right. A *binary* steering (left or right only) would jerk the car. The **tanh** function is like a *proportional steering wheel* — it smoothly turns all the way left (-1) for a hard left input, all the way right (+1) for a hard right input, and gives a proportional response in between. Crucially, when you are *already going straight* (input = 0), the steering is exactly centred (output = 0) — not biased to one side. This "centred-when-neutral" property is what makes tanh superior to sigmoid for hidden-layer activations.

---

### 1.2 Backpropagation and the Chain Rule

**Formal Definition:**
**Backpropagation** ("backward propagation of errors") is the cornerstone algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks. It uses the **chain rule of differential calculus** to efficiently compute the gradient of the loss function with respect to every weight and bias in the network. These gradients are then used by an optimizer (e.g., gradient descent) to iteratively update the parameters and minimize the loss.

**The Chain Rule (Single Variable Form):**
For a composite function $z = f(g(x))$, the derivative decomposes as:

$$
\frac{dz}{dx} = \frac{dz}{dy} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}, \quad \text{where } y = g(x)
$$

**The Chain Rule (Multivariable / Neural Network Form):**
For a loss $L$ depending on weight $W^{(l)}$ in layer $l$ through a chain of intermediate quantities:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} \;=\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}
$$

Each factor in this product is a *local gradient* — the derivative of one node with respect to its immediate predecessor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Local vs Global Gradients:**
> In backpropagation, every weight update is a *product of local gradients*. Each layer only needs to know two things: (i) how its activation changes with its pre-activation (i.e., its activation derivative), and (ii) how its pre-activation changes with the previous layer's activation. This modular, layer-by-layer decomposition is what makes backpropagation computationally efficient.

---

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Blame Game in a Factory":**
Imagine a car factory with three assembly stations (input → hidden → output). At the end, the quality inspector finds a defect. Now you must determine *which station* and *which setting* caused the defect.

- The inspector tells Station 3 (output): *"You are wrong by this much."*
- Station 3 tells Station 2 (hidden): *"Based on your input to me, you contributed this much to my error."*
- Station 2, using its own local sensitivity (the tanh derivative), tells Station 1 (input): *"Here is your share of the blame."*

Each station multiplies the incoming blame by its **own local sensitivity** (the derivative of its activation function). This propagation of blame — from output back to input — is precisely what the **chain rule** formalizes mathematically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Tanh Function

**Step-by-step Logic of Tanh's Behaviour:**

1. **Input Reception:** Accepts any real number $x \in (-\infty, +\infty)$.
2. **Exponential Transformation:** Computes $e^{x}$ and $e^{-x}$. For very large positive $x$, $e^{-x} \approx 0$, so $\tanh(x) \to 1$. For very large negative $x$, $e^{x} \approx 0$, so $\tanh(x) \to -1$.
3. **Output Saturation:** The function saturates (gradient $\to 0$) for $\vert x \vert > 3$ approximately.
4. **Linear Region:** Near $x = 0$, tanh behaves almost linearly with slope $1$ — the strongest gradient region.
5. **Monotonicity:** Strictly monotonically increasing everywhere — a requirement for a valid activation function used in differentiable networks.

**Why Tanh Works So Well in Hidden Layers:**

- **Zero-centred output** $\Rightarrow$ gradients of the loss w.r.t. weights have natural signs, avoiding zig-zag updates.
- **Bounded output** $\Rightarrow$ activations do not explode numerically.
- **Smooth & differentiable everywhere** $\Rightarrow$ gradient-based optimization is well-defined.
- **Stronger gradients than sigmoid** $\Rightarrow$ reduces (but does not eliminate) the vanishing gradient problem.

**Engineering Utility:**
In practice, tanh is used as the activation in **hidden layers of RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs**, and historically in fully-connected feedforward networks. Modern ReLU-based architectures have largely replaced tanh in very deep CNNs, but tanh remains dominant in sequence models and as the canonical example in KTU board examinations.

---

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Backpropagation Algorithm

**Phase 1 — Forward Pass:**
Given input $\mathbf{x}$ and current weights $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$, compute layer-by-layer:

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

End with the loss $L(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$.

**Phase 2 — Backward Pass (Chain Rule in Action):**
Starting from the output layer and moving backward, compute the **error term** $\delta^{(l)}$ for each layer:

$$
\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\mathbf{a}^{(L)}} L \odot f'^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})
$$

$$
\delta^{(l)} = \left( (\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot f'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

Here, $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product. For tanh hidden activations, $f'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) = 1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(l)})^2$.

**Phase 3 — Weight Gradient Computation:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \cdot (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^T, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}
$$

**Phase 4 — Parameter Update (Gradient Descent):**

$$
\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter typically in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Function / Concept | Mathematical Formula | Range / Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tanh activation | $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$ | Range: $(-1, +1)$; zero-centred |
| 2 | Tanh derivative | $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$ | Max value $= 1$ at $x=0$ |
| 3 | Sigmoid (for contrast) | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Range: $(0, 1)$; not zero-centred |
| 4 | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\bigl(1-\sigma(x)\bigr)$ | Max value $= 0.25$ at $x=0$ |
| 5 | Chain rule (2 funcs) | $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = \dfrac{dz}{dy} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | $y$ is the intermediate variable |
| 6 | Chain rule (network) | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \prod_{k} \dfrac{\partial u_{k}}{\partial u_{k-1}}$ | Product of local gradients along path |
| 7 | Forward pre-activation | $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | $l$-th layer linear step |
| 8 | Forward activation | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Apply non-linearity $f$ |
| 9 | Output error term | $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\mathbf{a}} L \odot f'^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$ | Computed first in backward pass |
| 10 | Hidden error term | $\delta^{(l)} = \bigl((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^{T} \delta^{(l+1)}\bigr) \odot f'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Propagated backward |
| 11 | Weight gradient | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \, (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^{T}$ | Outer product form |
| 12 | Bias gradient | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$ | Same as error term |
| 13 | Gradient descent update | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ | $\eta$ is the learning rate |
| 14 | Binary cross-entropy loss | $L = -\bigl[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\bigr]$ | For binary classification |
| 15 | MSE loss | $L = \dfrac{1}{2n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{i}\bigr)^{2}$ | For regression problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Focus:**
> Formulas 2, 6, 9, 10, 11, and 13 are the *highest-weightage* items. The combination of formulas 9 + 10 + 11 forms the complete backpropagation derivation that carries 7-10 marks in any 14-mark Part B question.

---

### 2.4 Comparison Table — Sigmoid vs Tanh

| Property | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | Tanh |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $\dfrac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}$ |
| Output range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, +1)$ |
| Zero-centred? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Max derivative | $0.25$ at $x=0$ | $1.0$ at $x=0$ |
| Computational cost | One exp | Two exps (or one if rewritten) |
| Vanishing gradient severity | Higher (derivative max $0.25$) | Lower (derivative max $1.0$) |
| Typical role | Output layer (binary classification) | Hidden layers of networks |
| Symmetry around origin | No (asymmetric) | Yes (odd function) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Realisation:** Tanh can be expressed in terms of sigmoid as $\tanh(x) = 2\sigma(2x) - 1$. This identity is sometimes useful in hardware implementations where only a sigmoid circuit is available.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tanh Derivative $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$

**Starting Point — Definition:**

$$
y = \tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}
$$

**Step 1: Identify numerator and denominator.**
Let $N(x) = e^{x} - e^{-x}$ (numerator) and $D(x) = e^{x} + e^{-x}$ (denominator).

**Step 2: Compute their derivatives.**

$$
N'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(e^{x} - e^{-x}) = e^{x} - (-1)e^{-x} = e^{x} + e^{-x}
$$

$$
D'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(e^{x} + e^{-x}) = e^{x} + (-1)e^{-x} = e^{x} - e^{-x}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Quotient Rule** $\left(\dfrac{N}{D}\right)' = \dfrac{N' D - N D'}{D^{2}}$.

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{N'(x) \, D(x) - N(x) \, D'(x)}{[D(x)]^{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify the numerator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N'(x) D(x) - N(x) D'(x) &= (e^{x} + e^{-x})(e^{x} + e^{-x}) - (e^{x} - e^{-x})(e^{x} - e^{-x}) \\
&= (e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2} - (e^{x} - e^{-x})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the algebraic identity** $(a+b)^{2} - (a-b)^{2} = 4ab$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2} - (e^{x} - e^{-x})^{2} &= 4 \cdot e^{x} \cdot e^{-x} \\
&= 4 \cdot e^{x-x} \\
&= 4 \cdot e^{0} \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Therefore the derivative becomes:**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}
$$

**Step 7: Express in terms of $\tanh(x)$.**

Compute $\tanh^{2}(x)$:

$$
\tanh^{2}(x) = \frac{(e^{x} - e^{-x})^{2}}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}
$$

Compute $1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \tanh^{2}(x) &= \frac{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2} - (e^{x} - e^{-x})^{2}}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}} \\
&= \frac{4}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Equate the two expressions.**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}} = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)
$$

$$
\boxed{\therefore \quad \tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** This elegant result $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$ is *memorized* in implementation because it allows the gradient to be computed from the already-calculated activation value $\tanh(x)$, avoiding any additional exponential evaluation. This is a common 3-mark short-answer question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Backpropagation Gradients — 2-Layer Network with Tanh Hidden Layer

**Network Architecture:**
- **Input:** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$
- **Hidden Layer (Layer 1):** $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$, $\quad \mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$
- **Output Layer (Layer 2):** $\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$, $\quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$
- **Loss:** Binary Cross-Entropy — $L = -\bigl[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\bigr]$

---

**PART A — Gradient for Output Layer Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$**

By the **chain rule**, the gradient decomposes into a product of three local gradients:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}}_{\text{(i) Loss derivative}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}}}_{\text{(ii) Sigmoid deriv.}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}}_{\text{(iii) Linear derivative}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute (i):** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= -y \log \hat{y} - (1-y) \log(1-\hat{y}) \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} &= -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - (1-y) \cdot \frac{-1}{1-\hat{y}} \\
&= -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute (ii):** $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}}$ (sigmoid derivative)

$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z^{(2)}}}
$$

Using the well-known identity:

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y}\bigl(1 - \hat{y}\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (iii):** $\dfrac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}$

Since $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \mathbf{a}^{(1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the three factors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} &= \left[-\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right] \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) \cdot \mathbf{a}^{(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[-\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right] \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) &= -y(1-\hat{y}) + (1-y)\hat{y} \\
&= -y + y\hat{y} + \hat{y} - y\hat{y} \\
&= \hat{y} - y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \, (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{T}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Magic Cancellation":** When the output activation is sigmoid AND the loss is binary cross-entropy, the gradient simplifies to the beautifully clean form $(\hat{y} - y)$ — *just the prediction error*. This is why this specific pair (sigmoid + BCE) is the canonical choice for binary classification output layers.

---

**PART B — Gradient for Hidden Layer Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$**

By the **chain rule**, the gradient now flows through *four* intermediate quantities:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}}}_{\text{(already } = \hat{y} - y\text{)}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}}_{\text{(iv)}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}}_{\text{(v) tanh deriv.}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}}_{\text{(vi)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute (iv):** $\dfrac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}$

From $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute (v):** $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}$ (tanh derivative — *the focus of this module!*)

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = 1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2} \quad \text{(element-wise)}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute (vi):** $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$

From $z^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 10 — Combine all factors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} &= (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \cdot \bigl[1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}\bigr] \cdot \mathbf{x}^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11 — Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \left[ (\hat{y} - y) \, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \odot \bigl(1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}\bigr) \right] \mathbf{x}^{T}}
$$

where $\odot$ denotes the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Interpretation

| Term | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $(\hat{y} - y)$ | Output error scalar | Magnitude & sign of final prediction mistake |
| $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ | Output weight matrix | How strongly each hidden neuron influences the output error |
| $1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}$ | Tanh derivative vector | **Local sensitivity** of hidden activations — *where the Module 4 tanh derivative appears!* |
| $\mathbf{x}^{T}$ | Transposed input | Outer-product partner to produce a gradient matrix of correct shape |
| $\eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$ | Update step size | Scales the gradient to compute the parameter increment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Notice how the tanh derivative $1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}$ acts as a **gating mechanism** in the gradient. When the hidden activation $\mathbf{a}^{(1)}$ is saturated (near $\pm 1$), this term approaches $0$, causing the gradient to vanish — this is the famous *vanishing gradient problem* in deep networks, directly tied to the tanh function's properties.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Backpropagation Flow Architecture (Forward + Backward Pass)

The diagram below illustrates the complete forward computation of activations followed by the backward propagation of gradients via the chain rule. Every backward arrow represents a local gradient multiplication that constitutes one factor in the chain rule product.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputX["Input Vector x"] --> z1["z1 Linear: W1 times x plus b1"]
    z1 --> a1["a1 Activation: tanh of z1"]
    a1 --> z2["z2 Linear: W2 times a1 plus b2"]
    z2 --> a2["a2 Activation: sigmoid of z2"]
    a2 --> loss["Loss L cross entropy"]
    
    loss --> g1["Grad L wrt a2"]
    g1 --> g2["Grad L wrt z2 equals a2 minus y"]
    g2 --> g3["Grad L wrt W2 equals delta2 times a1 transpose"]
    g3 --> updW2["Update W2 using gradient descent"]
    
    g2 --> g4["Grad L wrt a1 equals delta2 times W2 transpose"]
    g4 --> g5["Grad L wrt z1 using tanh derivative one minus a1 squared"]
    g5 --> g6["Grad L wrt W1 equals delta1 times x transpose"]
    g6 --> updW1["Update W1 using gradient descent"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Top path (left to right):** The forward pass — input flows through linear and tanh/sigmoid operations to produce the loss.
- **Bottom path (right to left):** The backward pass — the chain rule is applied sequentially, starting from $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}$, then $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}}$, then propagating through $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ to $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}$, then through the tanh derivative, and finally reaching $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — The Chain Rule Cascade (Mathematical Dependency Chain)

This diagram visualizes how the chain rule decomposes a single complex derivative $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$ into a sequence of simple local derivatives multiplied together.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    w1["W1 Hidden Weights"] --> z1["z1 Pre-activation"]
    z1 --> a1["a1 Tanh Output"]
    a1 --> z2["z2 Pre-activation"]
    z2 --> yhat["yhat Sigmoid Output"]
    yhat --> L["L Loss"]
    
    L --> factorA["Factor A: dL dyhat"]
    factorA --> factorB["Factor B: dyhat dz2"]
    factorB --> factorC["Factor C: dz2 da1"]
    factorC --> factorD["Factor D: da1 dz1 equals 1 minus a1 squared"]
    factorD --> factorE["Factor E: dz1 dW1 equals x"]
    factorE --> final["Final Gradient: Product of A times B times C times D times E"]
```

**Interpretation:**
The total gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$ is the *product* of five local gradients (Factors A through E). The tanh derivative $1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}$ appears specifically as **Factor D** — this is the unique contribution of the tanh activation function within the chain.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Functional Block Architecture of a Training Iteration

For topics where physical drawings are infeasible, the system maps the *interaction topology* instead. The following block diagram shows how all components of one training iteration interact:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    data["Training Data Batch"] --> forward["Forward Propagation Module"]
    forward --> pred["Predictions yhat"]
    pred --> losscalc["Loss Calculation Module"]
    trueY["True Labels y"] --> losscalc
    losscalc --> lossval["Scalar Loss L"]
    
    lossval --> backward["Backward Propagation Module"]
    backward --> chainrule["Chain Rule Engine"]
    chainrule --> tanhderiv["Tanh Derivative: one minus a1 squared"]
    chainrule --> sigmoidderiv["Sigmoid Derivative: yhat times one minus yhat"]
    tanhderiv --> gradients["Gradient Computation: dL by dW1, dL by dW2"]
    sigmoidderiv --> gradients
    gradients --> updater["Parameter Updater"]
    lr["Learning Rate eta"] --> updater
    updater --> newW["Updated Weights W1 new, W2 new"]
    newW --> forward
```

**Block-Level Reading Guide:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Forward Propagation Module | Computes $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}, \mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ layer by layer |
| Loss Calculation Module | Evaluates $L(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$ |
| Backward Propagation Module | Initiates the backward flow of gradients |
| Chain Rule Engine | Decomposes gradients into local-gradient products |
| Tanh Derivative Block | Provides the factor $1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}$ at hidden layer |
| Sigmoid Derivative Block | Provides the factor $\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$ at output layer |
| Parameter Updater | Applies $W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ |
| Loop-back arrow | Updated weights feed back into forward pass — *iterative training* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept to Remember:** The cycle in the diagram (Updated Weights → Forward Propagation) is what makes training *iterative*. One complete forward + backward + update cycle constitutes **one epoch of training** (for batch size = full dataset) or **one iteration** (for mini-batches).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define the tanh activation function. Write its mathematical form, output range, and derivative.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The hyperbolic tangent (tanh) is a non-linear activation function used in hidden layers of neural networks. It is defined as:

$$
\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}
$$

**Output Range:** $(-1, +1)$ — zero-centred, which is an advantage over the sigmoid function whose range is $(0, 1)$.

**Derivative:** The derivative of tanh is given by:

$$
\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)
$$

The maximum value of this derivative is **1**, occurring at $x = 0$. This stronger gradient (compared to sigmoid's max of $0.25$) makes tanh preferable in hidden layers for faster convergence.

---

**Q2. State and explain the chain rule of calculus. How is it applied during backpropagation in a neural network?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **chain rule** states that for composite functions, the derivative of the outer function with respect to the innermost variable equals the product of the derivatives of each intermediate function. Formally, if $z = f(g(x))$, then:

$$
\frac{dz}{dx} = \frac{dz}{dy} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

where $y = g(x)$.

**Application in Backpropagation:** In a neural network, the loss $L$ depends on a weight $W^{(l)}$ through a chain of intermediate computations across layers. The chain rule allows us to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ as a product of *local gradients*:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdots \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}
$$

This product formulation enables efficient gradient computation by propagating errors from the output layer backward to the input layer — hence the name **backpropagation**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the derivative of the tanh activation function starting from its exponential definition. Show all algebraic steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-layer feedforward neural network with tanh activation in the hidden layer and sigmoid activation in the output layer, derive the complete gradient expressions for both weight matrices $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$ and $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ using backpropagation with binary cross-entropy loss. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

- Write $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$.
- Apply the quotient rule to obtain $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4}{(e^{x} + e^{-x})^{2}}$.
- Express the result as $\frac{dy}{dx} = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$ using the algebraic identity $(a+b)^{2} - (a-b)^{2} = 4ab$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.2**. Key derived expressions:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y)\,(\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{T}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \left[ (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \odot \bigl(1 - (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^{2}\bigr) \right]\, \mathbf{x}^{T}
$$

The complete chain-rule walkthrough is:

- Output layer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial W^{(2)}}$ → simplifies to $(\hat{y} - y)(a^{(1)})^T$.
- Hidden layer: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial a^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial z^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}}$, with the tanh derivative $1-(a^{(1)})^2$ appearing explicitly in the third factor.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the sigmoid and tanh activation functions across at least six properties. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a single-neuron hidden layer network with input $x = 1$, weight $W^{(1)} = 0.5$, bias $b^{(1)} = 0$, and tanh activation. The hidden output feeds into an output neuron with weight $W^{(2)} = 0.8$, bias $b^{(2)} = 0$, sigmoid activation, and target $y = 1$. Using binary cross-entropy loss, compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}}$ step-by-step using the chain rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Comparison Table (refer to **Section 2.4**):

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, +1)$ |
| Zero-centred | No | Yes |
| Max derivative | $0.25$ | $1.0$ |
| Computational cost | 1 exp | 2 exps |
| Use case | Output (binary) | Hidden layers |
| Vanishing gradient | More severe | Less severe |

- **Sigmoid advantage:** Output naturally interpretable as probability.
- **Sigmoid disadvantage:** Not zero-centred → zig-zag gradient updates.
- **Tanh advantage:** Zero-centred → smoother optimization.
- **Tanh disadvantage:** Still saturates for large $\vert x \vert$, causing vanishing gradients.

**Model Solution for (b) — Worked Numerical Example:**

**Forward Pass:**

Step 1: Compute $z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)} = 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0 = 0.5$.

Step 2: Compute $a^{(1)} = \tanh(z^{(1)}) = \tanh(0.5) \approx 0.4621$.

Step 3: Compute $z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = 0.8 \cdot 0.4621 + 0 \approx 0.3697$.

Step 4: Compute $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.3697}} \approx 0.5914$.

Step 5: Compute $L = -\bigl[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\bigr] = -\bigl[1 \cdot \log(0.5914) + 0\bigr] \approx 0.5251$.

**Backward Pass — Gradient for $W^{(2)}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot a^{(1)} = (0.5914 - 1) \cdot 0.4621 \approx (-0.4086) \cdot 0.4621 \approx -0.1888
$$

**Backward Pass — Gradient for $W^{(1)}$:**

Step 6: Compute error signal $\delta^{(2)} = \hat{y} - y = -0.4086$.

Step 7: Compute $\frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial z^{(1)}} = 1 - (a^{(1)})^{2} = 1 - (0.4621)^{2} \approx 1 - 0.2135 = 0.7865$.

Step 8: Compute $\frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial a^{(1)}} = W^{(2)} = 0.8$.

Step 9: Combine via chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot W^{(2)} \cdot \bigl[1 - (a^{(1)})^{2}\bigr] \cdot x = (-0.4086)(0.8)(0.7865)(1) \approx -0.2571
$$

**Interpretation:** Both gradients are negative, indicating that increasing either weight would *decrease* the loss. With learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, the weight updates would be $W^{(2)} \leftarrow 0.8 + 0.01888$ and $W^{(1)} \leftarrow 0.5 + 0.02571$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Tanh + Backpropagation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule structure in the derivative of tanh:** Many students write $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$ without showing the quotient-rule derivation. KTU examiners award partial marks, but full marks require the algebraic derivation starting from $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing the layer indexing:** Students frequently mix up which layer uses tanh and which uses sigmoid. **Tanh is in the hidden layer, sigmoid is in the output layer** (for binary classification). Marking the wrong activation derivative in the wrong layer loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Missing the transposes in matrix derivatives:** For $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$, students often write $(a^{(l-1)})$ without the transpose, resulting in a dimensionally incorrect expression. This is a common 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Not stating the chain rule explicitly:** In 14-mark questions, students jump directly to the final gradient without explicitly listing the chain-rule factorization. Examiners expect the explicit product structure: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial W}$ — missing this loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in sigmoid + BCE simplification:** The clean result $(\hat{y} - y)$ comes from sign cancellation. A sign error during simplification produces $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} = (y - \hat{y}) a^{(1)}$, which is wrong by a sign — full credit is denied.
>
> 6. **Not specifying element-wise (Hadamard) product:** When combining vectors element-by-element, students forget to mention $\odot$. This loses 1 mark in derivation-heavy questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tanh & Backpropagation Chain Rule**

- **Tanh definition:** $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$, range $(-1, +1)$, zero-centred.
- **Tanh derivative (memorize):** $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$, max value = $1$ at $x = 0$.
- **Tanh advantage over sigmoid:** Zero-centred output + stronger gradient (max $1$ vs $0.25$).
- **Chain rule (single variable):** $\frac{dz}{dx} = \frac{dz}{dy} \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$.
- **Chain rule (neural network):** Product of local gradients across all intermediate layers.
- **Backpropagation output-layer gradient (sigmoid + BCE):** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y)(a^{(1)})^T$.
- **Backpropagation hidden-layer gradient (with tanh):** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot W^{(2)} \odot (1 - (a^{(1)})^2) \cdot x^T$.
- **The "magic cancellation":** Sigmoid output + Binary cross-entropy loss $\Rightarrow$ gradient = $(\hat{y} - y)$.
- **Error term propagation:** $\delta^{(l)} = ((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$.
- **Weight update:** $W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ with $\eta$ as learning rate.
- **Vanishing gradient link:** Tanh derivative $1 - (a^{(1)})^2 \to 0$ when $\vert a^{(1)} \vert \to 1$ → gradients vanish in saturated neurons.
- **Local gradient interpretation:** Each layer contributes ONE factor to the chain-rule product — its activation function's derivative evaluated at its pre-activation.
- **Zero-centred importance:** Tanh's symmetric range prevents gradient updates from being biased to one direction, enabling faster convergence than sigmoid in hidden layers.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Expect a 14-mark derivation question (tanh derivative + backprop gradients) and a 3-mark conceptual question (chain rule or activation function comparison).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regularization - L1
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regularization - L1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **L1 Regularization (LASSO — Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator):** A regularization technique that adds a penalty term equal to the **sum of the absolute values of the model weights (coefficients)** to the standard loss function. It is mathematically formulated by adding the term $\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ to the loss, where $\lambda \geq 0$ is the regularization hyperparameter and $\theta_i$ represents the model's learnable parameters.

The modified objective (cost) function for a regression/classification model under L1 regularization is:

$$J(\theta) = L(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$$

Where:
- $L(\theta)$ = Original loss function (e.g., MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification)
- $\lambda$ = Regularization strength (a **non-negative scalar hyperparameter**)
- $\vert \theta_i \vert$ = Absolute value of the $i^{th}$ weight
- $n$ = Total number of weights in the model

> [!NOTE]
> **L1 regularization is the cornerstone of *sparse learning***. Unlike L2, it actively drives many weights to *exactly zero*, producing compact, interpretable models and performing **automatic feature selection**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Strict Diet Coach"**
>
> Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a 7-day trip, but the airline enforces a strict **5 kg weight limit**. You have 20 items to choose from, each with a different weight. A "L2 coach" would tell you, *"Try to make every item a little lighter — share the burden."* A "L1 coach," however, is much more ruthless: *"You can only keep a few items — the rest, **leave behind completely**."* The suitcase (your model) ends up containing only a handful of essential items (features), and the rest have weight exactly **zero**.

**Key Intuition Highlights:**
- **L1 = "Selection"** → It eliminates unimportant features entirely.
- **L2 = "Shrinking"** → It shrinks all features but keeps them all.
- L1 is preferred when you suspect that **only a few features** out of many are truly useful (the **sparse assumption**).

---

## 1.3 The L1 Penalty Term — Visual Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Components in L1 Regularization**
>
> - **Penalty Term:** $\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ (also called the **L1 norm** of the weight vector, $\Vert \theta \Vert_1$)
> - **L1 Norm:** $\Vert \theta \Vert_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$
> - **Hyperparameter $\lambda$:** Controls the trade-off between fitting the data and keeping weights small. As $\lambda \to \infty$, all weights collapse to zero. As $\lambda \to 0$, the model reverts to standard empirical risk minimization.
> - **Unit:** Dimensionless (scalar multiplier on the loss).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational "Why" — Why L1 Produces Sparsity

L1 regularization drives weights to **exactly zero** because of a unique property of the absolute value function: it has a **non-differentiable kink at the origin**. At $\theta_i = 0$, the subgradient includes the entire range $[-1, +1]$, meaning the gradient penalty can perfectly cancel the loss gradient — *freezing* that weight at zero.

| Aspect | L1 Regularization | L2 Regularization |
|---|---|---|
| **Penalty Term** | $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_i \vert$ | $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ |
| **Norm Type** | L1 Norm ($\Vert \theta \Vert_1$) | L2 Norm squared ($\Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$) |
| **Sparsity** | ✅ Yes (many weights = 0) | ❌ No (weights shrink but stay non-zero) |
| **Feature Selection** | ✅ Built-in | ❌ Not built-in |
| **Differentiable at 0** | ❌ No (subgradient used) | ✅ Yes |
| **Geometric Shape of Constraint** | Diamond ($\vert\theta_1\vert + \vert\theta_2\vert \leq c$) | Circle ($\theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 \leq c$) |
| **Solution Method** | Soft-thresholding | Closed-form (normal equations) |
| **Computational Cost** | Higher (non-smooth optimization) | Lower (smooth, convex) |
| **Best Use Case** | High-dimensional, sparse data | Multicollinearity, all features useful |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Gradient Descent Update Rule)

The update rule for the $i^{th}$ weight during gradient descent is derived as follows:

> **Step 1:** Compute the standard loss gradient: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_i}$

> **Step 2:** Compute the gradient of the L1 penalty: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i}\left(\lambda \vert \theta_i \vert\right) = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$, where $\text{sign}(\theta_i) = +1$ if $\theta_i > 0$, $-1$ if $\theta_i < 0$, and any value in $[-1, +1]$ if $\theta_i = 0$.

> **Step 3:** Combine gradients: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_i} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_i} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$

> **Step 4:** Apply the update: $\theta_i^{(t+1)} = \theta_i^{(t)} - \alpha \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_i} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i) \right)$

Where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield L1 Regularization Formula Sheet**

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 Cost Function** | $J(\theta) = L(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Full objective with L1 penalty |
| **L1 Norm** | $\Vert \theta \Vert_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Sum of absolute values of weights |
| **Subgradient of $\vert x \vert$** | $\nabla \vert x \vert = \text{sign}(x)$ | Derivative (subgradient) of absolute value |
| **Sign Function** | $\text{sign}(x) = \begin{cases} +1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \\ -1 & x < 0 \end{cases}$ | Sign indicator |
| **Weight Update Rule** | $\theta_i := \theta_i - \alpha \left( \nabla L(\theta_i) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i) \right)$ | GD update with L1 |
| **Soft-Thresholding Operator** | $S_\lambda(z) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0)$ | Closed-form proximal solution |
| **Constrained Form** | $\min_\theta L(\theta) \;\; \text{s.t.} \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert \leq C$ | Equivalent constrained formulation |
| **Sparsity Property** | $\lim_{\lambda \to \infty} \theta_i = 0 \;\;\forall i$ | Large $\lambda$ forces all weights to zero |
| **MSE + L1 (Regression)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)})^2 + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Lasso Regression form |
| **Cross-Entropy + L1 (Classification)** | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\left[y^{(j)}\log(h_\theta(x^{(j)})) + (1-y^{(j)})\log(1-h_\theta(x^{(j)}))\right] + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Logistic Regression with L1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** L1 regularization is widely used in **bioinformatics** (gene selection from thousands of features), **text classification** (TF-IDF with millions of words), **signal processing** (compressed sensing), and **embedded ML** (deploying models on devices with limited memory). Its sparsity produces smaller, faster, and more interpretable models — critical for KTU-aligned industry 4.0 applications.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The L1 Regularized Cost Function for Linear Regression (Lasso)

### **Problem Setup**
Given a training dataset with $m$ samples $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$, where $x^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ (including bias) and $y^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}$. The hypothesis is $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$.

The **standard Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss is:

$$L(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2$$

Adding the **L1 penalty term**, the new regularized cost function (Lasso Regression) is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$$

> **Note:** The summation $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ typically *excludes* the bias term $\theta_0$ by convention, as regularizing the bias restricts the model's ability to shift the decision boundary appropriately.

### **Derivation of the Gradient (Subgradient)**

We compute the partial derivative of $J(\theta)$ with respect to a generic weight $\theta_i$ (for $i \geq 1$):

**Step 1: Differentiate the MSE term**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the L1 penalty term**

The absolute value function is non-differentiable at zero, so we use the **subgradient**:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} \left[ \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert \right] = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$$

**Step 3: Combine both terms**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_i} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$$

**Step 4: Apply the gradient descent update rule**

$$\theta_i^{(t+1)} = \theta_i^{(t)} - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i^{(t)}) \right]$$

This update is repeated iteratively until convergence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soft-Thresholding as the Proximal Operator of L1

L1-regularized problems are typically solved more efficiently via **proximal gradient methods** (e.g., ISTA — Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm).

### **Proximal Operator Definition**

The proximal operator of a function $g(\theta)$ is defined as:

$$\text{prox}_{\lambda g}(z) = \arg\min_{\theta} \left[ g(\theta) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \Vert \theta - z \Vert_2^2 \right]$$

### **Proximal Operator of L1 Norm**

For $g(\theta) = \Vert \theta \Vert_1 = \sum_i \vert \theta_i \vert$, we solve element-wise:

$$\text{prox}_{\lambda \vert \cdot \vert}(z_i) = \arg\min_{\theta_i} \left[ \vert \theta_i \vert + \frac{1}{2\lambda} (\theta_i - z_i)^2 \right]$$

**Step 1: Set derivative to zero (subgradient condition)**

The subgradient optimality condition is:

$$0 \in \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i^*) + \frac{1}{\lambda} (\theta_i^* - z_i)$$

**Step 2: Solve for three cases**

- **Case A:** If $\theta_i^* > 0$, then $\text{sign}(\theta_i^*) = +1$:

$$0 = \lambda + \frac{1}{\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i) \implies \theta_i^* = z_i - \lambda^2$$

- **Case B:** If $\theta_i^* < 0$, then $\text{sign}(\theta_i^*) = -1$:

$$0 = -\lambda + \frac{1}{\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i) \implies \theta_i^* = z_i + \lambda^2$$

- **Case C:** If $\theta_i^* = 0$, we need the subgradient to contain zero:

$$-\lambda \leq \frac{1}{\lambda}(0 - z_i) \leq \lambda \implies \vert z_i \vert \leq \lambda^2$$

**Step 3: Combine into the soft-thresholding formula**

After rescaling $\lambda$ appropriately (by absorbing constants), the final closed-form is:

$$\text{prox}_{\lambda \vert \cdot \vert}(z_i) = S_\lambda(z_i) = \text{sign}(z_i) \cdot \max(\vert z_i \vert - \lambda, \; 0)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Meaning:** Any input value with magnitude less than $\lambda$ is **crushed to zero**. Values with magnitude greater than $\lambda$ are **shrunk toward zero** by exactly $\lambda$. This is the mathematical reason L1 produces sparse solutions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Geometric Intuition: Why the L1 Ball Yields Sparse Solutions

Consider a 2D weight space $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$.

**Step 1: The L1 constraint region** $\vert \theta_1 \vert + \vert \theta_2 \vert \leq C$ forms a **diamond** (rotated square) with corners on the axes at $(\pm C, 0)$ and $(0, \pm C)$.

**Step 2: The L2 constraint region** $\theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 \leq C$ forms a **circle** with no corners on the axes.

**Step 3: The loss contours** (e.g., MSE) form **ellipsoids** centered at the unconstrained optimum $\theta^*$.

**Step 4: The regularized solution** is the point where the loss contour is tangent to the constraint region.

- For the **L1 diamond**, the ellipsoid is most likely to first touch the diamond at a **corner lying on an axis** — meaning one of $\theta_1$ or $\theta_2$ is **exactly zero** (sparsity).
- For the **L2 circle**, the ellipsoid touches tangentially at a point where both coordinates are non-zero (no sparsity).

This is the **fundamental geometric reason** L1 regularization produces sparse models while L2 does not.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example:** A linear regression model has weights $\theta = [\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3]^T = [2.0, -1.0, 0.3]^T$. Compute the L1 penalty and the new weight after **one gradient descent update** with $\alpha = 0.1$ and $\lambda = 0.5$, assuming the MSE gradient gives $\nabla L = [0.8, 0.2, -0.4]^T$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the L1 penalty**

$$P_{L1} = \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{3} \vert \theta_i \vert = 0.5 \times (\vert 2.0 \vert + \vert -1.0 \vert + \vert 0.3 \vert) = 0.5 \times 3.3 = 1.65$$

**Step 2: Compute subgradients of the L1 penalty**

$$\nabla P_{L1} = \lambda \cdot [\text{sign}(2.0), \; \text{sign}(-1.0), \; \text{sign}(0.3)]^T = 0.5 \times [1, -1, 1]^T = [0.5, -0.5, 0.5]^T$$

**Step 3: Compute total gradient**

$$\nabla J = \nabla L + \nabla P_{L1} = [0.8 + 0.5, \; 0.2 - 0.5, \; -0.4 + 0.5]^T = [1.3, -0.3, 0.1]^T$$

**Step 4: Apply the update rule**

$$\theta^{(1)} = \theta^{(0)} - \alpha \cdot \nabla J = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ -1.0 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \times \begin{bmatrix} 1.3 \\ -0.3 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 - 0.13 \\ -1.0 - (-0.03) \\ 0.3 - 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.87 \\ -0.97 \\ 0.29 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Result:** All three weights are pulled slightly toward zero, but none crosses zero in a single step. With continued training and a larger $\lambda$, the smallest weight ($\theta_3 = 0.3$) would likely be driven to exactly zero.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 L1 Regularization — Functional Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Training Data] --> B[Initialize Weights theta]
    B --> C[Forward Pass: Compute Prediction h_x]
    C --> D[Compute MSE Loss L_theta]
    D --> E[Compute L1 Penalty lambda times sum of absolute theta]
    E --> F[Compute Total Cost J = L + L1 Penalty]
    F --> G{Converged?}
    G -- No --> H[Compute Subgradient: sign theta times lambda]
    H --> I[Update Weights: theta = theta minus alpha times gradient]
    I --> C
    G -- Yes --> J[Output Sparse Model: many weights exactly zero]
    J --> K[Feature Selection: Keep non-zero weights]
    K --> L[End: Deploy Interpretable Model]
```

## 4.2 L1 vs L2 — Geometric Constraint Region Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2D Weight Space theta1, theta2] --> B[L1 Constraint: |theta1| + |theta2| <= C]
    A --> C[L2 Constraint: theta1 squared + theta2 squared <= C]
    B --> D[Diamond Shape with corners ON axes]
    C --> E[Circle Shape with NO corners on axes]
    D --> F[Loss ellipsoid touches at corner]
    E --> G[Loss ellipsoid touches tangentially off-axis]
    F --> H[Sparse Solution: one weight equals zero]
    G --> I[Dense Solution: both weights non-zero]
```

## 4.3 Soft-Thresholding Operator Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input value z] --> B{Magnitude of z compared to lambda}
    B -- |z| less than lambda --> C[Output = 0: weight eliminated]
    B -- |z| greater than lambda and z positive --> D[Output = z minus lambda: shrunk toward zero]
    B -- |z| greater than lambda and z negative --> E[Output = z plus lambda: shrunk toward zero]
    C --> F[Sparse Model: feature dropped]
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

## 4.4 L1 Regularization Effect on Model Complexity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Model Complexity vs Regularization] --> B[lambda small: High variance, overfitting, dense weights]
    A --> C[lambda medium: Balanced bias-variance, partial sparsity]
    A --> D[lambda large: High bias, underfitting, all weights near zero]
    B --> E[Many non-zero weights: high complexity]
    C --> F[Some zero weights: moderate complexity]
    D --> G[All weights zero: trivial model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:**
> - Diagram 4.1 shows the iterative training loop with the L1 penalty integrated.
> - Diagram 4.2 illustrates the *fundamental geometric reason* L1 produces sparsity (corners on axes).
> - Diagram 4.3 shows the *soft-thresholding* behavior — the engine that zeroes out small weights.
> - Diagram 4.4 captures the bias-variance trade-off controlled by $\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Define L1 regularization. Write the L1-regularized cost function for linear regression and explain how it differs from standard MSE.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** L1 regularization, also known as **LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)**, is a technique that prevents overfitting by adding a penalty term proportional to the sum of the **absolute values** of the model weights to the loss function.

**Cost Function (1 Mark):** The L1-regularized cost function is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$$

**Difference from MSE (1 Mark):** The standard MSE loss has only the first term (squared error). L1 adds a second term — the **L1 penalty** $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_i \vert$ — that penalizes large weights and drives many weights to **exactly zero**, producing a sparse model that performs automatic feature selection. Standard MSE has no such penalty and no sparsity guarantee.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Explain why L1 regularization produces sparse models while L2 regularization does not. Mention the role of the subgradient in L1.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Geometric Reason (2 Marks):** The L1 constraint region $\sum \vert \theta_i \vert \leq C$ forms a **diamond** with sharp corners lying *on the coordinate axes*. When the loss ellipsoid is expanded until it touches the constraint, contact is most likely to occur at one of these corners, forcing one or more weights to **exactly zero** (sparsity). In contrast, the L2 constraint $\sum \theta_i^2 \leq C$ is a smooth **circle** with no corners, so contact occurs at a point where all weights remain non-zero.

**Subgradient Role (1 Mark):** The L1 penalty $\vert \theta \vert$ is **non-differentiable at zero**, so we use a **subgradient** $\nabla \vert \theta \vert = \text{sign}(\theta)$. At $\theta = 0$, the subgradient can take any value in $[-1, +1]$, which allows the penalty gradient to exactly cancel the loss gradient — *freezing* the weight at zero. This is the analytical mechanism behind L1's sparsity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lasso Regression Derivation and Analysis**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule for **L1-regularized linear regression (LASSO)** starting from the regularized cost function. Clearly state the role of the subgradient. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **b)** Consider a 3-feature linear model with current weights $\theta = [1.5, 0.2, -0.8]^T$. Compute the **L1 penalty** and the **subgradient of the L1 term** for $\lambda = 0.4$. If the MSE gradient is $\nabla L = [0.5, 0.1, -0.3]^T$ and learning rate $\alpha = 0.05$, compute the **new weights** after one update. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Derivation of L1-Regularized Gradient Descent Update**

**Step 1: Start with the L1-regularized cost function (LASSO)** *(1 Mark)*

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the MSE term w.r.t. $\theta_i$** *(1 Mark)*

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the L1 penalty using the subgradient** *(2 Marks)*

Since $\vert \theta_i \vert$ is non-differentiable at zero, we use the subgradient:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} \left[ \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert \right] = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$$

where $\text{sign}(\theta_i) = +1$ if $\theta_i > 0$, $-1$ if $\theta_i < 0$, and any value in $[-1, +1]$ if $\theta_i = 0$.

**Step 4: Combine both terms into the total gradient** *(1 Mark)*

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_i} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i)$$

**Step 5: Apply the gradient descent update** *(1 Mark)*

$$\theta_i^{(t+1)} = \theta_i^{(t)} - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(j)}) - y^{(j)} \right) \cdot x_i^{(j)} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i^{(t)}) \right]$$

**Step 6: Role of the subgradient** *(1 Mark)*

The subgradient $\text{sign}(\theta_i)$ provides a constant-magnitude push toward zero (either $+1$ or $-1$ depending on the sign of $\theta_i$) **regardless of how small $\theta_i$ is**. This is the key contrast with L2, where the gradient $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i}(\lambda \theta_i^2) = 2\lambda \theta_i$ *shrinks toward zero as the weight shrinks* — never reaching zero in finite steps. L1's constant push makes exact zeros attainable, producing sparsity.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:** $\theta = [1.5, 0.2, -0.8]^T$, $\lambda = 0.4$, $\nabla L = [0.5, 0.1, -0.3]^T$, $\alpha = 0.05$

**Step 1: Compute the L1 penalty** *(2 Marks)*

$$P_{L1} = \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{3} \vert \theta_i \vert = 0.4 \times (\vert 1.5 \vert + \vert 0.2 \vert + \vert -0.8 \vert) = 0.4 \times 2.5 = 1.0$$

**Step 2: Compute the subgradient of the L1 term** *(2 Marks)*

$$\nabla P_{L1} = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta) = 0.4 \times [1, 1, -1]^T = [0.4, 0.4, -0.4]^T$$

**Step 3: Compute the total gradient** *(1 Mark)*

$$\nabla J = \nabla L + \nabla P_{L1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.1 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.4 \\ -0.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 0.5 \\ -0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply the weight update rule** *(2 Marks)*

$$\theta^{(1)} = \theta^{(0)} - \alpha \cdot \nabla J = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.8 \end{bmatrix} - 0.05 \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 0.5 \\ -0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 - 0.045 \\ 0.2 - 0.025 \\ -0.8 - (-0.035) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.455 \\ 0.175 \\ -0.765 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Answer:** The new weights after one update are $\theta^{(1)} = [1.455, 0.175, -0.765]^T$, and the L1 penalty is $1.0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Soft-Thresholding and Geometric Interpretation**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **a)** Define the **proximal operator** of the L1 norm. Derive the **soft-thresholding formula** $S_\lambda(z) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0)$ step by step. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **b)** With the help of a labeled diagram (or a precise textual description), explain the **geometric difference** between the L1 and L2 constraint regions in 2D weight space. Show why L1 produces sparse solutions while L2 does not. Mention two real-world applications where L1 is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Derivation of Soft-Thresholding**

**Step 1: Define the proximal operator** *(1 Mark)*

The proximal operator of a function $g(\theta)$ with parameter $\lambda$ is:

$$\text{prox}_{\lambda g}(z) = \arg\min_{\theta} \left[ g(\theta) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \Vert \theta - z \Vert_2^2 \right]$$

It finds a point $\theta$ close to $z$ while keeping $g(\theta)$ small.

**Step 2: Set $g(\theta) = \Vert \theta \Vert_1$ and decompose element-wise** *(1 Mark)*

$$\text{prox}_{\lambda \vert \cdot \vert}(z_i) = \arg\min_{\theta_i} \left[ \vert \theta_i \vert + \frac{1}{2\lambda} (\theta_i - z_i)^2 \right]$$

**Step 3: Write the subgradient optimality condition** *(1 Mark)*

$$0 \in \partial \left[ \vert \theta_i^* \vert + \frac{1}{2\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i)^2 \right] = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i^*) + \frac{1}{\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta_i^* > 0$** *(1 Mark)*

If $\theta_i^* > 0$, then $\text{sign}(\theta_i^*) = +1$:

$$\lambda + \frac{1}{\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i) = 0 \implies \theta_i^* = z_i - \lambda^2$$

This is valid only if $z_i > \lambda^2$ (so the result is positive).

**Step 5: Solve for $\theta_i^* < 0$** *(1 Mark)*

If $\theta_i^* < 0$, then $\text{sign}(\theta_i^*) = -1$:

$$-\lambda + \frac{1}{\lambda}(\theta_i^* - z_i) = 0 \implies \theta_i^* = z_i + \lambda^2$$

This is valid only if $z_i < -\lambda^2$ (so the result is negative).

**Step 6: Combine and simplify to the soft-thresholding formula** *(2 Marks)*

Rescaling $\lambda$ appropriately and combining all three cases:

$$S_\lambda(z_i) = \text{sign}(z_i) \cdot \max(\vert z_i \vert - \lambda, \; 0)$$

This means:
- If $\vert z_i \vert \leq \lambda$: output is $0$ (feature eliminated).
- If $\vert z_i \vert > \lambda$: output is $\text{sign}(z_i) \cdot (\vert z_i \vert - \lambda)$ (shrunk toward zero).

---

### **Part (b) — Geometric Interpretation and Applications**

**Geometric Description in 2D Weight Space** *(4 Marks)*

In the 2D weight space $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$:

- **L1 constraint region:** $\vert \theta_1 \vert + \vert \theta_2 \vert \leq C$ — this is a **diamond** (rotated square) with vertices at $(C, 0)$, $(-C, 0)$, $(0, C)$, and $(0, -C)$. The corners lie exactly **on the coordinate axes**.

- **L2 constraint region:** $\theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 \leq C$ — this is a **circle** centered at the origin with radius $\sqrt{C}$. The boundary never touches the coordinate axes at a corner.

The **unconstrained loss optimum** $\theta^*$ lies somewhere in the plane. The loss contours (e.g., MSE contours) form concentric ellipsoids around $\theta^*$.

The **regularized solution** is the point where an expanding loss contour is **tangent** to the constraint boundary.

- For L1 (diamond): the ellipsoid first contacts the diamond at a **corner on an axis** → one of the weights becomes **exactly zero** → **sparse solution**.
- For L2 (circle): the ellipsoid contacts the circle at a **point off both axes** → both weights remain non-zero → **dense solution**.

**Real-World Applications Where L1 is Preferred** *(3 Marks — 1.5 each)*

1. **Bioinformatics and Genomics:** Gene expression datasets often have **thousands of features** (genes) but only a few hundred samples. L1 automatically selects the small subset of genes that are truly predictive of a disease (e.g., cancer classification), producing interpretable biomarker panels.

2. **Text Classification and NLP:** In spam detection or sentiment analysis with bag-of-words or TF-IDF features, the vocabulary can exceed **100,000 words**. L1 selects only the few hundred most discriminative words, drastically reducing model size and inference time — critical for deployment on edge devices or mobile applications.

3. **Compressed Sensing and Signal Processing:** L1 enables reconstruction of sparse signals from far fewer measurements than the Nyquist rate requires (a foundational result from Candès, Romberg, and Tao, 2006). Used in MRI imaging acceleration, where fewer measurements mean faster scans and reduced patient discomfort.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in L1 Regularization Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the subgradient (loses ~2 marks):** Many students write $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_i} \vert \theta_i \vert = 1$ or $2\theta_i$ — both are **wrong**. The correct subgradient is $\text{sign}(\theta_i)$, and you **must** mention the non-differentiability at $\theta_i = 0$.
>
> 2. **Confusing L1 with L2 formulas (loses ~2 marks):** Mixing up $\sum \vert \theta_i \vert$ with $\sum \theta_i^2$ is a frequent error. Always state explicitly which norm you are using. KTU examiners deduct heavily for formula confusion.
>
> 3. **Omitting the bias term convention (loses ~1 mark):** The bias $\theta_0$ is **not** regularized by convention. Failing to mention this in derivation questions costs a mark.
>
> 4. **Not explaining the geometric intuition (loses ~2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme questions on Module 4 frequently test the **diamond vs. circle** geometric interpretation. Writing only the formula without explaining *why* the diamond produces sparsity will cost significant marks.
>
> 5. **Wrong sign in the update rule (loses ~1–2 marks):** The L1 penalty *increases* the gradient magnitude (it pushes weights toward zero, but the sign convention matters). Always write the update as $\theta_i := \theta_i - \alpha(\nabla L + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i))$ and verify that weights are moving toward zero in your numerical example.
>
> 6. **Ignoring scaling of $\lambda$ (loses ~1 mark):** State clearly that $\lambda \geq 0$ and explain the limiting behaviors: $\lambda = 0$ → no regularization, $\lambda \to \infty$ → all weights → 0.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — L1 Regularization (LASSO)**
>
> ✅ **Full Name:** Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO), introduced by **Robert Tibshirani (1996)**.
>
> ✅ **Penalty Term:** $\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ — sum of absolute values of weights (L1 norm).
>
> ✅ **Key Property:** Produces **sparse models** — many weights become **exactly zero**.
>
> ✅ **Subgradient:** $\nabla \vert \theta \vert = \text{sign}(\theta)$, non-differentiable at zero.
>
> ✅ **Geometric Shape:** Diamond (constraint $\sum \vert \theta_i \vert \leq C$) with corners on axes → **sparsity**.
>
> ✅ **Soft-Thresholding:** $S_\lambda(z) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(\vert z \vert - \lambda, 0)$ is the closed-form proximal solution.
>
> ✅ **Update Rule:** $\theta_i := \theta_i - \alpha(\nabla L(\theta_i) + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_i))$.
>
> ✅ **Hyperparameter $\lambda$:** $\lambda = 0$ → no regularization; large $\lambda$ → high bias, strong sparsity; tune via cross-validation.
>
> ✅ **Bias Term $\theta_0$:** NOT regularized by convention.
>
> ✅ **Feature Selection:** L1 performs **automatic feature selection** — non-zero weights = selected features.
>
> ✅ **vs. L2 Comparison:** L1 → sparse, selects features, diamond constraint. L2 → dense, shrinks all, circle constraint, differentiable everywhere.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Domains:** Bioinformatics (gene selection), NLP (vocabulary reduction), compressed sensing (MRI), embedded ML (edge deployment).
>
> ✅ **Optimization Methods:** Proximal gradient (ISTA/FISTA), coordinate descent, subgradient methods — L1 is **non-smooth**, so plain GD with a fixed learning rate is suboptimal.
>
> ✅ **Limitations:** Struggles with highly correlated features (arbitrarily picks one); can select at most $n$ features when $n < m$; less stable than Elastic Net for grouped variables.
